2.23 Visual: Coefficient ↔ Graph Shape

🖼️ Effect of leading coefficient (a) on parabola

If a > 0 the parabola is a smile (∪). If a < 0 it is a frown (∩). The magnitude of a controls how narrow/wide the curve is.

🖼️ Discriminant Decision Tree

2.24 Real-Life Applications

🌉 Engineering

The arch of a suspension bridge follows a quadratic curve. Engineers use polynomial models to find the maximum height (vertex) and the points where the cable touches the road (zeroes).

🚀 Physics — Projectile Motion

The height of any object thrown vertically is h(t) = −(1/2)gt² + ut + h₀. The time when it hits the ground is a zero of this quadratic in t.

💰 Economics

Profit functions P(x) = revenue − cost are often quadratic. The zeroes give break-even points; the vertex gives the production level for maximum profit.

🏗️ Architecture

Domes, arches, and antenna dishes are often modelled with quadratic or cubic polynomials. Zeroes help locate support points.

📡 Signal Processing

Polynomials describe wave-shapes; their roots locate signal nulls — important in radio antennas and audio engineering.

2.25 Confidence Quiz (10 quick Q's)

CQ-1. Degree of (2x + 1)(x² − 3) is ___ .

3.

CQ-2. Number of zeroes of a constant non-zero polynomial is ___ .

0.

CQ-3. Sum of zeroes of 5x² − 10x + 2 is ___ .

2.

CQ-4. A quadratic with zeroes ±√7 is ___ .

x² − 7.

CQ-5. p(x) = x³ − 1, find p(1).

0 (so 1 is a zero).

CQ-6. Is √3·x + 2 a polynomial?

Yes — coefficient is irrational but power of x is 1 (whole number).

CQ-7. Number of real zeroes of x² + 1.

0.

CQ-8. Polynomial with sum = 0, product = −9.

x² − 9.

CQ-9. Type of polynomial of degree 4 is called ___ .

Bi-quadratic / Quartic.

CQ-10. Product of zeroes of 7x² − 2x + 5 is ___ .

5/7.

2.26 Bonus — 12 Challenge Problems

B-1. If α, β, γ are zeroes of x³ − 6x² + 11x − 6, find α² + β² + γ².

α+β+γ = 6, αβ+βγ+γα = 11. So α²+β²+γ² = 36 − 22 = 14.

B-2. If α, β are zeroes of 2x² − 4x − 3, find (α − β)².

(α+β)² − 4αβ = 4 − 4·(−3/2) = 4 + 6 = 10.

B-3. Form a polynomial with zeroes 2 and (1 + √2).

For real-coefficient polynomial with irrational zero, conjugate (1 − √2) must also be a zero. Three zeroes: 2, 1 + √2, 1 − √2.

(x − 2)·[(x − 1)² − 2] = (x − 2)(x² − 2x − 1) = x³ − 2x² − x − 2x² + 4x + 2 = x³ − 4x² + 3x + 2.

B-4. If α and β are zeroes of x² − 8x + λ such that |α − β| = 4, find λ.

(α+β)² − 4αβ = 16 ⇒ 64 − 4λ = 16 ⇒ λ = 12.

B-5. If 3 is a zero of p(x) = x³ − 2x² − 5x + 6, find the other zeroes.

Divide by (x − 3): quotient = x² + x − 2 = (x + 2)(x − 1). Other zeroes: −2, 1.

B-6. If polynomial 6x⁴ + 8x³ − 5x² + ax + b is exactly divisible by 2x² − 5, find a and b.

Divide and set remainder = 0. After long division: quotient 3x² + 4x + 5, remainder (a + 10)x + (b + 25). So a = −10, b = −25.

B-7. Find quadratic with zeroes (α + β) and αβ, where α and β are zeroes of x² − 7x + 12.

α + β = 7, αβ = 12. New zeroes: 7 and 12. Sum = 19, Product = 84. x² − 19x + 84.

B-8. Show that 1/2 is a zero of 2x³ − 11x² + 17x − 6 and find the other zeroes.

p(1/2) = 2·(1/8) − 11·(1/4) + 17·(1/2) − 6 = 1/4 − 11/4 + 34/4 − 24/4 = 0 ✓.

Divide by (2x − 1): quotient x² − 5x + 6 = (x − 2)(x − 3). Other zeroes: 2, 3.

B-9. If α and β are zeroes of polynomial 6x² − 7x + 2, find quadratic whose zeroes are α² and β².

α + β = 7/6, αβ = 2/6 = 1/3.

New sum = α² + β² = 49/36 − 2/3 = 49/36 − 24/36 = 25/36.

New product = α²β² = (1/3)² = 1/9.

x² − (25/36)x + 1/9; multiply by 36: 36x² − 25x + 4.

B-10. Find k so that x² + 2x + k is a factor of 2x⁴ + x³ − 14x² + 5x + 6. Also find other factors.

Divide and equate remainder to 0: get k = −3. Quotient = 2x² − 3x − 2 = (2x + 1)(x − 2). All zeroes: roots of x² + 2x − 3 = (x + 3)(x − 1) → −3, 1; and from quotient → −1/2, 2.

B-11. Find a cubic polynomial whose sum, sum-of-products-of-pairs, and product of zeroes are 2, −7, and −14 respectively.

x³ − 2x² − 7x + 14.

B-12. If α, β are zeroes of x² + 4x + 3, form a polynomial whose zeroes are 1 + (β/α) and 1 + (α/β).

α + β = −4, αβ = 3. Let p = 1 + β/α and q = 1 + α/β.

p + q = 2 + (α² + β²)/(αβ) = 2 + (16 − 6)/3 = 2 + 10/3 = 16/3.

pq = (α + β)²/(αβ) = 16/3.

Polynomial: x² − (16/3)x + 16/3; ×3: 3x² − 16x + 16.

2.27 Activities to Try at Home

🎨 Activity 1 — Graph Plotting

On graph paper, plot y = x², y = x² − 1, y = x² − 4, y = x² + 1. Find the x-intercepts (where they cut the x-axis). Notice how the graph shifts up/down and how the number of real zeroes changes.

🎨 Activity 2 — Coefficient Card Game

Write 10 random polynomials of the form x² + bx + c. For each, compute sum and product of zeroes using the formulas, then verify by actually solving.

🎨 Activity 3 — Story Polynomial

Pick any two friends' favourite numbers (say 4 and 7). Build a quadratic having them as zeroes: x² − 11x + 28. Share with a classmate and ask them to find the zeroes back.

2.28 Common Identities You'll Use

  • α² + β² = (α + β)² − 2αβ
  • α³ + β³ = (α + β)³ − 3αβ(α + β)
  • (α − β)² = (α + β)² − 4αβ
  • 1/α + 1/β = (α + β)/(αβ)
  • α/β + β/α = (α² + β²)/(αβ)
  • α³β + αβ³ = αβ(α² + β²)
  • α² · β² = (αβ)²

2.29 Self-Test (15 minutes)

  1. State the definition of a polynomial.
  2. Find the zero of 7x + 21.
  3. Find zeroes of x² + 5x + 6.
  4. Form a quadratic whose zeroes are 0 and −4.
  5. If α, β are zeroes of 2x² − 8x + 6, find α + β and αβ.
  6. For x² − kx + 16 to have equal zeroes, find k.
  7. How many zeroes does a quartic polynomial have at most?
  8. Find α² + β² for x² − 7x + 12.
  9. Verify that 2 is a zero of x³ − 4x² + 5x − 2.
  10. Write a polynomial with zeroes 1, 2, 3.

Answer Key: 1. (definition above); 2. x = −3; 3. −2, −3; 4. x² + 4x = x(x + 4); 5. 4 and 3; 6. ±8; 7. 4; 8. 25; 9. p(2) = 8 − 16 + 10 − 2 = 0 ✓; 10. (x − 1)(x − 2)(x − 3) = x³ − 6x² + 11x − 6.

2.30 Final Recap Bullet List

  • A polynomial uses whole-number powers of x only.
  • Degree decides the type: linear, quadratic, cubic, quartic…
  • A zero is a value of x making p(x) = 0.
  • Number of real zeroes ≤ degree.
  • For quadratic: α + β = −b/a, αβ = c/a.
  • To build a quadratic with zeroes α, β: use x² − (α + β)x + αβ.
  • The graph crosses x-axis exactly at real zeroes.
  • For equal roots, discriminant = 0.
  • For no real roots, discriminant < 0.

2.31 Step-by-Step Solved Word Problems

📝 WP-1. The product of two consecutive positive integers is 132. Form a polynomial and find them.

Let the integers be n and n + 1. Then n(n + 1) = 132 ⇒ n² + n − 132 = 0.

Factorise: (n − 11)(n + 12) = 0 ⇒ n = 11 (positive). Integers: 11 and 12.

📝 WP-2. The sum of a number and its reciprocal is 50/7. Form a polynomial and find the number.

Let the number be x. x + 1/x = 50/7 ⇒ 7x² − 50x + 7 = 0.

Factorise: 7x² − 49x − x + 7 = 7x(x − 7) − (x − 7) = (x − 7)(7x − 1). Numbers: 7 or 1/7.

📝 WP-3. The perimeter of a rectangle is 24 m and area is 35 sq m. Form a polynomial whose zeroes are length and breadth.

Let length = ℓ, breadth = b. Sum = ℓ + b = 12, Product = ℓb = 35.

Polynomial with zeroes ℓ, b: x² − 12x + 35 = (x − 5)(x − 7). Dimensions: 5 m and 7 m.

📝 WP-4. The sides of a right triangle differ by 7 cm and hypotenuse is 13 cm. Form a polynomial in the shorter side.

Let shorter side = x, longer = x + 7. By Pythagoras: x² + (x + 7)² = 169 ⇒ 2x² + 14x + 49 = 169 ⇒ x² + 7x − 60 = 0.

Factorise: (x − 5)(x + 12) = 0 ⇒ x = 5. Sides: 5, 12, 13 cm.

📝 WP-5. A two-digit number is such that the product of its digits is 14. If 45 is added, digits are reversed. Form a polynomial in one digit.

Let units digit = x, tens digit = y. Then xy = 14 and 10y + x + 45 = 10x + y ⇒ 9x − 9y = 45 ⇒ x − y = 5.

From xy = 14: y = 14/x. Substitute: x − 14/x = 5 ⇒ x² − 5x − 14 = 0 ⇒ (x − 7)(x + 2) = 0 ⇒ x = 7. So y = 2 and the number is 27.

2.32 Pattern Recognition — Special Quadratics

🎯 Type 1: Difference of squares

x² − a² = (x − a)(x + a). Zeroes are x = ±a. Example: x² − 49 ⇒ x = ±7.

🎯 Type 2: Perfect square trinomial

x² ± 2ax + a² = (x ± a)². Zeroes are repeated x = ∓a. Example: x² − 10x + 25 = (x − 5)² ⇒ x = 5 (double).

🎯 Type 3: Sum of roots = 0

x² − k (k > 0) has zeroes ±√k. Useful when the middle term is missing.

🎯 Type 4: Negative constant term

If a > 0 and c < 0, the polynomial always has two real zeroes of opposite signs (because product = c/a < 0).

🎯 Type 5: Positive constant, positive middle term

If b > 0 and c > 0 (both, with a > 0), both zeroes are negative (if real).

2.33 Skill Builder — Splitting the Middle Term

📚 The 3-Step Trick

  1. Multiply a (leading coefficient) and c (constant): get p = a·c.
  2. Find two numbers whose product = p and sum = b.
  3. Split the middle term using these two numbers, then group by twos.

📝 Drill 1 — Factorise 6x² + 11x + 4

p = 6·4 = 24, need two numbers with product 24 and sum 11: 3 and 8.

6x² + 3x + 8x + 4 = 3x(2x + 1) + 4(2x + 1) = (2x + 1)(3x + 4). Zeroes: −1/2, −4/3.

📝 Drill 2 — Factorise 4x² − 4x − 15

p = −60, sum = −4: numbers −10 and 6.

4x² − 10x + 6x − 15 = 2x(2x − 5) + 3(2x − 5) = (2x − 5)(2x + 3). Zeroes: 5/2, −3/2.

📝 Drill 3 — Factorise 12x² − 17x + 6

p = 72, sum = −17: numbers −9 and −8.

12x² − 9x − 8x + 6 = 3x(4x − 3) − 2(4x − 3) = (4x − 3)(3x − 2). Zeroes: 3/4, 2/3.

📝 Drill 4 — Factorise 9x² + 9x − 4

p = −36, sum = 9: numbers 12 and −3.

9x² + 12x − 3x − 4 = 3x(3x + 4) − (3x + 4) = (3x + 4)(3x − 1). Zeroes: −4/3, 1/3.

📝 Drill 5 — Factorise 2x² + 7x + 3

p = 6, sum = 7: 6 and 1.

2x² + 6x + x + 3 = 2x(x + 3) + (x + 3) = (x + 3)(2x + 1). Zeroes: −3, −1/2.

2.34 Bonus Concept — Remainder Theorem (Preview)

If a polynomial p(x) is divided by (x − a), the remainder is p(a).

So checking whether (x − a) is a factor of p(x) is the same as checking whether p(a) = 0.

This is called the Factor Theorem and we have already used it implicitly while finding zeroes.

📝 Example — Use Factor Theorem to test if (x − 2) divides x³ − 3x² + 4.

p(2) = 8 − 12 + 4 = 0. So yes, (x − 2) is a factor.

Divide: x³ − 3x² + 4 = (x − 2)(x² − x − 2) = (x − 2)(x − 2)(x + 1) = (x − 2)²(x + 1). Zeroes: 2, 2, −1.

2.35 Quick Reference: Cheat Sheet

If you are given…Do this
a quadratic — find zeroesFactorise by splitting middle term OR use quadratic formula.
zeroes — find polynomialUse x² − (sum)x + product.
"verify relation"Compute sum and product from zeroes; check with −b/a and c/a.
"value of k" for equal rootsSet discriminant b² − 4ac = 0.
"sum/product of new zeroes"Use identities α² + β² etc. and rewrite using original sum & product.
"graph of polynomial"Count crossings to count real zeroes.
"cubic with given zeroes"Multiply (x − α)(x − β)(x − γ).

2.36 Closing Thoughts

Polynomials are the building blocks of algebra. You used them in Class 9 (identities, factorisation). Now you've understood the deep link between algebra (the coefficients) and geometry (the graph) through the idea of zeroes. In Chapter 4 you will use exactly these ideas to solve quadratic equations — both by factorisation and by the quadratic formula.

Top 3 things to remember:

  1. Sum of zeroes = −b/a, Product of zeroes = c/a (for quadratic).
  2. To make a quadratic with given zeroes use x² − (sum)x + product.
  3. Zeroes = x-axis crossings on the graph.

🌟 Quote

"The roots of education are bitter, but the fruit is sweet." — Aristotle

Practise daily. Even 3 problems a day will make you a polynomial master in a month! 💪

2.37 Mixed Practice Set — 25 Quick Questions

MP-1. Find the degree of (x² + 3)(x³ − x + 1).

Highest x²·x³ = x⁵; degree 5.

MP-2. Is 0 a polynomial? Of what degree?

Yes — called the zero polynomial. Its degree is not defined.

MP-3. Find the value of p(x) = x² − 2x + 1 at x = 3.

p(3) = 9 − 6 + 1 = 4.

MP-4. The zero of p(x) = ax + b (a ≠ 0) is …

x = −b/a.

MP-5. Find zeroes of t² + 2t.

t(t + 2) → t = 0, t = −2.

MP-6. If α and β are zeroes of x² + 3x − 4, find (α − β)².

(α+β)² − 4αβ = 9 + 16 = 25.

MP-7. Polynomial with zeroes (3 + √5) and (3 − √5).

Sum = 6, Product = 4. → x² − 6x + 4.

MP-8. If sum of zeroes = product of zeroes for quadratic x² + bx + c, then …

−b = c ⇒ b + c = 0.

MP-9. For x² − 6x + 5, zeroes are 1 and …

5.

MP-10. Find p(−2) for p(x) = 2x³ + x² − 5.

2·(−8) + 4 − 5 = −16 + 4 − 5 = −17.

MP-11. The polynomial 2x⁴ − 5 + x³ has degree …

4.

MP-12. Number of distinct zeroes of (x − 1)²(x + 2):

2 (namely 1 and −2).

MP-13. Find zeroes of x² − √2x − 12.

Split: x² − √2x − 12, p = −12·1, sum = −√2 — try a = 3√2, b = −2√2 ⇒ 3√2 − 2√2 = √2, product = −12.
Wait we need sum = −√2: use −3√2 and 2√2.
x² − 3√2x + 2√2x − 12 = x(x − 3√2) + √2·(2x − ...) — easier: use quadratic formula.

x = [√2 ± √(2 + 48)]/2 = [√2 ± √50]/2 = [√2 ± 5√2]/2 = 3√2 or −2√2.

MP-14. If α and β are zeroes of 2x² + 3x − 5, find α² + β².

α + β = −3/2, αβ = −5/2. α² + β² = 9/4 + 5 = 29/4.

MP-15. Quadratic with zeroes −1/3 and 1/2.

Sum = 1/6, Product = −1/6. x² − (1/6)x − 1/6; ×6: 6x² − x − 1.

MP-16. If 2 and −3 are zeroes of x³ + ax² + bx − 6, find a and b.

p(2) = 8 + 4a + 2b − 6 = 0 ⇒ 4a + 2b = −2 ⇒ 2a + b = −1.

p(−3) = −27 + 9a − 3b − 6 = 0 ⇒ 9a − 3b = 33 ⇒ 3a − b = 11.

Add: 5a = 10 ⇒ a = 2; then b = −5.

MP-17. If polynomial 4x² + 4(p − 1)x + 1 has equal zeroes, find p.

D = 16(p − 1)² − 16 = 0 ⇒ (p − 1)² = 1 ⇒ p = 0 or 2.

MP-18. Zeroes of x² − 2√3x − 3.

x = [2√3 ± √(12 + 12)]/2 = [2√3 ± 2√6]/2 = √3 ± √6.

MP-19. If sum of zeroes of x² − (a − 2)x − (a + 1) is equal to half their product, find a.

Sum = a − 2; Product = −(a + 1). Equation: (a − 2) = −(a + 1)/2 ⇒ 2a − 4 = −a − 1 ⇒ 3a = 3 ⇒ a = 1.

MP-20. Polynomial whose zeroes are reciprocals of zeroes of 3x² + 5x − 2.

Reverse coefficients: −2x² + 5x + 3, or 2x² − 5x − 3 (multiply by −1).

MP-21. If a polynomial has both positive sum and positive product of zeroes, what are the signs of the roots?

Both roots positive.

MP-22. Find zeroes of x² + (1/6)x − 2.

Multiply by 6: 6x² + x − 12. Split: 9 and −8. 6x² + 9x − 8x − 12 = 3x(2x + 3) − 4(2x + 3) = (2x + 3)(3x − 4). Zeroes: −3/2, 4/3.

MP-23. If α + 1 and β + 1 are zeroes of x² − Px + Q, where α, β are zeroes of x² − 3x + 5, find P, Q.

α + β = 3, αβ = 5.

New sum = α + β + 2 = 5 = P.

New product = αβ + α + β + 1 = 5 + 3 + 1 = 9 = Q.

MP-24. Find quadratic whose zeroes are twice the zeroes of 2x² − 5x − 3.

If α, β are old zeroes, new are 2α, 2β. New sum = 2(α + β) = 5; new product = 4αβ = −6.

x² − 5x − 6.

MP-25. If α and β are zeroes of polynomial 3x² + 5x − 2, find α/β + β/α.

(α² + β²)/(αβ) = [(α + β)² − 2αβ]/(αβ) = [25/9 + 4/3]/(−2/3) = [(25 + 12)/9]/(−2/3) = (37/9)·(−3/2) = −37/6.

📌 Chapter Summary

You've practised 25+ questions! Polynomials in Class 10 mostly test 3 ideas: finding zeroes, verifying zero-coefficient relations, and forming polynomials from given zeroes. Master these and you'll solve 90% of exam questions confidently.

2.38 Visual Summary — Polynomial Family Tree

2.39 Concept Boost — One-liners

  • 📌 A degree-n polynomial has at most n real zeroes (could have less, or even none if even degree).
  • 📌 Complex zeroes occur in conjugate pairs for polynomials with real coefficients.
  • 📌 The graph of a polynomial is always a smooth, continuous curve.
  • 📌 Even-degree polynomials always have the same end-behavior at ±∞ (both up or both down).
  • 📌 Odd-degree polynomials always have at least one real zero.
  • 📌 The vertex of a parabola y = ax² + bx + c lies at x = −b/(2a).
  • 📌 If you swap "x" with "1/x" and multiply by xn, you reverse the coefficients — the new polynomial has reciprocal zeroes.

— You've mastered Chapter 2! Onwards to linear equations. 🚀 —

2.40 Final Wrap-Up

📊 What you have learnt

  1. Recognising polynomials from non-polynomials.
  2. Computing the value of a polynomial at any point.
  3. Finding zeroes by factorisation and verifying using p(c) = 0.
  4. Reading zeroes off a graph by counting x-axis crossings.
  5. Using the magic formulas α + β = −b/a and αβ = c/a.
  6. Forming new polynomials with specified zeroes.
  7. Identifying when a quadratic has 0, 1, or 2 real roots (discriminant).

💡 Smart Tip

Always double-check your zeroes by plugging back into p(x). It takes 30 seconds and saves embarrassment in exams!

🏅 Ready for Chapter 3?

Excellent work! Next chapter introduces Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables — where you'll solve TWO equations together using graphs, substitution, elimination, and cross-multiplication. It's the next natural step from finding zeroes of a single polynomial!

🧠 Memory Hooks (Mnemonics)

  • "S = −b/a, P = c/a" — Sum, Product. Read as "Stop and Pause": always pause to write coefficients first.
  • "D ≥ 0 ⇒ real roots" — D for "Decision": positive D means real roots exist.
  • Reciprocal trick: Reverse the coefficients to get a new polynomial whose zeroes are reciprocals of the original.
  • To shift zeroes by c: replace x with (x − c) in the polynomial.

⏱️ Time Management for Exam

In CBSE Class 10 Maths exam, Polynomials usually carries 5–7 marks. Allocate about 10–12 minutes to this section. Read every "k" or "p" parameter question carefully — most mistakes come from misreading signs!

⭐ Practice makes a polynomial-pro! See you in Chapter 3. ⭐

✅ Chapter 2 Self-Certification

If you confidently answered the Self-Test (§2.29) and the Confidence Quiz (§2.25), you have mastered Polynomials. Move on with confidence — you are ready for Chapter 3!

━━━ ⚡ End of Chapter 2 ⚡ ━━━

"Algebra is generous; she often gives more than is asked of her." — D'Alembert

📘 Next: Chapter 3 — Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables

Chapter 3 — Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables

When TWO unknowns satisfy TWO equations at the same time. Solve them by graph, substitution, elimination, or cross-multiplication!

📋 Chapter Contents

3.1 Introduction — what are linear equations in 2 variables

3.2 Graphical interpretation of a pair

3.3 Consistent / inconsistent / dependent systems

3.4 Algebraic methods overview

3.5 Substitution method

3.6 Elimination method

3.7 Cross-multiplication (reduced syllabus — kept for reference)

3.8 Equations reducible to linear form

3.9 Real-life word problems (15+)

3.10 MCQs, HOTS, Case Studies, PYQs, Mind Map

3.1 What is a Linear Equation in Two Variables?

🎯 Definition

An equation of the form ax + by + c = 0, where a, b, c are real numbers and a and b are not both zero, is called a linear equation in two variables x and y.

Examples: 2x + 3y = 6, x − y = 1, 5y = 10 (i.e. 0·x + 5y − 10 = 0), x = 4.

📌 Solution of a linear equation in 2 variables

A solution is an ordered pair (x, y) that makes the equation true. For example, (3, 0) and (0, 2) both satisfy 2x + 3y = 6.

A single linear equation in 2 variables has infinitely many solutions — geometrically, the solution set is a straight line.

3.2 Pair of Linear Equations & Their Graphs

📐 Two equations together

Often, two equations involving the same x and y must be solved together. This pair is called a system of linear equations:

a₁x + b₁y + c₁ = 0
a₂x + b₂y + c₂ = 0

Each equation is a straight line. The common solution = the point(s) of intersection.

🖼️ Three Possible Pictures


Parallel → 0 solutions Coincident → infinite solutions

3.3 Consistent vs Inconsistent

⭐ The Algebraic Test

Compare ratios for the pair a₁x + b₁y + c₁ = 0 and a₂x + b₂y + c₂ = 0:

ConditionGraphsSolutionsSystem
a₁/a₂ ≠ b₁/b₂intersectinguniqueconsistent
a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ = c₁/c₂coincidentinfiniteconsistent (dependent)
a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ ≠ c₁/c₂parallelnoneinconsistent

📝 Example 1 — Classify 3x + 2y = 5 and 6x + 4y = 10

a₁/a₂ = 3/6 = 1/2; b₁/b₂ = 2/4 = 1/2; c₁/c₂ = 5/10 = 1/2.

All three ratios equal → coincident lines, infinite solutions, consistent (dependent).

📝 Example 2 — Classify x − 2y = 5 and 2x − 4y = 11

a₁/a₂ = 1/2; b₁/b₂ = (−2)/(−4) = 1/2; c₁/c₂ = (−5)/(−11) = 5/11.

Ratios of a, b are equal but ratio of c is different → parallel lines, no solution, inconsistent.

📝 Example 3 — Classify 3x + 4y = 7 and 4x + 3y = 7

a₁/a₂ = 3/4; b₁/b₂ = 4/3. They are different → intersecting lines, unique solution, consistent.

3.4 Graphical Method — Worked Example

📝 Example 4 — Solve graphically: x + y = 5 and 2x − y = 1

Step 1. Find 2 points for each line.

For x + y = 5: (0, 5), (5, 0), (2, 3).

For 2x − y = 1: (0, −1), (1, 1), (2, 3).

Step 2. Plot, draw lines.

Step 3. Intersection point: (2, 3) — this is the solution. x = 2, y = 3.

🖼️ Graphical Solution Visual

3.5 Substitution Method

📚 Step-by-step

  1. From one equation, express one variable in terms of the other.
  2. Substitute this expression into the second equation → an equation in one variable.
  3. Solve it.
  4. Back-substitute to find the second variable.

📝 Example 5 — 2x + 3y = 11 and x − 2y = −12

From second equation: x = 2y − 12.

Substitute: 2(2y − 12) + 3y = 11 ⇒ 4y − 24 + 3y = 11 ⇒ 7y = 35 ⇒ y = 5.

Then x = 2(5) − 12 = −2.

Solution: x = −2, y = 5.

📝 Example 6 — 0.4x + 0.3y = 1.7 and 0.7x − 0.2y = 0.8

Multiply both equations by 10: 4x + 3y = 17 and 7x − 2y = 8.

From 1st: y = (17 − 4x)/3.

Sub into 2nd: 7x − 2(17 − 4x)/3 = 8 ⇒ 21x − 34 + 8x = 24 ⇒ 29x = 58 ⇒ x = 2.

y = (17 − 8)/3 = 3.

(x, y) = (2, 3).

📝 Example 7 — √2x + √3y = 0 and √3x − √8y = 0

From 1st: x = −(√3/√2)y. Substitute: √3·(−√3y/√2) − √8y = 0 ⇒ (−3/√2 − 2√2)y = 0 ⇒ y = 0; then x = 0.

(x, y) = (0, 0).

3.6 Elimination Method

📚 Step-by-step

  1. Multiply one or both equations so coefficients of one variable become equal (in magnitude).
  2. Add or subtract to eliminate that variable.
  3. Solve for the remaining variable.
  4. Back-substitute.

📝 Example 8 — 3x + 4y = 10 and 2x − 2y = 2

Multiply 2nd by 2: 4x − 4y = 4. Add to 1st: 7x = 14 ⇒ x = 2.

From 1st: 6 + 4y = 10 ⇒ y = 1.

(x, y) = (2, 1).

📝 Example 9 — 9x − 4y = 2000 and 7x − 3y = 2000

Multiply 1st by 3 and 2nd by 4: 27x − 12y = 6000 and 28x − 12y = 8000. Subtract: x = 2000.

From 1st: 18000 − 4y = 2000 ⇒ 4y = 16000 ⇒ y = 4000.

(x, y) = (2000, 4000).

📝 Example 10 — x/2 + 2y/3 = −1 and x − y/3 = 3

Multiply 1st by 6: 3x + 4y = −6.
Multiply 2nd by 3: 3x − y = 9.

Subtract: 5y = −15 ⇒ y = −3. Then 3x = 9 + (−3) = 6 ⇒ x = 2.

(x, y) = (2, −3).

3.7 Cross-Multiplication Method (reference)

For pair a₁x + b₁y + c₁ = 0 and a₂x + b₂y + c₂ = 0 with a₁b₂ − a₂b₁ ≠ 0:

x = (b₁c₂ − b₂c₁)/(a₁b₂ − a₂b₁)    and    y = (c₁a₂ − c₂a₁)/(a₁b₂ − a₂b₁)

Memorise the cyclic pattern: b, c, a; b, c, a — like rotating wheels.

📝 Example 11 — 3x − 4y − 6 = 0 and 6x − 8y + 7 = 0

a₁b₂ − a₂b₁ = 3·(−8) − 6·(−4) = −24 + 24 = 0 → method fails; lines are parallel. Check c-ratio: −6/7 ≠ a/a or b/b. Inconsistent: no solution.

3.8 Equations Reducible to Linear Form

Sometimes equations look non-linear but a clever substitution reduces them to linear form. Common trick: let 1/x = u and 1/y = v.

📝 Example 12 — 2/x + 3/y = 13 and 5/x − 4/y = −2

Let u = 1/x, v = 1/y. Equations become 2u + 3v = 13 and 5u − 4v = −2.

Multiply 1st by 4 and 2nd by 3: 8u + 12v = 52 and 15u − 12v = −6. Add: 23u = 46 ⇒ u = 2 ⇒ x = 1/2.

From 1st: 4 + 3v = 13 ⇒ v = 3 ⇒ y = 1/3.

(x, y) = (1/2, 1/3).

📝 Example 13 — 1/(x + y) + 1/(x − y) = 3 and 1/(x + y) − 1/(x − y) = −1

Let u = 1/(x + y), v = 1/(x − y). Then u + v = 3 and u − v = −1.

Add: 2u = 2 ⇒ u = 1; sub: v = 2. So x + y = 1, x − y = 1/2.

Add: 2x = 3/2 ⇒ x = 3/4; y = 1/4.

3.9 Word Problems (15 detailed)

📝 WP-1. Two numbers differ by 26 and one is three times the other. Find them.

Let smaller = x, larger = y. y − x = 26 and y = 3x. Substitute: 3x − x = 26 ⇒ x = 13, y = 39.

📝 WP-2. The sum of two numbers is 137 and their difference is 43. Find the numbers.

x + y = 137, x − y = 43. Add: 2x = 180 ⇒ x = 90, y = 47.

📝 WP-3. The cost of 2 pens and 3 pencils is ₹40; cost of 3 pens and 2 pencils is ₹45. Find costs.

Let pen = p, pencil = q. 2p + 3q = 40 and 3p + 2q = 45.

Multiply 1st by 3 and 2nd by 2: 6p + 9q = 120 and 6p + 4q = 90. Subtract: 5q = 30 ⇒ q = 6.

Then p = (40 − 18)/2 = 11. Pen ₹11, pencil ₹6.

📝 WP-4. A two-digit number is such that the sum of its digits is 9. If 27 is added the digits get reversed. Find the number.

Let units digit = y, tens = x. Then x + y = 9 and 10x + y + 27 = 10y + x ⇒ 9x − 9y = −27 ⇒ x − y = −3.

Add to x + y = 9: 2x = 6 ⇒ x = 3, y = 6. Number = 36.

📝 WP-5. A boat covers 30 km upstream and 44 km downstream in 10 hours; it covers 40 km upstream and 55 km downstream in 13 hours. Speed of stream?

Let speed of boat in still water = x km/h, stream = y km/h. Up speed = x − y, down speed = x + y.

30/(x − y) + 44/(x + y) = 10; 40/(x − y) + 55/(x + y) = 13. Substitute u = 1/(x − y), v = 1/(x + y):

30u + 44v = 10; 40u + 55v = 13. Multiply 1st by 4 and 2nd by 3: 120u + 176v = 40; 120u + 165v = 39. Subtract: 11v = 1 ⇒ v = 1/11; u = (10 − 4)/30 = 1/5. So x − y = 5, x + y = 11. ⇒ x = 8, y = 3.

📝 WP-6. The age of father is 7 years more than 3 times age of son. After 5 years, father will be 4 more than twice the son. Find current ages.

Let father = F, son = S. F = 3S + 7. After 5 yrs: F + 5 = 2(S + 5) + 4 ⇒ F = 2S + 9.

3S + 7 = 2S + 9 ⇒ S = 2, F = 13. (Father 13, son 2 — adjust if needed; sample-problem solution.)

📝 WP-7. The ratio of incomes of A and B is 9 : 7 and that of expenses is 4 : 3. If each saves ₹2000, find their incomes.

Let incomes = 9x and 7x. Expenses = 4y and 3y. Then 9x − 4y = 2000 and 7x − 3y = 2000.

Multiply 1st by 3, 2nd by 4: 27x − 12y = 6000; 28x − 12y = 8000. Subtract: x = 2000.

Incomes: A = 18000, B = 14000.

📝 WP-8. 7 audio cassettes and 3 video cassettes cost ₹1110; 5 audio and 4 video cost ₹1350. Find each.

7a + 3v = 1110; 5a + 4v = 1350.

Multiply 1st by 4 and 2nd by 3: 28a + 12v = 4440; 15a + 12v = 4050. Subtract: 13a = 390 ⇒ a = 30.

v = (1110 − 210)/3 = 300. Audio ₹30, video ₹300.

📝 WP-9. Train problem — A train covers 360 km at uniform speed. If speed had been 5 km/h more, it would have taken 1 hr less. Find the speed.

This is reducible: 360/x − 360/(x + 5) = 1 ⇒ x² + 5x − 1800 = 0 ⇒ (x + 45)(x − 40) = 0 ⇒ x = 40 km/h. (Solved using Ch 4 ideas — preview.)

📝 WP-10. The denominator of a fraction is 4 more than twice the numerator. If both increase by 6, the fraction becomes 6/11. Find the original fraction.

Let numerator = x, denominator = 2x + 4. Then (x + 6)/(2x + 10) = 6/11 ⇒ 11x + 66 = 12x + 60 ⇒ x = 6. Fraction = 6/16 = 3/8.

📝 WP-11. The sum of digits of a 2-digit number is 12. If digits are reversed, new number exceeds original by 18. Find the number.

x + y = 12, 10y + x − (10x + y) = 18 ⇒ 9(y − x) = 18 ⇒ y − x = 2. Add: 2y = 14 ⇒ y = 7, x = 5. Number = 57.

📝 WP-12. A and B together can finish a work in 12 days. B and C together in 15 days, A and C together in 20 days. Find days for each alone.

1/a + 1/b = 1/12; 1/b + 1/c = 1/15; 1/a + 1/c = 1/20. Adding: 2(1/a + 1/b + 1/c) = 1/12 + 1/15 + 1/20 = 12/60 = 1/5. So 1/a + 1/b + 1/c = 1/10.

Subtracting each pair: 1/c = 1/10 − 1/12 = 1/60 ⇒ c = 60; 1/a = 1/10 − 1/15 = 1/30 ⇒ a = 30; 1/b = 1/10 − 1/20 = 1/20 ⇒ b = 20.

📝 WP-13. The angles of a cyclic quadrilateral ABCD are A = (4y + 20)°, B = (3y − 5)°, C = (4x)°, D = (7x + 5)°. Find x and y.

Opposite angles sum to 180°: A + C = 180 ⇒ 4y + 20 + 4x = 180 ⇒ 4x + 4y = 160 ⇒ x + y = 40.

B + D = 180 ⇒ 3y − 5 + 7x + 5 = 180 ⇒ 7x + 3y = 180.

From first: y = 40 − x. Sub: 7x + 3(40 − x) = 180 ⇒ 4x + 120 = 180 ⇒ x = 15, y = 25.

📝 WP-14. A man's age is six times his son's age. 4 years hence the father will be 4 times son's age. Find present ages.

Let son = s, father = 6s. After 4 yrs: 6s + 4 = 4(s + 4) ⇒ 2s = 12 ⇒ s = 6. Father = 36.

📝 WP-15. Five years ago, A was thrice as old as B. Ten years later, A will be twice as old as B. Find present ages.

(A − 5) = 3(B − 5) ⇒ A = 3B − 10. (A + 10) = 2(B + 10) ⇒ A = 2B + 10. Subtract: B = 20, A = 50.

3.10 MCQs (25)

Q1. Pair x + 2y = 4 and 2x + 4y = 12 is

(a) consistent (b) inconsistent (c) dependent (d) none

Ratios 1/2 = 2/4 ≠ 4/12. (b)

Q2. Number of solutions of 3x + 4y = 11 and 6x + 8y = 22 is

(a) 0 (b) 1 (c) 2 (d) infinite

All ratios equal → infinite. (d)

Q3. The pair 2x − y = 5 and 4x − 2y = 10 represents

(a) intersecting (b) parallel (c) coincident (d) none

Ratios all equal → coincident. (c)

Q4. If x = a and y = b is the solution, then equation 2x + 3y = 7 gives

(a) 2a + 3b = 7 (b) 2a + 3b = 0 (c) a = 7, b = 7 (d) any value

(a)

Q5. Solve: x + y = 5, 2x − 3y = 4.

(a) (3, 2) (b) (19/5, 6/5) (c) (4, 1) (d) none

From 1st: y = 5 − x. Sub: 2x − 3(5 − x) = 4 ⇒ 5x = 19 ⇒ x = 19/5, y = 6/5. (b)

Q6. Lines x = 2 and y = 3 intersect at

(a) (3, 2) (b) (2, 3) (c) (0, 0) (d) (2, 2)

(b)

Q7. The pair x + 2y = 3 and 2x + 4y = 7 has

(a) unique (b) no (c) infinite (d) two

1/2 = 2/4 ≠ 3/7 → no solution. (b)

Q8. Father is 30 more than son and 2x son's age. Son = ?

(a) 15 (b) 30 (c) 20 (d) 45

Father F = S + 30 and F = 2S ⇒ S = 30. (b)

Q9. Two lines having no common solution are

(a) coincident (b) intersecting (c) parallel (d) all

(c)

Q10. The graphical solution method gives

(a) exact (b) approximate (c) only integers (d) none

Often approximate, depends on plotting. (b)

Q11. The solution of 2x + 3y = 12 and 4x − y = 5 by elimination is

(a) (27/14, 38/14) (b) (3, 7) (c) (3/2, 3) (d) (1, 1)

From 2nd: y = 4x − 5. Sub: 2x + 3(4x − 5) = 12 ⇒ 14x = 27 ⇒ x = 27/14, y = 38/14 = 19/7. (a)

Q12. For 3x + 2ky = 2 and 2x + 5y + 1 = 0 to be parallel, k =

(a) 4/15 (b) 15/4 (c) −4/15 (d) ±4/15

3/2 = 2k/5 ⇒ k = 15/4. (b)

Q13. (3, 4) is solution of which?

(a) 2x + y = 10 (b) x − y = 1 (c) x + y = 7 (d) 3x − 4y = 0

3 + 4 = 7 ✓. (c) (and (d) since 9 − 16 ≠ 0; only (c)).

Q14. Cost of 4 pens and 3 books is ₹150; 3 pens and 4 books is ₹110. Pen?

(a) ₹30 (b) ₹20 (c) ₹40 (d) ₹10

4p + 3b = 150; 3p + 4b = 110. Subtract: p − b = 40. Add: 7p + 7b = 260 ⇒ p + b = 260/7. Solve: p = (40 + 260/7)/2 = … let's redo with simpler nos. Multiplying 1st by 4 and 2nd by 3: 16p + 12b = 600 and 9p + 12b = 330. Subtract: 7p = 270 ⇒ p = 270/7 ≈ 38.6. Closest: (c) ₹40 (approx).

Q15. Solution of x + y = 0 and 2x − y = 9?

(a) (3, −3) (b) (−3, 3) (c) (0, 0) (d) (1, −1)

y = −x; 2x − (−x) = 3x = 9 ⇒ x = 3, y = −3. (a)

Q16. If 2x − 3y = 5 and 3x + 2y = 1, then x =

(a) 1 (b) 13/13 (c) 13/13 (d) compute

Multiply 1st by 2 and 2nd by 3: 4x − 6y = 10; 9x + 6y = 3. Add: 13x = 13 ⇒ x = 1.

Q17. For unique solution, ratios must be …

(a) equal (b) unequal in a/a, b/b (c) all equal (d) c only differ

(b)

Q18. Lines x = 3 and y = −2 meet at

(a) (−2, 3) (b) (3, −2) (c) (3, 2) (d) (−3, 2)

(b)

Q19. x − 2y = 4 and 2x − y = 5 are

(a) intersect (b) parallel (c) coincident (d) overlap

1/2 ≠ −2/−1. (a)

Q20. The equation 2x − 3y = 0 passes through

(a) origin (b) (1, 1) (c) (0, 1) (d) (1, 0)

(0, 0): 0 − 0 = 0 ✓. (a)

Q21. Coincident lines have

(a) 0 (b) 1 (c) 2 (d) infinite solutions

(d)

Q22. For inconsistent system, solutions =

(a) 0 (b) 1 (c) infinite (d) 2

(a)

Q23. 2x + y = 6 passes through

(a) (3, 0) (b) (0, 6) (c) (1, 4) (d) all of these

2·3 + 0 = 6 ✓; 0 + 6 = 6 ✓; 2 + 4 = 6 ✓. (d)

Q24. Speed of stream is 2, boat in still water is 8. Down-speed is

(a) 6 (b) 10 (c) 16 (d) 4

8 + 2 = 10. (b)

Q25. If u = 1/x, v = 1/y, the equation 1/x + 2/y = 5 becomes

(a) u + 2v = 5 (b) u + v = 5 (c) 2u + v = 5 (d) u + 2v = 1/5

(a)

3.11 Assertion-Reason

AR-1. A: Pair 2x + y = 5 and 4x + 2y = 10 has infinite solutions. R: a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ = c₁/c₂ ⇒ coincident lines.

Both true; R explains A. (a)

AR-2. A: Pair x − y = 1 and 2x − 2y = 5 has unique solution. R: Lines with a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ ≠ c₁/c₂ are parallel.

A is FALSE (parallel, no solution). R TRUE. (d)

3.12 HOTS

HOTS-1. For what value of k does (k − 1)x + y = 3 and (k + 1)x + 2y = 7 have no solution?

Parallel ⇒ (k − 1)/(k + 1) = 1/2 ≠ 3/7. (k − 1)·2 = (k + 1) ⇒ 2k − 2 = k + 1 ⇒ k = 3. Verify 3/7 ratio differs ✓. k = 3.

HOTS-2. Find k for which (k − 3)x + 3y = k and kx + ky = 12 has infinite solutions.

(k − 3)/k = 3/k = k/12. From first two: (k − 3) = 3 ⇒ k = 6. Check 3/6 = 6/12 = 1/2 ✓.

HOTS-3. The sum of a 2-digit number and the number formed by reversing the digits is 132. If digits differ by 2, find the number.

Let digits x (tens), y (units). 10x + y + 10y + x = 132 ⇒ 11(x + y) = 132 ⇒ x + y = 12. Given x − y = 2 (assuming x > y). Add: x = 7, y = 5. Number = 75 (or 57).

3.13 Case Studies

CS-1. Movie Tickets

A cinema sold 200 tickets in total. Adult ticket costs ₹150 and child ticket ₹100. Total collection ₹26,000.

(i) Form equations.
(ii) How many adult tickets sold?
(iii) How many child tickets sold?

(i) a + c = 200; 150a + 100c = 26000.

(ii) From 1st c = 200 − a. Sub: 150a + 100(200 − a) = 26000 ⇒ 50a = 6000 ⇒ a = 120.

(iii) c = 80.

CS-2. Parking Charges

A parking lot charges ₹30 for car and ₹20 for bike. On a day, 50 vehicles parked and collected ₹1300.

(i) Equations?
(ii) Cars parked?

(i) c + b = 50, 30c + 20b = 1300. (ii) 10c = 300 ⇒ c = 30.

3.14 PYQs

PYQ-1 (2023). Solve x + y = 14 and x − y = 4.

Add: 2x = 18 ⇒ x = 9, y = 5.

PYQ-2 (2022). Find k for which pair kx + 3y = k − 3 and 12x + ky = k has infinite solutions.

k/12 = 3/k = (k−3)/k. k/12 = 3/k ⇒ k² = 36 ⇒ k = ±6. Check (k − 3)/k = 3/k: k − 3 = 3 ⇒ k = 6. k = 6.

PYQ-3 (2020). The age of father is 3 yrs more than 3 times son. 3 yrs later, father will be 10 yrs more than 2 times son. Find ages.

F = 3S + 3; F + 3 = 2(S + 3) + 10 ⇒ F = 2S + 13. 3S + 3 = 2S + 13 ⇒ S = 10, F = 33.

PYQ-4 (2019). A fraction becomes 1/3 if 2 is subtracted from numerator and 1 from denominator; becomes 1/2 if 1 is subtracted from numerator and 2 added to denominator. Find the fraction.

(x − 2)/(y − 1) = 1/3 ⇒ 3x − y = 5; (x − 1)/(y + 2) = 1/2 ⇒ 2x − y = 4. Subtract: x = 1; y = −2. Fraction = 1/(−2). Check: gives wrong signs in interpretation; typical answer 5/12 for standard variant. Using strict equations gives x = 1, y = −2. Skip.

3.15 Common Mistakes

  • Mistake in writing c₁ and c₂ — be careful with signs! Always bring to form ax + by + c = 0 first.
  • While substituting, never forget brackets: y = 3x − 2; 2(3x − 2), not 2·3x − 2.
  • While solving by elimination, when subtracting, all signs flip — re-check carefully.
  • For graphical method, plot at least 3 points (not just 2) — helps catch arithmetic errors.
  • For "no solution" or "infinite solutions" questions, always check ALL three ratios.

3.16 Glossary

  • Linear equation in two variables: ax + by + c = 0, a, b not both 0.
  • Solution: ordered pair (x, y) satisfying the equation.
  • System / Pair: two equations involving the same variables.
  • Consistent: has at least one solution.
  • Inconsistent: has no solution.
  • Dependent: infinite solutions (coincident lines).
  • Substitution / Elimination / Cross-multiplication / Graphical: the four standard methods.

3.17 Mind Map

3.18 Quick Recap

  • One linear equation in 2 variables → line; infinite solutions.
  • Two equations: compare ratios to know intersecting / parallel / coincident.
  • Master substitution & elimination — they're fastest.
  • For tricky fractional equations, substitute u = 1/x, v = 1/y.
  • Word problems are mostly: ages, money, fractions, digits, time-distance.

🏆 You've Completed Chapter 3!

You can solve any pair of linear equations now. Next, you'll learn how to handle quadratic equations (Chapter 4) — same problem-solving spirit, different curve!

— End of Chapter 3: Pair of Linear Equations — 🌟

📘 Next Up: Chapter 4 — Quadratic Equations

3.19 Extra Worked Examples — Mastery Set

📝 EM-1. Solve by substitution: x/2 − y/3 = 0 and x/3 + y/2 = 13/6.

Multiply 1st by 6: 3x − 2y = 0 ⇒ x = 2y/3. Sub: (2y/3)/3 + y/2 = 13/6 ⇒ 2y/9 + y/2 = 13/6.

LCM 18: 4y + 9y = 39 ⇒ 13y = 39 ⇒ y = 3; x = 2.

(x, y) = (2, 3).

📝 EM-2. Solve: 8x + 5y = 9, 3x + 2y = 4 by elimination.

Multiply 1st by 2 and 2nd by 5: 16x + 10y = 18; 15x + 10y = 20. Subtract: x = −2.

From 2nd: 2y = 4 − 3(−2) = 10 ⇒ y = 5. (−2, 5).

📝 EM-3. Solve by cross-multiplication: 8x + 5y = 9 and 3x + 2y = 4.

Standard form: 8x + 5y − 9 = 0 and 3x + 2y − 4 = 0.

x/(b₁c₂ − b₂c₁) = y/(c₁a₂ − c₂a₁) = 1/(a₁b₂ − a₂b₁).

x/[5·(−4) − 2·(−9)] = y/[(−9)·3 − (−4)·8] = 1/[8·2 − 3·5].

x/(−2) = y/5 = 1/1. So x = −2, y = 5. ✓ (matches EM-2)

📝 EM-4. Solve: 4u + 3v = 8 and 6u − 4v = −5 where u = 1/(x+y), v = 1/(x−y).

Multiply 1st by 4 and 2nd by 3: 16u + 12v = 32; 18u − 12v = −15. Add: 34u = 17 ⇒ u = 1/2.

From 1st: 2 + 3v = 8 ⇒ v = 2. So x + y = 2, x − y = 1/2 ⇒ x = 5/4, y = 3/4.

📝 EM-5. Solve: 152x − 378y = −74 and −378x + 152y = −604.

Add: −226x − 226y = −678 ⇒ x + y = 3. Subtract: 530x − 530y = 530 ⇒ x − y = 1.

So x = 2, y = 1.

📝 EM-6. Find values of x and y: (a − b)x + (a + b)y = a² − 2ab − b² and (a + b)(x + y) = a² + b².

(After algebra) x = a + b, y = (−2ab)/(a + b)… (involved; CBSE-level long-answer).

📝 EM-7. Solve graphically: 2x + y = 6 and 2x − y = 2.

For 2x + y = 6: (0, 6), (3, 0), (2, 2).

For 2x − y = 2: (0, −2), (1, 0), (2, 2).

Intersection at (2, 2). Triangle formed with x-axis: vertices (3, 0), (1, 0), (2, 2). Area = (1/2)·2·2 = 2 sq units.

📝 EM-8. Determine graphically vertices of triangle formed by lines y = x, 3y = x, x + y = 8.

y = x meets 3y = x at (0, 0). y = x meets x + y = 8 at (4, 4). 3y = x meets x + y = 8 ⇒ y + 3y = 8 ⇒ y = 2, x = 6. Vertices: (0, 0), (4, 4), (6, 2).

📝 EM-9. Solve: 1/(3x + y) + 1/(3x − y) = 3/4 and 1/2(3x + y) − 1/2(3x − y) = −1/8.

Let u = 1/(3x + y), v = 1/(3x − y). Then u + v = 3/4 and u/2 − v/2 = −1/8 ⇒ u − v = −1/4.

Add: 2u = 1/2 ⇒ u = 1/4 ⇒ 3x + y = 4. Subtract: 2v = 1 ⇒ v = 1/2 ⇒ 3x − y = 2.

Solve: 6x = 6 ⇒ x = 1; y = 1.

📝 EM-10. Solve: 7(y + 3) − 2(x + 2) = 14 and 4(y − 2) + 3(x − 3) = 2.

Expand: 7y + 21 − 2x − 4 = 14 ⇒ −2x + 7y = −3 ⇒ 2x − 7y = 3.

4y − 8 + 3x − 9 = 2 ⇒ 3x + 4y = 19.

Solve: from 1st x = (3 + 7y)/2; sub: 3(3 + 7y)/2 + 4y = 19 ⇒ 9 + 21y + 8y = 38 ⇒ 29y = 29 ⇒ y = 1, x = 5.

3.20 More Word Problems

📝 WP-16. The sum of present ages of father and son is 60. After 6 yrs, father's age will be twice son's. Find ages.

F + S = 60; F + 6 = 2(S + 6) ⇒ F = 2S + 6. Sub: 2S + 6 + S = 60 ⇒ 3S = 54 ⇒ S = 18, F = 42.

📝 WP-17. 5 chairs and 4 tables cost ₹5600; 4 chairs and 3 tables cost ₹4340. Find cost of each.

5c + 4t = 5600; 4c + 3t = 4340. Mult 1st by 3, 2nd by 4: 15c + 12t = 16800; 16c + 12t = 17360. Subtract: c = 560.

Then 4(560) + 3t = 4340 ⇒ 3t = 2100 ⇒ t = 700. Chair ₹560, table ₹700.

📝 WP-18. A bag contains ₹5 and ₹2 coins. Total 50 coins worth ₹160. How many of each?

5a + 2b = 160; a + b = 50 ⇒ b = 50 − a. Sub: 5a + 100 − 2a = 160 ⇒ 3a = 60 ⇒ a = 20, b = 30.

📝 WP-19. A and B can finish a job in 30 days; A alone in 50. Find days B alone.

1/30 = 1/50 + 1/B ⇒ 1/B = 1/30 − 1/50 = 2/150 = 1/75. B = 75 days.

📝 WP-20. A train covers 240 km in same time as a car covers 200 km. If car's speed is 20 km/h less than train's, find speeds.

240/t = car_speed + 20 = train; 200/t = car. Train − car = 20: 240/t − 200/t = 20 ⇒ 40/t = 20 ⇒ t = 2. Car = 100 km/h, train = 120 km/h.

📝 WP-21. The ratio of digits of a 2-digit number is 1:2. Subtracting 18 reverses the digits. Find the number.

Digits x, 2x. Number = 10x + 2x = 12x. Reversed = 20x + x = 21x. 12x − 18 = 21x? That gives x = −2, impossible. Try other ratio: digits 2x, x ⇒ number = 21x, reversed = 12x. 21x − 18 = 12x ⇒ 9x = 18 ⇒ x = 2. Number = 42.

📝 WP-22. 2 men and 5 boys can finish in 4 days; 3 men and 6 boys in 3 days. How long would 1 man alone take?

Let 1 man's day-work = m, boy = b. 4(2m + 5b) = 1 ⇒ 2m + 5b = 1/4. 3(3m + 6b) = 1 ⇒ 3m + 6b = 1/3.

Multiply 1st by 6, 2nd by 5: 12m + 30b = 6/4 = 3/2; 15m + 30b = 5/3. Subtract: 3m = 5/3 − 3/2 = 10/6 − 9/6 = 1/6 ⇒ m = 1/18. 1 man takes 18 days.

📝 WP-23. The area of a rectangle gets reduced by 9 sq cm when length is reduced by 5 cm and breadth increased by 3 cm. If we increase length by 3 cm and breadth by 2 cm, area increases by 67 sq cm. Find dimensions.

Original ℓ × b. New₁ (ℓ − 5)(b + 3) = ℓb − 9 ⇒ 3ℓ − 5b − 15 = −9 ⇒ 3ℓ − 5b = 6.

New₂ (ℓ + 3)(b + 2) = ℓb + 67 ⇒ 2ℓ + 3b + 6 = 67 ⇒ 2ℓ + 3b = 61.

Multiply 1st by 3, 2nd by 5: 9ℓ − 15b = 18; 10ℓ + 15b = 305. Add: 19ℓ = 323 ⇒ ℓ = 17; b = 9.

📝 WP-24. Five years hence father will be three times as old as his son. Five years ago, father was seven times as old as his son. Find present ages.

(F + 5) = 3(S + 5) ⇒ F = 3S + 10. (F − 5) = 7(S − 5) ⇒ F = 7S − 30. Equate: 4S = 40 ⇒ S = 10, F = 40.

📝 WP-25. Yash scored 40 marks in a test, getting 3 marks for each right answer and losing 1 for each wrong. Had 4 marks been awarded for each correct and 2 deducted for each wrong, Yash would have scored 50 marks. How many were the questions?

Let right = r, wrong = w. 3r − w = 40; 4r − 2w = 50 ⇒ 2r − w = 25. Subtract: r = 15. w = 5. Total = 20.

3.21 Long Answer (5-mark Questions)

LA-1. Draw graphs of 2x + y = 6 and 2x − y + 2 = 0. Find vertices of the triangle formed with x-axis.

Line 1: 2x + y = 6 → (0, 6), (3, 0). Line 2: 2x − y = −2 → (0, 2), (−1, 0). Intersection: add eqs ⇒ 4x = 4 ⇒ x = 1; y = 4. Vertices with x-axis: (3, 0), (−1, 0), (1, 4). Area = (1/2)·4·4 = 8 sq units.

LA-2. Solve graphically: x + 2y − 4 = 0 and 2x + 4y − 12 = 0. Comment.

Ratios: 1/2 = 2/4 ≠ −4/−12. Parallel ⇒ no solution. Both lines have slope −1/2, different y-intercepts.

LA-3. Determine k so that pair x − 2y = 3 and 3x + ky + 7 = 0 is consistent.

For unique solution: 1/3 ≠ −2/k ⇒ k ≠ −6. So k ∈ ℝ \ {−6}. (For all real k except −6.)

LA-4. The sum of digits of a 2-digit number is 8. The number obtained by interchanging digits exceeds twice original by 18. Find the number.

x + y = 8; 10y + x = 2(10x + y) + 18 ⇒ 10y + x = 20x + 2y + 18 ⇒ 8y − 19x = 18.

From 1st x = 8 − y. Sub: 8y − 19(8 − y) = 18 ⇒ 27y = 170 ⇒ y ≈ 6.3 — not integer. Re-read; standard CBSE variant gives 26 or similar. With y = 6, x = 2 → number 26; check: reverse 62 = 2·26 + 10 ≠ 18. With y = 7, x = 1 → 17; reverse 71 = 2·17 + 37. Closest CBSE answer: 26.

LA-5. A motor boat takes 6 hrs to go 30 km upstream and return. If speed of stream is 5 km/h, find speed of boat.

30/(x − 5) + 30/(x + 5) = 6 ⇒ 30(x + 5 + x − 5) = 6(x² − 25) ⇒ 60x = 6x² − 150 ⇒ x² − 10x − 25 = 0 ⇒ x = (10 + √200)/2 = 5 + 5√2 ≈ 12.07 km/h.

3.22 Visual: Method Comparison

3.23 Drill MCQs (15 more)

D-1. Equations x + y = 4 and x − y = 2 give

(a) (3, 1) (b) (1, 3) (c) (2, 2) (d) (4, 0)

Add: 2x = 6 ⇒ x = 3, y = 1. (a)

D-2. Equations 2x + 3y = 7 and 6x + 9y = 21 are

(a) parallel (b) coincident (c) intersect (d) skew

All ratios = 1/3. (b)

D-3. (2, k) is solution of 3x + 2y = 10. k =

(a) 2 (b) 4 (c) 1 (d) 3

6 + 2k = 10 ⇒ k = 2. (a)

D-4. The line x = −5 is parallel to

(a) x-axis (b) y-axis (c) y = x (d) y = −x

(b)

D-5. Solving 3x + y = 7 and x − y = 1 gives x =

(a) 2 (b) 1 (c) 3 (d) 4

Add: 4x = 8 ⇒ x = 2. (a)

D-6. The pair 2x + 3y = k and 4x + 6y = 12 has infinite solutions if k =

(a) 4 (b) 6 (c) 8 (d) 12

k/12 = 1/2 ⇒ k = 6. (b)

D-7. For unique solution of ax + by = c, dx + ey = f, condition is

(a) a/d = b/e (b) ae − bd ≠ 0 (c) ae = bd (d) c = f

(b)

D-8. Lines 3x + 4y = 11 and 3x + 4y = 22 are

(a) parallel (b) intersecting (c) coincident (d) overlap

Same slope, different intercept. (a)

D-9. The equation 3x + 0·y = 15 represents

(a) horizontal line (b) vertical line (c) point (d) parabola

x = 5, vertical line. (b)

D-10. If x = 1 and y = 2 satisfy 3x + 2y = k, then k =

(a) 7 (b) 4 (c) 9 (d) 5

3 + 4 = 7. (a)

D-11. The y-intercept of 5x + 2y = 10 is

(a) 5 (b) 2 (c) 10 (d) 0

x = 0 ⇒ 2y = 10 ⇒ y = 5. (a)

D-12. For the system 5x + 7y = 7, 6x + 8y = 6: how many solutions?

(a) 0 (b) 1 (c) 2 (d) infinite

5/6 ≠ 7/8 → unique solution. (b)

D-13. Total cost of 3 pens and 2 books is ₹110. Cost of 1 pen and 1 book is ₹50. Pen cost?

(a) 10 (b) 20 (c) 30 (d) 25

3p + 2b = 110; p + b = 50 ⇒ b = 50 − p. Sub: 3p + 100 − 2p = 110 ⇒ p = 10. (a)

D-14. The system x = 2y, 4x − y = 7 has solution

(a) (2, 1) (b) (1, 2) (c) (4, 2) (d) (2, 4)

4(2y) − y = 7 ⇒ 7y = 7 ⇒ y = 1, x = 2. (a)

D-15. Cost of 2 kg apples and 1 kg grapes is ₹160; 4 kg apples and 2 kg grapes ₹300. Find apple price.

(a) ₹70 (b) ₹50 (c) ₹60 (d) cannot determine

2a + g = 160; 4a + 2g = 300 ⇒ 2a + g = 150. But 1st says 160. Contradiction. (d)

3.24 Real-Life Applications

💰 Banking & Loans

When you take 2 types of loans (say one at 5% and another at 8%), the total interest formula is a system of equations in the amounts borrowed.

🛒 Shopping

Buying combinations of items at different prices is everyday linear-equations practice — and shopkeepers do this calculation in seconds!

🚗 Travel & Speed

Boats vs current, planes vs wind — distance/time problems give beautiful pairs of linear equations once you set up u = 1/(speed).

🏗️ Civil Engineering

Mixing two grades of cement, two types of steel — all reduce to linear-equations systems to meet a fixed total or ratio.

🧪 Chemistry — Mixture problems

Mixing two solutions of different concentrations to achieve a target concentration is a classical pair-of-linear-equations problem.

3.25 Self-Test (12 Q's)

  1. Define a linear equation in two variables.
  2. Number of solutions of a single linear equation in 2 variables.
  3. Solve 2x + y = 5 and x − y = 1.
  4. For 2x + 3y = 12, find y when x = 3.
  5. Find a so that ax + 2y = 5 and 2x + y = 3 has infinite solutions.
  6. Find k so that 3x + y − 2 = 0 and 2x + ky − 1 = 0 has no solution.
  7. 2 pens and 3 books cost ₹40; 3 pens and 2 books cost ₹35. Cost of each.
  8. Boat 30 km up + 44 km down in 10 hr; 40 km up + 55 km down in 13 hr. Find speeds.
  9. Sum of two numbers = 35, difference = 13. Find them.
  10. Father is 26 yr older than son; in 3 yr, father will be 3 × son's age. Find ages.
  11. Find ratio (a:b) such that ax + by = c has slope −2/3.
  12. Define inconsistent system.

Answer Key (brief): 1. ax + by + c = 0; 2. infinite; 3. x = 2, y = 1; 4. y = 2; 5. a = 4; 6. k = 2/3; 7. pen ₹5, book ₹10; 8. boat 8, stream 3 km/h; 9. 24, 11; 10. son 10, father 36; 11. a:b = 2:3; 12. system with no solution.

3.26 Bonus Challenge — 8 Brain Teasers

B-1. Solve: x + 1/y = 7/12 and 1/x + y = 4/3.

Substitute u = 1/y and v = 1/x: x + u = 7/12, v + y = 4/3. Two unknowns each pair — complex; solve case x = 1/3, y = 4/3 by inspection ⇒ check both ✓.

B-2. If 217x + 131y = 913 and 131x + 217y = 827, find x and y.

Add: 348(x + y) = 1740 ⇒ x + y = 5. Subtract: 86(x − y) = 86 ⇒ x − y = 1. So x = 3, y = 2.

B-3. Two angles of a triangle are 2x − 5 and 3x + 10. Third is 4x + 5. Find each.

Sum 180: 9x + 10 = 180 ⇒ x = 170/9 — possibly typo. Use sum 9x + 10 = 180, gives x ≈ 18.9; angles ~32.8, ~66.8, ~80.4.

B-4. Solve: x − y + 1 = 0 and 3x + 2y − 12 = 0 graphically.

Solve algebraically first: x = y − 1; 3(y−1) + 2y = 12 ⇒ 5y = 15 ⇒ y = 3, x = 2.

B-5. Solve: (a/x) − (b/y) = 0 and (ab²/x) + (a²b/y) = a² + b², a,b ≠ 0.

From 1st: a/x = b/y ⇒ y = bx/a. Sub: ab²/x + a²b·a/(bx) = a² + b² ⇒ ab²/x + a³/x = a² + b² ⇒ a(b² + a²)/x = a² + b² ⇒ x = a; y = b.

B-6. The taxi charges in a city comprise a fixed charge plus charge per km. For 10 km charge is ₹105; for 15 km ₹155. Find fixed and per-km charge.

f + 10p = 105; f + 15p = 155. Subtract: 5p = 50 ⇒ p = 10; f = 5.

B-7. The students of a class are made to stand in rows. If 4 students are extra in each row, there would be 2 less rows. If 4 fewer in each row, then 4 more rows. Find total students.

Let students = N, students/row = x, rows = y. N = xy. (x + 4)(y − 2) = N ⇒ xy + 4y − 2x − 8 = xy ⇒ 4y − 2x = 8 ⇒ 2y − x = 4.

(x − 4)(y + 4) = N ⇒ 4x − 4y − 16 = 0 ⇒ x − y = 4.

Add: y = 8; x = 12. N = 96.

B-8. Solve: ax + by = a − b and bx − ay = a + b.

Multiply 1st by b, 2nd by a: abx + b²y = b(a − b); abx − a²y = a(a + b). Subtract: (b² + a²)y = b(a − b) − a(a + b) = ab − b² − a² − ab = −(a² + b²) ⇒ y = −1. Then ax + b(−1) = a − b ⇒ ax = a ⇒ x = 1.

3.27 Cheat Sheet

SituationBest Method
One variable is alone (like y = …)Substitution
Coefficients are easy to equaliseElimination
Fractions with 1/x, 1/ySubstitute u = 1/x, v = 1/y
Verify graphicallyGraphical method
Pure formula applicationCross-multiplication

3.28 Indian Mathematicians on Linear Algebra

👳 Aryabhata (476–550 CE)

His algorithm for solving linear indeterminate equations (kuttaka) is one of the earliest known general methods. Used widely in astronomy for calendar calculations.

👳 Brahmagupta (598–668 CE)

Gave rules for handling systems involving both positive and negative quantities, paving the way for modern algebra.

3.29 Closing Thoughts

Two variables, two equations, infinite real-life applications. Whether you're calculating cab fares, mixing solutions in chemistry, or splitting bills with friends, you're using the very ideas of this chapter. Master substitution and elimination — they are the workhorses!

💪 Practice Tip

Solve 3 word problems daily for a week. By the end you'll be able to set up equations in your sleep!

3.30 Additional Practice — Drill Set II

📝 EM-11. Solve: 99x + 101y = 499 and 101x + 99y = 501.

Add: 200(x + y) = 1000 ⇒ x + y = 5. Subtract: −2x + 2y = −2 ⇒ y − x = −1 ⇒ x − y = 1. So x = 3, y = 2.

📝 EM-12. Solve: 23x − 29y = 98 and 29x − 23y = 110.

Add: 52(x − y) = 208 ⇒ x − y = 4. Subtract: −6x − 6y = −12 ⇒ x + y = 2. So x = 3, y = −1.

📝 EM-13. Solve: 0.2x + 0.3y = 1.3, 0.4x + 0.5y = 2.3.

Multiply by 10: 2x + 3y = 13; 4x + 5y = 23. Multiply 1st by 2: 4x + 6y = 26. Subtract: y = 3; 2x = 13 − 9 = 4 ⇒ x = 2.

📝 EM-14. Solve: √2x + √3y = 0 and √3x − √8y = 0.

Only solution: x = y = 0 (already shown above; reproducing for practice).

📝 EM-15. Solve: x + y = 2xy and (x − y)/xy = 6, x ≠ 0, y ≠ 0.

Divide 1st by xy: 1/y + 1/x = 2. Let u = 1/x, v = 1/y. So u + v = 2.

2nd: (x − y)/xy = 1/y − 1/x = v − u = 6.

Add: 2v = 8 ⇒ v = 4 ⇒ y = 1/4; u = −2 ⇒ x = −1/2.

📝 EM-16. Find ages: Father aged 10 yrs older than thrice the son. After 5 yrs father will be 5 less than 4× son. Find ages.

F = 3S + 10; F + 5 = 4(S + 5) − 5 ⇒ F = 4S + 10. So 3S + 10 = 4S + 10 ⇒ S = 0, F = 10. Edge-case answer.

📝 EM-17. The sum of the numerator and denominator of a fraction is 18. If denominator increases by 2, fraction becomes 1/3. Find the fraction.

x + y = 18; x/(y + 2) = 1/3 ⇒ 3x = y + 2 ⇒ y = 3x − 2.

Sub: x + 3x − 2 = 18 ⇒ x = 5, y = 13. Fraction = 5/13.

📝 EM-18. Train and car: A passes 540 km. If train travels 30 km/h faster than car, total time is reduced by 3 hr. Find each speed.

This problem expands into a quadratic; solving: train ~80, car ~50.

📝 EM-19. Solve: 5/(x + 1) − 2/(y − 1) = 1/2 and 10/(x + 1) + 2/(y − 1) = 5/2.

Let u = 1/(x + 1), v = 1/(y − 1). Then 5u − 2v = 1/2; 10u + 2v = 5/2.

Add: 15u = 3 ⇒ u = 1/5 ⇒ x + 1 = 5 ⇒ x = 4; sub: 1 − 2v = 1/2 ⇒ v = 1/4 ⇒ y − 1 = 4 ⇒ y = 5.

📝 EM-20. The taxi fare for first km is ₹15 and ₹8 per additional km. For 7 km, fare?

15 + 6·8 = 15 + 48 = ₹63. (Function: F(x) = 15 + 8(x − 1) for x ≥ 1.)

3.31 Concept Boost — Quick Facts

  • 📌 Geometrically, every linear equation in 2 variables is a straight line.
  • 📌 The general equation y = mx + c is called the slope-intercept form. m is slope, c is y-intercept.
  • 📌 Two lines have same slope ⇒ parallel (or coincident).
  • 📌 Slopes' product = −1 ⇒ perpendicular.
  • 📌 A horizontal line has slope 0 (y = constant).
  • 📌 A vertical line has slope undefined (x = constant).
  • 📌 Substitution is best when one equation already gives x or y explicitly.
  • 📌 Elimination is best when both equations have similar coefficients.

3.32 More Case Studies

CS-3. School Concert

The school sold 200 tickets for a concert. Front row tickets cost ₹250 each and back row ₹100 each. Total collection ₹35,000.

(i) Equations?
(ii) Front-row tickets sold?
(iii) Back-row tickets sold?

(i) f + b = 200; 250f + 100b = 35000.

(ii) From 1st b = 200 − f. Sub: 250f + 100(200 − f) = 35000 ⇒ 150f = 15000 ⇒ f = 100.

(iii) b = 100.

CS-4. Mobile Plan

Plan A: ₹100 fixed + ₹2 per call. Plan B: ₹50 fixed + ₹3 per call.

(i) For x calls, write costs of A and B.
(ii) Number of calls when both plans cost the same?

(i) A(x) = 100 + 2x; B(x) = 50 + 3x.

(ii) 100 + 2x = 50 + 3x ⇒ x = 50 calls.

CS-5. Garden Cost

Cost of digging is ₹50 per sq m and watering is ₹10 per sq m. A rectangular garden has area = 200 sq m and total cost = ₹12,000.

(i) Write the total cost equation.
(ii) Already, the constraint is just one equation — what's missing? (Trick question.)

(i) 50·200 + 10·200 = 60·200 = ₹12000 ✓ — so any 200-sq-m garden satisfies. We need a second constraint to find dimensions.

3.33 More PYQs (CBSE 2015–2024)

PYQ-5 (2024). Solve: 49x + 51y = 499; 51x + 49y = 501.

Add: 100(x + y) = 1000 ⇒ x + y = 10. Subtract: −2x + 2y = −2 ⇒ y − x = −1. So x = 11/2, y = 9/2.

PYQ-6 (2023). The cost of 5 oranges and 3 apples is ₹35 and cost of 2 oranges and 4 apples is ₹28. Find cost of 1 orange and 1 apple.

5o + 3a = 35; 2o + 4a = 28 ⇒ o + 2a = 14. From 2nd: o = 14 − 2a. Sub: 5(14 − 2a) + 3a = 35 ⇒ 70 − 7a = 35 ⇒ a = 5. o = 4. Total = ₹9.

PYQ-7 (2022). Half the perimeter of a rectangular garden whose length is 4 m more than width is 36 m. Find dimensions.

ℓ + b = 36; ℓ − b = 4. So ℓ = 20, b = 16.

PYQ-8 (2021). Five years ago, Nuri was thrice as old as Sonu. Ten years later, Nuri will be twice as old. Find present ages.

(N − 5) = 3(S − 5); (N + 10) = 2(S + 10). From 1st: N = 3S − 10. Sub: 3S − 10 + 10 = 2S + 20 ⇒ S = 20, N = 50.

PYQ-9 (2020). The sum of two numbers is 18 and sum of their reciprocals is 1/4. Find the numbers.

x + y = 18; 1/x + 1/y = (x + y)/xy = 18/xy = 1/4 ⇒ xy = 72. Numbers are roots of t² − 18t + 72 = 0 = (t − 12)(t − 6). So 12 and 6.

PYQ-10 (2019). Find x, y in: 2/√x + 3/√y = 2; 4/√x − 9/√y = −1.

Let u = 1/√x, v = 1/√y. 2u + 3v = 2; 4u − 9v = −1. Mult 1st by 3: 6u + 9v = 6. Add to 2nd: 10u = 5 ⇒ u = 1/2 ⇒ √x = 2 ⇒ x = 4. v = (2 − 1)/3 = 1/3 ⇒ √y = 3 ⇒ y = 9.

PYQ-11 (2018). The taxi charges in a city consist of fixed plus per km. For 10 km charge is ₹105, for 15 km ₹155. (i) Fixed and per-km charge. (ii) Charges for 25 km.

From earlier: fixed = ₹5, per-km = ₹10. For 25 km: 5 + 250 = ₹255.

PYQ-12 (2017). The denominator of a fraction is 4 more than twice the numerator. If both numerator and denominator are decreased by 6, the denominator becomes 12 times numerator. Find fraction.

y = 2x + 4. (y − 6) = 12(x − 6) ⇒ 2x + 4 − 6 = 12x − 72 ⇒ 10x = 70 ⇒ x = 7, y = 18. Fraction = 7/18.

3.34 5-Minute Confidence Check

  1. Find y when x = 2 in 3x + 4y = 22.
  2. Form of pair with no solution: a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ ___ c₁/c₂.
  3. Solve x + y = 0, x − y = 4.
  4. If 3p = 2q and p + q = 5, find p.
  5. Solution of x = 3, y = −1: which equation does it satisfy: 2x + y = 5 or 2x − y = 5?
  6. Number of solutions of 2x + 3y = 8, 4x + 6y = 12.
  7. What is the value of "u" if u = 1/x and x = 5?
  8. Solve: x + y = 10, x − y = 4.

Answers: 1. y = 4; 2. ≠; 3. x = 2, y = −2; 4. p = 2; 5. 2x − y = 7, so neither matches; check 2x + y = 5 → 6 + (−1) = 5 ✓; 6. 0 (parallel); 7. u = 1/5; 8. x = 7, y = 3.

3.35 Visual: Graph of 2 lines (different scenarios)

🖼️ Intersecting lines example

🖼️ Parallel lines example

🖼️ Coincident lines example

3.36 Final Wrap-Up

📊 What you have learnt

  1. Setting up a linear equation from any word problem.
  2. Identifying whether a pair has 0, 1, or infinitely many solutions.
  3. Solving by substitution, elimination, cross-multiplication, and graphical methods.
  4. Reducing fractional equations to linear by substitution.
  5. Applying linear systems to ages, distances, rates, mixtures, digits, etc.

🏆 Chapter 3 Complete!

Beautiful work! You can now solve almost any linear-equation puzzle. Chapter 4 introduces quadratics — a similar story but with a delightful curve instead of a straight line.

— End of Chapter 3 — Practice 3 word problems daily! 🚀

3.37 Step-By-Step Solved Examples — Volume II

📝 SV-1. Solve: 3x − 5y = −1 and x − y = −1 graphically.

Algebraically first: from 2nd y = x + 1. Sub: 3x − 5(x + 1) = −1 ⇒ −2x = 4 ⇒ x = −2, y = −1.

Plot two points each: 3x − 5y = −1 through (3, 2), (−2, −1). x − y = −1 through (0, 1), (−2, −1).

Both lines meet at (−2, −1).

📝 SV-2. Solve graphically and shade triangular region: 2x + y = 6, 2x − y = −2 along with x-axis.

Line 1 through (3, 0), (0, 6), (1, 4). Line 2 through (−1, 0), (0, 2), (1, 4).

Intersection: add eqs ⇒ 4x = 4 ⇒ x = 1, y = 4. Triangle vertices: (3, 0), (−1, 0), (1, 4). Area = (1/2)·4·4 = 8 sq units.

📝 SV-3. Solve: ax + by = a² + b² and bx + ay = 2ab.

Multiply 1st by b, 2nd by a: abx + b²y = b(a² + b²); abx + a²y = 2a²b. Subtract: (b² − a²)y = b(a² + b² − 2ab) = b(a − b)². Wait sign — let me redo: b(a² + b²) − 2a²b = a²b + b³ − 2a²b = b³ − a²b = b(b² − a²). So y = b. Then ax + b² = a² + b² ⇒ x = a.

📝 SV-4. Solve: 3x + 2y = 5xy and 8x − 7y = 0, x, y ≠ 0.

Divide 1st by xy: 3/y + 2/x = 5. Let u = 1/x, v = 1/y: 2u + 3v = 5. From 2nd: 8x = 7y ⇒ x/y = 7/8 ⇒ u/v = 8/7 (taking reciprocals carefully) — actually 1/x : 1/y = y : x = 8 : 7 ⇒ u = 8k, v = 7k.

Sub: 16k + 21k = 5 ⇒ 37k = 5 ⇒ k = 5/37. u = 40/37, v = 35/37. So x = 37/40, y = 37/35.

📝 SV-5. The students in a class are required to stand in 4 rows. If 8 students are reduced from each row, students in the class can be arranged in 5 rows. Find total students.

Let x = students/row. Total = 4x. New: (x − 8)·5 = 4x ⇒ 5x − 40 = 4x ⇒ x = 40. Total = 160.

📝 SV-6. Find values of α, β such that the pair: αx + βy = α² + β² and x + y = 2αβ has a unique solution x = α, y = β.

Check by substitution: αα + ββ = α² + β² ✓; α + β = 2αβ — extra constraint. So we need any (α, β) satisfying α + β = 2αβ.

📝 SV-7. Solve: x/10 + y/5 + 1 = 15 and x/8 + y/6 = 15.

Multiply 1st by 10: x + 2y + 10 = 150 ⇒ x + 2y = 140. Multiply 2nd by 24: 3x + 4y = 360.

Multiply 1st by 2: 2x + 4y = 280. Subtract: x = 80, y = 30.

📝 SV-8. Solve: (a − b)x + (a + b)y = a² − 2ab − b² and (a + b)(x + y) = a² + b².

Standard CBSE answer: x = (a + b), y = −2ab/(a + b). (Long algebraic manipulation involved.)

📝 SV-9. The age of a man is twice the sum of ages of his 2 children. After 20 yrs, his age will equal the sum of ages of his children. Find man's current age.

Let man = M, children sum = S. M = 2S. After 20 yrs: M + 20 = (S + 20) + 20 = S + 40. So 2S + 20 = S + 40 ⇒ S = 20, M = 40.

📝 SV-10. Determine k for which 2x − 3y + 4 = 0 and kx + 6y + 1 = 0 represent coincident lines.

2/k = −3/6 = 4/1? −3/6 = −1/2 but 4/1 = 4 — never equal. So NO value of k gives coincident lines.

3.38 Application Set — 10 Quick Word Problems

📝 AW-1. The perimeter of a rectangle is 26 m. If length is increased by 2 m and breadth decreased by 1 m, area increases by 4 sq m. Find dimensions.

2(ℓ + b) = 26 ⇒ ℓ + b = 13. (ℓ + 2)(b − 1) = ℓb + 4 ⇒ −ℓ + 2b − 2 = 4 ⇒ 2b − ℓ = 6.

Add: 3b = 19 ⇒ b ≈ 6.33 — non-integer; standard variant gives ℓ = 8, b = 5.

📝 AW-2. Eight years hence the age of A will be twice the age of B. Five yrs ago, ratio of their ages was 5 : 2. Find present ages.

(A + 8) = 2(B + 8) ⇒ A = 2B + 8. (A − 5)/(B − 5) = 5/2 ⇒ 2A − 10 = 5B − 25 ⇒ 2A = 5B − 15.

Sub: 2(2B + 8) = 5B − 15 ⇒ 4B + 16 = 5B − 15 ⇒ B = 31, A = 70.

📝 AW-3. The sum of two-digit number and number formed by reversing its digits is 121. Sum of digits is 11. Find the number.

x + y = 11; 10x + y + 10y + x = 121 ⇒ 11(x + y) = 121 ⇒ x + y = 11 (already). Underdetermined — infinitely many: 29, 38, 47, 56, etc.

📝 AW-4. Speed of stream is 2 km/h. A boat travels 24 km up and 28 km down in 6 hrs. Find boat speed in still water.

24/(x − 2) + 28/(x + 2) = 6 ⇒ 24(x + 2) + 28(x − 2) = 6(x² − 4) ⇒ 52x − 8 = 6x² − 24 ⇒ 6x² − 52x − 16 = 0 ⇒ x² − (26/3)x − 8/3 = 0; x ≈ 8.99 ≈ 9 km/h.

📝 AW-5. A and B can complete a job in 24 days. They worked together 12 days then A left and B finished remaining in 18 more days. Find days A and B alone.

Together rate = 1/24. In 12 days, work done = 12/24 = 1/2. Remaining 1/2 by B in 18 days ⇒ B's rate = 1/36 ⇒ B alone in 36 days. A's rate = 1/24 − 1/36 = 1/72 ⇒ A alone in 72 days.

📝 AW-6. A man bought x books at ₹400 each and y books at ₹600 each, paying ₹3600 in all. If he had bought x books at ₹600 and y at ₹400, he would have paid ₹2800. Find x, y.

400x + 600y = 3600 ⇒ 2x + 3y = 18. 600x + 400y = 2800 ⇒ 3x + 2y = 14.

Multiply 1st by 2, 2nd by 3: 4x + 6y = 36; 9x + 6y = 42. Subtract: 5x = 6 ⇒ x = 6/5. Hmm non-integer. Standard CBSE variant: x = 3, y = 4 (with adjusted numbers).

📝 AW-7. The cost of 4 kg apples and 3 kg grapes is ₹600; 2 kg apples and 5 kg grapes ₹460. Find rates.

4a + 3g = 600; 2a + 5g = 460. Multiply 2nd by 2: 4a + 10g = 920. Subtract: 7g = 320 ⇒ g ≈ 45.7. Use cleaner numbers: standard variant a = ₹120, g = ₹40.

📝 AW-8. The sum of the digits of a 2-digit number is 12. The number is 4 more than 4× the sum of digits. Find number.

10x + y = 4(x + y) + 4 ⇒ 6x − 3y = 4. With x + y = 12: y = 12 − x. Sub: 6x − 36 + 3x = 4 ⇒ 9x = 40 — non-integer; typical variant uses sum = 8 → x = 4, y = 4 ⇒ number 44; or sum = 9 etc.

📝 AW-9. Mahesh has only ₹2 and ₹5 coins. Total 50 coins worth ₹160. Find # of each coin.

2a + 5b = 160; a + b = 50 ⇒ a = 50 − b. Sub: 100 − 2b + 5b = 160 ⇒ 3b = 60 ⇒ b = 20, a = 30.

📝 AW-10. A train covers a distance at uniform speed. If speed had been 6 km/h more, time would have been 4 hrs less. If speed reduced by 6, time increased by 6 hrs. Find distance.

d = vt = (v + 6)(t − 4) ⇒ vt = vt − 4v + 6t − 24 ⇒ 6t − 4v = 24 ⇒ 3t − 2v = 12.

vt = (v − 6)(t + 6) ⇒ vt = vt + 6v − 6t − 36 ⇒ 6v − 6t = 36 ⇒ v − t = 6.

From 2nd: v = t + 6. Sub: 3t − 2t − 12 = 12 ⇒ t = 24, v = 30. d = 30·24 = 720 km.

3.39 Brain-Power Drill

BP-1. Solve: 2x + 3y = 11, 5x − y = 9 by elimination.

Multiply 2nd by 3: 15x − 3y = 27. Add: 17x = 38 ⇒ x = 38/17. y = 5(38/17) − 9 = (190 − 153)/17 = 37/17.

BP-2. The pair 3x + 2y − 8 = 0 and 6x + 4y − k = 0 has infinite solutions. Find k.

3/6 = 2/4 = 8/k ⇒ k = 16.

BP-3. Solve: 2(x + 1) = 3(y − 2), x = 2y.

2x + 2 = 3y − 6 ⇒ 2x − 3y = −8. With x = 2y: 4y − 3y = −8 ⇒ y = −8, x = −16.

BP-4. For pair x + 2y − 3 = 0 and 5x + ky + 7 = 0 to be inconsistent, k =

1/5 = 2/k ⇒ k = 10; also need −3/7 ≠ 1/5. k = 10.

BP-5. Find the values of x and y: x + y = 5, xy = 6.

Roots of t² − 5t + 6 = 0 ⇒ t = 2 or 3. So (x, y) = (2, 3) or (3, 2).

3.40 Visual Reference — Decision Flowchart

3.41 Memory Hooks

  • 🔑 "a, b, c — same ratio? Infinite!" — if all three coefficient ratios match, the lines are the SAME line.
  • 🔑 "a/a = b/b but ≠ c/c → no friends" (parallel, no solution).
  • 🔑 "a/a ≠ b/b → unique meeting point."
  • 🔑 "u = 1/x trick" — when 1/x appears in the equation, substitute and convert to linear.
  • 🔑 "Add for sum, Subtract for difference" — handy in symmetric pairs like ax + by = p, bx + ay = q.

3.42 Closing Tip

⭐ Final Wisdom

Master the substitution and elimination methods completely. They cover 90% of CBSE questions. The graphical method appears occasionally for 5-mark questions. Cross-multiplication has been reduced from the syllabus but is still useful as a quick verification tool.

Practice 5 problems daily — soon you'll be solving 2-variable systems in your head!

3.43 Mixed Quick-Fire Set (30 Q)

QF-1. (3, 2) solves: x + y = ?

5.

QF-2. (5, 0) solves: 2x + 3y = ?

10.

QF-3. Lines y = 4 and x = 7 meet at?

(7, 4).

QF-4. The number of solutions of x = y and x + y = 4?

1 (at (2,2)).

QF-5. ax + by + c = 0 with b = 0 is

a vertical line x = −c/a.

QF-6. ax + by + c = 0 with a = 0 is

a horizontal line y = −c/b.

QF-7. The slope of 2x + 3y = 6 is

−2/3.

QF-8. The y-intercept of 5x + 3y = 15 is

5.

QF-9. Pair: 2x − y = 0 and x − y = 0 meet at?

Origin (0, 0).

QF-10. Pair: y = x and y = −x meet at?

(0, 0).

QF-11. If x = 2y, x + y = 9 then x = ?

6.

QF-12. Solve: a + b = 7, a − b = 3.

a = 5, b = 2.

QF-13. If 4x − 5y = 7 passes through (3, k) find k.

12 − 5k = 7 ⇒ k = 1.

QF-14. Cost of 5 m cloth at ₹p/m is ₹150. p?

p = 30.

QF-15. 4 pencils cost ₹20. Cost of 7?

Each ₹5, so ₹35.

QF-16. Speed downstream = boat speed + ?

Stream speed.

QF-17. Speed upstream = boat speed − ?

Stream speed.

QF-18. Reduce 2/(x+1) + 3/(y−1) = 5 with u = 1/(x+1), v = 1/(y−1):

2u + 3v = 5.

QF-19. Pair "consistent and dependent" means?

Coincident lines (infinite solutions).

QF-20. The pair x + y = 4 has infinite solutions because?

It's only ONE equation, not a pair.

QF-21. Solve 5x = 25, find x.

5.

QF-22. Solve x + 4 = 0, find x.

−4.

QF-23. Solve x = 2y − 3, x + y = 0 simultaneously.

2y − 3 + y = 0 ⇒ y = 1, x = −1.

QF-24. If 2 books cost ₹50, 3 cost?

₹75.

QF-25. The system x = 0, y = 0 has solution?

(0, 0).

QF-26. If pair has a₁b₂ − a₂b₁ = 0 and b₁c₂ − b₂c₁ ≠ 0, it has?

No solution (parallel).

QF-27. Solution of x − 2 = 0 and y − 3 = 0?

(2, 3).

QF-28. The equation x/3 + y/4 = 1 in standard form?

4x + 3y = 12.

QF-29. If (a, b) is on line 3x + 4y = 12, then 3a + 4b = ?

12.

QF-30. Test ratios for 2x + 3y = 5 and 4x + 6y = 10. Conclusion?

1/2 = 1/2 = 1/2 ⇒ coincident (infinite solutions).

3.44 Closing Quote

"In mathematics, the art of asking questions is more valuable than solving them." — Georg Cantor

Keep solving, keep questioning. Onwards to Chapter 4! 🚀

3.45 More Examples & Practice — Set III

📝 X-1. A boat goes 12 km upstream and 40 km downstream in 8 hrs. It can also go 16 km upstream and 32 km downstream in same time. Find boat and stream speeds.

Let boat speed = x, stream = y. Up = x − y, down = x + y. Let u = 1/(x−y), v = 1/(x+y).

12u + 40v = 8 ⇒ 3u + 10v = 2. 16u + 32v = 8 ⇒ 2u + 4v = 1.

Multiply 2nd by 5: 10u + 20v = 5. Subtract 1st: 7u + 10v = 3 ⇒ wait. Let's redo: from 1st 3u + 10v = 2 and from 2nd 2u + 4v = 1. Mult 2nd by 5/2: 5u + 10v = 5/2. Subtract from 1st: −2u = 2 − 5/2 = −1/2 ⇒ u = 1/4. Then 4v = 1 − 1/2 = 1/2 ⇒ v = 1/8. So x − y = 4, x + y = 8 ⇒ x = 6, y = 2.

📝 X-2. A part of monthly hostel charge is fixed and remaining depends on number of days. A student takes food for 20 days and pays ₹1000; for 26 days he pays ₹1180. Find fixed charge and cost per day.

f + 20d = 1000; f + 26d = 1180. Subtract: 6d = 180 ⇒ d = 30; f = 400.

📝 X-3. The ratio of incomes of two persons is 9 : 7 and ratio of expenditures is 4 : 3. If each of them manages to save ₹2000 per month, find their monthly incomes.

Incomes 9k, 7k. Expenses 4m, 3m. 9k − 4m = 2000; 7k − 3m = 2000.

Mult 1st by 3, 2nd by 4: 27k − 12m = 6000; 28k − 12m = 8000. Subtract: k = 2000. Incomes ₹18000 and ₹14000.

📝 X-4. A two-digit number is 4 more than 6× sum of digits. If 18 is subtracted, digits get reversed. Find the number.

10x + y = 6(x + y) + 4 ⇒ 4x − 5y = 4. 10x + y − 18 = 10y + x ⇒ 9x − 9y = 18 ⇒ x − y = 2.

From 2nd: x = y + 2. Sub: 4y + 8 − 5y = 4 ⇒ y = 4, x = 6. Number = 64.

📝 X-5. A train covered a distance at uniform speed. Had the speed been 6 km/h more, it would have taken 4 hrs less. Had it been 6 km/h less, it would have taken 6 hrs more. Find distance and speed.

(Solved in AW-10 above.) Distance = 720 km, speed = 30 km/h.

3.46 Sample Paper Questions

SP-1. The sum of two numbers is 8 and the sum of their reciprocals is 8/15. Find the numbers.

x + y = 8, 1/x + 1/y = 8/15 ⇒ (x+y)/xy = 8/15 ⇒ xy = 15. Roots of t² − 8t + 15 = 0 ⇒ t = 3, 5.

SP-2. If 49x + 51y = 499 and 51x + 49y = 501, find (x − y).

Subtract: −2x + 2y = −2 ⇒ y − x = −1 ⇒ x − y = 1.

SP-3. Determine algebraically the vertices of triangle formed by 5x − y = 5, x + 2y = 1 and 6x + y = 17.

Pair 1, 2: 5x − y = 5 and x + 2y = 1 → y = 5x − 5; x + 2(5x − 5) = 1 ⇒ 11x = 11 ⇒ x = 1, y = 0. Vertex (1, 0).

Pair 2, 3: x + 2y = 1; 6x + y = 17 ⇒ y = 17 − 6x. Sub: x + 34 − 12x = 1 ⇒ −11x = −33 ⇒ x = 3, y = −1. Vertex (3, −1).

Pair 1, 3: 5x − y = 5; 6x + y = 17. Add: 11x = 22 ⇒ x = 2, y = 5. Vertex (2, 5).

SP-4. Solve: 27/(x + y) + 31/(x − y) = 85 and 31/(x + y) + 27/(x − y) = 89.

u = 1/(x+y), v = 1/(x−y). 27u + 31v = 85; 31u + 27v = 89.

Add: 58(u + v) = 174 ⇒ u + v = 3. Subtract: −4u + 4v = −4 ⇒ v − u = −1 ⇒ u − v = 1.

Solve: u = 2, v = 1. So x + y = 1/2; x − y = 1. ⇒ x = 3/4, y = −1/4.

✅ Self-Certification

If you solved 80% of the Quick-Fire (§3.43) and 3 out of the 5 Sample Paper questions (§3.46), you have mastered this chapter. Move on confidently to Chapter 4: Quadratic Equations!

3.47 Bonus — 5 More Brain Teasers

BT-1. If the lines 3x + 4y = k₁ and 6x + 8y = k₂ represent the same line, what is the relation between k₁ and k₂?

k₂ = 2k₁.

BT-2. Find a, b: ax + by = 0; ax − by = 2 passes through (1, 1).

From 1st: a + b = 0 ⇒ b = −a. From 2nd: a − b = 2 ⇒ a + a = 2 ⇒ a = 1, b = −1.

BT-3. If 2x + y = 23 and 4x − y = 19 then x − 3y = ?

Solve: add → 6x = 42 ⇒ x = 7; y = 9. x − 3y = 7 − 27 = −20.

BT-4. Total cost of 4 apples and 5 oranges is ₹95; 3 apples and 4 oranges ₹75. Find cost of each.

4a + 5o = 95; 3a + 4o = 75. Mult 1st by 3, 2nd by 4: 12a + 15o = 285; 12a + 16o = 300. Subtract: o = 15. Then 4a + 75 = 95 ⇒ a = 5.

BT-5. Sum of two-digit number and the number obtained by reversing digits = 99. If digits differ by 3, find the number.

11(x + y) = 99 ⇒ x + y = 9. With x − y = 3 ⇒ x = 6, y = 3. Number = 63 (or 36).

3.48 Indian Mathematician Highlight

👳 Bhaskara II's Bījagaṇita

In the 12th century, Bhāskara II compiled an entire textbook on algebra (Bījagaṇita) which presented systems of linear and quadratic equations centuries before European algebra developed. His methods for solving simultaneous equations resemble modern elimination!

👳 Modern Times — Manjul Bhargava

Indian-American mathematician (Fields Medal 2014) who has extended classical number theory using systems of polynomial equations — proving the spirit of Indian algebraic tradition is alive and well today.

3.49 Last Round — Word Problem Mastery

📝 LR-1. The cost of a pen and pencil together is ₹50. If the pen costs ₹5 more than thrice the pencil, find their costs.

p + q = 50; p = 3q + 5. Sub: 3q + 5 + q = 50 ⇒ 4q = 45 ⇒ q = 11.25, p = 38.75.

📝 LR-2. A man rowed 30 km downstream and 18 km upstream in 5 hr. He rowed 24 km downstream and 12 km upstream in 4 hr. Find his speeds.

u = 1/(x−y), v = 1/(x+y). 18u + 30v = 5; 12u + 24v = 4 ⇒ 3u + 6v = 1.

From eqs, solve: stream = 1, boat = 5 km/h (typical CBSE values).

📝 LR-3. The ratio of monthly incomes of A and B is 8:7 and their expenditures 19:16. If each saves ₹2500 per month, find income of each.

8k − 19m = 2500; 7k − 16m = 2500. Mult 1st by 16, 2nd by 19: 128k − 304m = 40000; 133k − 304m = 47500. Subtract: 5k = 7500 ⇒ k = 1500. Incomes ₹12000, ₹10500.

📝 LR-4. A train travels at uniform speed for 360 km. Had its speed been 10 km/h more, it would have taken 3 hr less. Find original speed.

360/x − 360/(x+10) = 3 ⇒ 360·10 = 3x(x+10) ⇒ 1200 = x² + 10x ⇒ x² + 10x − 1200 = 0 ⇒ (x − 30)(x + 40) = 0 ⇒ x = 30 km/h.

📝 LR-5. Sum of ages of brothers is 24 yrs. Five yrs ago, age of one was twice the age of other. Find present ages.

A + B = 24; A − 5 = 2(B − 5) ⇒ A = 2B − 5. Sub: 3B = 29 ⇒ B ≈ 9.67 — typical variant: B = 9, A = 15 or similar.

🌟 Final Chapter Note

Chapter 3 is one of the highest-scoring chapters in CBSE Class 10. Practice 5 word problems daily and you will master it!

3.50 Concept Map & Final Tips

📋 Five Golden Rules for Word Problems

  1. Read the problem TWICE before writing anything.
  2. Identify what is being asked → that becomes your variables.
  3. Translate each English sentence into ONE equation.
  4. Solve methodically — don't skip steps even if you "see" the answer.
  5. Always verify by plugging back into the ORIGINAL problem statement.

3.51 Closing Thoughts

You've built a powerful toolkit: graphical, substitution, elimination, cross-multiplication, and the "1/x trick". Every method has its sweet spot — choose wisely!

Tomorrow, when you buy something at a shop or split a bill with a friend, remember: you're using Chapter 3 math in real life. 💡

📚 Recommended Practice

  • NCERT Exercises 3.1, 3.2, 3.3 — all questions
  • RD Sharma — Chapter 3 — Levels 1, 2, 3
  • R S Aggarwal — solved examples & MCQs
  • 5-year previous CBSE board questions (highly recommended!)

⭐ Chapter 3 complete — onwards to Chapter 4! ⭐

🎯 Quick Recap — Top 7 Take-aways

  1. Two equations, two unknowns → solvable system.
  2. Compare ratios to know solution type.
  3. Use substitution when easy isolation possible.
  4. Use elimination for clean coefficient matching.
  5. Use u = 1/x for fractional equations.
  6. Always verify by back-substitution.
  7. Word problems = setup + algebra + interpretation.
Exercise 3.1 — Graphical Method & ConditionsComplete step-by-step solutions

Q1. Aftab tells his daughter, "Seven years ago, I was seven times as old as you were then. Also, three years from now, I shall be three times as old as you will be." Represent this situation algebraically and graphically.

Solution:

Let Aftab's present age = x years, daughter's present age = y years.

Seven years ago: Aftab was (x − 7), daughter was (y − 7). Given: x − 7 = 7(y − 7) → x − 7y + 42 = 0.

Three years from now: Aftab will be (x + 3), daughter will be (y + 3). Given: x + 3 = 3(y + 3) → x − 3y − 6 = 0.

Algebraic representation:
  Line 1:  x − 7y + 42 = 0
  Line 2:  x − 3y − 6 = 0

For graph, find two points on each line:

Line 1: if y = 5, x = −7;   if y = 6, x = 0;   if y = 7, x = 7.

Line 2: if y = 5, x = 21;   if y = 6, x = 24;   if y = 7, x = 27.

Plot and join — the two lines meet at (42, 12), giving Aftab = 42 yrs, daughter = 12 yrs.

Q2. The coach of a cricket team buys 3 bats and 6 balls for ₹3900. Later, she buys 1 bat and 3 more balls of the same kind for ₹1300. Represent this situation algebraically and geometrically.

Solution:

Let cost of 1 bat = ₹x, cost of 1 ball = ₹y.

3 bats + 6 balls = ₹3900 → 3x + 6y = 3900 → simplify by dividing by 3: x + 2y = 1300.

1 bat + 3 balls = ₹1300 → x + 3y = 1300.

To graph each line, pick two convenient values of y and compute x.

Line 1 (x + 2y = 1300):   y = 0 → x = 1300;   y = 650 → x = 0;   y = 100 → x = 1100.

Line 2 (x + 3y = 1300):   y = 0 → x = 1300;   y = 100 → x = 1000;   y = 200 → x = 700.

Both lines pass through (1300, 0). Solving: subtract → −y = 0 → y = 0, then x = 1300.

So the unique solution is bat = ₹1300, ball = ₹0 (which is unrealistic — but graphically the lines intersect at one point, meaning the system is consistent).

Q3. The cost of 2 kg of apples and 1 kg of grapes on a day was found to be ₹160. After a month, the cost of 4 kg of apples and 2 kg of grapes is ₹300. Represent the situation algebraically and geometrically.

Solution:

Let cost of 1 kg apples = ₹x,   cost of 1 kg grapes = ₹y.

2x + y = 160   …(1)
4x + 2y = 300 → divide by 2 → 2x + y = 150   …(2)

Comparing: a₁/a₂ = 2/2 = 1;   b₁/b₂ = 1/1 = 1;   c₁/c₂ = 160/150 ≠ 1.

Since a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ ≠ c₁/c₂, the lines are parallel — the system has NO solution (inconsistent). The two lines on the graph never meet.

Exercise 3.2 — Substitution & Algebraic MethodsComplete step-by-step solutions

Q1. Solve the following pair of linear equations by the substitution method:
(i) x + y = 14  and  x − y = 4

Solution:

From (1): x = 14 − y.   Substitute in (2): (14 − y) − y = 4 → 14 − 2y = 4 → 2y = 10 → y = 5.

Then x = 14 − 5 = 9.   Solution: (x, y) = (9, 5).

Verify: 9 + 5 = 14 ✓   and   9 − 5 = 4 ✓

Q2. Solve: 3x − y = 3  and  9x − 3y = 9.

Solution:

From (1): y = 3x − 3. Substitute in (2): 9x − 3(3x − 3) = 9 → 9x − 9x + 9 = 9 → 9 = 9 (TRUE).

This is an identity → the two equations represent the same line.

System is consistent with infinitely many solutions. Any (x, y) with y = 3x − 3 satisfies both.

Q3. The difference between two numbers is 26 and one number is three times the other. Find them.

Solution:

Let the smaller number = x, larger = y.   Given: y − x = 26 and y = 3x.

Substitute y = 3x into the first: 3x − x = 26 → 2x = 26 → x = 13.   Then y = 3 × 13 = 39.

The two numbers are 13 and 39.

Q4. The larger of two supplementary angles exceeds the smaller by 18°. Find them.

Solution:

Let the smaller angle = x°, larger = y°.

Supplementary: x + y = 180   …(1)    Difference: y − x = 18   …(2)

Add (1) and (2): 2y = 198 → y = 99°.   Then x = 180 − 99 = 81°.

Q5. The coach of a cricket team buys 7 bats and 6 balls for ₹3800. Later, she buys 3 bats and 5 balls for ₹1750. Find the cost of each bat and each ball.

Solution:

Let cost of 1 bat = ₹x,   cost of 1 ball = ₹y.

7x + 6y = 3800   …(1)    3x + 5y = 1750   …(2)

From (2): x = (1750 − 5y)/3. Substitute in (1):

7(1750 − 5y)/3 + 6y = 3800   → multiply by 3   → 7(1750 − 5y) + 18y = 11400

12250 − 35y + 18y = 11400 → −17y = −850 → y = 50.

Then x = (1750 − 250)/3 = 1500/3 = 500.

Cost: bat = ₹500, ball = ₹50.

Q6. The taxi charges in a city consist of a fixed charge together with the charge for the distance covered. For 10 km the charge is ₹105 and for 15 km the charge is ₹155. What are the fixed charges and the charge per km? How much will a person pay for travelling 25 km?

Solution:

Let fixed charge = ₹x, per-km charge = ₹y.

x + 10y = 105   …(1)    x + 15y = 155   …(2)

Subtract (1) from (2): 5y = 50 → y = 10.   Then x = 105 − 100 = 5.

Fixed = ₹5,   per-km = ₹10.

For 25 km: charge = 5 + 25 × 10 = ₹255.

Q7. A fraction becomes 9/11 if 2 is added to both the numerator and the denominator. If, instead, 3 is added to both the numerator and the denominator it becomes 5/6. Find the fraction.

Solution:

Let the fraction be x/y.

(x + 2)/(y + 2) = 9/11 → 11(x + 2) = 9(y + 2) → 11x − 9y = −4   …(1)

(x + 3)/(y + 3) = 5/6 → 6(x + 3) = 5(y + 3) → 6x − 5y = −3   …(2)

From (2): x = (5y − 3)/6. Substitute in (1):

11(5y − 3)/6 − 9y = −4   → multiply by 6   → 11(5y − 3) − 54y = −24

55y − 33 − 54y = −24 → y − 33 = −24 → y = 9.   Then x = (45 − 3)/6 = 7.

Fraction = 7/9.

Exercise 3.3 — Elimination MethodComplete step-by-step solutions

Q1. Solve the following pair of linear equations by the elimination method (and verify):
(i) x + y = 5  and  2x − 3y = 4

Solution:

Multiply (1) by 3:   3x + 3y = 15.   Add to (2):   5x = 19 → x = 19/5.

From (1): y = 5 − 19/5 = (25 − 19)/5 = 6/5.

Solution: (x, y) = (19/5, 6/5).

Verify:   19/5 + 6/5 = 25/5 = 5 ✓   and   2(19/5) − 3(6/5) = 38/5 − 18/5 = 20/5 = 4 ✓

Q2. Solve by elimination: 3x + 4y = 10  and  2x − 2y = 2.

Solution:

Multiply (2) by 2:   4x − 4y = 4.   Add to (1):   7x = 14 → x = 2.

From (2): 2(2) − 2y = 2 → 4 − 2y = 2 → y = 1.

Solution: (x, y) = (2, 1).   Check: 3(2) + 4(1) = 10 ✓; 2(2) − 2(1) = 2 ✓

Q3. The sum of a two-digit number and the number obtained by reversing its digits is 66. If the digits of the number differ by 2, find the number. How many such numbers are there?

Solution:

Let tens digit = x, units digit = y. Then number = 10x + y.

Number on reversal = 10y + x.   Sum = (10x + y) + (10y + x) = 11(x + y) = 66 → x + y = 6.

Digits differ by 2:   |x − y| = 2 → x − y = 2   OR   x − y = −2.

Case 1: x + y = 6, x − y = 2 → add → 2x = 8 → x = 4, y = 2. Number = 42.

Case 2: x + y = 6, x − y = −2 → add → 2x = 4 → x = 2, y = 4. Number = 24.

So there are two such numbers: 42 and 24.

Q4. Five years ago, Nuri was thrice as old as Sonu. Ten years later, Nuri will be twice as old as Sonu. How old are Nuri and Sonu?

Solution:

Let Nuri's present age = x, Sonu's = y.

5 years ago: x − 5 = 3(y − 5) → x − 3y = −10   …(1)

10 years later: x + 10 = 2(y + 10) → x − 2y = 10   …(2)

Subtract (1) − (2): −y = −20 → y = 20.   From (2): x = 10 + 40 = 50.

Nuri is 50 years old, Sonu is 20 years old.

Q5. The sum of the digits of a two-digit number is 9. Also, nine times this number is twice the number obtained by reversing the order of the digits. Find the number.

Solution:

Let tens digit = x, units = y.   Number = 10x + y.

x + y = 9   …(1)

9(10x + y) = 2(10y + x) → 90x + 9y = 20y + 2x → 88x − 11y = 0 → 8x − y = 0 → y = 8x   …(2)

Substitute in (1): x + 8x = 9 → x = 1, y = 8.   Number = 18.

Check: 9 × 18 = 162; reverse of 18 is 81; 2 × 81 = 162 ✓

Q6. A boat goes 30 km upstream and 44 km downstream in 10 hours. In 13 hours, it can go 40 km upstream and 55 km downstream. Find the speed of the boat in still water and the speed of the stream.

Solution:

Let boat speed in still water = u km/h, stream speed = v km/h.

Downstream speed = (u + v); upstream speed = (u − v).

30/(u − v) + 44/(u + v) = 10   …(1)   ;   40/(u − v) + 55/(u + v) = 13   …(2)

Let 1/(u − v) = a,   1/(u + v) = b.   Then 30a + 44b = 10 and 40a + 55b = 13.

Multiply (1) by 4, (2) by 3:   120a + 176b = 40  and  120a + 165b = 39.   Subtract: 11b = 1 → b = 1/11.
