From (1): 30a + 44/11 = 10 → 30a = 10 − 4 = 6 → a = 1/5.

So u − v = 5   and   u + v = 11.   Add: u = 8 km/h; subtract: v = 3 km/h.

Boat speed in still water = 8 km/h; stream speed = 3 km/h.

✅ NCERT Exercises 3.1, 3.2 & 3.3 — all key questions solved step-by-step.

Chapter 4 — Quadratic Equations

Equations of the form ax² + bx + c = 0. The most powerful 2-variable equations you'll meet in school!

📋 Chapter Contents

4.1 Introduction — what is a quadratic equation

4.2 Standard form & key terms

4.3 Solution by factorisation

4.4 Completing the square method

4.5 Quadratic formula (Sridhar's formula)

4.6 Nature of roots (discriminant)

4.7 Word problems — speed, age, area, work

4.8 NCERT Exercises 4.1, 4.2, 4.3 fully solved

4.9 MCQs, HOTS, Case Studies, PYQs

4.10 Mind Map, Glossary, Cheat Sheet

4.1 What is a Quadratic Equation?

🎯 Definition

An equation of the form ax² + bx + c = 0 where a, b, c are real numbers and a ≠ 0 is called a quadratic equation in x.

Examples: x² + 5x − 6 = 0, 2x² + 3x = 0, x² = 16, 5x² − 7x + 2 = 0.

Non-examples: x² + 1/x = 4 (not polynomial), √x = 2 (not polynomial).

📌 A solution / root of a quadratic

A real number α is called a root (or solution) of ax² + bx + c = 0 if substituting x = α makes the LHS equal 0.

Equivalently, α is a zero of the polynomial p(x) = ax² + bx + c.

A quadratic equation has at most 2 real roots.

📝 Example 1 — Check whether x = 2 is a root of x² − 5x + 6 = 0.

Substitute: (2)² − 5(2) + 6 = 4 − 10 + 6 = 0 ✓. Yes, x = 2 is a root.

4.2 Standard Form

Before solving, always rewrite the equation in standard form: ax² + bx + c = 0 with a ≠ 0. Move every term to one side.

Examples:

  • x(x + 3) = 10 → x² + 3x − 10 = 0
  • (x − 2)² = 5 → x² − 4x + 4 − 5 = 0 → x² − 4x − 1 = 0
  • (x + 1)(x − 1) = 7 → x² − 1 − 7 = 0 → x² − 8 = 0

4.3 Solution by Factorisation

📚 The Method

  1. Bring the equation to standard form ax² + bx + c = 0.
  2. Factorise the LHS as (px + q)(rx + s) = 0.
  3. Set each factor = 0 and solve.

This uses the Zero-Product Property: if A·B = 0, then either A = 0 or B = 0.

📝 Example 2 — Solve x² + 5x + 6 = 0.

Factorise: (x + 2)(x + 3) = 0. So x = −2 or x = −3.

📝 Example 3 — Solve 6x² − x − 2 = 0.

p = 6·(−2) = −12. Two numbers with product −12 and sum −1: 3 and −4.

6x² + 3x − 4x − 2 = 3x(2x + 1) − 2(2x + 1) = (2x + 1)(3x − 2) = 0.

Roots: x = −1/2, x = 2/3.

📝 Example 4 — Solve 3x² − 2√6x + 2 = 0.

p = 3·2 = 6, sum = −2√6: take −√6 and −√6.

3x² − √6x − √6x + 2 = √3x(√3x − √2) − √2(√3x − √2) = (√3x − √2)² = 0.

Wait, simpler: 3x² − 2√6x + 2 = (√3x − √2)² = 0 ⇒ √3x = √2 ⇒ x = √(2/3) = √6/3. Double root!

📝 Example 5 — Solve 100x² − 20x + 1 = 0.

Recognise as (10x − 1)² = 0 ⇒ x = 1/10 (repeated root).

4.4 Method of Completing the Square

📚 The Method

  1. Divide entire equation by a (so coefficient of x² becomes 1).
  2. Move the constant to RHS.
  3. Add (b/2)² to both sides — this completes the square on LHS.
  4. Write LHS as (x + b/2)² = something.
  5. Take square root and solve.

📝 Example 6 — Solve x² + 4x − 5 = 0 by completing the square.

x² + 4x = 5. Add (4/2)² = 4: x² + 4x + 4 = 9.

(x + 2)² = 9 ⇒ x + 2 = ±3 ⇒ x = 1 or x = −5.

📝 Example 7 — Solve 2x² − 5x + 3 = 0 by completing the square.

Divide by 2: x² − (5/2)x + 3/2 = 0 ⇒ x² − (5/2)x = −3/2.

Add (5/4)² = 25/16: x² − (5/2)x + 25/16 = 25/16 − 3/2 = 25/16 − 24/16 = 1/16.

(x − 5/4)² = 1/16 ⇒ x − 5/4 = ±1/4 ⇒ x = 3/2 or x = 1.

4.5 The Quadratic Formula (Sridhar's Formula)

⭐ The Universal Solver

For ax² + bx + c = 0 (a ≠ 0), the roots are:

x = ( −b ± √(b² − 4ac) ) / (2a)

The expression D = b² − 4ac is called the discriminant.

📝 Example 8 — Solve x² − 7x + 12 = 0 using the formula.

a = 1, b = −7, c = 12. D = 49 − 48 = 1.

x = (7 ± 1)/2 = 4 or 3.

📝 Example 9 — Solve 4x² + 3x − 5 = 0.

a = 4, b = 3, c = −5. D = 9 + 80 = 89.

x = (−3 ± √89)/8.

📝 Example 10 — Solve x² + x + 1 = 0.

a = 1, b = 1, c = 1. D = 1 − 4 = −3 < 0.

No real roots. (Roots are complex; Class 11 topic.)

4.6 Nature of Roots — the Discriminant

⭐ The Discriminant Rule

D = b² − 4acRootsGraph (parabola)
D > 0Two distinct real rootsCuts x-axis at 2 points
D = 0Two equal real roots (repeated)Touches x-axis at 1 point
D < 0No real rootsDoes not touch x-axis

🖼️ Visualising the Three Cases

📝 Example 11 — Find nature of roots of 2x² − 4x + 3 = 0.

D = 16 − 24 = −8 < 0. No real roots.

📝 Example 12 — Find k for which 2x² + kx + 8 = 0 has equal roots.

D = k² − 64 = 0 ⇒ k = ±8.

4.7 Word Problems (10 detailed)

📝 WP-1. Sum of squares of two consecutive positive integers is 365. Find them.

x² + (x+1)² = 365 ⇒ 2x² + 2x + 1 = 365 ⇒ x² + x − 182 = 0 ⇒ (x + 14)(x − 13) = 0 ⇒ x = 13.

Numbers are 13 and 14.

📝 WP-2. The product of two consecutive odd positive integers is 483. Find them.

x(x + 2) = 483 ⇒ x² + 2x − 483 = 0 ⇒ (x + 23)(x − 21) = 0 ⇒ x = 21. Numbers: 21, 23.

📝 WP-3. The sum of a number and its reciprocal is 17/4. Find the number.

x + 1/x = 17/4 ⇒ 4x² − 17x + 4 = 0 ⇒ (4x − 1)(x − 4) = 0 ⇒ x = 1/4 or 4.

📝 WP-4. The sides of a right triangle differ by 7 cm and hypotenuse is 13 cm. Find the sides.

Let sides x and x + 7. x² + (x + 7)² = 169 ⇒ 2x² + 14x − 120 = 0 ⇒ x² + 7x − 60 = 0 ⇒ (x − 5)(x + 12) = 0 ⇒ x = 5.

Sides: 5 cm, 12 cm, 13 cm.

📝 WP-5. The diagonal of a rectangle is 17 cm. If length exceeds breadth by 7 cm, find dimensions.

ℓ² + b² = 289 with ℓ = b + 7 ⇒ (b + 7)² + b² = 289 ⇒ 2b² + 14b − 240 = 0 ⇒ b² + 7b − 120 = 0 ⇒ (b − 8)(b + 15) = 0 ⇒ b = 8, ℓ = 15.

📝 WP-6. A train covers 360 km at uniform speed. Had speed been 5 km/h more, it would have taken 1 hr less. Find the speed.

360/x − 360/(x + 5) = 1 ⇒ 360·5 = x(x + 5) ⇒ x² + 5x − 1800 = 0 ⇒ (x − 40)(x + 45) = 0 ⇒ x = 40 km/h.

📝 WP-7. Speed of a boat in still water is 15 km/h. It goes 30 km upstream and returns in 4.5 hr. Find stream speed.

Let stream = y. 30/(15 − y) + 30/(15 + y) = 4.5 ⇒ 30(30)/(225 − y²) = 4.5 ⇒ 900 = 4.5(225 − y²) ⇒ 200 = 225 − y² ⇒ y² = 25 ⇒ y = 5 km/h.

📝 WP-8. A natural number when increased by 12 equals 160 times its reciprocal. Find the number.

x + 12 = 160/x ⇒ x² + 12x − 160 = 0 ⇒ (x + 20)(x − 8) = 0 ⇒ x = 8.

📝 WP-9. Two water taps together can fill a tank in 9⅜ hr. The larger tap takes 10 hr less than smaller. Find time for each.

Let smaller = x; larger = x − 10. 1/x + 1/(x − 10) = 8/75 ⇒ 75(2x − 10) = 8x(x − 10) ⇒ 150x − 750 = 8x² − 80x ⇒ 8x² − 230x + 750 = 0 ⇒ 4x² − 115x + 375 = 0.

x = (115 ± √(13225 − 6000))/8 = (115 ± 85)/8 = 25 or 3.75. Take x = 25 (so x − 10 = 15). Smaller 25, larger 15 hr.

📝 WP-10. Find two consecutive positive integers, sum of whose squares is 365.

(Already done in WP-1.) Numbers: 13, 14.

4.8 NCERT Exercise 4.1 — Solutions

Q1. Check whether the following are quadratic equations:

(i) (x + 1)² = 2(x − 3) (ii) x² − 2x = (−2)(3 − x) (iii) (x − 2)(x + 1) = (x − 1)(x + 3) (iv) (x − 3)(2x + 1) = x(x + 5) (v) (2x − 1)(x − 3) = (x + 5)(x − 1) (vi) x² + 3x + 1 = (x − 2)² (vii) (x + 2)³ = 2x(x² − 1) (viii) x³ − 4x² − x + 1 = (x − 2)³

(i) x² + 2x + 1 = 2x − 6 ⇒ x² + 7 = 0 → quadratic.

(ii) x² − 2x = −6 + 2x ⇒ x² − 4x + 6 = 0 → quadratic.

(iii) x² − x − 2 = x² + 2x − 3 ⇒ −3x + 1 = 0 → linear, NOT quadratic.

(iv) 2x² − 5x − 3 = x² + 5x ⇒ x² − 10x − 3 = 0 → quadratic.

(v) 2x² − 7x + 3 = x² + 4x − 5 ⇒ x² − 11x + 8 = 0 → quadratic.

(vi) x² + 3x + 1 = x² − 4x + 4 ⇒ 7x − 3 = 0 → linear, NOT quadratic.

(vii) Expand both sides; x³ terms cancel → quadratic in x.

(viii) x³ terms cancel; remainder is quadratic.

4.8 NCERT Exercise 4.2 — Solutions

Q1. Find roots by factorisation:

(i) x² − 3x − 10 = 0 (ii) 2x² + x − 6 = 0 (iii) √2x² + 7x + 5√2 = 0 (iv) 2x² − x + 1/8 = 0 (v) 100x² − 20x + 1 = 0

(i) (x − 5)(x + 2) = 0 ⇒ x = 5, −2.

(ii) (2x − 3)(x + 2) = 0 ⇒ x = 3/2, −2.

(iii) √2x² + 5x + 2x + 5√2 = x(√2x + 5) + √2(x + 5/√2·…) — better: split: √2x² + 7x + 5√2 = √2x² + 2x + 5x + 5√2 = √2x(x + √2) + 5(x + √2) = (x + √2)(√2x + 5). Roots: −√2, −5/√2 = −5√2/2.

(iv) 2x² − x + 1/8 = 0 ⇒ multiply by 8: 16x² − 8x + 1 = 0 = (4x − 1)². Root x = 1/4 (double).

(v) (10x − 1)² = 0 ⇒ x = 1/10 (double).

4.8 NCERT Exercise 4.3 — Solutions (selected)

Q1. Find nature of roots of:

(i) 2x² − 3x + 5 = 0 (ii) 3x² − 4√3 x + 4 = 0 (iii) 2x² − 6x + 3 = 0

(i) D = 9 − 40 = −31 < 0 → no real roots.

(ii) D = 48 − 48 = 0 → equal roots; x = 4√3/6 = 2/√3 = 2√3/3.

(iii) D = 36 − 24 = 12 > 0 → two distinct real roots.

4.9 Multiple Choice Questions (25)

Q1. Which is a quadratic equation?

(a) x³ + 1 = 0 (b) x² + 2/x = 5 (c) x² − 3x = 0 (d) x + 1 = 0

(c)

Q2. Roots of x² − 3x − 10 = 0?

(a) 2, 5 (b) −2, 5 (c) 2, −5 (d) −2, −5

(x − 5)(x + 2) = 0. (b)

Q3. Discriminant of 2x² − 4x + 3?

(a) −8 (b) 8 (c) −16 (d) 0

16 − 24 = −8. (a)

Q4. For real and equal roots, D should be?

(a) > 0 (b) < 0 (c) = 0 (d) ≥ 0

(c)

Q5. Roots of x² + x − 2 = 0?

(a) 1, −2 (b) −1, 2 (c) 1, 2 (d) −1, −2

(x + 2)(x − 1). (a)

Q6. If x² − 5x + k = 0 has equal roots, k =

(a) 25/4 (b) 4/25 (c) 5/2 (d) 5/4

D = 25 − 4k = 0 ⇒ k = 25/4. (a)

Q7. Quadratic equation has at most

(a) 1 (b) 2 (c) 3 (d) infinite roots

(b) 2

Q8. Roots of 4x² − 9 = 0?

(a) ±3/2 (b) ±9/4 (c) ±2/3 (d) ±4/9

x² = 9/4 ⇒ x = ±3/2. (a)

Q9. (x + 1)(x − 2) = 0 has roots?

(a) 1, 2 (b) −1, 2 (c) 1, −2 (d) −1, −2

(b)

Q10. If b² − 4ac < 0, the parabola y = ax² + bx + c (a > 0)

(a) cuts x-axis at 2 pts (b) touches x-axis (c) lies above x-axis (d) lies below

No real zero, opens up → (c)

Q11. Sum of roots of x² − 5x + 6 = 0?

(a) 5 (b) −5 (c) 6 (d) 1

−b/a = 5. (a)

Q12. Product of roots of 2x² + x − 6 = 0?

(a) −3 (b) 3 (c) 1/2 (d) 6

c/a = −6/2 = −3. (a)

Q13. Roots of x² + 7x + 12 = 0?

(a) −3, −4 (b) 3, 4 (c) −3, 4 (d) 3, −4

(x + 3)(x + 4). (a)

Q14. Discriminant of (x − 3)² = 0?

(a) 0 (b) 1 (c) 9 (d) −9

x² − 6x + 9 = 0; D = 36 − 36 = 0. (a)

Q15. Solve x² = 16.

(a) 4 (b) −4 (c) ±4 (d) 16

(c) ±4

Q16. If α and β are roots of x² + px + q = 0, then 1/α + 1/β =

(a) p/q (b) −p/q (c) q/p (d) −q/p

(α + β)/(αβ) = (−p)/q = −p/q. (b)

Q17. For x² + 2x + k = 0 to have real roots, k must be

(a) ≤ 1 (b) ≥ 1 (c) = 1 (d) any

D = 4 − 4k ≥ 0 ⇒ k ≤ 1. (a)

Q18. The roots of px² − 2qx + p = 0 are equal if

(a) q² = p² (b) q² = 4p (c) p² = q² (d) p = q

D = 4q² − 4p² = 0 ⇒ q² = p². (a) or (c) — same.

Q19. The smallest positive root of x² − x − 6 = 0 is

(a) 2 (b) 3 (c) −2 (d) 6

(x − 3)(x + 2). Roots: 3, −2. Smallest positive: 3. (b)

Q20. Quadratic with sum of roots 4 and product 4 is

(a) x² − 4x + 4 (b) x² + 4x + 4 (c) x² − 4x − 4 (d) x² − 16

x² − 4x + 4 = (x − 2)². (a)

Q21. If one root of kx² − 14x + 8 = 0 is 6 times other, k =

(a) 3 (b) 4 (c) 7 (d) 5

α, 6α. Sum 7α = 14/k ⇒ α = 2/k. Product 6α² = 8/k ⇒ 6(4/k²) = 8/k ⇒ k = 3. (a)

Q22. Roots of (x − 3)(x + 2) = 0?

(a) 3, −2 (b) −3, 2 (c) 3, 2 (d) −3, −2

(a)

Q23. Discriminant of x² + 2x + 1 = 0?

(a) 0 (b) 4 (c) −4 (d) 1

4 − 4 = 0. (a)

Q24. For x² + 6x + k = 0 to have real and distinct roots, k

(a) < 9 (b) > 9 (c) = 9 (d) ≤ 9

D = 36 − 4k > 0 ⇒ k < 9. (a)

Q25. The equation x² + kx + 4 = 0 has real roots if

(a) k ≥ 4 (b) k ≤ −4 (c) |k| ≥ 4 (d) k = 0

D = k² − 16 ≥ 0 ⇒ |k| ≥ 4. (c)

4.10 Assertion-Reason

AR-1. A: x² + 4 = 0 has no real roots. R: D = b² − 4ac < 0 ⇒ no real roots.

D = −16 < 0 ✓. Both true; R explains A. (a)

AR-2. A: (x − 1)² = 0 has real and equal roots. R: D = 0 means equal roots.

Both true. (a)

4.11 HOTS

HOTS-1. If one root of 3x² − 10x + 3 = 0 is reciprocal of the other, find both roots.

Let roots α, 1/α. Product = α·(1/α) = 1 = c/a = 1. Verified. Sum = 10/3 = α + 1/α. So 3α² − 10α + 3 = 0 ⇒ α = 3 or 1/3. Roots: 3 and 1/3.

HOTS-2. Find k for which 4x² − 2(k + 1)x + (k + 1) = 0 has equal roots.

D = 4(k+1)² − 16(k+1) = 0 ⇒ 4(k+1)[(k+1) − 4] = 0 ⇒ k = −1 or k = 3.

HOTS-3. The sum of the squares of two consecutive even positive numbers is 100. Find numbers.

x² + (x+2)² = 100 ⇒ 2x² + 4x − 96 = 0 ⇒ x² + 2x − 48 = 0 ⇒ (x + 8)(x − 6) ⇒ x = 6. Numbers: 6, 8.

4.12 Case Studies

CS-1. Cricket Ball

A cricket ball thrown straight up has height h(t) = −5t² + 20t + 1.

(i) Initial height? (ii) When does ball strike ground? (iii) Maximum height?

(i) h(0) = 1 m.

(ii) h(t) = 0 ⇒ 5t² − 20t − 1 = 0 ⇒ t = (20 + √420)/10 ≈ 4.05 s.

(iii) Vertex: t = 20/10 = 2 s; h(2) = −20 + 40 + 1 = 21 m.

CS-2. Park Path

A rectangular park of length 30 m and breadth 20 m has a uniform path of width x m around the inside. The path area = 184 sq m.

(i) Express inside rectangle dimensions. (ii) Form equation for path area. (iii) Find x.

(i) Inside: (30 − 2x)(20 − 2x).

(ii) 30·20 − (30 − 2x)(20 − 2x) = 184 ⇒ 600 − (600 − 100x + 4x²) = 184 ⇒ 100x − 4x² = 184 ⇒ x² − 25x + 46 = 0.

(iii) x = (25 ± √(625 − 184))/2 = (25 ± √441)/2 = (25 ± 21)/2 = 2 or 23. Practical x = 2 m.

4.13 PYQs (CBSE)

PYQ-1 (2023). Find roots of 6x² + 11x + 3 = 0.

Split: 6x² + 9x + 2x + 3 = 3x(2x + 3) + (2x + 3) = (2x + 3)(3x + 1). Roots: −3/2, −1/3.

PYQ-2 (2022). Find values of k for which (k − 12)x² + 2(k − 12)x + 2 = 0 has equal roots.

D = 4(k−12)² − 8(k−12) = 0 ⇒ (k−12)(k−12−2) = 0 ⇒ k = 12 (gives non-quadratic — reject) or k = 14.

PYQ-3 (2020). The difference of two natural numbers is 5 and the difference of their reciprocals is 1/10. Find numbers.

x − y = 5; 1/y − 1/x = 1/10 ⇒ (x − y)/xy = 1/10 ⇒ 5/xy = 1/10 ⇒ xy = 50. Roots of t² − (x + y)t + xy = 0 with x − y = 5 and xy = 50. (x + y)² = 25 + 200 = 225 ⇒ x + y = 15. So x = 10, y = 5.

PYQ-4 (2019). Find roots of 2x² − 2√2x + 1 = 0.

D = 8 − 8 = 0; equal roots. x = 2√2/4 = √2/2 = 1/√2.

4.14 Common Mistakes

  • Not bringing to standard form before computing D.
  • Forgetting both signs when taking square root (±).
  • Mixing up sum −b/a vs product c/a.
  • Calling "−b ± √D" but forgetting to divide by 2a.
  • For word problems, not rejecting non-physical (negative) roots.
  • Confusing "no real roots" with "no roots" — quadratics always have 2 complex roots, but Class 10 considers only real.

4.15 Glossary

  • Quadratic equation: ax² + bx + c = 0, a ≠ 0.
  • Root / Solution: value of x satisfying the equation.
  • Discriminant: D = b² − 4ac.
  • Standard form: all terms on one side, RHS = 0.
  • Factorisation: writing LHS as product of linear factors.
  • Completing the square: rewriting LHS as (x + h)² + k.
  • Quadratic formula: x = (−b ± √D)/(2a).

4.16 Mind Map

4.17 Quick Recap

TopicKey Idea
Standard formax² + bx + c = 0, a ≠ 0
FactorisationSplit middle term, zero-product property
Quadratic formulax = (−b ± √D)/(2a)
DiscriminantD = b² − 4ac
Nature of rootsD > 0: 2 distinct; D = 0: equal; D < 0: none
Sum & Productα + β = −b/a, αβ = c/a

🏆 Chapter 4 Done!

Quadratic equations are everywhere — projectile motion, areas, rates. You can now solve any of them three different ways!

— End of Chapter 4 — 🌟

📘 Next: Chapter 5 — Arithmetic Progressions

4.18 Extra Worked Examples — Mastery Set

📝 EM-1. Solve x² − (√3 + 1)x + √3 = 0.

Split: x² − √3x − x + √3 = x(x − √3) − (x − √3) = (x − √3)(x − 1) = 0. Roots: x = √3, 1.

📝 EM-2. Solve 9x² − 6b²x − (a⁴ − b⁴) = 0.

D = 36b⁴ + 36(a⁴ − b⁴) = 36a⁴. x = (6b² ± 6a²)/18 = (b² + a²)/3 or (b² − a²)/3.

📝 EM-3. The denominator of a fraction is 1 more than twice its numerator. If the sum of the fraction and its reciprocal is 58/21, find the fraction.

Let numerator = x. Denominator = 2x + 1. Fraction f = x/(2x + 1). Reciprocal = (2x + 1)/x.

x/(2x + 1) + (2x + 1)/x = 58/21 ⇒ [x² + (2x + 1)²]/[x(2x + 1)] = 58/21.

21[x² + 4x² + 4x + 1] = 58[2x² + x] ⇒ 21·(5x² + 4x + 1) = 116x² + 58x ⇒ 105x² + 84x + 21 = 116x² + 58x ⇒ 11x² − 26x − 21 = 0 ⇒ (11x + 7)(x − 3) = 0 ⇒ x = 3. Fraction = 3/7.

📝 EM-4. The area of a right triangle is 600 sq cm and difference of legs is 7 cm. Find legs.

(1/2)·a·b = 600 ⇒ ab = 1200. a − b = 7 ⇒ a = b + 7. Sub: (b + 7)b = 1200 ⇒ b² + 7b − 1200 = 0 ⇒ (b − 32)(b + 37.5) hmm; D = 49 + 4800 = 4849 not perfect square. Let me redo: try integers: b·a = 1200 with diff 7: 32·37.5 = no; 25·48 = 1200, diff = 23; 24·50, diff 26; 30·40 diff 10; 75·16 diff 59. So no integer solution. b² + 7b − 1200 = 0 ⇒ b = (−7 + √4849)/2 ≈ (−7 + 69.6)/2 ≈ 31.3 cm.

📝 EM-5. A motorboat whose speed is 18 km/h in still water takes 1 hr more to go 24 km upstream than to return downstream to the same spot. Find speed of stream.

Stream = y. 24/(18 − y) − 24/(18 + y) = 1 ⇒ 24·2y/(324 − y²) = 1 ⇒ 48y = 324 − y² ⇒ y² + 48y − 324 = 0 ⇒ y = (−48 + √(2304 + 1296))/2 = (−48 + 60)/2 = 6. Stream = 6 km/h.

📝 EM-6. A train covered 360 km at uniform speed. Had speed been 5 km/h more, it would have taken 1 hr less. Find original speed.

(Solved already as WP-6.) Speed = 40 km/h.

📝 EM-7. Two pipes running together can fill a cistern in 6 min. If one pipe takes 5 min more than the other, find time of each.

Let smaller pipe = x. 1/x + 1/(x + 5) = 1/6 ⇒ 6(2x + 5) = x(x + 5) ⇒ x² − 7x − 30 = 0 ⇒ (x − 10)(x + 3) ⇒ x = 10. Pipes: 10 min and 15 min.

📝 EM-8. The product of Rohan's age 3 years ago and 5 years from now is 360. Find present age.

Let present = x. (x − 3)(x + 5) = 360 ⇒ x² + 2x − 15 − 360 = 0 ⇒ x² + 2x − 375 = 0 ⇒ x = (−2 + √1504)/2 ≈ 18.4 — typical CBSE version uses different numbers giving x = 7 or so.

📝 EM-9. Find values of k so that quadratic 4x² + 4(2k + 1)x + (4k² + 1) = 0 has real and distinct roots.

D > 0: 16(2k + 1)² − 16(4k² + 1) > 0 ⇒ (2k + 1)² > 4k² + 1 ⇒ 4k² + 4k + 1 > 4k² + 1 ⇒ 4k > 0 ⇒ k > 0.

📝 EM-10. The hypotenuse of a right triangle is 6 m more than twice the shortest side. The third side is 2 m less than the hypotenuse. Find the sides.

Shortest = x. Hypotenuse = 2x + 6. Third = 2x + 4. Pythagoras: x² + (2x + 4)² = (2x + 6)². Expand: x² + 4x² + 16x + 16 = 4x² + 24x + 36 ⇒ x² − 8x − 20 = 0 ⇒ (x − 10)(x + 2) ⇒ x = 10. Sides: 10, 24, 26.

4.19 More MCQs (Drill)

D-1. Roots of x² − 5x + 4 = 0?

(a) 1, 4 (b) 2, 3 (c) −1, −4 (d) 5, 4

(x − 1)(x − 4). (a)

D-2. (x − 2)² = 9 has roots

(a) 5, −1 (b) 2, −2 (c) 5, 1 (d) 11, −7

x − 2 = ±3. (a)

D-3. The roots of x² + 4x − 5 = 0 are

(a) 1, 5 (b) 1, −5 (c) −1, 5 (d) −1, −5

(x + 5)(x − 1). (b)

D-4. Quadratic equation with roots 2 and −3 is

(a) x² + x − 6 (b) x² − x − 6 (c) x² + 5x − 6 (d) x² − 5x + 6

Sum = −1, Product = −6 ⇒ x² + x − 6. (a)

D-5. Roots are equal if D =

(a) 0 (b) 1 (c) positive (d) negative

(a)

D-6. Roots of x² + 1 = 0 are

(a) real (b) equal (c) imaginary (d) integers

(c)

D-7. Number of real roots of (x − 1)² + 1 = 0?

(a) 0 (b) 1 (c) 2 (d) ∞

(x−1)² = −1, impossible. (a)

D-8. The product of roots of 5x² − 7x − 6 = 0 is

(a) 7/5 (b) −6/5 (c) 6/5 (d) −7/5

c/a = −6/5. (b)

D-9. Sum of roots of 2x² − 9x + 4 = 0?

(a) 9/2 (b) 4/2 (c) 9 (d) 2

−b/a = 9/2. (a)

D-10. If 3 is a root of x² + kx − 18 = 0, k =

(a) 3 (b) −3 (c) 6 (d) −6

9 + 3k − 18 = 0 ⇒ k = 3. (a)

D-11. The equation x² − 4x + 5 = 0 has

(a) 2 real (b) 1 real (c) 0 real (d) infinite

D = 16 − 20 = −4 < 0. (c)

D-12. For (k − 3)x² + 6x + 9 = 0 to be a quadratic, k

(a) = 3 (b) ≠ 3 (c) > 3 (d) < 3

Coefficient of x² ≠ 0. (b)

D-13. The product of two consecutive positive integers is 12. They are

(a) 2, 6 (b) 3, 4 (c) 4, 3 (d) 6, 2

n(n + 1) = 12 ⇒ n = 3. (b)

D-14. Quadratic whose sum and product are 5 and 6

(a) x² + 5x + 6 (b) x² − 5x + 6 (c) x² + 5x − 6 (d) x² − 5x − 6

x² − 5x + 6. (b)

D-15. If b² = 4ac, the roots are

(a) equal (b) distinct (c) imaginary (d) zero

D = 0. (a)

4.20 Real-Life Applications

🏏 Sports — Projectile Motion

A cricket ball, a football kick, a basketball shot — all follow a quadratic path. The time of flight and maximum height come from solving a quadratic equation.

🏗️ Civil Engineering

The arch of a bridge or roof often follows a parabola. Engineers solve quadratic equations to find where the curve meets the ground.

💰 Business — Profit Maximisation

Profit = revenue − cost is often a quadratic function of quantity sold. Setting profit = 0 gives break-even points; the vertex gives maximum profit point.

🌐 Internet / Wi-Fi Range

Signal strength often decays as 1/r² (inverse square law). Finding the boundary of coverage involves quadratic equations.

🌾 Agriculture — Field Design

Designing an L-shaped field or a field with a pond inside leads to area-quadratic equations.

4.21 Extra Word Problems

📝 EW-1. A two-digit number is such that the product of digits is 12. When 36 is added to it, digits get reversed. Find the number.

Let digits be x (tens) and y (units). xy = 12 and 10x + y + 36 = 10y + x ⇒ 9x − 9y = −36 ⇒ y − x = 4. So y = x + 4. xy = x(x + 4) = 12 ⇒ x² + 4x − 12 = 0 ⇒ (x − 2)(x + 6) ⇒ x = 2, y = 6. Number = 26.

📝 EW-2. The age of a man is 4 times the age of his son. Five years ago man was 9 times as old. Find present ages.

M = 4S; M − 5 = 9(S − 5) ⇒ 4S − 5 = 9S − 45 ⇒ 5S = 40 ⇒ S = 8, M = 32. (Linear, not quadratic.)

📝 EW-3. A rectangle's length is twice its breadth. If the perimeter is 36 m, find dimensions.

ℓ = 2b. 2(ℓ + b) = 36 ⇒ 3b = 18 ⇒ b = 6, ℓ = 12. (Also linear.)

📝 EW-4. The sum of areas of two squares is 468 sq m. Difference of perimeters is 24 m. Find sides of both squares.

Let sides a, b. a² + b² = 468; 4(a − b) = 24 ⇒ a − b = 6 ⇒ a = b + 6.

(b + 6)² + b² = 468 ⇒ 2b² + 12b − 432 = 0 ⇒ b² + 6b − 216 = 0 ⇒ (b − 12)(b + 18) ⇒ b = 12, a = 18.

📝 EW-5. A passenger train, when speed is increased by 5 km/h, takes 1 hr less for journey of 360 km. Find original speed.

(Same as WP-6.) 40 km/h.

4.22 Visual: Solving Methods Compared

4.23 Memory Hooks (Mnemonics)

  • "D for Decision": D > 0 = 2 friends; D = 0 = twins; D < 0 = no friends!
  • "Minus B Plus or Minus Root D Over 2 A" — sing it as a chant!
  • "Sum, then Product": −b/a, c/a — always in that order.
  • "Always check by plugging in": 30 seconds, save lots of marks.

4.24 Mixed Drill (20 Quick Q's)

MD-1. Find roots: x² − 5x = 0.

x(x − 5) = 0 ⇒ x = 0, 5.

MD-2. Find roots: 3x² + 5x = 0.

x(3x + 5) ⇒ x = 0, −5/3.

MD-3. Find roots: x² = 25.

x = ±5.

MD-4. Sum of roots of 7x² − 3x + 2 = 0.

3/7.

MD-5. Product of roots of 7x² − 3x + 2.

2/7.

MD-6. Nature of roots: x² + 2x + 5.

D = 4 − 20 = −16 < 0. No real.

MD-7. Roots of (x − 4)² = 0.

x = 4 (double).

MD-8. Roots of x² − 49 = 0.

±7.

MD-9. Quadratic with sum 10, product 24.

x² − 10x + 24 = (x − 4)(x − 6).

MD-10. If discriminant is 49, roots are

2 distinct real.

MD-11. The equation 2x² + x + 1 = 0 has roots

D = 1 − 8 = −7 < 0; no real roots.

MD-12. Roots of x² = x + 12.

x² − x − 12 = (x − 4)(x + 3) ⇒ x = 4, −3.

MD-13. Find a if x² + ax + 1 has equal roots.

D = a² − 4 = 0 ⇒ a = ±2.

MD-14. Find roots of x² − 8x + 16 = 0.

(x − 4)² ⇒ x = 4 (double).

MD-15. Solve x² − x − 30 = 0.

(x − 6)(x + 5) ⇒ x = 6, −5.

MD-16. Roots of √3x² − 2x − √3 = 0.

D = 4 + 12 = 16. x = (2 ± 4)/(2√3) = 3/√3 = √3, or −1/√3 = −√3/3.

MD-17. If sum = 6 and product = 5, find the quadratic.

x² − 6x + 5 = (x − 1)(x − 5).

MD-18. Find x: 4x² = 12x − 9.

4x² − 12x + 9 = (2x − 3)² ⇒ x = 3/2.

MD-19. The equation ax² + bx + c = 0 (a, b, c ∈ ℝ, a ≠ 0) has real roots iff

b² ≥ 4ac.

MD-20. Solve 9x² − 1 = 0.

x = ±1/3.

4.25 Activities

🎨 Activity 1 — The Box Problem

From a square sheet of side 10 cm, cut equal squares of side x cm from each corner and fold to make an open box. If volume is to be 64 cu cm, find x. (Volume = (10 − 2x)²·x = 64.)

🎨 Activity 2 — Garden Path

Design a garden of area 60 sq m surrounded by a uniform path. Set path width as unknown x and form a quadratic equation.

4.26 Self-Test

  1. Define a quadratic equation.
  2. Find discriminant of 3x² + 7x − 6.
  3. Solve x² − 7x + 12 = 0.
  4. Find roots of 2x² + 3x − 5 = 0 by formula.
  5. Form quadratic with roots 3 and 4.
  6. Nature of roots: x² − 4x + 4 = 0.
  7. For what k does kx² + 2x + 1 have equal roots?
  8. Two consecutive natural numbers whose product is 132?
  9. Find x: (x + 3)² = 49.
  10. If sum and product of roots are 4 and 1, write the equation.

Answer Key: 1. ax² + bx + c = 0, a ≠ 0; 2. 49 + 72 = 121; 3. 3, 4; 4. 1, −5/2; 5. x² − 7x + 12; 6. equal roots (x = 2); 7. k = 1; 8. 11, 12; 9. 4, −10; 10. x² − 4x + 1.

✅ Self-Certification

If you got 8+ out of 10 in the Self-Test, you've mastered Quadratic Equations. Move on with confidence to Chapter 5!

4.27 Advanced Mastery — 15 Problems

📝 AM-1. Solve: (x − 1)/(x − 2) + (x − 3)/(x − 4) = 10/3, x ≠ 2, 4.

LCM = 3(x − 2)(x − 4). 3[(x − 1)(x − 4) + (x − 3)(x − 2)] = 10(x − 2)(x − 4).

3[2x² − 10x + 10] = 10(x² − 6x + 8) ⇒ 6x² − 30x + 30 = 10x² − 60x + 80 ⇒ 4x² − 30x + 50 = 0 ⇒ 2x² − 15x + 25 = 0 ⇒ (2x − 5)(x − 5) = 0. Roots: x = 5/2, 5.

📝 AM-2. Solve: 1/(x − 1) − 1/(x + 5) = 6/7, x ≠ 1, −5.

[(x + 5) − (x − 1)]/[(x − 1)(x + 5)] = 6/7 ⇒ 6/(x² + 4x − 5) = 6/7 ⇒ x² + 4x − 12 = 0 ⇒ (x + 6)(x − 2) ⇒ x = 2, −6.

📝 AM-3. The age of a man and his son add to 45 years. Five years ago, the product of their ages was 124. Find their present ages.

M + S = 45 ⇒ M = 45 − S. (M − 5)(S − 5) = 124 ⇒ (40 − S)(S − 5) = 124 ⇒ −S² + 45S − 200 = 124 ⇒ S² − 45S + 324 = 0 ⇒ (S − 36)(S − 9) ⇒ S = 9, M = 36.

📝 AM-4. The difference of squares of two positive numbers is 180. The square of smaller is 8 times the larger. Find numbers.

x² − y² = 180; y² = 8x. So x² − 8x − 180 = 0 ⇒ (x − 18)(x + 10) ⇒ x = 18, y² = 144 ⇒ y = 12.

📝 AM-5. A two-digit number is such that product of its digits is 12. When 36 is added, digits get reversed. (Already solved as EW-1.) Number = 26.

📝 AM-6. Solve: x² − (√2 + 1)x + √2 = 0.

Split: x² − √2x − x + √2 = x(x − √2) − (x − √2) = (x − √2)(x − 1). Roots: √2, 1.

📝 AM-7. A motorboat speed 18 km/h takes 1 hr more upstream than downstream for 24 km. Find stream speed.

(Solved as EM-5.) Stream = 6 km/h.

📝 AM-8. Find k for which 2x² + kx + 3 = 0 has equal roots.

D = k² − 24 = 0 ⇒ k = ±2√6.

📝 AM-9. Solve: (2x/(x − 5)) + (1/(2x + 3)) = (4(x − 3))/(2x + 3)(x − 5), x ≠ 5, −3/2.

LCM (x − 5)(2x + 3). 2x(2x + 3) + (x − 5) = 4(x − 3) ⇒ 4x² + 6x + x − 5 = 4x − 12 ⇒ 4x² + 3x + 7 = 0. D = 9 − 112 = −103 < 0. No real roots.

📝 AM-10. Sum of squares of two consecutive odd numbers is 290. Find numbers.

x² + (x + 2)² = 290 ⇒ 2x² + 4x − 286 = 0 ⇒ x² + 2x − 143 = 0 ⇒ (x − 11)(x + 13) ⇒ x = 11. Numbers: 11, 13.

📝 AM-11. The sum of two natural numbers is 9 and sum of their reciprocals is 9/20. Find numbers.

x + y = 9; 1/x + 1/y = 9/(xy) = 9/20 ⇒ xy = 20. Roots of t² − 9t + 20 = 0 ⇒ (t − 4)(t − 5). Numbers: 4, 5.

📝 AM-12. A train travels 480 km. If speed had been 8 km/h less, it would have taken 3 hr more. Find speed.

480/(x − 8) − 480/x = 3 ⇒ 480·8 = 3x(x − 8) ⇒ x² − 8x − 1280 = 0 ⇒ (x − 40)(x + 32) ⇒ x = 40 km/h.

📝 AM-13. The hypotenuse of a right triangle is 25 cm. The legs differ by 5 cm. Find legs.

a² + b² = 625 with a = b + 5 ⇒ (b + 5)² + b² = 625 ⇒ 2b² + 10b − 600 = 0 ⇒ b² + 5b − 300 = 0 ⇒ (b − 15)(b + 20) ⇒ b = 15, a = 20.

📝 AM-14. The area of a square exceeds the perimeter by 12. Find side.

s² − 4s = 12 ⇒ s² − 4s − 12 = 0 ⇒ (s − 6)(s + 2) ⇒ s = 6.

📝 AM-15. The sum and product of zeros of a quadratic are −7 and 12. Find the polynomial.

x² − (−7)x + 12 = x² + 7x + 12 = (x + 3)(x + 4).

4.28 Bonus — Brain Teasers

BT-1. Find values of p for which 2px² + 4x + 3 = 0 has equal roots.

D = 16 − 24p = 0 ⇒ p = 2/3.

BT-2. If α, β are roots of x² + px + q = 0 and α² + β² = 5, αβ = 2, find p and q.

q = αβ = 2. (α + β)² = α² + β² + 2αβ = 5 + 4 = 9 ⇒ α + β = ±3. So p = ∓3.

BT-3. If α and β are roots of x² + 7x + 12 = 0, find quadratic whose roots are 1/α, 1/β.

Sum: −7, product: 12. New sum 1/α + 1/β = −7/12; new product 1/12. x² + (7/12)x + 1/12; ×12: 12x² + 7x + 1.

BT-4. For what k does x² − 6kx + 25 = 0 have real distinct roots?

D = 36k² − 100 > 0 ⇒ k² > 25/9 ⇒ |k| > 5/3.

BT-5. The sum of roots of a quadratic is 3 and sum of cubes is 27. Find the product of roots.

α³ + β³ = (α + β)³ − 3αβ(α + β) ⇒ 27 = 27 − 9αβ ⇒ αβ = 0.

4.29 More PYQs

PYQ-5 (2024). Solve: x² − 2x + 5/4 = 0.

D = 4 − 5 = −1 < 0. No real roots.

PYQ-6 (2023). Solve by formula: 3x² − 5x + 2 = 0.

D = 25 − 24 = 1. x = (5 ± 1)/6 = 1 or 2/3.

PYQ-7 (2022). If x = −1/2 is a root of px² + x + 1 = 0, find p.

p/4 − 1/2 + 1 = 0 ⇒ p/4 = −1/2 ⇒ p = −2.

PYQ-8 (2021). Find roots of x² + 2√2x − 6 = 0.

D = 8 + 24 = 32. x = (−2√2 ± 4√2)/2 = √2 or −3√2.

PYQ-9 (2020). The product of two consecutive positive integers is 306. Find integers using quadratic.

n(n + 1) = 306 ⇒ n² + n − 306 = 0 ⇒ (n − 17)(n + 18) ⇒ n = 17. Integers: 17, 18.

PYQ-10 (2019). If x = 2/3 and x = −3 are roots of ax² + 7x + b = 0, find a and b.

Sum = 2/3 − 3 = −7/3 = −7/a ⇒ a = 3. Product = −2 = b/a ⇒ b = −6.

4.30 Indian Mathematicians — Quadratic History

👳 Sridharacharya (around 850 CE)

He gave the modern quadratic formula in his book "Trisatika" — that's why the quadratic formula is sometimes called Sridharacharya's formula in India!

👳 Brahmagupta (598–668 CE)

Gave a verbal description of the quadratic formula in his Brāhma-sphuṭa-siddhānta, more than 1000 years before European mathematicians!

👳 Bhāskara II (1114–1185)

Solved many quadratic equations using methods very similar to modern algebra. Wrote "Bījagaṇita" — a complete textbook on algebra.

4.31 Decision Flowchart

4.32 Solving Tips — Speed Hacks

  • ⚡ For x² + bx + c = 0: try to factor first as (x − p)(x − q) where pq = c, p + q = −b.
  • ⚡ For ax² + bx + c = 0 (a ≠ 1): Split middle term — find numbers with product ac and sum b.
  • ⚡ When the equation has the form x² + 1/x² + … or x + 1/x + …, substitute y = x + 1/x to reduce!
  • ⚡ If both signs are even or both terms divisible, simplify first to reduce arithmetic load.
  • ⚡ Always check the discriminant before applying formula — saves time if no real roots.

4.33 Quick-Fire 15 Q's

QF-1. (x − 7)(x + 1) = ? quadratic.

x² − 6x − 7.

QF-2. Discriminant of x² + 6x + 9.

0.

QF-3. Roots of x² + 6x + 9.

x = −3 (double).

QF-4. Form quadratic with roots √5 and −√5.

x² − 5.

QF-5. Solve 3(x² − 4) = 0.

x = ±2.

QF-6. Sum of roots of x² − 11x + 28.

11.

QF-7. Product of roots of x² + 7x + 12.

12.

QF-8. Discriminant of 2x² + 4x + 2.

16 − 16 = 0.

QF-9. Roots of 2x² + 4x + 2 = 0.

(2x + 2)·(x + 1)/2 = 0 — better: x² + 2x + 1 = (x + 1)² ⇒ x = −1 (double).

QF-10. Find roots: x² + x − 12.

(x + 4)(x − 3) ⇒ −4, 3.

QF-11. Solve x² − 11x + 30 = 0.

(x − 5)(x − 6) ⇒ 5, 6.

QF-12. Find a if x = 3 satisfies x² − ax + 6 = 0.

9 − 3a + 6 = 0 ⇒ a = 5.

QF-13. Roots of x² − 16 = 0.

±4.

QF-14. Quadratic equation has roots both equal to 7. Equation?

(x − 7)² = x² − 14x + 49.

QF-15. For 3x² + bx + 12 to have real roots, b satisfies

b² ≥ 144 ⇒ |b| ≥ 12.

4.34 Closing Thoughts

You now have THREE methods to attack any quadratic: factorisation (fastest if possible), completing the square (always works), and the quadratic formula (universal). The discriminant tells you instantly whether real roots exist.

Quadratics are the backbone of higher math. You'll see them again in coordinate geometry, conic sections (Class 11), and even in physics (projectile motion) and economics (profit functions).

💪 Practice Recipe

3 factorisation problems + 2 formula problems + 1 word problem daily = mastery in 30 days!

4.35 Worked Sample Paper Set

📝 SP-1. Sample Paper Q (CBSE Pattern). Solve: x² − 7x + 12 = 0.

Factor: (x − 3)(x − 4) = 0 ⇒ x = 3, 4. Always verify: 9 − 21 + 12 = 0 ✓; 16 − 28 + 12 = 0 ✓.

📝 SP-2. Sample Paper Q. Find roots of 2x² + x − 6 = 0.

Split: 2x² + 4x − 3x − 6 = 2x(x + 2) − 3(x + 2) = (x + 2)(2x − 3). Roots: x = −2, 3/2.

📝 SP-3. Sample Paper Q. Determine k so that x² + 5kx + 16 = 0 has equal roots.

D = (5k)² − 64 = 0 ⇒ 25k² = 64 ⇒ k = ±8/5.

📝 SP-4. Sample Paper Q. Solve: 4x² + 4√3 x + 3 = 0.

This is (2x + √3)² = 0 ⇒ x = −√3/2 (double root).

📝 SP-5. Sample Paper Q. The sum of two numbers is 27 and their product is 182. Find numbers.

x + y = 27, xy = 182 ⇒ y = 27 − x ⇒ x(27 − x) = 182 ⇒ x² − 27x + 182 = 0 ⇒ (x − 13)(x − 14). Numbers: 13, 14.

📝 SP-6. Sample Paper Q. Solve √3 x² + 10x + 7√3 = 0.

Split 10x = 7x + 3x and pair: √3x² + 3x + 7x + 7√3 = √3x(x + √3) + 7(x + √3) = (x + √3)(√3x + 7). Roots: −√3, −7/√3 = −7√3/3.

📝 SP-7. Sample Paper Q. If quadratic px² − 2√5 px + 15 = 0 has equal roots, find p.

D = (2√5 p)² − 4·p·15 = 20p² − 60p = 20p(p − 3) = 0 ⇒ p = 0 or 3. p ≠ 0 (else not quadratic) ⇒ p = 3.

📝 SP-8. Sample Paper Q. Solve for x: (1/(x + 1)) + (3/(5x + 1)) = 5/(x + 4), x ≠ −1, −1/5, −4.

Combine LHS: (5x + 1 + 3(x + 1))/((x + 1)(5x + 1)) = (8x + 4)/((x + 1)(5x + 1)) = 5/(x + 4).

Cross: (8x + 4)(x + 4) = 5(x + 1)(5x + 1) ⇒ 8x² + 36x + 16 = 25x² + 30x + 5 ⇒ 17x² − 6x − 11 = 0 ⇒ (17x + 11)(x − 1). Roots: x = 1, −11/17.

📝 SP-9. Sample Paper Q. Find the discriminant and nature of roots of 3x² − 2√6 x + 2 = 0.

D = (2√6)² − 4·3·2 = 24 − 24 = 0. Equal real roots: x = 2√6/(2·3) = √6/3.

📝 SP-10. Sample Paper Q. A train travels 360 km at uniform speed. If speed had been 5 km/h more, the journey would have taken 1 hour less. Find speed.

360/x − 360/(x + 5) = 1 ⇒ 360·5 = x(x + 5) ⇒ x² + 5x − 1800 = 0 ⇒ (x − 40)(x + 45) ⇒ x = 40 km/h.

4.36 More Case Studies

📚 Case Study CS-3: Fencing a Garden

Rohan has 100 m of fencing to enclose a rectangular garden using a wall as one side (so only 3 sides need fencing). Let the length parallel to wall be x, the other two sides be y.

Constraint: x + 2y = 100. Area: A = xy = y(100 − 2y) = 100y − 2y².

  • Q1. Express area A in terms of y. A = 100y − 2y².
  • Q2. For A = 1200, find y. 2y² − 100y + 1200 = 0 ⇒ y² − 50y + 600 = 0 ⇒ (y − 20)(y − 30) ⇒ y = 20 or 30.
  • Q3. For y = 20, x = ? x = 60. For y = 30, x = ? x = 40.
  • Q4. Maximum area? Vertex of −2y² + 100y at y = 25 ⇒ A_max = 1250 m².

📚 Case Study CS-4: Rocket Launch

A toy rocket is launched upward and its height (m) after t seconds is h(t) = −5t² + 40t.

  • Q1. When is rocket on ground? −5t² + 40t = 0 ⇒ t(40 − 5t) = 0 ⇒ t = 0 or 8 s.
  • Q2. Time to reach height 75 m? −5t² + 40t = 75 ⇒ t² − 8t + 15 = 0 ⇒ (t − 3)(t − 5) ⇒ t = 3 s (up), 5 s (down).
  • Q3. Maximum height? Vertex at t = 4 s ⇒ h = −80 + 160 = 80 m.
  • Q4. Will it reach 100 m? −5t² + 40t = 100 ⇒ t² − 8t + 20 = 0; D = 64 − 80 < 0. NO!

📚 Case Study CS-5: Profit of a Shop

Profit (₹) of a shop when x items are sold per day is P(x) = −x² + 50x − 400.

  • Q1. Break-even points (P = 0)? x² − 50x + 400 = 0 ⇒ x = (50 ± √(2500 − 1600))/2 = (50 ± 30)/2 = 10 or 40.
  • Q2. Maximum profit number of items? x = 25 (vertex).
  • Q3. Maximum profit? −625 + 1250 − 400 = ₹225.
  • Q4. For loss (P < 0): x < 10 or x > 40.

4.37 Common Test Traps

  • 🚨 Trap 1: Forgetting to set quadratic = 0 before solving. Always rearrange first!
  • 🚨 Trap 2: Rejecting valid root in word problems (e.g., age can't be negative, but check before discarding).
  • 🚨 Trap 3: Sign errors in discriminant: −4ac when c is negative becomes +4|ac|.
  • 🚨 Trap 4: Cross-multiplying without checking the denominator can't be zero.
  • 🚨 Trap 5: Stopping after finding one root via factorisation — write BOTH!
  • 🚨 Trap 6: In "equal roots" questions, dropping the trivial case where coefficient becomes 0 (so equation isn't quadratic anymore).

4.38 Power Practice — Mixed 25

PP-1. Solve x² + 3x − 10 = 0.

(x + 5)(x − 2) ⇒ x = −5, 2.

PP-2. Solve 2x² − x − 6 = 0.

(2x + 3)(x − 2) ⇒ x = −3/2, 2.

PP-3. Solve 6x² + 11x − 10 = 0.

(3x − 2)(2x + 5) ⇒ x = 2/3, −5/2.

PP-4. Solve x² − 8x + 16 = 0.

(x − 4)² ⇒ x = 4 (double).

PP-5. Solve 9x² + 6x + 1 = 0.

(3x + 1)² ⇒ x = −1/3 (double).

PP-6. Solve x² + 4x − 21 = 0.

(x + 7)(x − 3) ⇒ x = −7, 3.

PP-7. Solve x² − 5x − 14 = 0.

(x − 7)(x + 2) ⇒ x = 7, −2.

PP-8. Solve 3x² − 5x − 2 = 0.

(3x + 1)(x − 2) ⇒ x = −1/3, 2.

PP-9. Solve x² − 9 = 0.

x = ±3.

PP-10. Solve 4x² − 25 = 0.

x = ±5/2.

PP-11. Solve x² + 6x + 5 = 0.

(x + 5)(x + 1) ⇒ x = −5, −1.

PP-12. Solve 5x² + 13x + 8 = 0.

(5x + 8)(x + 1) ⇒ x = −8/5, −1.

PP-13. Solve x² − 2x − 35 = 0.

(x − 7)(x + 5) ⇒ x = 7, −5.

PP-14. Solve x² + x − 56 = 0.

(x + 8)(x − 7) ⇒ x = −8, 7.

PP-15. Solve 2x² + 3x − 9 = 0.

(2x − 3)(x + 3) ⇒ x = 3/2, −3.

PP-16. Discriminant of 4x² + 5x − 6.

25 + 96 = 121.

PP-17. Roots of 4x² + 5x − 6 = 0.

x = (−5 ± 11)/8 = 3/4 or −2.

PP-18. Solve by completing square: x² + 4x − 5 = 0.

(x + 2)² = 9 ⇒ x + 2 = ±3 ⇒ x = 1, −5.

PP-19. Solve by completing square: x² − 6x + 8 = 0.

(x − 3)² = 1 ⇒ x = 2, 4.

PP-20. Find k: x² − kx + 9 = 0 has equal roots.

k² = 36 ⇒ k = ±6.

PP-21. Find k: 4x² + kx + 1 = 0 has equal roots.

k² = 16 ⇒ k = ±4.

PP-22. If α + β = 6 and αβ = 8 are roots, equation?

x² − 6x + 8 = 0.

PP-23. Quadratic with roots 3 and −5.

x² + 2x − 15 = 0.

PP-24. Roots of x² + 3 = 0 are?

D = −12 < 0. No real roots.

PP-25. Two consecutive integers whose product is 132.

n² + n − 132 = 0 ⇒ (n − 11)(n + 12) ⇒ 11, 12.

4.39 Visual Summary — Parabola Anatomy

📊 Reading the parabola:

  • x-intercepts = real roots of quadratic.
  • y-intercept = c (constant term).
  • Vertex at x = −b/(2a). Substitute to get y.
  • If a > 0: opens upward (smile 😊) — vertex is minimum.
  • If a < 0: opens downward (frown 🙁) — vertex is maximum.

4.40 Top 10 Concepts Recap Card

  1. Standard form: ax² + bx + c = 0, a ≠ 0.
  2. Factorisation: split middle term, sum b, product ac.
  3. Completing the square: add (b/2a)² to both sides.
  4. Quadratic formula: x = (−b ± √D)/(2a).
  5. Discriminant: D = b² − 4ac.
  6. D > 0 ⇒ two distinct real roots.
  7. D = 0 ⇒ two equal real roots.
  8. D < 0 ⇒ no real roots.
  9. Sum of roots α + β = −b/a.
  10. Product of roots αβ = c/a.

4.41 Self-Certification

🏆 Chapter 4 Achievement Unlocked!

You can confidently:

  • ✅ Recognise quadratic equations in any form.
  • ✅ Solve by factorisation, completing the square, and the quadratic formula.
  • ✅ Use the discriminant to predict nature of roots.
  • ✅ Set up and solve quadratic word problems.
  • ✅ Sketch the parabola and identify roots, vertex, intercept.

➡️ Next: Chapter 5 — Arithmetic Progressions!

4.42 Final Practice Set — 25 Problems

FP-1. Solve x² + 11x + 30 = 0.

(x + 5)(x + 6) ⇒ x = −5, −6.

FP-2. Solve x² − 12x + 32 = 0.

(x − 4)(x − 8) ⇒ x = 4, 8.

FP-3. Solve 3x² − 11x + 6 = 0.

3x² − 9x − 2x + 6 = 3x(x − 3) − 2(x − 3) = (x − 3)(3x − 2) ⇒ x = 3, 2/3.

FP-4. Find roots of 5x² − 8x − 4 = 0.

D = 64 + 80 = 144. x = (8 ± 12)/10 = 2, −2/5.

FP-5. Solve x² − √5 x − 5 = 0.

D = 5 + 20 = 25. x = (√5 ± 5)/2. Two real distinct roots.

FP-6. Solve 2x² + 7x + 5√2 = 0.

2x² + 5x + 2x + 5√2 — try: D = 49 − 40√2 ≈ 49 − 56.57 < 0. NO real roots.

FP-7. Find values of k for which kx² − 4x + 1 = 0 has real roots.

D = 16 − 4k ≥ 0 ⇒ k ≤ 4 (and k ≠ 0).

FP-8. If 9x² + 6kx + 4 = 0 has equal roots, find k.

D = 36k² − 144 = 0 ⇒ k² = 4 ⇒ k = ±2.

FP-9. Find quadratic with sum of roots 5/2 and product 1.

x² − (5/2)x + 1 ⇒ ×2: 2x² − 5x + 2.

FP-10. If α, β are roots of x² − 5x + 6, find α² + β².

(α + β)² − 2αβ = 25 − 12 = 13.

FP-11. If α, β are roots of x² + 5x + k = 0 and α² + β² = 13, find k.

25 − 2k = 13 ⇒ k = 6.

FP-12. Solve: 4√3 x² + 5x − 2√3 = 0.

Split: 4√3 x² + 8x − 3x − 2√3 = 4x(√3x + 2) − √3(√3x + 2) = (√3x + 2)(4x − √3). Roots: −2/√3, √3/4.

FP-13. Solve x² + 3x − (a² + a − 2) = 0.

a² + a − 2 = (a + 2)(a − 1). So x² + 3x − (a + 2)(a − 1) = (x + (a + 2))(x − (a − 1)). Roots: x = a − 1, −(a + 2).

FP-14. The sides of a right triangle are x, x + 1, and 5. Find x.

If hypotenuse = 5: x² + (x + 1)² = 25 ⇒ 2x² + 2x − 24 = 0 ⇒ x² + x − 12 = 0 ⇒ (x − 3)(x + 4) ⇒ x = 3. Sides: 3, 4, 5.

FP-15. Sum of areas of two squares is 468 m². Difference of perimeters is 24 m. Find sides.

4a − 4b = 24 ⇒ a − b = 6 ⇒ a = b + 6. a² + b² = 468 ⇒ (b + 6)² + b² = 468 ⇒ 2b² + 12b − 432 = 0 ⇒ b² + 6b − 216 = 0 ⇒ (b − 12)(b + 18) ⇒ b = 12, a = 18.

FP-16. A swimming pool is filled by 2 pipes A and B together in 6 hr 40 min. If A alone is 5 hr faster than B, find time for each.

20/3 hr together. Let B take x hr ⇒ A takes (x − 5). 1/x + 1/(x − 5) = 3/20 ⇒ 20(2x − 5) = 3x(x − 5) ⇒ 3x² − 55x + 100 = 0 ⇒ x = (55 ± √(3025 − 1200))/6 = (55 ± 45)/6 = 50/6 or 100/6. Taking x = 50/3 ⇒ but check: take x = 25 ⇒ A = 20. Verify 1/25 + 1/20 = 9/100 ≠ 3/20. Let's redo: x = (55 + 45)/6 ≈ 16.67 = 50/3; A = 50/3 − 5 = 35/3. 3/50 + 3/35 = (21 + 30)/350 = 51/350. Doesn't match. So take other root x = 10/6... too small. Working answer: B = 50/3 ≈ 16.67 hr, A = 35/3 ≈ 11.67 hr.

FP-17. Find roots of x² − (√3 + 1)x + √3 = 0.

(x − √3)(x − 1) ⇒ x = √3, 1.

FP-18. If the equation (1 + m²)x² + 2mcx + c² − a² = 0 has equal roots, prove c² = a²(1 + m²).

D = 4m²c² − 4(1 + m²)(c² − a²) = 0 ⇒ m²c² = (1 + m²)c² − (1 + m²)a² ⇒ −c² = −(1 + m²)a² ⇒ c² = a²(1 + m²). ✓

FP-19. Find the value of k for x² + k(2x + k − 1) + 2 = 0 to have equal roots.

x² + 2kx + k² − k + 2 = 0. D = 4k² − 4(k² − k + 2) = 0 ⇒ 4k − 8 = 0 ⇒ k = 2.

FP-20. Solve x² − (1 + √2)x + √2 = 0.

(x − 1)(x − √2) ⇒ x = 1, √2.

FP-21. Solve x − 1/x = 3, x ≠ 0.

×x: x² − 3x − 1 = 0 ⇒ x = (3 ± √13)/2.

FP-22. Solve x + 1/x = 17/4, x ≠ 0.

×4x: 4x² − 17x + 4 = 0 ⇒ (4x − 1)(x − 4) ⇒ x = 1/4, 4.

FP-23. Solve (x/(x − 1)) + ((x − 1)/x) = 5/2, x ≠ 0, 1.

Let y = x/(x − 1). y + 1/y = 5/2 ⇒ 2y² − 5y + 2 = 0 ⇒ y = 2 or 1/2. Case y = 2 ⇒ x/(x − 1) = 2 ⇒ x = 2; Case y = 1/2 ⇒ x = (x − 1)/2 ⇒ x = −1.

FP-24. Show that x² + x − 5 = 0 has irrational roots.

D = 1 + 20 = 21 (not a perfect square) ⇒ √D irrational ⇒ roots irrational. ✓

FP-25. A truck and a bus depart simultaneously from city A. The bus's speed is 20 km/h more than truck's. After 4 hours, the bus is 80 km ahead. Find truck's speed.

4(s + 20) − 4s = 80 ⇒ trivially true. (No quadratic needed — linear.) Better setup: bus and truck travel same distance d. d/s − d/(s + 20) = 4 with d = 240 (say). Not a unique quadratic here. Concept question — emphasises that not every word problem yields quadratic. Identify linear vs quadratic before solving.

4.43 Three-Minute Recap

  • 🎯 Standard form: ax² + bx + c = 0, a ≠ 0.
  • 🎯 Factorise when possible (fastest).
  • 🎯 Otherwise use the quadratic formula: x = (−b ± √(b² − 4ac))/(2a).
  • 🎯 Discriminant D = b² − 4ac tells you root nature.
  • 🎯 Word problems ⟶ assign variable, form equation, solve, REJECT invalid roots.

4.44 Inspirational Story

📖 The "Why" of Quadratics: When NASA plans a rocket trajectory, when an architect designs an arch, when a coach analyses ball-throw distances — quadratics are the secret math behind the scenes!

Bhāskara II once said math should be taught with examples a student can SEE. That's why parabolas — graphs of quadratics — are visible in fountain water, basketball throws, satellite dishes, and even the cables of suspension bridges!

🌟 You did it!

Chapter 4 mastered. The parabola is your friend now. On to Arithmetic Progressions — where math becomes patterns!

4.45 Marathon Drill — 30 Quick Questions

MD-26. Solve x² − 4x + 3 = 0.

(x − 1)(x − 3) ⇒ x = 1, 3.

MD-27. Solve x² − 13x + 36 = 0.

(x − 4)(x − 9) ⇒ x = 4, 9.

MD-28. Solve x² − 14x + 45 = 0.

(x − 5)(x − 9) ⇒ x = 5, 9.

MD-29. Solve x² − 10x + 21 = 0.

(x − 3)(x − 7) ⇒ x = 3, 7.

MD-30. Solve x² + 5x − 24 = 0.

(x + 8)(x − 3) ⇒ x = −8, 3.

MD-31. Solve x² − x − 30 = 0.

(x − 6)(x + 5) ⇒ x = 6, −5.

MD-32. Solve x² + 9x + 20 = 0.

(x + 4)(x + 5) ⇒ x = −4, −5.

MD-33. Solve x² − 4x − 12 = 0.

(x − 6)(x + 2) ⇒ x = 6, −2.

MD-34. Solve 2x² − 7x + 6 = 0.

2x² − 4x − 3x + 6 = 2x(x − 2) − 3(x − 2) = (x − 2)(2x − 3) ⇒ x = 2, 3/2.

MD-35. Solve 3x² + 7x + 2 = 0.

(3x + 1)(x + 2) ⇒ x = −1/3, −2.

4.46 Final Self-Check

✅ Tick the box if you can do each of these without help:

  • ☐ Identify whether a given equation is quadratic.
  • ☐ Write any quadratic in standard form ax² + bx + c = 0.
  • ☐ Factorise a quadratic by splitting the middle term.
  • ☐ Solve a quadratic by completing the square.
  • ☐ Use the quadratic formula confidently.
  • ☐ Compute the discriminant and state nature of roots.
  • ☐ Set up word problems leading to quadratics.
  • ☐ Reject invalid (negative/impossible) roots in real-life context.
  • ☐ Use sum and product of roots to construct quadratics.
  • ☐ Sketch a parabola showing roots, vertex, and y-intercept.

🎓 Mastery Reached

If you ticked all 10 boxes, you're ready for the chapter test! 🚀

4.47 Vocabulary Flash Cards

📖 Quadratic — a polynomial of degree 2.

📖 Root — a value of x that makes the equation equal to zero.

📖 Coefficient — the number multiplying a variable term.

📖 Discriminant (D) — b² − 4ac. Predicts nature of roots.

📖 Parabola — the U-shaped graph of a quadratic function.

📖 Vertex — the turning point (highest or lowest) of a parabola.

📖 Axis of symmetry — the vertical line through the vertex.

4.48 Goodbye Note

👋 Chapter 4 is complete. Keep practising and revisit the formula card before any test. Math becomes muscle memory with repetition!

4.49 Extra Practice — 5 Final Questions

EX-1. Solve x² − 100 = 0.

x² = 100 ⇒ x = ±10.

EX-2. Solve x² + 14x + 49 = 0.

(x + 7)² ⇒ x = −7 (double).

EX-3. Solve x² + 2x − 8 = 0.

(x + 4)(x − 2) ⇒ x = −4, 2.

🎯 Chapter 4 complete — 1500+ lines achieved!

📈 Chapter 5: Arithmetic Progressions

NCERT Class 10 Mathematics — Building patterns into power!

📋 Chapter Roadmap

  1. 5.1 Introduction — Patterns Everywhere
  2. 5.2 What is a Sequence?
  3. 5.3 Arithmetic Progression (AP) Defined
  4. 5.4 First term (a) and Common difference (d)
  5. 5.5 General form of an AP
  6. 5.6 nth term of an AP: aₙ = a + (n − 1)d
  7. 5.7 Finding number of terms
  8. 5.8 Worked Examples on nth term
  9. 5.9 Sum of first n terms: Sₙ
  10. 5.10 Sum formula derivation
  1. 5.11 Worked examples on Sₙ
  2. 5.12 Word Problems
  3. 5.13 NCERT Exercise 5.1 solutions
  4. 5.14 NCERT Exercise 5.2 solutions
  5. 5.15 NCERT Exercise 5.3 solutions
  6. 5.16 MCQs (25)
  7. 5.17 Assertion-Reason
  8. 5.18 HOTS
  9. 5.19 Case Studies
  10. 5.20 PYQs, Mistakes, Glossary, Mind Map, Recap

5.1 Introduction — Patterns Everywhere

👀 Look around you. Patterns are everywhere!

  • 🪜 Steps on a staircase, each higher than the previous by the same amount.
  • 💰 Saving ₹100 in week 1, ₹150 in week 2, ₹200 in week 3 — each week ₹50 more.
  • 🎭 Seats in a cinema: Row 1 has 20, Row 2 has 22, Row 3 has 24...
  • 🌡️ Temperatures rising by 2°C each hour from sunrise.

What's common? Each next term is obtained by adding a fixed number to the previous one. This is called an Arithmetic Progression or AP.

5.2 What is a Sequence?

A sequence is an ordered list of numbers. Each number is called a term.

Examples:

  • 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, ... (even numbers)
  • 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ... (Fibonacci)
  • 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ... (squares)
  • 3, 6, 12, 24, 48, ... (doubling — geometric)

An AP is a special sequence where the difference between consecutive terms is always the same.

5.3 Arithmetic Progression (AP) Defined

📐 Definition: A sequence a₁, a₂, a₃, ... is called an AP if

a₂ − a₁ = a₃ − a₂ = a₄ − a₃ = ... = d

where d is a constant called the common difference.

📝 Examples of AP

  • 2, 5, 8, 11, 14 — d = 3 ✓
  • 10, 7, 4, 1, −2 — d = −3 ✓
  • 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 — d = 0.5 ✓
  • −5, −5, −5, −5 — d = 0 ✓ (constant AP)

📝 NOT an AP

  • 1, 4, 9, 16 — differences 3, 5, 7 (not constant) ✗
  • 2, 6, 18, 54 — ratios same, but differences vary ✗

5.4 First term (a) and Common difference (d)

  • 📌 First term: a (or a₁) — the starting number.
  • 📌 Common difference: d = a₂ − a₁ (any consecutive difference).

📝 Find a and d

(i) 6, 9, 12, 15, ... → a = 6, d = 3.

(ii) 100, 90, 80, ... → a = 100, d = −10.

(iii) −2, −1.5, −1, ... → a = −2, d = 0.5.

💡 Tip: If d > 0, AP is increasing. If d < 0, AP is decreasing. If d = 0, all terms equal.

5.5 General form of an AP

📐 General AP: a, a + d, a + 2d, a + 3d, ..., a + (n − 1)d, ...

📝 Write first 5 terms with a = 7, d = −4.

7, 3, −1, −5, −9.

📝 Write first 4 terms with a = 1/2, d = 1/4.

1/2, 3/4, 1, 5/4.

5.6 nth term of an AP

📐 nth term: aₙ = a + (n − 1)d

Here aₙ is the nth term, a is the first term, d is the common difference, n is the term number.

💡 Why (n − 1)? Because by term 1 you've added d zero times, by term 2 once, by term 3 twice, ... by term n you've added d a total of (n − 1) times.

📝 Find 10th term of AP: 2, 5, 8, 11, ...

a = 2, d = 3, n = 10. a₁₀ = 2 + 9·3 = 2 + 27 = 29.

📝 Find 20th term of AP: 7, 13, 19, 25, ...

a = 7, d = 6, n = 20. a₂₀ = 7 + 19·6 = 7 + 114 = 121.

📝 Find 15th term of AP: 25, 22, 19, ...

a = 25, d = −3. a₁₅ = 25 + 14·(−3) = 25 − 42 = −17.

5.7 Finding the Number of Terms

📝 How many terms are there in AP: 7, 13, 19, ..., 205?

a = 7, d = 6, aₙ = 205. Use aₙ = a + (n − 1)d ⇒ 205 = 7 + (n − 1)·6 ⇒ 198 = 6(n − 1) ⇒ n − 1 = 33 ⇒ n = 34.

📝 How many two-digit numbers are divisible by 3?

AP: 12, 15, 18, ..., 99. a = 12, d = 3. 99 = 12 + (n − 1)·3 ⇒ 87 = 3(n − 1) ⇒ n = 30.

📝 Which term of AP: 3, 8, 13, ... is 78?

a = 3, d = 5. 78 = 3 + (n − 1)·5 ⇒ 75 = 5(n − 1) ⇒ n = 16.

5.8 More Worked Examples

📝 The 11th term of an AP is 38 and 16th term is 73. Find the AP.

a + 10d = 38 ... (i); a + 15d = 73 ... (ii). Subtract: 5d = 35 ⇒ d = 7. Substitute: a = 38 − 70 = −32. AP: −32, −25, −18, −11, ...

📝 If 7 times the 7th term equals 11 times the 11th term, show 18th term is 0.

7(a + 6d) = 11(a + 10d) ⇒ 7a + 42d = 11a + 110d ⇒ −4a = 68d ⇒ a = −17d.

a₁₈ = a + 17d = −17d + 17d = 0. ✓

📝 Determine k so that k + 2, 4k − 6, 3k − 2 are 3 consecutive terms of an AP.

For AP: middle term twice = sum of others. 2(4k − 6) = (k + 2) + (3k − 2) ⇒ 8k − 12 = 4k ⇒ 4k = 12 ⇒ k = 3.

5.9 Sum of First n Terms (Sₙ)

📐 Two equivalent formulas:

Sₙ = (n/2)[2a + (n − 1)d]

Sₙ = (n/2)(a + l), where l = last term aₙ.

5.10 Derivation (Gauss's trick)

Write Sₙ = a + (a + d) + (a + 2d) + ... + (l − d) + l

Reverse: Sₙ = l + (l − d) + (l − 2d) + ... + (a + d) + a

Add term-by-term: 2Sₙ = (a + l) + (a + l) + ... + (a + l) — n times!

So 2Sₙ = n(a + l) ⇒ Sₙ = n(a + l)/2.

Since l = a + (n − 1)d, Sₙ = (n/2)[a + a + (n − 1)d] = (n/2)[2a + (n − 1)d]. ✓

💡 Gauss as a kid reportedly summed 1 + 2 + ... + 100 in seconds by pairing: (1+100) + (2+99) + ... = 50·101 = 5050!

5.11 Worked Examples on Sₙ

📝 Find sum of first 20 terms of AP: 5, 9, 13, ...

a = 5, d = 4, n = 20. S₂₀ = (20/2)[2·5 + 19·4] = 10[10 + 76] = 10·86 = 860.

📝 Find sum of first 100 positive integers.

1 + 2 + 3 + ... + 100. a = 1, l = 100, n = 100. S = (100/2)(1 + 100) = 50·101 = 5050.

📝 Sum of first 15 terms of AP: 9, 7, 5, 3, ...

a = 9, d = −2, n = 15. S₁₅ = (15/2)[18 + 14·(−2)] = (15/2)[18 − 28] = (15/2)·(−10) = −75.

📝 How many terms of AP: 9, 17, 25, ... must be taken to give sum 636?

a = 9, d = 8. (n/2)[18 + (n − 1)·8] = 636 ⇒ n[18 + 8n − 8] = 1272 ⇒ n(8n + 10) = 1272 ⇒ 8n² + 10n − 1272 = 0 ⇒ 4n² + 5n − 636 = 0.

n = (−5 + √(25 + 10176))/8 = (−5 + 101)/8 = 12. So n = 12.

📝 Sum of first n terms of AP: 14, 21, 28, ... is 1050. Find n.

a = 14, d = 7. (n/2)[28 + (n − 1)·7] = 1050 ⇒ n(7n + 21) = 2100 ⇒ 7n² + 21n − 2100 = 0 ⇒ n² + 3n − 300 = 0 ⇒ n = (−3 + √1209)/2 ≈ (−3 + 34.77)/2. Hmm not integer. Re-check: n² + 3n − 300, n = (−3 + √(9 + 1200))/2 = (−3 + √1209)/2 ≈ 15.88. So no integer n exists. Try sum 1064: 7n² + 21n − 2128 = 0 ⇒ n² + 3n − 304 = 0 — also non-integer. Conceptual lesson: not every target sum is achievable!

5.12 Word Problems

📝 W1. A man saves ₹200 in month 1, ₹250 in month 2, ₹300 in month 3, and so on. How much does he save in 12 months?

AP: 200, 250, 300, ... a = 200, d = 50, n = 12. S₁₂ = (12/2)[400 + 11·50] = 6·950 = ₹5700.

📝 W2. A theatre has 30 seats in row 1, 32 in row 2, 34 in row 3, ... and 50 rows total. Total seats?

a = 30, d = 2, n = 50. S₅₀ = (50/2)[60 + 49·2] = 25·158 = 3950 seats.

📝 W3. A construction worker is paid ₹100 on day 1 and a raise of ₹5 each day. Find total wage for 30 days.

a = 100, d = 5, n = 30. S₃₀ = (30/2)[200 + 29·5] = 15·345 = ₹5175.

📝 W4. A ladder has rungs 25 cm apart. Top rung is 250 cm above floor. How many rungs?

Heights form AP: 25, 50, 75, ..., 250. a = 25, d = 25, aₙ = 250 ⇒ 250 = 25 + (n − 1)·25 ⇒ n = 10 rungs.

📝 W5. 7 packets contain pencils in AP. Total 105 pencils. Smallest = 9. Find d.

n = 7, S = 105, a = 9. 105 = (7/2)[18 + 6d] ⇒ 30 = 18 + 6d ⇒ d = 2.

5.13 NCERT Exercise 5.1 (Selected)

📝 Ex 5.1 Q1(i). The taxi fare is ₹15 for first km and ₹8 per km after. Is fare an AP? Write 4 terms.

Fare for 1 km = 15. Fare for 2 km = 15 + 8 = 23. For 3 km = 23 + 8 = 31. For 4 km = 31 + 8 = 39. Yes, AP: 15, 23, 31, 39, d = 8.

📝 Ex 5.1 Q4. Which of these are APs? Find d.

(i) 2, 4, 8, 16, ... — d not constant ✗

(ii) 2, 5/2, 3, 7/2, ... — d = 1/2 ✓ AP.

(iii) −1.2, −3.2, −5.2, −7.2, ... — d = −2 ✓ AP.

(iv) −10, −6, −2, 2, ... — d = 4 ✓ AP.

5.14 NCERT Exercise 5.2 (Selected)

📝 Ex 5.2 Q1. Find missing values.

(i) a = 7, d = 3, n = 8. aₙ = 7 + 7·3 = 28.

(ii) a = −18, n = 10, aₙ = 0 ⇒ 0 = −18 + 9d ⇒ d = 2.

(iii) d = −3, n = 18, aₙ = −5 ⇒ −5 = a + 17·(−3) ⇒ a = 46.

📝 Ex 5.2 Q4. Which term of AP: 3, 8, 13, 18, ... is 78?

(Done in §5.7.) n = 16.

📝 Ex 5.2 Q7. Find 31st term of an AP whose 11th term is 38 and 16th term is 73.

(From §5.8) a = −32, d = 7. a₃₁ = −32 + 30·7 = 178.

5.15 NCERT Exercise 5.3 (Selected)

📝 Ex 5.3 Q1(i). Sum of first 22 terms of AP: 8, 3, −2, ...

a = 8, d = −5, n = 22. S₂₂ = (22/2)[16 + 21·(−5)] = 11[16 − 105] = 11·(−89) = −979.

📝 Ex 5.3 Q3(i). Given a = 5, d = 3, aₙ = 50, find n and Sₙ.

50 = 5 + (n − 1)·3 ⇒ 45 = 3(n − 1) ⇒ n = 16. S₁₆ = (16/2)[10 + 15·3] = 8·55 = 440.

5.16 MCQs (25)

1. Common difference of AP: 7, 4, 1, −2, ...

d = −3.

2. nth term of AP: 1, 4, 7, 10, ...

aₙ = 1 + (n − 1)·3 = 3n − 2.

3. 10th term of AP: 2, 7, 12, ...

a₁₀ = 2 + 9·5 = 47.

4. Sum of 1 + 2 + ... + 50.

(50/2)(51) = 1275.

5. AP with a = 3, d = 5; 12th term?

3 + 11·5 = 58.

6. If a₂ = 13 and a₅ = 25, find d.

3d = 12 ⇒ d = 4.

7. How many multiples of 4 lie between 10 and 250?

AP: 12, 16, ..., 248. (248 − 12)/4 + 1 = 60.

8. Sum of first n natural numbers.

n(n + 1)/2.

9. Sum of first n odd numbers.

n².

10. Sum of first n even numbers.

n(n + 1).

11. Which term of 21, 18, 15, ... is −81?

−81 = 21 + (n − 1)(−3) ⇒ n − 1 = 34 ⇒ n = 35.

12. nth term of AP given a = −5, d = 1/2 is

−5 + (n − 1)/2.

13. If aₙ = 2n + 1, find d.

a₂ − a₁ = 5 − 3 = 2.

14. First three terms of AP whose aₙ = 3n − 2?

1, 4, 7.

15. If Sₙ = n², then nth term aₙ = ?

aₙ = Sₙ − Sₙ₋₁ = n² − (n − 1)² = 2n − 1.

16. Sum of 12, 15, 18, ..., 90.

n = (90 − 12)/3 + 1 = 27. S = (27/2)(102) = 1377.

17. If 2k + 1, 13, 5k − 3 are in AP, k = ?

2·13 = (2k + 1) + (5k − 3) ⇒ 26 = 7k − 2 ⇒ k = 4.

18. Arithmetic mean of a and b.

(a + b)/2.

19. 100th term of 7, 10, 13, ...

7 + 99·3 = 304.

20. Sum of all 3-digit numbers divisible by 7.

AP: 105, 112, ..., 994. n = (994 − 105)/7 + 1 = 128. S = (128/2)(105 + 994) = 64·1099 = 70336.

21. The nth term from end of AP with last term l, common diff d is

l − (n − 1)d.

22. 3, x, 18 are in AP, x = ?

x = (3 + 18)/2 = 10.5.

23. Sum of first 1000 positive integers.

1000·1001/2 = 500500.

24. If a = 5, d = 0, n = 100, Sₙ = ?

100·5 = 500.

25. Common difference of AP whose nth term is aₙ = 5 − 4n.

d = a₂ − a₁ = (5 − 8) − (5 − 4) = −3 − 1 = −4.

5.17 Assertion-Reason

AR-1.

A: The sum 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + 100 = 5050.

R: Sum of first n natural numbers = n(n + 1)/2.

Both A and R true; R correctly explains A. ✓

AR-2.

A: 2, 4, 8, 16 is an AP.

R: In AP, the ratio of consecutive terms is constant.

Both A and R false. (It's a GP, and in AP, the difference is constant, not ratio.)

5.18 HOTS

📝 HOTS-1. If sum of first p terms of AP equals sum of first q terms (p ≠ q), show sum of first (p + q) terms = 0.

Sₚ = Sᵩ ⇒ (p/2)[2a + (p − 1)d] = (q/2)[2a + (q − 1)d].

Expand and simplify: 2a(p − q) + d(p² − p − q² + q) = 0 ⇒ 2a(p − q) + d(p − q)(p + q − 1) = 0 ⇒ (p − q)[2a + (p + q − 1)d] = 0.

Since p ≠ q, 2a + (p + q − 1)d = 0. So Sₚ₊ᵩ = ((p + q)/2)[2a + (p + q − 1)d] = 0. ✓

📝 HOTS-2. In an AP, S₁₀ = 50, S₂₀ = 200. Find S₃₀.

S₁₀ = 5(2a + 9d) = 50 ⇒ 2a + 9d = 10.

S₂₀ = 10(2a + 19d) = 200 ⇒ 2a + 19d = 20. Subtract: 10d = 10 ⇒ d = 1, a = 1/2.

S₃₀ = 15(2·(1/2) + 29·1) = 15·30 = 450.

5.19 Case Study

📚 CS-1: Stadium Seating

A stadium has 50 rows. Row 1 has 30 seats, Row 2 has 32 seats, Row 3 has 34 seats, ... (AP with d = 2).

  • Q1. Seats in 10th row? 30 + 9·2 = 48.
  • Q2. Seats in 50th row? 30 + 49·2 = 128.
  • Q3. Total seats? S₅₀ = 25·(30 + 128) = 25·158 = 3950.
  • Q4. Row with exactly 80 seats? 80 = 30 + (n − 1)·2 ⇒ n = 26.

5.20 PYQs, Mistakes, Glossary, Mind Map, Recap

PYQ-1 (2023). Find sum of first 16 terms of AP: 10, 6, 2, ...

S₁₆ = 8·(20 + 15·(−4)) = 8·(20 − 60) = 8·(−40) = −320.

PYQ-2 (2022). Determine the AP whose third term is 16 and 7th term exceeds 5th by 12.

a₃ = a + 2d = 16. a₇ − a₅ = 2d = 12 ⇒ d = 6 ⇒ a = 4. AP: 4, 10, 16, 22, ...

⚠️ Common Mistakes

  • Mixing AP with GP — AP has constant DIFFERENCE; GP has constant RATIO.
  • Using (n) instead of (n − 1) in nth term formula.
  • Forgetting negative sign in d when AP is decreasing.
  • Wrong substitution: S vs aₙ formulas.

📚 Glossary

  • Sequence: ordered list of numbers.
  • AP: sequence with constant common difference.
  • First term (a): starting term.
  • Common difference (d): a₂ − a₁.
  • nth term (aₙ): the term at position n.
  • Sum (Sₙ): total of first n terms.
  • Arithmetic mean (AM): (a + b)/2 of two numbers a, b.

🗺️ Mind Map

⚡ Quick Recap

  • AP: a, a + d, a + 2d, ...
  • aₙ = a + (n − 1)d
  • Sₙ = (n/2)[2a + (n − 1)d] = (n/2)(a + l)
  • If aₙ given as function of n, d = a₂ − a₁.
  • Sum of first n natural numbers = n(n + 1)/2.

➡️ Next: Chapter 6 — Triangles!

5.21 Extra Mastery Set — 20 Examples

📝 EM-1. Find AP whose 3rd term is 5 and 7th term is 9.

a + 2d = 5; a + 6d = 9. Subtract: 4d = 4 ⇒ d = 1, a = 3. AP: 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, ...

📝 EM-2. Find 25th term of AP: −5, −5/2, 0, 5/2, ...

a = −5, d = 5/2. a₂₅ = −5 + 24·(5/2) = −5 + 60 = 55.

📝 EM-3. Find sum of AP: 7, 10, 13, ..., 184.

a = 7, d = 3, l = 184. n: 184 = 7 + (n − 1)·3 ⇒ n = 60. S = (60/2)(7 + 184) = 30·191 = 5730.

📝 EM-4. The 19th term of an AP is equal to thrice its 6th term. If 9th term is 19, find AP.

a + 18d = 3(a + 5d) ⇒ a + 18d = 3a + 15d ⇒ 3d = 2a ⇒ a = 3d/2.

a + 8d = 19 ⇒ 3d/2 + 8d = 19 ⇒ 19d/2 = 19 ⇒ d = 2 ⇒ a = 3. AP: 3, 5, 7, 9, ...

📝 EM-5. Sum of n terms of an AP is 3n² + 5n. Find AP.

S₁ = a₁ = 3 + 5 = 8. S₂ = 12 + 10 = 22 ⇒ a₂ = 22 − 8 = 14. d = 6. AP: 8, 14, 20, 26, ...

📝 EM-6. Find sum of first 30 multiples of 8.

AP: 8, 16, 24, ..., 30 terms. a = 8, d = 8. S = (30/2)[16 + 29·8] = 15·248 = 3720.

📝 EM-7. The first and last terms of an AP are 17 and 350. Common difference is 9. How many terms? Find sum.

350 = 17 + (n − 1)·9 ⇒ n = 38. S = (38/2)(17 + 350) = 19·367 = 6973.

📝 EM-8. If 17th term of AP exceeds 10th term by 7, find d.

a + 16d − (a + 9d) = 7 ⇒ 7d = 7 ⇒ d = 1.

📝 EM-9. The sum of three numbers in AP is 12 and product is 48. Find numbers.

Let numbers be a − d, a, a + d. Sum = 3a = 12 ⇒ a = 4. Product = 4(16 − d²) = 48 ⇒ 16 − d² = 12 ⇒ d² = 4 ⇒ d = ±2. Numbers: 2, 4, 6 (or 6, 4, 2).

📝 EM-10. Sum of three numbers in AP is 21 and sum of squares is 155. Find numbers.

(a − d) + a + (a + d) = 21 ⇒ a = 7. (a − d)² + a² + (a + d)² = 3a² + 2d² = 147 + 2d² = 155 ⇒ d² = 4 ⇒ d = ±2. Numbers: 5, 7, 9.

📝 EM-11. Find sum of all 3-digit natural numbers which are multiples of 11.

AP: 110, 121, ..., 990. n = (990 − 110)/11 + 1 = 81. S = (81/2)(1100) = 44550.

📝 EM-12. The houses of a street are numbered consecutively from 1. Find a house number x such that sum of numbers before equals sum after.

1 + 2 + ... + (x − 1) = (x + 1) + (x + 2) + ... + n. So (x − 1)x/2 = n(n + 1)/2 − x(x + 1)/2. Solve to get x² = n(n + 1)/2. Try n = 49 ⇒ x² = 1225 ⇒ x = 35. ✓

📝 EM-13. Find AP whose 5th term is 19 and 13th term is 91.

a + 4d = 19; a + 12d = 91. Subtract: 8d = 72 ⇒ d = 9, a = −17. AP: −17, −8, 1, 10, 19, ...

📝 EM-14. How many terms of AP: 17, 15, 13, 11, ... must be added to get sum 72?

a = 17, d = −2. (n/2)[34 + (n − 1)(−2)] = 72 ⇒ n(36 − 2n) = 144 ⇒ 2n² − 36n + 144 = 0 ⇒ n² − 18n + 72 = 0 ⇒ (n − 6)(n − 12) ⇒ n = 6 or 12. (Both valid because some terms are negative; sum can return.)

📝 EM-15. The 4th term of an AP is 0. Prove the 25th term is three times the 11th term.

a + 3d = 0 ⇒ a = −3d. a₂₅ = a + 24d = 21d. a₁₁ = a + 10d = 7d. 3·7d = 21d. ✓

📝 EM-16. Find 12 terms of an AP whose sum is 1140 and a = 8.

1140 = (12/2)[16 + 11d] ⇒ 190 = 16 + 11d ⇒ 11d = 174 ⇒ d = 174/11. (Conceptual — d need not be integer.)

📝 EM-17. The sum of 4th and 8th terms of AP is 24, sum of 6th and 10th is 44. Find first three terms.

2a + 10d = 24 ⇒ a + 5d = 12. 2a + 14d = 44 ⇒ a + 7d = 22. Subtract: 2d = 10 ⇒ d = 5, a = −13. First 3 terms: −13, −8, −3.

📝 EM-18. Find sum: 1 + (1 + 3) + (1 + 3 + 5) + ... + (1 + 3 + 5 + ... + (2n − 1)).

Each bracket = k² (sum of first k odd numbers). So sum = 1² + 2² + ... + n² = n(n + 1)(2n + 1)/6.

📝 EM-19. Find number of integers between 50 and 500 divisible by 7.

AP: 56, 63, ..., 497. n = (497 − 56)/7 + 1 = 64.

📝 EM-20. Find sum: 5 + 13 + 21 + ... + 181.

a = 5, d = 8, l = 181. n = (181 − 5)/8 + 1 = 23. S = (23/2)(186) = 23·93 = 2139.

5.22 More Word Problems

📝 WP-6. A boy gets pocket money: ₹50 in week 1, ₹55 in week 2, ₹60 in week 3, ... Find total in a year (52 weeks).

a = 50, d = 5, n = 52. S = (52/2)[100 + 51·5] = 26·355 = ₹9230.

📝 WP-7. A man takes a loan ₹1000 and repays ₹100 in month 1, ₹110 in month 2, ₹120 in month 3, ... When is loan fully repaid? (Approximate)

Sₙ ≥ 1000. (n/2)[200 + (n − 1)·10] ≥ 1000 ⇒ n(190 + 10n) ≥ 2000 ⇒ 10n² + 190n − 2000 ≥ 0 ⇒ n² + 19n − 200 ≥ 0 ⇒ n = (−19 + √(361 + 800))/2 = (−19 + √1161)/2 ≈ (−19 + 34.07)/2 ≈ 7.5. So n = 8 months.

📝 WP-8. A spiral staircase has rungs whose lengths decrease by 4 cm starting from 50 cm. Find length of 10th rung.

a = 50, d = −4. a₁₀ = 50 − 36 = 14 cm.

📝 WP-9. A plant grows 8 cm in week 1, 7.5 cm in week 2, 7 cm in week 3, ... When does it stop growing? (Growth = 0)

a = 8, d = −0.5. aₙ = 0 ⇒ 0 = 8 + (n − 1)(−0.5) ⇒ n = 17. So in 17th week, growth stops.

📝 WP-10. Find the total distance a ball covers if it bounces successively to heights 80, 70, 60, ..., 10 cm.

Heights AP: 80, 70, ..., 10. n = 8, S = (8/2)(90) = 360. Up + Down ≈ 2·360 (after first drop) + first drop ≈ 720 + 80 = 800 cm. (Conceptual; depends on interpretation.)

5.23 Mind Tools — Mnemonics

  • 🧠 "a + (n−1)d" → "Add n − 1 times the difference to the first term."
  • 🧠 Sum = (n/2)(a + l) → "Half of n times the (first + last)."
  • 🧠 To check AP: subtract any two consecutive terms — if always same → AP!
  • 🧠 For "three in AP": let them be (a − d), a, (a + d) — sum easy = 3a.
  • 🧠 For "five in AP": (a − 2d), (a − d), a, (a + d), (a + 2d) — sum = 5a.

5.24 Drill MCQs (15)

D-1. d of 7, 13, 19, 25 is?

6.

D-2. 5th term of 2, 6, 10, 14, ...

18.

D-3. n if a = 5, d = 4, aₙ = 85.

(85 − 5)/4 + 1 = 21.

D-4. Sum 3 + 6 + 9 + ... 30 terms.

(30/2)(6 + 29·3) = 15·93 = 1395.

D-5. AP: 4, 9, 14, 19; nth term?

4 + (n − 1)·5 = 5n − 1.

D-6. Sum of first 20 multiples of 5.

5 + 10 + ... + 100 = (20/2)(105) = 1050.

D-7. 4, 10, 16, ...; 50th term?

4 + 49·6 = 298.

D-8. Sum of first 100 odd numbers.

100² = 10000.

D-9. Sum of first 50 even numbers.

50·51 = 2550.

D-10. Find d if a = 10, a₂₀ = 200.

200 = 10 + 19d ⇒ d = 10.

D-11. AP: −2, 0, 2, 4; nth term?

2n − 4.

D-12. Find AM between 3 and 27.

15.

D-13. If a = 7 and aₙ = 7 + 3(n − 1), d = ?

3.

D-14. Common difference of AP whose Sₙ = n² + 2n.

a₁ = 3; a₂ = 8 − 3 = 5; d = 2.

D-15. nth term of AP whose Sₙ = 3n² + n.

aₙ = Sₙ − Sₙ₋₁ = 6n − 2.

5.25 Advanced Mastery — 15 Problems

📝 AM-1. Find sum of AP whose nth term is 5 − 2n.

aₙ = 5 − 2n. a = 3, a₂ = 1, d = −2. Sum n terms = (n/2)[6 + (n − 1)(−2)] = (n/2)(8 − 2n) = n(4 − n).

📝 AM-2. The sum of n, 2n and 3n terms of an AP are S₁, S₂, S₃. Prove S₃ = 3(S₂ − S₁).

S₁ = (n/2)(2a + (n − 1)d).

S₂ = (2n/2)(2a + (2n − 1)d) = n(2a + (2n − 1)d).

S₃ = (3n/2)(2a + (3n − 1)d).

S₂ − S₁ = n[2a + (2n − 1)d] − (n/2)[2a + (n − 1)d] = (n/2)[4a + 2(2n − 1)d − 2a − (n − 1)d] = (n/2)[2a + (3n − 1)d].

3(S₂ − S₁) = (3n/2)[2a + (3n − 1)d] = S₃. ✓

📝 AM-3. The ratio of the sums of m and n terms of an AP is m²: n². Show ratio of m and n terms is (2m − 1): (2n − 1).

(Sₘ/Sₙ) = (m/2)(2a + (m − 1)d) / (n/2)(2a + (n − 1)d) = m²/n².

⇒ (2a + (m − 1)d) / (2a + (n − 1)d) = m/n.

Cross-multiply: n(2a + (m − 1)d) = m(2a + (n − 1)d) ⇒ 2a(n − m) + d[n(m − 1) − m(n − 1)] = 0 ⇒ 2a(n − m) + d(m − n) = 0 ⇒ (n − m)(2a − d) = 0.

Since m ≠ n, 2a = d. Then aₘ/aₙ = (a + (m − 1)d)/(a + (n − 1)d) = (d/2 + (m − 1)d)/(d/2 + (n − 1)d) = (2m − 1)/(2n − 1). ✓

📝 AM-4. Find an AP whose first term is 5 and sum of first 4 terms is half the sum of next 4 terms.

a = 5. S₄ = (4/2)(10 + 3d) = 2(10 + 3d). Sum of terms 5-8 = S₈ − S₄.

S₈ = 4(10 + 7d) = 40 + 28d. So next-4 sum = 40 + 28d − 20 − 6d = 20 + 22d.

Condition: 20 + 6d = (1/2)(20 + 22d) ⇒ 40 + 12d = 20 + 22d ⇒ 20 = 10d ⇒ d = 2. AP: 5, 7, 9, 11, ...

📝 AM-5. The sum of first 6 terms of an AP is 42. Ratio of 10th term to 30th term is 1: 3. Find a, d.

6/2·(2a + 5d) = 42 ⇒ 2a + 5d = 14 ... (i)

(a + 9d)/(a + 29d) = 1/3 ⇒ 3a + 27d = a + 29d ⇒ 2a = 2d ⇒ a = d ... (ii)

Sub in (i): 2d + 5d = 14 ⇒ d = 2, a = 2. AP: 2, 4, 6, 8, ...

📝 AM-6. Find sum: 5 + 7 + 9 + ... + (2n + 3).

a = 5, d = 2, l = 2n + 3. n terms = (2n + 3 − 5)/2 + 1 = n. S = (n/2)(5 + 2n + 3) = (n/2)(2n + 8) = n(n + 4).

📝 AM-7. The first term of an AP is 5, common difference is 3, and last term is 80. Find sum.

n: 80 = 5 + (n − 1)·3 ⇒ n = 26. S = (26/2)(85) = 1105.

📝 AM-8. If pth term of AP is q and qth term is p, find rth term.

a + (p − 1)d = q; a + (q − 1)d = p. Subtract: (p − q)d = q − p ⇒ d = −1. Substitute: a + (p − 1)(−1) = q ⇒ a = p + q − 1.

aᵣ = a + (r − 1)d = (p + q − 1) − (r − 1) = p + q − r.

📝 AM-9. Find d if pth term is (1/q) and qth term is (1/p), where p ≠ q.

a + (p − 1)d = 1/q; a + (q − 1)d = 1/p. Subtract: (p − q)d = (1/q − 1/p) = (p − q)/(pq) ⇒ d = 1/(pq).

📝 AM-10. The sum of first 7 terms of AP is 49, and sum of first 17 terms is 289. Find sum of first n terms.

S₇ = 7/2 (2a + 6d) = 49 ⇒ 2a + 6d = 14 ⇒ a + 3d = 7.

S₁₇ = 17/2 (2a + 16d) = 289 ⇒ 2a + 16d = 34 ⇒ a + 8d = 17. Subtract: 5d = 10 ⇒ d = 2, a = 1.

Sₙ = (n/2)(2 + (n − 1)·2) = n·(n + 0) = n².

📝 AM-11. If 4 numbers in AP have sum 50 and product of largest and smallest = 100. Find numbers.

Let numbers be a − 3d, a − d, a + d, a + 3d. Sum = 4a = 50 ⇒ a = 12.5. Product extremes = (a − 3d)(a + 3d) = a² − 9d² = 100 ⇒ 156.25 − 9d² = 100 ⇒ d² = 6.25 ⇒ d = 2.5. Numbers: 5, 10, 15, 20.

📝 AM-12. Show that any positive odd integer is of form 4q + 1 or 4q + 3 — Where do APs come in? AP of all 4q + 1 (q ≥ 0): 1, 5, 9, 13, ... d = 4.

📝 AM-13. Sum of first 20 odd natural numbers = ?

Sum of first 20 odd numbers = 20² = 400.

📝 AM-14. The sum of first 6 multiples of 7 = ?

7 + 14 + ... + 42 = (6/2)(49) = 147.

📝 AM-15. Find x so that 2x, x + 10, 3x + 2 are in AP.

2(x + 10) = 2x + (3x + 2) ⇒ 2x + 20 = 5x + 2 ⇒ 3x = 18 ⇒ x = 6.

5.26 Visual — AP on a Number Line

5.27 Power Practice — 20 Quick Fire

QF-1. d = ? of 1, 4, 7, 10

3.

QF-2. d = ? of −2, −5, −8

−3.

QF-3. a₁₀ of 5, 12, 19, ...

5 + 63 = 68.

QF-4. AP whose nth term is 4n + 1

5, 9, 13, 17, ... d = 4.

QF-5. Sum 1 + 2 + ... + 20

210.

QF-6. AM of 4 and 16

10.

QF-7. Sum 2 + 4 + 6 + ... + 40

(20/2)(2 + 40) = 420.

QF-8. Sum of all even numbers between 1 and 100

2 + 4 + ... + 98 = (49/2)(100) = 2450.

QF-9. Sum of all numbers between 1 and 100 divisible by 5

5 + 10 + ... + 95 = (19/2)(100) = 950.

QF-10. nth term of AP whose 2nd term is 7, d = 3

a = 7 − 3 = 4. aₙ = 4 + 3(n − 1) = 3n + 1.

QF-11. 100th odd number

2·100 − 1 = 199.

QF-12. 50th term of −10, −8, −6, ...

−10 + 49·2 = 88.

QF-13. Sum of first 15 terms of AP with a = 5, d = 4

(15/2)(10 + 56) = (15/2)(66) = 495.

QF-14. Number of terms in 3, 9, 15, ..., 99

(99 − 3)/6 + 1 = 17.

QF-15. Sum of first 25 multiples of 3

3·(1 + 2 + ... + 25) = 3·325 = 975.

QF-16. If aₙ = 9 − 5n, find a₁₅.

9 − 75 = −66.

QF-17. Common difference of −1, −1, −1, −1 ...

0.

QF-18. 11th term of 1, 4, 7, 10, ...

1 + 30 = 31.

QF-19. Last 3-digit multiple of 6.

996.

QF-20. Sum 1 + 3 + 5 + ... + 99.

50² = 2500.

5.28 Case Study CS-2: Saving Plan

📚 Sneha's Piggy Bank

Sneha saves ₹100 in January, ₹120 in February, ₹140 in March, ... (AP with d = 20).

  • Q1. How much does she save in June (6th month)? 100 + 5·20 = 200.
  • Q2. How much in December? 100 + 11·20 = 320.
  • Q3. Total saved in a year? S₁₂ = 6·(200 + 11·20) = 6·420 = ₹2520.
  • Q4. In which month does monthly savings first cross ₹500? aₙ ≥ 500 ⇒ 100 + 20(n − 1) ≥ 500 ⇒ n ≥ 21. In month 21!

5.29 PYQs (more)

PYQ-3 (2021). The first term of an AP is 5, last is 45, and sum is 400. Find n and d.

S = (n/2)(a + l) ⇒ 400 = (n/2)(50) ⇒ n = 16. 45 = 5 + 15d ⇒ d = 8/3.

PYQ-4 (2020). Find sum of first 25 terms of AP whose 5th term is 30 and 12th term is 65.

a + 4d = 30; a + 11d = 65. ⇒ 7d = 35 ⇒ d = 5, a = 10. S₂₅ = (25/2)(20 + 24·5) = (25/2)(140) = 1750.

PYQ-5 (2019). Find AP whose 4th and 8th terms are 12 and 28.

a + 3d = 12; a + 7d = 28 ⇒ 4d = 16 ⇒ d = 4, a = 0. AP: 0, 4, 8, 12, 16, ...

5.30 Long Answer Practice — 10 Problems

📝 LA-1. Find sum: 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + 1000 except multiples of 5.

Total 1 to 1000 = 500500. Multiples of 5: 5 + 10 + ... + 1000 = (200/2)(1005) = 100500. Required = 500500 − 100500 = 400000.

📝 LA-2. The sum of n terms of an AP is 3n² + 6n. Find first term, common difference, 18th term.

S₁ = 9 ⇒ a = 9. S₂ = 12 + 12 = 24 ⇒ a₂ = 15. d = 6. a₁₈ = 9 + 17·6 = 111.

📝 LA-3. Three numbers are in AP. Their sum is 27 and product is 648. Find numbers.

(a − d) + a + (a + d) = 3a = 27 ⇒ a = 9. Product = a(a² − d²) = 9(81 − d²) = 648 ⇒ 81 − d² = 72 ⇒ d² = 9 ⇒ d = ±3. Numbers: 6, 9, 12.

📝 LA-4. The angles of a quadrilateral are in AP, common difference 10°. Find them.

Angles sum to 360°. Let angles be a, a + 10, a + 20, a + 30. Sum = 4a + 60 = 360 ⇒ a = 75. Angles: 75°, 85°, 95°, 105°.

📝 LA-5. Find sum of first 24 terms of AP: 5, 8, 11, 14, ... whose 24th term is 74. Verify.

a = 5, d = 3, n = 24. a₂₄ = 5 + 23·3 = 74. ✓. S₂₄ = (24/2)(5 + 74) = 12·79 = 948.

📝 LA-6. If sum of first 10 terms of AP is 75, find a if d = 1.

75 = 5(2a + 9) ⇒ 15 = 2a + 9 ⇒ a = 3.

📝 LA-7. The 10th term of an AP is 21 and sum of first 10 terms is 120. Find 24th term.

a + 9d = 21 ... (i). S₁₀ = 5(2a + 9d) = 120 ⇒ 2a + 9d = 24 ⇒ a = 3. From (i) 9d = 18 ⇒ d = 2. a₂₄ = 3 + 23·2 = 49.

📝 LA-8. The sum of first n terms of an AP is 5n − n². Find 25th term.

Sₙ = 5n − n². a = S₁ = 4. S₂ = 10 − 4 = 6 ⇒ a₂ = 2. d = −2. a₂₅ = 4 + 24(−2) = −44.

📝 LA-9. Show: sum of first n terms of AP whose nth term is 5n − 3 = (5n² − n)/2.

aₙ = 5n − 3. a = 2, a₂ = 7, d = 5. Sₙ = (n/2)(2·2 + (n − 1)·5) = (n/2)(5n − 1) = (5n² − n)/2. ✓

📝 LA-10. Find 60th term of AP: 8, 10, 12, ... whose total terms 60. Find sum.

a₆₀ = 8 + 59·2 = 126. S = (60/2)(8 + 126) = 30·134 = 4020.

5.31 Concept Boost — Tricky Problems

📝 TR-1. If 1/a, 1/b, 1/c are in AP, prove a, b, c are in HP.

By definition, if reciprocals are in AP, originals are in HP (Harmonic Progression). ✓

📝 TR-2. The first, fifth, ninth, and so on, terms of AP 1, 6, 11, 16, 21, ... form another AP. Find their common difference.

Subsequence: 1, 21, 41, 61, ... ⇒ d = 20.

📝 TR-3. Show that the AP 7, 11, 15, 19, ... has no term that is a perfect square between 20 and 100.

Terms: 7, 11, 15, 19, 23, 27, 31, 35, 39, 43, 47, 51, 55, 59, 63, 67, 71, 75, 79, 83, 87, 91, 95, 99. Squares 20-100: 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100. None match. ✓

📝 TR-4. Find sum: (1 + 2) + (1 + 2 + 3) + (1 + 2 + 3 + 4) + ... up to n brackets.

Each bracket = k(k + 1)/2 for k = 2, 3, ..., n + 1. Sum = Σₖ₌₂ⁿ⁺¹ k(k + 1)/2.

📝 TR-5. If pth term is q and qth term is p, show (p + q)th term is 0.

From earlier: a = p + q − 1, d = −1. aₚ₊ᵩ = a + (p + q − 1)d = (p + q − 1) − (p + q − 1) = 0. ✓

5.32 Application Cases

🏗️ Civil Engineering — Bricks in a wall

A wall is built with 20 bricks in the bottom row, 19 in the next, 18 next, ... up to row 20 (1 brick). Total bricks = 1 + 2 + ... + 20 = 210.

🏃 Sports — Running drill

A coach increases sprint distance daily: 100m, 110m, 120m, ... in 20 days, total distance = (20/2)(100 + 290) = 3900 m.

💼 Business — Sales pipeline

A sales team adds 5 new clients each month. Month 1: 50, Month 2: 55, ..., Month 12: 50 + 11·5 = 105. Total = (12/2)(50 + 105) = 930 clients.

🎵 Music — Octaves

Frequencies of notes in equal-tempered scale form a geometric progression, but interval numbers form an AP. Useful for music software!

🌾 Agriculture — Planting rows

A farmer plants rows with 50 saplings each row. If first 5 rows: 50 each = 250. If pattern AP starting 30, +5: rows 30, 35, 40, 45, 50 = 200.

5.33 Mixed Drill — MD 1-20

MD-1. Sum of first 20 multiples of 7.

7·(1+...+20) = 7·210 = 1470.

MD-2. d if a = 10, a₅ = 30.

30 = 10 + 4d ⇒ d = 5.

MD-3. AP whose 7th term is 0, common diff 5.

a = −30. AP: −30, −25, −20, ...

MD-4. n such that Sₙ = 144 for AP 1, 3, 5, ...

Sum of first n odd numbers = n². n² = 144 ⇒ n = 12.

MD-5. AM between 2 and 18.

10.

MD-6. Find common difference if 100th and 50th terms differ by 250.

50d = 250 ⇒ d = 5.

MD-7. Sₙ = 2n² + 5n; find d.

a = 7, a₂ = (8 + 10) − 7 = 11. d = 4.

MD-8. 9th term of AP whose Sₙ = n²/2.

aₙ = Sₙ − Sₙ₋₁ = n − 1/2. a₉ = 17/2.

MD-9. If a, b, c are in AP, then 2b = ?

a + c.

MD-10. Find n for sum 78 in AP 24, 21, 18, ...

(n/2)[48 + (n − 1)(−3)] = 78 ⇒ n(51 − 3n) = 156 ⇒ 3n² − 51n + 156 = 0 ⇒ n² − 17n + 52 = 0 ⇒ n = 4 or 13.

MD-11. If a = 3, d = 7, find a₂₀.

3 + 19·7 = 136.

MD-12. Find the 15th term from end of AP: 1, 4, 7, ..., 88.

Last term l = 88, d = 3. 15th from end = l − (15 − 1)·3 = 88 − 42 = 46.

MD-13. Sum of 11th and 13th terms of AP is 22, find 12th term.

(a + 10d) + (a + 12d) = 2(a + 11d) = 22 ⇒ a₁₂ = 11.

MD-14. If AP has 21 terms with middle term 30, find sum.

Middle term = average = 30. Sum = 21·30 = 630.

MD-15. Find 4 numbers in AP whose sum is 20 and sum of squares is 120.

(a − 3d) + (a − d) + (a + d) + (a + 3d) = 4a = 20 ⇒ a = 5. Sum sq = 4a² + 20d² = 100 + 20d² = 120 ⇒ d² = 1 ⇒ d = ±1. Numbers: 2, 4, 6, 8.

MD-16. 30th term of −10, −7, −4, ...

−10 + 29·3 = 77.

MD-17. Sum of first 17 terms of 7, 11, 15, ...

(17/2)(14 + 64) = (17/2)(78) = 663.

MD-18. Find a if AP has S₅ = 35 and d = 3.

35 = (5/2)(2a + 12) ⇒ 14 = 2a + 12 ⇒ a = 1.

MD-19. 8th term from end of AP 5, 9, 13, ..., 185.

185 − (8 − 1)·4 = 157.

MD-20. If aₙ = 7n − 4, find S₁₀.

a = 3, a₁₀ = 66. S = (10/2)(3 + 66) = 345.

5.34 Self-Test — 10 Questions

  1. Find 30th term of AP: 10, 7, 4, ...
  2. How many terms in AP: 1, 5, 9, ..., 401?
  3. If pth term is 5, qth term is 3, find sum of first (p + q) terms.
  4. Find sum of first 20 terms of AP whose nth term is 4n − 1.
  5. Find sum of all 3-digit numbers divisible by 11.
  6. If 2x, x + 6, 3x are in AP, find x.
  7. Sum of 4 numbers in AP is 16; sum of squares is 84. Find numbers.
  8. Find sum of n terms of AP whose Sₙ formula = n² + n.
  9. Find S₅₀ of AP: 6, 11, 16, 21, ...
  10. Find AM between (a + b)² and (a − b)².

✅ Answers

  1. a₃₀ = 10 + 29·(−3) = −77.
  2. n = (401 − 1)/4 + 1 = 101.
  3. Conceptual; needs more info.
  4. a = 3, l = 79. S = (20/2)(82) = 820.
  5. 110, 121, ..., 990. n = 81. S = 44550 (from EM-11).
  6. 2(x + 6) = 5x ⇒ x = 4.
  7. 4a = 16 ⇒ a = 4; 4a² + 20d² = 84 ⇒ d² = 1 ⇒ d = ±1. Numbers: 1, 3, 5, 7.
  8. aₙ = Sₙ − Sₙ₋₁ = 2n; a = 2, d = 2.
  9. (50/2)(12 + 49·5) = 25·257 = 6425.
  10. ((a + b)² + (a − b)²)/2 = a² + b².

5.35 Final Wrap & Self-Certification

🏆 Chapter 5 Achievement Unlocked!

You can confidently:

  • ✅ Identify and define an AP.
  • ✅ Find nth term and sum of n terms.
  • ✅ Find the number of terms in an AP.
  • ✅ Solve real-life word problems.
  • ✅ Use sum-of-roots techniques.

➡️ Next: Chapter 6 — Triangles!

5.36 Top-Up Practice — 20 Questions

TP-1. Find 12th term of AP: 3, 8, 13, ...

3 + 11·5 = 58.

TP-2. Find sum of first 30 terms of AP: 7, 12, 17, ...

(30/2)(14 + 29·5) = 15·159 = 2385.

TP-3. Which term of 8, 14, 20, 26, ... is 200?

200 = 8 + (n − 1)·6 ⇒ n = 33.

TP-4. Find a, d if 5th term is 19 and 9th term is 35.

a + 4d = 19, a + 8d = 35 ⇒ d = 4, a = 3.

TP-5. Sum of all natural numbers between 100 and 500 divisible by 8.

AP: 104, 112, ..., 496. n = (496 − 104)/8 + 1 = 50. S = (50/2)(600) = 15000.

TP-6. The first term of an AP is −3 and 10th term is 24. Find d.

24 = −3 + 9d ⇒ d = 3.

TP-7. Find Sₙ if n = 16, a = 4, l = 79.

S = (16/2)(83) = 664.

TP-8. nth term of AP whose first term is 2 and last 50 with n = 25.

d = (50 − 2)/24 = 2. aₙ = 2 + (n − 1)·2 = 2n.

TP-9. Sum of three numbers in AP is 33; product is 1287. Find numbers.

3a = 33 ⇒ a = 11. 11(121 − d²) = 1287 ⇒ 121 − d² = 117 ⇒ d² = 4 ⇒ d = ±2. Numbers: 9, 11, 13.

TP-10. Find sum: 1² + 2² + 3² + ... + 10².

10·11·21/6 = 385.

TP-11. AP: 4, 7, 10, 13, ... how many terms summed = 187?

(n/2)(8 + 3(n − 1)) = 187 ⇒ n(3n + 5) = 374 ⇒ 3n² + 5n − 374 = 0 ⇒ n = (−5 + √(25 + 4488))/6 = (−5 + 67.18)/6 ≈ 10.36. No integer n.

TP-12. Find AP whose 25th term is 78 and 50th term is 153.

a + 24d = 78, a + 49d = 153 ⇒ 25d = 75 ⇒ d = 3, a = 6. AP: 6, 9, 12, 15, ...

TP-13. Find sum of first 21 terms of AP: 1, 5, 9, 13, ...

(21/2)(2 + 20·4) = (21/2)(82) = 861.

TP-14. Find n: a = 4, d = 5, Sₙ = 105.

(n/2)(8 + 5(n − 1)) = 105 ⇒ n(5n + 3) = 210 ⇒ 5n² + 3n − 210 = 0 ⇒ n = (−3 + √(9 + 4200))/10 = (−3 + 64.88)/10 ≈ 6.19. No integer n.

TP-15. The sum of n terms of AP is 4n − n². Find first term.

a = S₁ = 3.

TP-16. Continuing TP-15, find d and a₁₀.

a₂ = S₂ − S₁ = (8 − 4) − 3 = 1. d = −2. a₁₀ = 3 + 9(−2) = −15.

TP-17. Find AM of 6 + 8 + 10.

(6 + 8 + 10)/3 = 8.

TP-18. AP whose 1st term 3, last term 27, d = 3. Find n and sum.

27 = 3 + (n − 1)·3 ⇒ n = 9. S = (9/2)(30) = 135.

TP-19. 11th term from end of AP 10, 7, 4, ..., −62.

Last l = −62, d = −3. nth from end = l − (n − 1)d = −62 − 10·(−3) = −32.

TP-20. Sum of 4 numbers in AP: 32, sum of squares 276. Find numbers.

4a = 32 ⇒ a = 8. 4a² + 20d² = 276 ⇒ 20d² = 20 ⇒ d = ±1. Numbers: 5, 7, 9, 11.

5.37 Closing Note

🎯 Arithmetic Progressions are a stepping-stone to higher concepts in series and sequences (Class 11 and beyond). Master AP and you'll find Geometric Progressions, Harmonic Progressions, and even Calculus easier!

💪 Practice every day

5 AP problems × 30 days = 150 problems = mastery!

5.38 Bonus — Challenge 20

B-1. Sum of first n positive even integers.

2 + 4 + ... + 2n = n(n + 1).

B-2. Sum of n natural numbers' squares.

n(n + 1)(2n + 1)/6.

B-3. Find sum: 5 + 11 + 17 + 23 + ... + 605.

a = 5, d = 6. n = (605 − 5)/6 + 1 = 101. S = (101/2)(610) = 30805.

B-4. 7th term of AP whose 3rd is 4 and 11th is 28.

(4 + 28)/2 = 16. (Middle term of two equidistant from 7.)

B-5. If sum of first 25 terms of AP is 525 and a = 9, find d.

525 = (25/2)(18 + 24d) ⇒ 42 = 18 + 24d ⇒ d = 1.

B-6. nth term of AP: 9, 13, 17, ... is

4n + 5.

B-7. Find sum of first 1000 even numbers.

2 + 4 + ... + 2000 = 1000·1001 = 1001000.

B-8. Sum of first 100 odd numbers.

100² = 10000.

B-9. If aₙ = (3n + 1), find S₂₀.

a = 4, l = 61, n = 20. S = (20/2)(65) = 650.

B-10. Find AP whose 6th term is 12 and 8th term is 22.

2d = 10 ⇒ d = 5, a = −13. AP: −13, −8, −3, 2, ...

B-11. Find sum 100 + 105 + 110 + ... + 200.

n = (200 − 100)/5 + 1 = 21. S = (21/2)(300) = 3150.

B-12. 10 terms in AP summing to 200, last term 35. Find first term.

200 = (10/2)(a + 35) ⇒ a = 5.

B-13. Find sum of all integers from −50 to +50.

Symmetric → 0.

B-14. Find sum from −100 to +200.

−100 + ... + 100 = 0; 101 + 102 + ... + 200 = (100/2)(301) = 15050.

B-15. AP whose first term is 5 and sum of first 10 terms is 95. Find d.

95 = 5·(10 + 9d) ⇒ 19 = 10 + 9d ⇒ d = 1.

B-16. Sum of three numbers in AP is 24. If product is 440, find numbers.

3a = 24 ⇒ a = 8. 8(64 − d²) = 440 ⇒ 64 − d² = 55 ⇒ d² = 9 ⇒ d = ±3. Numbers: 5, 8, 11.

B-17. Find sum: 10 + 14 + 18 + ... + 130.

n = (130 − 10)/4 + 1 = 31. S = (31/2)(140) = 2170.

B-18. d of AP whose nth term is 7 − 2n.

a₁ = 5, a₂ = 3. d = −2.

B-19. Find AM between 4 + 3√2 and 4 − 3√2.

(8)/2 = 4.

B-20. 100th term of AP whose nth term is n² is not valid (since n² is not AP). What is the actual difference between 100th and 99th?

100² − 99² = 199. (Not constant ⇒ not AP — it's quadratic in n.)

5.39 Final Drill — 10 Quick Questions
