FD-1. Sum of first 50 natural numbers.

50·51/2 = 1275.

FD-2. Sum of all multiples of 3 up to 99.

3 + 6 + ... + 99 = (33/2)(102) = 1683.

FD-3. AP 5, 10, 15, ...; 12th term?

60.

FD-4. Find x: x, x + 2, x + 4 are 3 consecutive terms ⇒ d?

d = 2 (for any x).

FD-5. Sum of first 6 terms of AP 1, 3, 5, 7, ...

36.

FD-6. nth term of 2, 5, 8, 11, ...

3n − 1.

FD-7. AM between 6 and 24.

15.

FD-8. AP 25, 22, 19, ...; 8th term?

25 + 7(−3) = 4.

FD-9. Sum 100 + 99 + 98 + ... + 1.

5050.

FD-10. AP 12, 15, 18, ...; how many terms ≤ 45?

45 = 12 + (n − 1)·3 ⇒ n = 12.

🎯 Chapter 5 — Arithmetic Progressions complete!

5.40 Final 5

F-1. nth term of 3, 7, 11, ...

4n − 1.

F-2. nth term of 100, 90, 80, ...

110 − 10n.

F-3. Sum 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n.

n(n + 1)/2.

🔺 Chapter 6: Triangles

NCERT Class 10 Mathematics — Similar triangles, Thales theorem & Pythagoras!

📋 Chapter Roadmap

  1. 6.1 Introduction — Triangles Everywhere
  2. 6.2 Similar Figures
  3. 6.3 Similarity of Triangles
  4. 6.4 Basic Proportionality Theorem (Thales)
  5. 6.5 Converse of BPT
  6. 6.6 Criteria for Similarity (AAA, SAS, SSS)
  7. 6.7 Worked examples — similarity proofs
  8. 6.8 Pythagoras Theorem (Class 10 version)
  9. 6.9 Converse of Pythagoras
  10. 6.10 Areas of similar triangles (knowledge)
  1. 6.11 NCERT Exercise 6.1 solutions
  2. 6.12 NCERT Exercise 6.2 solutions
  3. 6.13 NCERT Exercise 6.3 solutions
  4. 6.14 NCERT Exercise 6.4 solutions
  5. 6.15 NCERT Exercise 6.5 solutions
  6. 6.16 MCQs (25)
  7. 6.17 Assertion-Reason
  8. 6.18 HOTS
  9. 6.19 Case Studies
  10. 6.20 PYQs, Mistakes, Glossary, Mind Map, Recap

6.1 Introduction — Triangles Everywhere

🔺 Look at the world: pyramids, rooftops, sails, traffic signs, mountain peaks! Triangles are everywhere because they are the most stable 2D shape.

In this chapter, we focus on similar triangles — pairs of triangles having the same shape but possibly different sizes (like enlarging or shrinking a photo).

💡 Why study similarity? Used in surveying (finding height of mountain), photography, scale models, maps, ancient astronomy!

6.2 Similar Figures

Two figures are similar if:

  • They have the same shape.
  • Corresponding angles are equal.
  • Corresponding sides are in the same ratio (proportional).

All circles are similar. All squares are similar. All equilateral triangles are similar.

But not all rectangles are similar — only when the ratio of sides matches!

📝 Example

A rectangle 3 × 4 is similar to 6 × 8 (ratio 1: 2). But not to 6 × 9 (ratio 1: 2 and 1: 2.25 — different).

6.3 Similarity of Triangles

📐 Two triangles ΔABC and ΔPQR are similar (written ΔABC ~ ΔPQR) if:

  • ∠A = ∠P, ∠B = ∠Q, ∠C = ∠R (all corresponding angles equal).
  • AB/PQ = BC/QR = CA/RP (all corresponding sides proportional).

6.4 Basic Proportionality Theorem (Thales)

📐 BPT (Thales): If a line is drawn parallel to one side of a triangle, intersecting the other two sides in distinct points, it divides those two sides in the same ratio.

In ΔABC, if DE ∥ BC, with D on AB and E on AC, then:

AD/DB = AE/EC

📖 Proof sketch: Draw perpendiculars from D and E to BC. Triangles ADE and ABC are similar (corresponding angles equal because DE ∥ BC). Use area-ratio method to show AD/DB = AE/EC.

6.5 Converse of BPT

📐 Converse: If a line divides any two sides of a triangle in the same ratio, then the line is parallel to the third side.

So if AD/DB = AE/EC, then DE ∥ BC.

📝 Example: In ΔABC, DE ∥ BC. AD = 4, DB = 6, AE = 3. Find EC.

By BPT: 4/6 = 3/EC ⇒ EC = 18/4 = 4.5.

6.6 Criteria for Similarity

📐 AA (or AAA) — Angle-Angle

If two angles of one triangle equal two angles of another, triangles are similar.

📐 SAS — Side-Angle-Side

If two pairs of corresponding sides are proportional and the included angles are equal, triangles are similar.

📐 SSS — Side-Side-Side

If all three pairs of corresponding sides are proportional, triangles are similar.

💡 Difference from congruence: For similarity, we need proportional sides, not equal. For congruence, sides must be equal.

6.7 Worked Examples — Similarity Proofs

📝 Ex 1: In ΔABC, DE ∥ BC. Prove ΔADE ~ ΔABC.

∠A is common. ∠ADE = ∠ABC (corresponding angles, DE ∥ BC). By AA, ΔADE ~ ΔABC. ✓

📝 Ex 2: In ΔABC and ΔPQR, ∠A = ∠P and AB/PQ = AC/PR. Is ΔABC ~ ΔPQR?

Yes, by SAS criterion of similarity.

📝 Ex 3: Two triangles have sides 3, 4, 5 and 6, 8, 10. Are they similar?

Ratios: 3/6 = 4/8 = 5/10 = 1/2. Yes, by SSS, they are similar (in fact both are right triangles).

📝 Ex 4: In ΔABC, AB = 6, AC = 8, BC = 10. In ΔPQR, PQ = 9, PR = 12, QR = 15. Show similar.

6/9 = 8/12 = 10/15 = 2/3. By SSS, similar.

6.8 Pythagoras Theorem (Class 10 — Proof)

📐 In a right triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of squares of the other two sides.

In ΔABC with right angle at B: AC² = AB² + BC².

📖 Proof using similar triangles:

Drop perpendicular BD from B onto AC. Then ΔADB ~ ΔABC (AA), so AD/AB = AB/AC ⇒ AB² = AD·AC.

Similarly ΔBDC ~ ΔABC ⇒ DC/BC = BC/AC ⇒ BC² = DC·AC.

Add: AB² + BC² = AC·(AD + DC) = AC·AC = AC². ✓

6.9 Converse of Pythagoras

📐 In a triangle, if square of one side equals sum of squares of other two sides, the triangle is right-angled at the vertex opposite to the first side.

📝 A triangle has sides 7, 24, 25. Is it right-angled?

25² = 625; 7² + 24² = 49 + 576 = 625. Yes, right-angled at the vertex opposite to 25.

6.10 Areas of Similar Triangles (concept only — for awareness)

📐 If ΔABC ~ ΔPQR, then ratio of areas = (ratio of corresponding sides)².

Area(ABC)/Area(PQR) = (AB/PQ)² = (BC/QR)² = (CA/RP)²

6.11 NCERT Exercise 6.1 (Selected)

📝 Q1. Fill in blanks:

(i) All circles are similar.

(ii) All squares are similar.

(iii) All equilateral triangles are similar.

(iv) Two polygons with same number of sides are similar if their corresponding angles are equal and corresponding sides are in same ratio.

📝 Q2. Give two different examples of similar figures.

Photographs of different size, two equilateral triangles of different sides.

6.12 NCERT Exercise 6.2 (Selected)

📝 Q1(i). In ΔABC, DE ∥ BC. AD = 1.5 cm, DB = 3 cm, AE = 1 cm. Find EC.

1.5/3 = 1/EC ⇒ EC = 2 cm.

📝 Q2. E and F are on AB and AC of ΔABC. Check if EF ∥ BC.

If AE/EB = AF/FC, by converse of BPT, EF ∥ BC.

6.13 NCERT Exercise 6.3 (Selected)

📝 Q1. State which pairs are similar; mention criterion.

(i) ΔABC with angles 60°, 80°, 40° vs ΔPQR with 60°, 80°, 40° → similar by AAA.

(ii) Sides 4, 6, 8 and 6, 9, 12 — ratios 2/3 — similar by SSS.

📝 Q3. Diagonals AC and BD of trapezium ABCD (AB ∥ DC) intersect at O. Show OA/OC = OB/OD.

In ΔAOB and ΔCOD: ∠AOB = ∠COD (vertically opposite), ∠OAB = ∠OCD (alternate interior). By AA, ΔAOB ~ ΔCOD ⇒ OA/OC = OB/OD. ✓

6.14 NCERT Exercise 6.4 (Removed from 2023 syllabus — areas of similar triangles is for reference)

📝 Reference: If ΔABC ~ ΔPQR and ratio of areas 16:25, find ratio of corresponding sides.

√16/√25 = 4/5.

6.15 NCERT Exercise 6.5 — Pythagoras

📝 Q1. Sides of triangles are: (i) 7, 24, 25 (ii) 3, 8, 6 (iii) 50, 80, 100 (iv) 13, 12, 5. Which is right-angled?

(i) 7² + 24² = 625 = 25² ✓ right-angled.

(ii) 3² + 6² = 45 ≠ 64 — NOT.

(iii) 50² + 80² = 8900 ≠ 100² = 10000 — NOT.

(iv) 5² + 12² = 169 = 13² ✓ right-angled.

📝 Q2. PQR is right-angled at Q, PR = 13 cm, PQ = 5 cm. Find QR.

QR² = PR² − PQ² = 169 − 25 = 144 ⇒ QR = 12 cm.

📝 Q4. ABC is isosceles right-angled at C. Prove AB² = 2·AC².

By Pythagoras: AB² = AC² + BC². Since AC = BC, AB² = 2·AC². ✓

6.16 MCQs (25)

1. Two similar triangles have ratio of sides 2:3. Ratio of areas?

4:9.

2. In ΔABC, DE ∥ BC, AD/DB = 2/3. Then AE:EC = ?

2:3.

3. All ___ triangles are similar.

Equilateral.

4. In ΔABC, if AB² = BC² + AC², which angle is 90°?

∠C.

5. A ladder 13 m long touches a wall 12 m above ground. Distance of foot from wall?

√(169 − 144) = 5 m.

6. In two similar triangles, ratio of perimeters = ?

Ratio of corresponding sides.

7. Which is NOT a criterion of similarity?

SSA (not a similarity criterion).

8. If ΔABC ~ ΔDEF and ∠A = 60°, what is ∠D?

60°.

9. Two figures are similar but not always congruent. T/F?

True.

10. All squares are similar. T/F?

True.

11. In ΔABC, ∠A = 90°, AB = 6, AC = 8. BC = ?

10.

12. Hypotenuse of a right triangle is opposite to which angle?

Right angle (90°).

13. Sides 6, 8, 10. Is it right triangle?

Yes, 6² + 8² = 100 = 10².

14. Sides 9, 40, 41. Right triangle?

9² + 40² = 81 + 1600 = 1681 = 41². Yes.

15. If AD/DB = AE/EC in ΔABC, DE is __ to BC.

Parallel (Converse BPT).

16. Statement of BPT?

A line parallel to one side of a triangle divides the other two sides in the same ratio.

17. If two angles of one triangle equal two angles of another, triangles are __.

Similar (by AA).

18. Ratio of areas of similar triangles is square of ratio of __.

Corresponding sides.

19. If a triangle has sides 1, 1, √2, what type?

Right isosceles (1² + 1² = 2 = (√2)²).

20. In two similar triangles, corresponding angles are __.

Equal.

21. Foot of 17 m ladder is 8 m from wall; how high does it reach?

√(289 − 64) = 15 m.

22. Diagonal of square side a?

a√2.

23. Diagonal of rectangle 3 × 4?

5.

24. The altitude of equilateral triangle side 2a?

a√3.

25. In ΔABC, AB = 6, BC = 8, AC = 10. Right angle at?

∠B (6² + 8² = 100 = 10²).

6.17 Assertion-Reason

AR-1.

A: All congruent triangles are similar.

R: Similar triangles have ratio 1:1 if also congruent.

Both A and R true; R explains A.

AR-2.

A: If two triangles have sides in ratio 1:2, areas are in 1:4.

R: Area ratio = (side ratio)².

Both A and R true; R explains A.

6.18 HOTS

📝 HOTS-1. In ΔABC, AD is median. Prove AB² + AC² = 2AD² + 2BD².

By Apollonius' theorem (a special case of the median length formula). Drop perpendicular from A to BC, use Pythagoras in two sub-triangles.

📝 HOTS-2. ABC is a right triangle right-angled at C. Let p be perpendicular from C to AB. Prove 1/p² = 1/a² + 1/b² where a = BC, b = AC.

Area = (1/2)·a·b = (1/2)·AB·p ⇒ p = ab/AB. AB² = a² + b² ⇒ p² = a²b²/(a² + b²) ⇒ 1/p² = (a² + b²)/(a²b²) = 1/b² + 1/a². ✓

6.19 Case Study

📚 CS-1: Surveying a Tower

An engineer wants to know height of a tower without climbing it. He places a vertical stick 2 m high at distance and observes the shadow.

Stick's shadow = 1 m, Tower's shadow = 20 m.

  • Q1. By similar triangles (sun angle same), tower height/2 = 20/1 ⇒ height = 40 m.
  • Q2. Why does this work? Sun rays at same angle ⇒ similar right triangles.

6.20 PYQs, Mistakes, Glossary, Mind Map, Recap

PYQ-1 (2023). Prove that the ratio of areas of two similar triangles is equal to the square of the ratio of their corresponding sides.

Drop altitudes; use area = (1/2)·base·height; show altitudes are in same ratio; multiply.

PYQ-2 (2022). Prove Pythagoras using similar triangles (already done in §6.8).

Drop altitude from right-angle vertex; two new triangles similar to original ⇒ AB² = AD·AC, BC² = DC·AC; add.

⚠️ Common Mistakes

  • Confusing similarity ratios with area ratios — area is square of side ratio!
  • Writing sides in wrong correspondence in proportionality.
  • Forgetting to check whether the triangle is right-angled (apply Pythagoras only then).
  • Using SSA — it is NOT a similarity criterion!

📚 Glossary

  • Similar: same shape, corresponding angles equal, sides proportional.
  • Congruent: same shape AND size (special case of similar with ratio 1).
  • BPT (Thales): Line parallel to one side divides other two in same ratio.
  • Pythagoras: In right triangle, hypotenuse² = sum of squares of other two sides.
  • Hypotenuse: Side opposite to the right angle.

🗺️ Mind Map

⚡ Quick Recap

  • Similar: same shape, proportional sides.
  • AA, SAS, SSS criteria for similarity.
  • BPT: DE ∥ BC ⇒ AD/DB = AE/EC.
  • Pythagoras: c² = a² + b² (right triangle).
  • Area ratio of similar = (side ratio)².

➡️ Next: Chapter 7 — Coordinate Geometry!

6.21 Extra Mastery — 20 Worked Examples

📝 EM-1. In ΔABC, DE ∥ BC, AD = 4 cm, DB = 8 cm, AE = 5 cm. Find AC.

AE/EC = AD/DB ⇒ 5/EC = 4/8 ⇒ EC = 10. AC = AE + EC = 15 cm.

📝 EM-2. In ΔABC, D, E are on AB, AC. AD = 2, DB = 6, AE = 3, EC = 9. Is DE ∥ BC?

AD/DB = 2/6 = 1/3; AE/EC = 3/9 = 1/3. Equal ⇒ DE ∥ BC (Converse BPT).

📝 EM-3. ΔABC ~ ΔPQR. AB = 4, PQ = 6, BC = 6. Find QR.

4/6 = 6/QR ⇒ QR = 9.

📝 EM-4. In a right ΔABC, right angle at B. AB = 9, BC = 12. Find AC.

AC² = 81 + 144 = 225 ⇒ AC = 15.

📝 EM-5. The areas of two similar triangles are 144 cm² and 81 cm². Ratio of corresponding sides?

√(144/81) = 12/9 = 4/3.

📝 EM-6. A ladder 25 m long is placed against a wall such that its foot is 7 m from wall. How high up the wall does it reach?

h² = 625 − 49 = 576 ⇒ h = 24 m.

📝 EM-7. The diagonals of trapezium ABCD with AB ∥ DC intersect at O. AB = 6, DC = 9. Find ratio OA:OC.

ΔAOB ~ ΔCOD ⇒ OA/OC = AB/DC = 6/9 = 2/3.

📝 EM-8. The corresponding sides of two similar triangles are 4 cm and 6 cm. If the area of larger is 54 cm², find area of smaller.

Smaller/Larger = (4/6)² = 4/9. Smaller = 54·(4/9) = 24 cm².

📝 EM-9. In ΔABC right-angled at C, find AC if AB = 13, BC = 5.

AC² = AB² − BC² = 169 − 25 = 144 ⇒ AC = 12.

📝 EM-10. ABCD is a square with side 'a'. Find length of its diagonal.

d² = a² + a² = 2a² ⇒ d = a√2.

📝 EM-11. In an equilateral triangle of side a, find length of altitude.

h² = a² − (a/2)² = 3a²/4 ⇒ h = (a√3)/2.

📝 EM-12. In ΔABC, ∠B = 90°. If AB = a, BC = b, find length of median to AC.

AC = √(a² + b²). Median to hypotenuse = half hypotenuse = (1/2)√(a² + b²).

📝 EM-13. A tree breaks at a point such that the upper part touches the ground 12 m from base. If the tree is 16 m tall, find height where it broke.

Let break height = h. Top part length = 16 − h. By Pythagoras: h² + 12² = (16 − h)² ⇒ h² + 144 = 256 − 32h + h² ⇒ 32h = 112 ⇒ h = 3.5 m.

📝 EM-14. Two poles of heights 6 m and 11 m stand on a plane ground. If distance between feet is 12 m, find distance between tops.

Difference in heights = 5 m. Horizontal = 12 m. Diagonal² = 25 + 144 = 169 ⇒ 13 m.

📝 EM-15. In ΔABC, ∠B = 90°, AB = 8, BC = 6. Find length of altitude from B to AC.

AC = √(64 + 36) = 10. Area = (1/2)·8·6 = 24 = (1/2)·10·h ⇒ h = 4.8.

📝 EM-16. The perimeters of two similar triangles are 30 cm and 20 cm. If one side of the first is 12 cm, find corresponding side of second.

Ratio of perimeters = ratio of sides = 30/20 = 3/2. So x/12 = 2/3 ⇒ x = 8.

📝 EM-17. In ΔABC, DE ∥ BC. AD = x, DB = x + 1, AE = x + 3, EC = x + 5. Find x.

x/(x + 1) = (x + 3)/(x + 5) ⇒ x(x + 5) = (x + 1)(x + 3) ⇒ x² + 5x = x² + 4x + 3 ⇒ x = 3.

📝 EM-18. Sides of ΔPQR are 3 cm, 4 cm and 5 cm. Sides of ΔABC are 6 cm, 8 cm and 10 cm. Verify similar and find ratio of perimeters.

3/6 = 4/8 = 5/10 = 1/2 ⇒ similar by SSS. Perimeters: 12 and 24 ⇒ ratio 1:2.

📝 EM-19. In ΔABC, ∠A = 90°, AD ⊥ BC. Prove BD/AD = AD/CD.

ΔABD ~ ΔCAD (both right-angled, ∠B = 90° − ∠C = ∠DAC). Hence BD/AD = AD/CD. ✓

📝 EM-20. In ΔABC, if ∠A = ∠Q and ∠B = ∠R, write the proportional sides of ΔABC ~ ΔQRP.

AB/QR = BC/RP = CA/PQ.

6.22 Drill MCQs (15)

D-1. Ratio of areas of two similar triangles with sides 5 and 10.

1:4.

D-2. In ΔABC, DE ∥ BC, AD:DB = 1:2; AE:EC?

1:2.

D-3. Hypotenuse if legs 8 and 15.

17.

D-4. Hypotenuse if legs 9 and 12.

15.

D-5. Hypotenuse if legs 20 and 21.

29.

D-6. Hypotenuse if legs 11 and 60.

61.

D-7. Two similar triangles area ratio 1:9; ratio of corresponding altitudes?

1:3.

D-8. Two squares similar? T/F

True.

D-9. Two rectangles similar?

Not always (only when side ratios equal).

D-10. Two right triangles with a common acute angle are similar?

Yes (AA).

D-11. AD ⊥ BC in right ΔABC at A. Triangles formed?

ΔABD ~ ΔABC ~ ΔACD.

D-12. The longest side of a right triangle is the __.

Hypotenuse.

D-13. Sides of triangle 10, 24, 26. Right triangle?

10² + 24² = 676 = 26². Yes.

D-14. Sides 5, 12, 13. Right triangle?

Yes (5² + 12² = 169 = 13²).

D-15. Sides 8, 15, 17. Right triangle?

Yes (8² + 15² = 289 = 17²).

6.23 Real-Life Applications

📏 Surveying

Surveyors use similar triangles to measure distance across rivers without crossing them — set up similar triangles on banks!

📷 Photography

Images on a camera sensor are similar to actual objects. Scale changes based on lens distance — direct application of similar triangles.

🗺️ Maps

Every map uses a scale — say 1 cm = 1 km. The map and reality are similar figures!

🌅 Sun-Shadow Method

An ancient method used by Greek mathematician Thales to measure the height of the Egyptian pyramids! He used the length of his own shadow.

🎬 Movie Special Effects

Miniature models in movies use similar triangles to look realistic on camera.

6.24 Famous Pythagorean Triples

📐 A Pythagorean triple is a set of 3 positive integers a, b, c such that a² + b² = c².

Common triples:

  • (3, 4, 5), (6, 8, 10), (9, 12, 15), (12, 16, 20), ...
  • (5, 12, 13), (10, 24, 26), (15, 36, 39), ...
  • (8, 15, 17), (7, 24, 25), (20, 21, 29), (9, 40, 41), (11, 60, 61), (12, 35, 37), (13, 84, 85)...

Multiplying any triple by a positive integer gives another triple!

📝 Example: Is (15, 20, 25) a Pythagorean triple?

15² + 20² = 225 + 400 = 625 = 25². Yes! (It's 5× the (3,4,5) triple.)

6.25 Theorems Quick Reference

📌 BPT (Thales)

Line parallel to one side of triangle divides other two sides in same ratio.

📌 Converse of BPT

If line divides two sides of triangle in same ratio, line is parallel to third side.

📌 Similarity Criteria

AA, SAS, SSS.

📌 Pythagoras

Right triangle: hypotenuse² = sum of squares of legs.

📌 Converse of Pythagoras

If a² + b² = c² in a triangle, it's right-angled (opposite c).

6.26 Advanced Mastery — 15 Problems

📝 AM-1. In ΔABC, ∠B = 90°, BD ⊥ AC. Prove BD² = AD·DC.

ΔABD ~ ΔBCD (both right-angled at D; ∠A = 90° − ∠C, ∠C = 90° − ∠A) ⇒ BD/CD = AD/BD ⇒ BD² = AD·CD. ✓

📝 AM-2. In ΔABC, M is midpoint of BC; AD bisects ∠A. Prove AB/AC = BD/DC.

This is the Angle Bisector Theorem: bisector of an angle divides opposite side in ratio of adjacent sides. Standard proof uses similar triangles or area method.

📝 AM-3. The perimeters of two similar triangles ABC and PQR are 32 cm and 24 cm. If PQ = 12 cm, find AB.

Ratio of perimeters = 32/24 = 4/3. So AB/PQ = 4/3 ⇒ AB = 16 cm.

📝 AM-4. ΔABC ~ ΔDEF. Area of ABC = 64 cm², area of DEF = 121 cm². If AB = 8 cm, find DE.

(AB/DE)² = 64/121 ⇒ AB/DE = 8/11 ⇒ DE = 11 cm.

📝 AM-5. In ΔPQR, right-angled at Q. PR + QR = 25 cm, PQ = 5 cm. Find PR and QR.

Let QR = x ⇒ PR = 25 − x. (25 − x)² = 25 + x² ⇒ 625 − 50x = 25 ⇒ x = 12. So QR = 12, PR = 13.

📝 AM-6. In a rhombus ABCD, diagonals AC and BD intersect at O perpendicularly. AC = 24, BD = 10. Find side.

Half-diagonals = 12, 5. Side² = 144 + 25 = 169 ⇒ side = 13.

📝 AM-7. The diagonals of a rhombus are 16 and 30. Find perimeter.

Half-diagonals = 8, 15. Side = √(64 + 225) = 17. Perimeter = 68.

📝 AM-8. ABC is a right triangle, right-angled at A. AD is perpendicular from A onto BC. Prove AB·AC = AD·BC.

Area = (1/2)·AB·AC = (1/2)·BC·AD ⇒ AB·AC = BC·AD. ✓

📝 AM-9. In ΔABC, P, Q are on AB, AC. AP = 3, AB = 12, AQ = 4, AC = 16. Is PQ ∥ BC?

AP/AB = 3/12 = 1/4; AQ/AC = 4/16 = 1/4. So AP/PB = AQ/QC ⇒ PQ ∥ BC. ✓

📝 AM-10. The corresponding altitudes of two similar triangles are 6 cm and 9 cm. Ratio of areas?

(6/9)² = 4/9.

📝 AM-11. A 25 m ladder reaches a window 24 m above ground. Distance of foot of ladder from wall?

√(625 − 576) = 7 m.

📝 AM-12. The hypotenuse of a right triangle is 17 cm. One leg is 8 cm. Find other.

√(289 − 64) = √225 = 15 cm.

📝 AM-13. An aeroplane leaves an airport and flies due North at 1000 km/h. At same time another flies due West at 1200 km/h. How far apart after 1.5 hr?

Distances: 1500, 1800. Diagonal = √(1500² + 1800²) = √(2250000 + 3240000) = √5490000 ≈ 2342.99 km.

📝 AM-14. Two poles 6 m and 11 m tall. Distance between their feet 12 m. Find distance between tops.

(11 − 6)² + 12² = 25 + 144 = 169 ⇒ 13 m.

📝 AM-15. The areas of similar triangles ABC and PQR are 64 cm² and 144 cm². If perimeter of ABC = 24 cm, find perimeter of PQR.

Ratio of sides = √(64/144) = 8/12 = 2/3. Ratio of perimeters = 2/3 ⇒ 24/P_PQR = 2/3 ⇒ P_PQR = 36 cm.

6.27 Mixed Practice — 15 Questions

MP-1. In ΔABC, DE ∥ BC. AD = 5, DB = 7, AC = 18. Find AE.

AE/EC = 5/7. Let AE = 5k, EC = 7k, sum = 12k = 18 ⇒ k = 1.5, AE = 7.5.

MP-2. ΔABC ~ ΔDEF. AB = 4, BC = 5, AC = 6; DE = 8. Find EF and DF.

Ratio 4/8 = 1/2. EF = 10, DF = 12.

MP-3. Two similar triangles have areas 49 and 81. Ratio of corresponding medians?

7/9.

MP-4. Triangle sides 6, 8, 9. Right-angled?

36 + 64 = 100 ≠ 81. Not right.

MP-5. In ΔABC, ∠A = 30°, ∠B = 60°. Side opposite to which angle is longest?

∠C = 90° ⇒ side opposite C (hypotenuse) longest.

MP-6. The diagonals of a square of side 8 cm are?

8√2 cm.

MP-7. The altitudes of two similar triangles are 8 cm and 12 cm. Find ratio of perimeters.

8:12 = 2:3.

MP-8. In right ΔABC, AD ⊥ BC with right angle at A. If BD = 4, DC = 9, find AD.

AD² = BD·DC = 36 ⇒ AD = 6.

MP-9. In a triangle, ratio of sides 3:4:5. If perimeter is 36, sides?

9, 12, 15. (Pythagorean triple)

MP-10. In right triangle, area = 24, hypotenuse = 10. Legs?

Legs a, b with ab/2 = 24 ⇒ ab = 48; a² + b² = 100. (a + b)² = 196 ⇒ a + b = 14. So a, b roots of x² − 14x + 48 = 0 ⇒ (x − 6)(x − 8). Legs: 6, 8.

MP-11. ABCD is a quadrilateral with AB = 5, BC = 12, CD = 5, DA = 12, and ∠ABC = 90°. Find AC.

AC² = 25 + 144 = 169 ⇒ AC = 13.

MP-12. The altitude of equilateral triangle side 6 cm.

3√3 ≈ 5.196 cm.

MP-13. In ΔABC and ΔDEF, ∠A = ∠D = 90°, ∠B = ∠E. Are they similar?

Yes, by AA.

MP-14. The areas of two similar triangles are in ratio 1:25. Ratio of any two corresponding sides?

1:5.

MP-15. Two similar triangles with corresponding sides 10 and 15. If one has perimeter 30, perimeter of other?

30·15/10 = 45.

6.28 Theorem Memory Aid

  • 🧠 BPT: "Parallel divides in Proportion."
  • 🧠 AA: "Two angles match ⇒ all three match (since sum is 180°)."
  • 🧠 SSS: "All sides in Same ratio ⇒ Similar."
  • 🧠 SAS: "Two sides + the angle between them."
  • 🧠 Pythagoras: "c² = a² + b² only when right-angled!"
  • 🧠 Area ratio: Square of side ratio. "Side squared = Area scared!"

6.29 More Case Studies

📚 CS-2: TV Screen

A TV screen 32 inches (diagonal) has aspect ratio 16:9 (width: height). Find width and height.

Let width = 16k, height = 9k. Diagonal² = 256k² + 81k² = 337k². 337k² = 1024 ⇒ k² ≈ 3.04 ⇒ k ≈ 1.74.

  • Width ≈ 27.9 inches.
  • Height ≈ 15.7 inches.

📚 CS-3: Surveying a Pond

Across a pond, A and B are 200 m apart. To measure across without crossing, surveyor uses similar triangles. By placing points C and D on bank with CD = 20 m, AC = 50 m, BD = 60 m, finds DE on opposite bank using similarity. (Set up using AA and BPT.)

📚 CS-4: Roof Truss

A roof truss has two similar triangles each side. If one has sides 6, 8, 10 m and the smaller has hypotenuse 5 m, find other sides.

  • Ratio = 5/10 = 1/2. Other sides: 3 m, 4 m.

6.30 PYQs (extended)

PYQ-3 (2021). State and prove BPT.

Statement: A line parallel to one side of a triangle divides the other two sides in same ratio. Proof: Draw perpendiculars from D and E to BC. Use area-ratio method.

PYQ-4 (2020). The areas of two similar triangles are 100 and 49. If one side of larger is 15, corresponding side of smaller?

(15/x)² = 100/49 ⇒ 15/x = 10/7 ⇒ x = 10.5.

PYQ-5 (2019). A ladder 10 m long is placed against a wall such that it reaches a height of 8 m. Find distance of foot from wall.

√(100 − 64) = 6 m.

PYQ-6 (2018). The hypotenuse of right triangle is 13 cm. If sum of other two sides is 17 cm, find them.

a + b = 17, a² + b² = 169. (a + b)² = 289 ⇒ 2ab = 120 ⇒ ab = 60. a, b roots of x² − 17x + 60 = 0 ⇒ (x − 5)(x − 12). Sides: 5, 12.

6.31 Long Answer Practice — 10 Problems

📝 LA-1. Prove that the sum of squares of sides of a rhombus equals sum of squares of diagonals.

Let rhombus ABCD have side a, diagonals 2p and 2q (so half-diagonals p, q). Each side is hypotenuse of right triangle with legs p and q (since diagonals bisect at right angles).

a² = p² + q². Sum of squares of 4 sides = 4a² = 4(p² + q²) = (2p)² + (2q)². ✓

📝 LA-2. In ΔABC, right-angled at C. AC = b, BC = a, AB = c. Let CD ⊥ AB. Prove (1) AB·CD = AC·BC; (2) CD² = AD·BD.

(1) Area = (1/2)·AB·CD = (1/2)·AC·BC ⇒ AB·CD = AC·BC.

(2) ΔACD ~ ΔBCD ⇒ AD/CD = CD/BD ⇒ CD² = AD·BD. ✓

📝 LA-3. ABCD is trapezium with AB ∥ DC. Its diagonals intersect at O. Prove AO/OC = BO/OD.

In ΔAOB and ΔCOD: ∠AOB = ∠COD (vertically opposite), ∠OAB = ∠OCD (alternate interior, since AB ∥ DC). By AA, ΔAOB ~ ΔCOD ⇒ AO/CO = BO/DO. ✓

📝 LA-4. In ΔABC, AD is median. Prove AB² + AC² = 2·AD² + 2·BD².

(Apollonius' theorem.) Drop perpendicular AE from A to BC. In ΔABD and ΔACD use Pythagoras and combine.

AB² = AE² + BE², AC² = AE² + CE². Sum: AB² + AC² = 2AE² + BE² + CE².

Express BE = BD − DE, CE = CD + DE (or vice versa). Since BD = CD = (1/2)BC, simplify: AB² + AC² = 2(AE² + DE²) + 2BD² = 2AD² + 2BD². ✓

📝 LA-5. In a triangle ABC, perpendiculars AD, BE, CF are drawn from A, B, C to opposite sides. Prove they meet at a point (orthocenter).

This is a famous classical theorem. Use similar triangles formed at the foot of perpendiculars and apply Ceva's theorem.

📝 LA-6. In ΔABC, PQ ∥ BC. AP = 3, PB = 5, AQ = x, QC = 7.5. Find x.

3/5 = x/7.5 ⇒ x = 4.5.

📝 LA-7. ΔABC ~ ΔDEF. Their perimeters are 30 and 18. If DE = 6, find AB.

30/18 = AB/6 ⇒ AB = 10.

📝 LA-8. In ΔPQR, PT bisects ∠P meeting QR at T. PQ = 6, PR = 9, QR = 10. Find QT.

By angle bisector theorem, QT/TR = PQ/PR = 6/9 = 2/3. QT = (2/5)·QR = (2/5)·10 = 4.

📝 LA-9. In ΔABC, ∠B = 90°. If a² + c² = 5b², find ∠A. (Here a = BC, b = AC, c = AB.)

By Pythagoras AC² = a² + c² (since ∠B = 90°). So b² = a² + c² but also a² + c² = 5b² ⇒ b² = 5b² which is impossible unless b = 0. Contradiction → no such triangle.

📝 LA-10. In ΔABC, AB = 12, BC = 9, AC = 6. Determine whether right-angled.

Largest² = 144 = AB². Sum of other squares: 81 + 36 = 117 ≠ 144. Not right.

6.32 Diagrams Library

Right Triangle with Altitude

Similar Triangles Comparison

6.33 Concept Check — 20 Quick Fire

QF-1. Hypotenuse of right triangle 1, 1?

√2.

QF-2. Legs of right triangle hypotenuse 13, one leg 5?

12.

QF-3. Right triangle? 5, 12, 14?

25 + 144 = 169 ≠ 196. No.

QF-4. In a right triangle, side opposite right angle is __.

Hypotenuse.

QF-5. All ___ triangles are similar.

Equilateral.

QF-6. AA criterion needs how many equal angle pairs?

Two.

QF-7. Hypotenuse 25, leg 24, other leg?

7.

QF-8. Areas of similar triangles 4:9; perimeter ratio?

2:3.

QF-9. Two squares similar? T/F

True.

QF-10. Two circles similar?

Always true.

QF-11. In ΔABC, DE ∥ BC; if AD = DB, what is AE: EC?

1:1.

QF-12. Altitude in equilateral triangle of side 4?

2√3.

QF-13. Hypotenuse of triangle 0.6 and 0.8?

1.

QF-14. Diagonal of square side √2?

2.

QF-15. ΔABC ~ ΔPQR. ∠A = 50°. ∠P?

50°.

QF-16. (2, 3, 4) Pythagorean triple?

4 + 9 = 13 ≠ 16. No.

QF-17. Area of right triangle legs 6 and 8?

24.

QF-18. Hypotenuse of triangle with sides in ratio 3:4:?

5.

QF-19. Right isosceles triangle: leg 1, hypotenuse?

√2.

QF-20. ΔABC ~ ΔDEF means corresponding sides are __.

Proportional.

6.34 Concept Map

6.35 Self-Test (10 Q)

  1. State BPT.
  2. State converse of BPT.
  3. State and prove Pythagoras using similar triangles.
  4. State and prove converse of Pythagoras (think!).
  5. Two similar triangles have ratio of corresponding sides 5:7. Ratio of areas?
  6. Sides of right triangle? 9, 12, 15.
  7. Lateral height of building, shadow 30 m, vertical stick 1.5 m has shadow 0.5 m. Find building height.
  8. Diagonal of rectangle 9 × 12.
  9. Areas of two similar triangles 81 cm² and 144 cm². Ratio of corresponding altitudes?
  10. In ΔABC, DE ∥ BC, AD:DB = 3:5. AE:AC?

✅ Answers

  1. Line parallel to one side of triangle divides other two in same ratio.
  2. Line dividing two sides in same ratio is parallel to third.
  3. (Done in §6.8.)
  4. If a² + b² = c² in triangle, it is right-angled opposite c. Proof: Construct right triangle with legs a, b. Hypotenuse = c. By SSS, same as given triangle.
  5. 25:49.
  6. Yes (Pythagorean triple).
  7. Building/30 = 1.5/0.5 ⇒ height = 90 m.
  8. √(81 + 144) = √225 = 15.
  9. √(81/144) = 9/12 = 3/4.
  10. AE/EC = 3/5 ⇒ AE/AC = AE/(AE + EC) = 3/8.

6.36 Bonus Brain Teasers

📝 BT-1. ABCD is a square. P is midpoint of CD. Find length of AP if side = 10.

AP² = 10² + 5² = 125 ⇒ AP = 5√5 ≈ 11.18.

📝 BT-2. The diagonal of a rectangle is 25 and one side is 15. Find the other side.

√(625 − 225) = √400 = 20.

📝 BT-3. Two similar triangles have corresponding sides in ratio 2:5. If smaller has area 8 cm², larger area?

(2/5)² = 4/25 = 8/x ⇒ x = 50 cm².

📝 BT-4. In an equilateral triangle of side 12, find length of median.

Median = altitude = (12√3)/2 = 6√3.

📝 BT-5. ABCD is a quadrilateral with AB = AD and ∠BAC = ∠DAC. Show ΔABC ≅ ΔADC and BC = DC.

SAS: AB = AD; ∠BAC = ∠DAC; AC common. ⇒ ΔABC ≅ ΔADC ⇒ BC = DC. ✓

📝 BT-6. In a right ΔABC, ∠A = 90°. If AD is altitude to hypotenuse, prove ΔADB ~ ΔADC ~ ΔBAC.

All three share angles using AA criterion.

📝 BT-7. In ΔABC right at B, BD ⊥ AC, AB = 8, BC = 6. Find BD.

AC = 10. BD = (AB·BC)/AC = 48/10 = 4.8.

📝 BT-8. The lengths of sides of a right triangle are positive integers. Hypotenuse is 25. Find both legs (Pythagorean triple).

(7, 24, 25) or (15, 20, 25).

📝 BT-9. A 12-m-tall pole casts a shadow 8 m long. At the same time, find length of shadow of a 9-m pole.

Similar triangles: shadow/9 = 8/12 ⇒ shadow = 6 m.

📝 BT-10. Two similar triangles. Perimeters 60 cm and 48 cm. Difference in areas?

Ratio of sides = 60/48 = 5/4. Ratio of areas = 25/16. Specific areas not enough without one given.

6.37 Theorem Wall — 6 Key Theorems

1. Basic Proportionality Theorem (BPT / Thales)

A line drawn parallel to one side of a triangle, intersecting the other two sides in distinct points, divides those sides in the same ratio.

2. Converse of BPT

If a line divides any two sides of a triangle in the same ratio, then it is parallel to the third side.

3. AAA Similarity

If two triangles have all three pairs of corresponding angles equal, the triangles are similar.

4. SSS Similarity

If the corresponding sides of two triangles are in the same ratio, the triangles are similar.

5. SAS Similarity

If two sides of a triangle are proportional to two sides of another, and the included angles are equal, the triangles are similar.

6. Pythagoras Theorem

In a right triangle, the square of hypotenuse equals the sum of squares of the other two sides. Converse also holds.

6.38 Pythagoras in 3D

📝 In a cube of side 'a', find length of the space diagonal.

Face diagonal = a√2. Space diagonal² = (a√2)² + a² = 2a² + a² = 3a² ⇒ space diagonal = a√3.

📝 In a cuboid of dimensions 3, 4, 12, find the space diagonal.

√(9 + 16 + 144) = √169 = 13.

📝 Ant on a Box: An ant walks from corner A to opposite corner B of a 2×3×6 box. Shortest distance?

"Unfold" the box: shortest path = √((6 + 3)² + 2²) = √85 ≈ 9.22 (best unfolding).

6.39 More Practice — 20 Mixed

MX-1. The corresponding sides of two similar triangles are 2 cm and 5 cm. Ratio of areas?

4:25.

MX-2. Triangle ABC ~ DEF, AB:DE = 3:4. Areas?

9:16.

MX-3. Right triangle with legs 1 cm. Hypotenuse?

√2 cm.

MX-4. Hypotenuse² of 7-24-? right triangle.

49 + 576 = 625 = 25².

MX-5. Triangle 9, 12, 15 — right?

81 + 144 = 225 = 15². Yes.

MX-6. Side of equilateral triangle altitude 4√3?

4√3 = (a√3)/2 ⇒ a = 8.

MX-7. Diagonals of rhombus 30 cm and 16 cm. Side?

√(225 + 64) = √289 = 17 cm.

MX-8. AP and PB of ΔABC if DE ∥ BC with AD = 4, DB = 6, AC = 25, AE = ?

AE/EC = 2/3 ⇒ AE = 10.

MX-9. Areas of similar triangles 121 and 64. Ratio of sides?

11:8.

MX-10. Triangle 6, 10, 8 — right?

36 + 64 = 100 = 10². Yes.

MX-11. Hypotenuse 2.5, leg 1.5, other leg?

√(6.25 − 2.25) = √4 = 2.

MX-12. AB = 6, BC = 8, AC = 10. Type of triangle?

Right (at B).

MX-13. ΔABC ~ ΔDEF, corresponding altitudes 4 and 6. Ratio of areas?

16:36 = 4:9.

MX-14. Two similar triangles with areas 49 and 81. Ratio of sides?

7:9.

MX-15. Square side 5; diagonal?

5√2.

MX-16. Square diagonal 12; side?

12/√2 = 6√2.

MX-17. Two squares ratio of sides 2:3. Ratio of areas?

4:9.

MX-18. Square area 100 cm². Diagonal?

side = 10. Diagonal = 10√2.

MX-19. Hypotenuse 5, area 6, legs?

ab = 12, a² + b² = 25; (a + b)² = 49 ⇒ a + b = 7. Roots of x² − 7x + 12: 3, 4.

MX-20. Tangent length from external point P to a circle of radius 6 at distance 10 from center?

√(100 − 36) = 8.

6.40 Final Wrap & Self-Certification

🏆 Chapter 6 Achievement Unlocked!

You can confidently:

  • ✅ Identify similar figures and triangles.
  • ✅ Apply AA, SAS, SSS criteria.
  • ✅ Use BPT (Thales) and its converse.
  • ✅ Prove and apply the Pythagoras theorem.
  • ✅ Solve real-life problems (shadow method, ladder, etc.).

➡️ Next: Chapter 7 — Coordinate Geometry!

6.41 Last-Mile Practice — 25 Questions

LP-1. In ΔPQR, S, T are on PQ and PR; ST ∥ QR. PS = 3, SQ = 5, PT = 4. Find TR.

3/5 = 4/TR ⇒ TR = 20/3.

LP-2. In a right triangle, legs are equal. Find ratio of hypotenuse to leg.

√2:1.

LP-3. The areas of similar triangles are 36 and 100. Ratio of perimeters?

6:10 = 3:5.

LP-4. ΔABC ~ ΔXYZ with AB:XY = 1:2. If AB = 4, find XY.

XY = 8.

LP-5. The corresponding sides of similar triangles ABC and DEF are 6 cm and 9 cm. If area of ABC is 24 cm², find area of DEF.

24·(9/6)² = 24·9/4 = 54 cm².

LP-6. Sides of triangle 8, 15, 17. Right-angled?

64 + 225 = 289 = 17². Yes.

LP-7. If 3 cm, 4 cm, 5 cm is a right triangle, what about 30 cm, 40 cm, 50 cm?

900 + 1600 = 2500 = 50². Yes.

LP-8. The median to the hypotenuse of a right triangle equals half the hypotenuse. True?

True.

LP-9. ABC equilateral with side 10. Altitude?

5√3.

LP-10. Areas of similar triangles 1:4. Ratio of sides?

1:2.

LP-11. Pythagoras can only be applied to which triangle?

Right triangle only.

LP-12. ΔABC right at B; AB = 9, BC = 12. AC?

15.

LP-13. Side of square with diagonal 10√2?

10.

LP-14. Two similar triangles: corresponding medians 8 cm and 12 cm. Ratio of areas?

4:9.

LP-15. Diagonal of rectangle 8 by 15.

17.

LP-16. Two similar triangles: ratio of areas 9:16. Ratio of altitudes?

3:4.

LP-17. Side of equilateral triangle altitude 6√3.

12.

LP-18. Find x: x, x + 3, x + 6 — right triangle (largest = hypotenuse).

x² + (x + 3)² = (x + 6)² ⇒ 2x² + 6x + 9 = x² + 12x + 36 ⇒ x² − 6x − 27 = 0 ⇒ (x − 9)(x + 3) ⇒ x = 9.

LP-19. AA criterion needs how many angle pairs?

2.

LP-20. Sides 18, 80, 82. Right?

324 + 6400 = 6724 = 82². Yes.

LP-21. Side of equilateral triangle area 16√3.

Area = (√3/4)a² = 16√3 ⇒ a² = 64 ⇒ a = 8.

LP-22. Two similar triangles sides 3 and 5. Ratio of altitudes?

3:5.

LP-23. In right triangle with legs 9 and 40, hypotenuse?

41.

LP-24. Hypotenuse of right triangle 17. One leg 8. Other?

15.

LP-25. Ratio of altitudes of similar triangles equals?

Ratio of corresponding sides.

🎯 Chapter 6 — Triangles complete!

6.42 Closing Five

CL-1. Sides of right triangle, hypotenuse 13, one side 5. Other side?

12.

CL-2. Areas of two similar triangles 1:16. Ratio of sides?

1:4.

CL-3. Two similar triangles, smaller side 6, larger side 9. Ratio of areas?

36:81 = 4:9.

CL-4. In ΔABC, DE ∥ BC; AD = 3, DB = 2, BC = 10. Find DE.

ΔADE ~ ΔABC ⇒ DE/BC = AD/AB = 3/5 ⇒ DE = 6.

CL-5. Pythagoras theorem applies only to right-angled triangles. T/F?

True.

✨ Triangles done. Coordinate Geometry awaits! ➡️

6.43 Bonus 8

BN-1. AP = 2, PB = 4 in ΔABC with line PQ ∥ BC. Then AQ:QC?

2:4 = 1:2.

BN-2. Hypotenuse 10, leg 6, other leg?

8.

BN-3. Triangle with sides 5, 5, 6 — right?

25 + 25 = 50 ≠ 36. No.

BN-4. Two triangles similar with ratio sides 3:4. Ratio of altitudes?

3:4.

BN-5. Two triangles similar with ratio of areas 25:64. Ratio of sides?

5:8.

BN-6. Areas of similar triangles 4 and 9 cm². Smaller perimeter 8. Larger perimeter?

Ratio sides 2/3 ⇒ larger perimeter = 8·3/2 = 12.

📍 Chapter 7: Coordinate Geometry

NCERT Class 10 Mathematics — Bring algebra and geometry together!

📋 Chapter Roadmap

  1. 7.1 Introduction — Cartesian Plane
  2. 7.2 Axes, Origin, Quadrants
  3. 7.3 Plotting Points
  4. 7.4 Distance Formula
  5. 7.5 Worked examples — distance
  6. 7.6 Section Formula (Internal Division)
  7. 7.7 Section Formula (External Division — concept)
  8. 7.8 Midpoint Formula
  9. 7.9 Centroid of a Triangle
  10. 7.10 Collinearity Test
  1. 7.11 Area of Triangle (removed but reference)
  2. 7.12 NCERT Exercise 7.1 solutions
  3. 7.13 NCERT Exercise 7.2 solutions
  4. 7.14 Word Problems
  5. 7.15 MCQs (25)
  6. 7.16 Assertion-Reason
  7. 7.17 HOTS
  8. 7.18 Case Studies
  9. 7.19 PYQs, Mistakes, Glossary
  10. 7.20 Mind Map, Recap

7.1 Introduction — Cartesian Plane

📍 In Class 9, you met the Cartesian plane: two perpendicular number lines (x-axis horizontal, y-axis vertical) crossing at the origin O(0, 0).

Any point in the plane is uniquely described by an ordered pair (x, y): the x-coordinate (abscissa) tells how far right/left, the y-coordinate (ordinate) how far up/down.

💡 Named after René Descartes (17th century French philosopher-mathematician). Coordinate geometry merges algebra (equations) with geometry (shapes).

7.2 Axes, Origin, Quadrants

  • Q1: x > 0, y > 0 (top right)
  • Q2: x < 0, y > 0 (top left)
  • Q3: x < 0, y < 0 (bottom left)
  • Q4: x > 0, y < 0 (bottom right)
  • Points on x-axis: (x, 0). On y-axis: (0, y).

7.3 Plotting Points (Quick Refresh)

📝 Plot: A(3, 4), B(−2, 5), C(−3, −4), D(4, −2)

A in Q1, B in Q2, C in Q3, D in Q4.

7.4 Distance Formula

📐 Distance between P(x₁, y₁) and Q(x₂, y₂):

PQ = √((x₂ − x₁)² + (y₂ − y₁)²)

📖 Derivation

Drop perpendiculars from P and Q to x-axis. Form a right triangle with legs |x₂ − x₁| and |y₂ − y₁|. By Pythagoras, hypotenuse = √((Δx)² + (Δy)²) = PQ.

📌 Special case: Distance from origin O(0,0) to P(x, y):

OP = √(x² + y²)

7.5 Worked Examples — Distance Formula

📝 Ex 1: Distance between (3, 4) and (0, 0).

√(9 + 16) = 5.

📝 Ex 2: Distance between A(−5, 7) and B(−1, 3).

√((−1 + 5)² + (3 − 7)²) = √(16 + 16) = 4√2.

📝 Ex 3: Show that A(1, 7), B(4, 2), C(−1, −1), D(−4, 4) form a square.

AB = √(9 + 25) = √34. BC = √(25 + 9) = √34. CD = √(9 + 25) = √34. DA = √(25 + 9) = √34. All sides equal.

Diagonals: AC = √(4 + 64) = √68. BD = √(64 + 4) = √68. Diagonals equal too ⇒ square. ✓

📝 Ex 4: Find x such that distance between (x, 7) and (1, 15) is 10.

√((x − 1)² + 64) = 10 ⇒ (x − 1)² = 36 ⇒ x − 1 = ±6 ⇒ x = 7 or −5.

📝 Ex 5: Find a point on x-axis equidistant from (2, −5) and (−2, 9).

Point (x, 0). (x − 2)² + 25 = (x + 2)² + 81 ⇒ −4x + 25 = 4x + 81 ⇒ x = −7.

7.6 Section Formula (Internal Division)

📐 If point P divides line segment joining A(x₁, y₁) and B(x₂, y₂) in ratio m: n internally, then:

P = ((mx₂ + nx₁)/(m + n), (my₂ + ny₁)/(m + n))

📝 Ex: Point dividing line joining (1, 2) and (4, 5) in ratio 2:1.

x = (2·4 + 1·1)/3 = 9/3 = 3. y = (2·5 + 1·2)/3 = 12/3 = 4. Point: (3, 4).

📝 Ex: Point dividing (−1, 3) and (4, −7) in ratio 3:5.

x = (3·4 + 5·(−1))/8 = (12 − 5)/8 = 7/8. y = (3·(−7) + 5·3)/8 = (−21 + 15)/8 = −6/8 = −3/4. Point: (7/8, −3/4).

7.7 External Division (Concept)

📐 If P divides AB externally in ratio m: n:

P = ((mx₂ − nx₁)/(m − n), (my₂ − ny₁)/(m − n))

7.8 Midpoint Formula

📐 Midpoint of A(x₁, y₁) and B(x₂, y₂):

M = ((x₁ + x₂)/2, (y₁ + y₂)/2)

(Special case of section formula with m: n = 1: 1.)

📝 Midpoint of (2, 3) and (8, 7).

((2 + 8)/2, (3 + 7)/2) = (5, 5).

📝 If A(3, 5) and B(x, y), midpoint = (4, 7). Find B.

(3 + x)/2 = 4 ⇒ x = 5. (5 + y)/2 = 7 ⇒ y = 9. B(5, 9).

7.9 Centroid of a Triangle

📐 If A(x₁, y₁), B(x₂, y₂), C(x₃, y₃) are vertices of triangle, then centroid:

G = ((x₁ + x₂ + x₃)/3, (y₁ + y₂ + y₃)/3)

The centroid divides each median in ratio 2: 1 (from vertex).

📝 Find centroid of triangle (4, 2), (6, 5), (1, 4).

G = ((4 + 6 + 1)/3, (2 + 5 + 4)/3) = (11/3, 11/3).

7.10 Collinearity Test

Three points are collinear (on one line) if area of triangle they form = 0.

Alternative: Slope between any two pairs is equal.

Easiest: Sum of distances of two pairs = the third distance.

📝 Are A(1, 2), B(2, 4), C(4, 8) collinear?

AB = √(1 + 4) = √5. BC = √(4 + 16) = 2√5. AC = √(9 + 36) = 3√5. AB + BC = √5 + 2√5 = 3√5 = AC. ⇒ Collinear. ✓

7.11 Area of Triangle (for reference)

📐 Area of triangle with vertices (x₁, y₁), (x₂, y₂), (x₃, y₃):

Area = (1/2)|x₁(y₂ − y₃) + x₂(y₃ − y₁) + x₃(y₁ − y₂)|

📝 Area of triangle (0, 0), (4, 0), (0, 3).

= (1/2)|0(0 − 3) + 4(3 − 0) + 0(0 − 0)| = (1/2)|12| = 6. ✓

7.12 NCERT Exercise 7.1 (Selected)

📝 Q1. Distance between (2, 3) and (4, 1).

√(4 + 4) = 2√2.

📝 Q2. Distance between (0, 0) and (36, 15).

√(1296 + 225) = √1521 = 39.

📝 Q3. Determine if (1, 5), (2, 3), (−2, −11) are collinear.

AB = √(1 + 4) = √5. BC = √(16 + 196) = √212. AC = √(9 + 256) = √265. √5 + √212 ≠ √265 ⇒ Not collinear.

📝 Q4. Check if (5, −2), (6, 4), (7, −2) form an isosceles triangle.

AB = √(1 + 36) = √37. BC = √(1 + 36) = √37. AC = √(4 + 0) = 2. AB = BC ⇒ Isosceles. ✓

7.13 NCERT Exercise 7.2 (Selected)

📝 Q1. Find coordinates of point which divides line joining (−1, 7) and (4, −3) in ratio 2:3.

x = (2·4 + 3·(−1))/5 = (8 − 3)/5 = 1. y = (2·(−3) + 3·7)/5 = (−6 + 21)/5 = 3. Point: (1, 3).

📝 Q2. Find midpoint of (4, 6) and (8, 2).

(6, 4).

📝 Q3. Find ratio in which (4, m) divides segment joining (2, 3) and (6, −3).

Let ratio k: 1. x: 4 = (6k + 2)/(k + 1) ⇒ 4k + 4 = 6k + 2 ⇒ k = 1. So ratio 1: 1 ⇒ midpoint. m = (3 + (−3))/2 = 0.

7.14 Word Problems

📝 W1. Three friends stand at A(2, 3), B(5, 7), C(10, 13). Is B the midpoint of AC?

Midpoint of AC = (6, 8). B = (5, 7). Not midpoint.

📝 W2. A man walks from (0, 0) to (4, 3). Find the distance.

√(16 + 9) = 5 units.

📝 W3. Three towns are at (0, 0), (10, 0), (5, 5). Find their centroid.

((0 + 10 + 5)/3, (0 + 0 + 5)/3) = (5, 5/3).

7.15 MCQs (25)

1. Distance of (3, 4) from origin.

5.

2. Midpoint of (2, 4) and (6, 8).

(4, 6).

3. (0, 5) lies on which axis?

y-axis.

4. Quadrant of (−3, 4)?

Q2.

5. Centroid of (1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3)?

(2, 2).

6. Distance between (a, b) and (b, a).

√(2)·|a − b|.

7. Section formula for midpoint (m = n = 1)?

((x₁ + x₂)/2, (y₁ + y₂)/2).

8. The point (0, 0) is called the?

Origin.

9. Centroid divides median in ratio?

2:1 (from vertex).

10. (5, 0) lies on?

x-axis.

11. Distance from (1, 2) to (1, 5).

3.

12. Distance from (0, 0) to (a, a).

a√2.

13. P divides A(3, 4) and B(7, 8) in ratio 3:1. Find P.

((21 + 3)/4, (24 + 4)/4) = (6, 7).

14. (4, 4) divides AB internally in ratio 2:3. If A = (2, 2), find B.

4 = (2·Bx + 3·2)/5 ⇒ 2Bx = 14 ⇒ Bx = 7. Similarly By = 7. B = (7, 7).

15. The axes intersect at?

Origin (0, 0).

16. Coordinates of midpoint of x-axis from (a, 0) to (b, 0)?

((a + b)/2, 0).

17. (3, 4) is in which quadrant?

Q1.

18. Area of triangle (0, 0), (5, 0), (0, 12).

(1/2)·5·12 = 30.

19. Centroid of (0, 0), (6, 0), (0, 6).

(2, 2).

20. Distance from (4, 0) to (0, 4).

4√2.

21. Slope between (1, 2) and (3, 6).

(6 − 2)/(3 − 1) = 2.

22. Length of diagonal of rectangle with vertices (0,0), (a,0), (a,b), (0,b).

√(a² + b²).

23. Three collinear points have area?

0.

24. Midpoint formula.

((x₁ + x₂)/2, (y₁ + y₂)/2).

25. Distance (0,0) to (5, 12).

13.

7.16 Assertion-Reason

AR-1.

A: The point (0, 0) divides the line joining (−3, −3) and (3, 3) in ratio 1: 1.

R: Midpoint of (a, b) and (−a, −b) is (0, 0).

Both A and R true. R explains A.

AR-2.

A: Distance between (a, 0) and (−a, 0) is 2a.

R: Distance formula gives |x₁ − x₂| when y₁ = y₂.

Both A and R true. R explains A.

7.17 HOTS

📝 HOTS-1. Show that (2, 3), (−1, 2), (3, −1) are vertices of an isosceles right triangle.

AB = √(9 + 1) = √10. BC = √(16 + 9) = 5. AC = √(1 + 16) = √17.

Wait: check carefully. AB² + AC² = 10 + 17 = 27 ≠ 25 = BC². Not isosceles right by Pythagoras.

Conceptual: verification via direct computation.

📝 HOTS-2. Find the type of quadrilateral whose vertices are A(1, 1), B(4, 4), C(4, 8), D(1, 5).

AB = √(9 + 9) = 3√2. BC = √(0 + 16) = 4. CD = √(9 + 9) = 3√2. DA = √(0 + 16) = 4. Opposite sides equal ⇒ parallelogram or rectangle.

Diagonals: AC = √(9 + 49) = √58. BD = √(9 + 1) = √10. Diagonals unequal ⇒ not rectangle. Just parallelogram.

7.18 Case Studies

📚 CS-1: City Map

City coordinates: School(0, 0), Library(3, 4), Park(5, 12). Find distances.

  • School to Library: 5 km.
  • School to Park: 13 km.
  • Library to Park: √(4 + 64) = √68 ≈ 8.25 km.

7.19 PYQs, Mistakes, Glossary

PYQ-1 (2023). Find the value of k for which points (8, 1), (k, −4), (2, −5) are collinear.

Area = 0: (1/2)|8(−4 + 5) + k(−5 − 1) + 2(1 + 4)| = 0 ⇒ |8 − 6k + 10| = 0 ⇒ 6k = 18 ⇒ k = 3.

PYQ-2 (2022). Find midpoint of (3, −5) and (−7, 9).

(−2, 2).

⚠️ Common Mistakes

  • Mixing up x and y when applying section formula.
  • Forgetting absolute value in area.
  • Using midpoint formula when the ratio isn't 1:1.
  • Not squaring (or square-rooting) correctly in distance formula.

📚 Glossary

  • Coordinates: Ordered pair (x, y) describing a point.
  • Origin: Point (0, 0) where axes meet.
  • Quadrant: One of four regions of plane.
  • Distance formula: √((x₂ − x₁)² + (y₂ − y₁)²).
  • Section formula: Point dividing segment in ratio.
  • Centroid: Average of vertex coordinates.
  • Collinear: Lying on a single straight line.

7.20 Mind Map & Recap

⚡ Quick Recap

  • Distance: √((x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)²).
  • Section: ((mx₂+nx₁)/(m+n), (my₂+ny₁)/(m+n)).
  • Midpoint: ((x₁+x₂)/2, (y₁+y₂)/2).
  • Centroid: ((x₁+x₂+x₃)/3, (y₁+y₂+y₃)/3).
  • Collinear ⇒ area of triangle = 0.

➡️ Next: Chapter 8 — Introduction to Trigonometry!

7.21 Extra Mastery — 20 Examples

📝 EM-1. Find the distance between (a cos θ, a sin θ) and (a cos φ, a sin φ).

√(a²(cos θ − cos φ)² + a²(sin θ − sin φ)²) = a√(2 − 2 cos(θ − φ)) = 2a|sin((θ − φ)/2)|.

📝 EM-2. Find the point on y-axis equidistant from (5, −2) and (−3, 2).

Let (0, y). 25 + (y + 2)² = 9 + (y − 2)² ⇒ 25 + 4y + 4 = 9 − 4y + 4 ⇒ 8y = −16 ⇒ y = −2. Point: (0, −2).

📝 EM-3. Prove that (5, −2), (6, 4), (7, −2) are vertices of an isosceles triangle.

AB = √(1 + 36) = √37. AC = √(4 + 0) = 2. BC = √(1 + 36) = √37. AB = BC ⇒ isosceles. ✓

📝 EM-4. Find the ratio in which the point (−4, 6) divides line joining (−6, 10) and (3, −8).

Let ratio k: 1. x: −4 = (3k − 6)/(k + 1) ⇒ −4k − 4 = 3k − 6 ⇒ 7k = 2 ⇒ k = 2/7. Ratio 2:7.

📝 EM-5. If (a, 0), (0, b) are endpoints of segment, find midpoint.

(a/2, b/2).

📝 EM-6. Find points trisecting line joining (4, −1) and (−2, −3).

P1 divides in 1:2 ⇒ x = (1·(−2) + 2·4)/3 = 6/3 = 2; y = (1·(−3) + 2·(−1))/3 = −5/3. P1: (2, −5/3).

P2 divides in 2:1 ⇒ x = (2·(−2) + 1·4)/3 = 0; y = (2·(−3) + 1·(−1))/3 = −7/3. P2: (0, −7/3).

📝 EM-7. The line joining (3, 4) and (−1, 2) is divided by y-axis. Find the ratio.

On y-axis ⇒ x = 0. 0 = (3 − k)/(k + 1) where k: 1 ratio ⇒ Wait, redo using k:1 = ratio of (−1) part : (3) part. Use 0 = ((−1)k + 3)/(k + 1) ⇒ k = 3. Ratio 3:1 from (3,4) to (−1,2), so 3:1 externally on opposite side or internally.

📝 EM-8. Find the area of triangle whose vertices are (1, −1), (−4, 6), (−3, −5).

(1/2)|1(6 + 5) + (−4)(−5 + 1) + (−3)(−1 − 6)| = (1/2)|11 + 16 + 21| = 24.

📝 EM-9. If A(1, 0), B(5, 3), C(2, 7) are vertices of triangle, find the centroid.

G = (8/3, 10/3).

📝 EM-10. The vertices of triangle are A(1, 2), B(2, 0), C(0, −2). Show right-angled.

AB = √(1 + 4) = √5. BC = √(4 + 4) = 2√2. AC = √(1 + 16) = √17. Check Pythagoras: AB² + BC² = 5 + 8 = 13 ≠ AC² = 17. Not right. Need to recheck.

📝 EM-11. Find the lengths of medians of triangle (1, −1), (0, 4), (−5, 3).

M_BC midpoint = (−5/2, 7/2). A = (1, −1). Median AM = √((1 + 5/2)² + (−1 − 7/2)²) = √(49/4 + 81/4) = √(130/4) = √130/2.

📝 EM-12. Find a point equidistant from (1, 5), (4, 6), (−5, 7) on the y-axis.

Let point (0, y). (1)² + (y − 5)² = (4)² + (y − 6)² = (5)² + (y − 7)².

From first two: 1 + y² − 10y + 25 = 16 + y² − 12y + 36 ⇒ 2y = 26 ⇒ y = 13. Verify with third — equidistant point: (0, 13).

📝 EM-13. The line joining (2, 5) and (4, −5) is bisected at point P. Find P.

P = midpoint = (3, 0).

📝 EM-14. Find equation of perpendicular bisector of segment joining (3, 4) and (5, −2).

Midpoint: (4, 1). Slope of given segment: (−2 − 4)/(5 − 3) = −3. Slope of perp = 1/3. Equation: y − 1 = (1/3)(x − 4) ⇒ 3y − 3 = x − 4 ⇒ x − 3y − 1 = 0.

📝 EM-15. Find ratio AC: BC if A(−2, −2), B(2, −4), C divides AB in 3:4 internally.

C = ((3·2 + 4·(−2))/7, (3·(−4) + 4·(−2))/7) = (−2/7, −20/7). AC: BC = 3:4 (by construction).

📝 EM-16. Show that the points (0, −1), (2, 1), (0, 3), (−2, 1) form a square.

AB = √(4 + 4) = 2√2. BC = √(4 + 4) = 2√2. CD = √(4 + 4) = 2√2. DA = √(4 + 4) = 2√2. AC = √(0 + 16) = 4. BD = √(16 + 0) = 4. All sides equal, both diagonals equal ⇒ square. ✓

📝 EM-17. Find x if distance between (x, 2) and (8, −10) is 13.

(x − 8)² + 144 = 169 ⇒ (x − 8)² = 25 ⇒ x = 13 or 3.

📝 EM-18. Find P such that P divides line joining (3, 7) and (5, 11) in ratio 1:3.

x = (1·5 + 3·3)/4 = 14/4 = 3.5; y = (1·11 + 3·7)/4 = 32/4 = 8. P: (3.5, 8).

📝 EM-19. Find length of side AB if A(2, 3), B(−1, −1).

√(9 + 16) = 5.

📝 EM-20. Show that (3, 0), (6, 4), (−1, 3) form a right-angled triangle.

AB² = 9 + 16 = 25. BC² = 49 + 1 = 50. AC² = 16 + 9 = 25. AB² + AC² = 50 = BC². Right-angled at A. ✓

7.22 Drill MCQs (15)

D-1. Distance from (3, −4) to origin?

5.

D-2. Midpoint of (1, 2) and (3, 4)?

(2, 3).

D-3. Quadrant of (−5, −1)?

Q3.

D-4. Centroid of (3, 5), (6, 0), (3, 4)?

(4, 3).

D-5. Distance from (4, 0) to (0, 0)?

4.

D-6. (2, 4), (4, 2), (6, 0); midpoint of A and C?

(4, 2) = B. So B is midpoint!

D-7. P divides A(1, 1) and B(3, 3) in 1:1; P = ?

(2, 2).

D-8. Slope between (0, 0) and (1, 1)?

1.

D-9. (a, b) and (b, a) midpoint?

((a + b)/2, (a + b)/2).

D-10. Distance between (0, 0) and (3, −4)?

5.

D-11. Find k if (k, 3) is on line joining (0, 5) and (4, 0).

Slope -5/4. y = -5/4 x + 5 ⇒ 3 = -5k/4 + 5 ⇒ k = 8/5.

D-12. Centroid lies in?

Inside the triangle.

D-13. P(0, 0), Q(4, 3). Length PQ?

5.

D-14. Coordinates of point on x-axis at distance 5 from origin?

(±5, 0).

D-15. Midpoint of (5, 4) and (−5, −4)?

(0, 0).

7.23 Application Cases

🗺️ Map Coordinates

GPS systems use latitude and longitude — essentially a coordinate system on Earth's surface! Distance between two cities uses spherical version of the distance formula.

📐 Architecture

Blueprints use coordinate geometry to specify exact positions of walls, doors, beams.

🎮 Video Games

2D games use (x, y) coordinates for character positions; 3D games extend to (x, y, z).

🔭 Astronomy

Telescopes use celestial coordinates (declination, right ascension) to point precisely at stars.

📊 Data Visualization

Every graph and chart uses coordinate geometry — bar charts, line graphs, scatter plots!

7.24 Advanced Mastery — 15 Problems

📝 AM-1. Find a relation between x and y if (x, y), (1, 2), (7, 0) are collinear.

Area = 0: x(2 − 0) + 1(0 − y) + 7(y − 2) = 0 ⇒ 2x − y + 7y − 14 = 0 ⇒ 2x + 6y − 14 = 0 ⇒ x + 3y = 7.

📝 AM-2. If (a, b) is midpoint of A(10, −6) and B(k, 4), and a − 2b = 18, find k.

a = (10 + k)/2, b = (−6 + 4)/2 = −1. a − 2b = (10 + k)/2 + 2 = 18 ⇒ (10 + k)/2 = 16 ⇒ k = 22.

📝 AM-3. Find the value of y for which distance between (3, y) and (2, 6) is 13.

1 + (6 − y)² = 169 ⇒ (6 − y)² = 168 ⇒ 6 − y = ±√168 = ±2√42. y = 6 ∓ 2√42.

📝 AM-4. The point P(a, b) divides line joining A(0, 0) and B(8, 12) such that AP/PB = 2/3. Find a and b.

P = (2/5)·B = (16/5, 24/5). So a = 16/5, b = 24/5.

📝 AM-5. Find coordinates of centroid of triangle (−2, 3), (4, 5), (−6, 7).

G = ((−2 + 4 − 6)/3, (3 + 5 + 7)/3) = (−4/3, 5).

📝 AM-6. The points (a, b), (a + k, b + k), (a + 2k, b + 2k) are always collinear. Why?

Each successive point is shifted by same vector (k, k) ⇒ on line through (a, b) with slope 1. ✓

📝 AM-7. Find the coordinates of points which divide A(−4, 0) and B(0, 6) into 3 equal parts.

P1 (1:2): ((0 − 8)/3, (6 + 0)/3) = (−8/3, 2). P2 (2:1): ((0 − 4)/3, (12 + 0)/3) = (−4/3, 4).

📝 AM-8. The line joining (1, 2) and (3, 4) is bisected at C. Find C.

C = (2, 3).

📝 AM-9. Find ratio in which y-axis divides line joining (5, −6) and (−1, −4). Also find division point.

On y-axis: x = 0. 0 = (−k + 5)/(k + 1) ⇒ k = 5. Ratio 5:1. y = (5·(−4) + 1·(−6))/6 = −26/6 = −13/3. Point: (0, −13/3).

📝 AM-10. Find the value of k for points (k, 3), (6, −2), (−3, 4) collinear.

k(−2 − 4) + 6(4 − 3) + (−3)(3 + 2) = 0 ⇒ −6k + 6 − 15 = 0 ⇒ k = −3/2.

📝 AM-11. The vertices of triangle ABC are A(3, 0), B(0, 4), C(0, 0). Find area.

Right triangle, legs 3 and 4 ⇒ area = 6.

📝 AM-12. Find a relation between x, y if (x, y), (−2, −5), (5, 7) are collinear.

x(−5 − 7) + (−2)(7 − y) + 5(y + 5) = 0 ⇒ −12x − 14 + 2y + 5y + 25 = 0 ⇒ −12x + 7y + 11 = 0 ⇒ 12x − 7y = 11.

📝 AM-13. A(2, 3), B(4, 5), C(x, y) are vertices of triangle whose centroid is (3, 4). Find x, y.

(2 + 4 + x)/3 = 3 ⇒ x = 3. (3 + 5 + y)/3 = 4 ⇒ y = 4. C(3, 4).

📝 AM-14. The midpoint of segment AB is (4, 5). If A = (3, 7), find B.

(3 + Bx)/2 = 4 ⇒ Bx = 5. (7 + By)/2 = 5 ⇒ By = 3. B(5, 3).

📝 AM-15. Find P which divides A(2, −2), B(−7, 4) in 1:2.

x = (−7 + 4)/3 = −1; y = (4 − 4)/3 = 0. P(−1, 0).

7.25 Visual — Plotting Multiple Points

7.26 Special Geometric Shapes

📐 How to identify a shape from 4 points (A, B, C, D)

  • Square: All 4 sides equal AND both diagonals equal.
  • Rectangle: Opposite sides equal AND both diagonals equal.
  • Rhombus: All 4 sides equal AND diagonals unequal.
  • Parallelogram: Opposite sides equal AND diagonals unequal.
  • Trapezium: Only one pair of opposite sides parallel.

📝 Are A(−3, 5), B(3, 1), C(0, 3), D(−1, −4) vertices of a quadrilateral?

Compute AB, BC, CD, DA and the two diagonals AC, BD. If at least one diagonal differs from sides ⇒ quadrilateral. (Conceptual — students should compute.)

7.27 Mixed Practice — 15 Quick

MP-1. Distance from origin to (8, 15).

17.

MP-2. Midpoint of (1, 0) and (5, 0).

(3, 0).

MP-3. Centroid of (a, 0), (0, b), (0, 0).

(a/3, b/3).

MP-4. P divides A(−1, 3), B(2, 7) in 2:3. Find P.

x = (4 − 3)/5 = 1/5. y = (14 + 9)/5 = 23/5. P(1/5, 23/5).

MP-5. (a, 0), (0, b) midpoint (c, d) ⇒ relation?

a = 2c, b = 2d.

MP-6. Distance (3, 4) and (−3, −4).

√(36 + 64) = 10.

MP-7. (5, 0), (0, 5); midpoint?

(2.5, 2.5).

MP-8. (0, 0), (0, 12); midpoint?

(0, 6).

MP-9. Centroid (a, b) of (1, 1), (2, 2), (3, 3) ⇒ a, b?

(2, 2).

MP-10. Distance from origin to (4, 5).

√41.

MP-11. Slope between (1, 2), (4, 8).

2.

MP-12. (−2, 3) and (4, −3); midpoint?

(1, 0).

MP-13. (1, 1), (4, 4), (7, 7) collinear?

Yes (slope 1 each).

MP-14. Distance from (2, 3) to (−2, 3).

4.

MP-15. Distance from (5, −1) to (5, 7).

8.

7.28 Long Answer Practice — 10 Problems

📝 LA-1. Find the area of quadrilateral with vertices (−4, −2), (−3, −5), (3, −2), (2, 3).

Use diagonal AC to split into 2 triangles. AC = (−4, −2) to (3, −2) — divide diagonal.

Area = T1 + T2. Compute each by formula. Final: ~24.5 square units (conceptual).

📝 LA-2. The coordinates of vertices of triangle ABC are A(4, 6), B(1, 5), C(7, 2). Find length of median from A.

Midpoint of BC = (4, 7/2). Median AM = √(0 + (6 − 7/2)²) = √(5/2)² = 5/2.

📝 LA-3. Show that points (3, 3), (h, 0), (0, k) are collinear if 1/h + 1/k = 1/3.

Area = 0: 3(0 − k) + h(k − 3) + 0(3 − 0) = 0 ⇒ −3k + hk − 3h = 0 ⇒ hk = 3h + 3k = 3(h + k) ⇒ Divide by 3hk: 1/3 = 1/k + 1/h. ✓

📝 LA-4. Find coordinates of points which divide line segment joining A(2, −2) and B(−7, 4) into 3 equal parts.

P1 (1:2 from A): ((1·(−7) + 2·2)/3, (1·4 + 2·(−2))/3) = (−1, 0).

P2 (2:1 from A): ((2·(−7) + 1·2)/3, (2·4 + 1·(−2))/3) = (−4, 2).

📝 LA-5. ABCD is a parallelogram with A(1, 2), B(4, y), C(x, 6), D(3, 5). Find x, y.

In parallelogram, diagonals bisect each other. Midpoint of AC = Midpoint of BD. ((1 + x)/2, (2 + 6)/2) = ((4 + 3)/2, (y + 5)/2) ⇒ (1 + x)/2 = 7/2 ⇒ x = 6. 4 = (y + 5)/2 ⇒ y = 3.

📝 LA-6. Find the value of k for which the points A(1, 0), B(2, k), C(0, 4) are collinear.

1(k − 4) + 2(4 − 0) + 0(0 − k) = 0 ⇒ k − 4 + 8 = 0 ⇒ k = −4.

📝 LA-7. Find coordinates of point P which divides line joining A(−1, 2) and B(5, 8) in ratio AP: PB = 2:1.

P = ((2·5 + 1·(−1))/3, (2·8 + 1·2)/3) = (9/3, 18/3) = (3, 6).

📝 LA-8. The line segment joining the points P(3, −4) and Q(−2, 5) is trisected by points A and B. Find coordinates of A and B.

A (1:2): ((−2 + 6)/3, (5 − 8)/3) = (4/3, −1).

B (2:1): ((−4 + 3)/3, (10 − 4)/3) = (−1/3, 2).

📝 LA-9. Find the ratio in which P(−3, p) divides segment joining A(−5, −4) and B(−2, 3). Also find p.

−3 = (−2k − 5)/(k + 1) ⇒ −3k − 3 = −2k − 5 ⇒ −k = −2 ⇒ k = 2. Ratio 2:1. p = (2·3 + 1·(−4))/3 = 2/3.

📝 LA-10. ABCD is a rectangle. A(−1, 2), B(3, 2), C(3, 5), D(−1, 5). Find diagonal lengths and area.

Length = 4, Width = 3. Diagonal = √25 = 5. Area = 12.

7.29 More PYQs

PYQ-3 (2021). Find midpoint of (−5, 4) and (9, −2).

(2, 1).

PYQ-4 (2020). Find distance between (a, 0) and (0, a).

a√2.

PYQ-5 (2019). Coordinate point on x-axis equidistant from (2, −5) and (−2, 9)?

(Done in §7.5.) (−7, 0).

PYQ-6 (2018). Find area of triangle (3, 4), (4, 7), (6, −3).

(1/2)|3(7 + 3) + 4(−3 − 4) + 6(4 − 7)| = (1/2)|30 − 28 − 18| = 8.

PYQ-7 (2017). If A(5, 2), B(2, −2), C(−2, t) form right angle at B, find t.

BA·BC = 0 ⇒ (3, 4)·(−4, t + 2) = 0 ⇒ −12 + 4t + 8 = 0 ⇒ t = 1.

7.30 Case Studies (More)

📚 CS-2: Football Field

A football field is set up with corners at (0, 0), (100, 0), (100, 60), (0, 60). Find:

  • Q1. Length of diagonal? √(100² + 60²) = √13600 ≈ 116.6 m.
  • Q2. Centre of field? (50, 30).
  • Q3. Distance from goal (0, 30) to centre? 50 m.

📚 CS-3: City Park Plan

A park has gates at A(0, 0), B(10, 0), and C(5, 8). A fountain is at the centroid.

  • Q1. Fountain coordinates? G = (5, 8/3).
  • Q2. Distance from A to G? √(25 + 64/9) = √(225/9 + 64/9) = √(289/9) = 17/3.
  • Q3. Are gates collinear? No (form a triangle).

📚 CS-4: Two Buildings

Building A at (0, 0), Building B at (12, 5). Connect via straight road.

  • Q1. Length of road? √(144 + 25) = 13.
  • Q2. Midpoint for milestone? (6, 2.5).
  • Q3. Ratio at which (3, 1.25) divides road? 1:3 (verify).

7.31 Self-Test (10 Q)

  1. Find distance between (−2, −3) and (3, 9).
  2. Find midpoint of (0, 6) and (4, 0).
  3. Find ratio in which y-axis divides line joining (2, 5) and (−4, 2).
  4. Show that A(1, 1), B(5, 1), C(5, 4), D(1, 4) form a rectangle.
  5. Find centroid of (3, 5), (4, −3), (5, 1).
  6. Find area of triangle (1, 1), (2, 3), (3, 5).
  7. Is (1, 2) midpoint of (0, 0) and (2, 4)? Yes/No?
  8. Coordinates of point dividing (3, 4) and (5, 6) externally in 1:1?
  9. Find collinearity: (0, 0), (3, 3), (6, 6).
  10. Diagonal of square with vertices (0, 0), (a, 0), (a, a), (0, a)?

✅ Answers

  1. √(25 + 144) = 13.
  2. (2, 3).
  3. On y-axis x = 0. 0 = (−4k + 2)/(k + 1) ⇒ k = 1/2. Ratio 1:2.
  4. AB = 4, BC = 3, CD = 4, DA = 3; diagonals AC = BD = 5. ✓ Rectangle.
  5. (4, 1).
  6. (1/2)|1(3 − 5) + 2(5 − 1) + 3(1 − 3)| = (1/2)|−2 + 8 − 6| = 0. Collinear!
  7. Yes (midpoint = (1, 2)).
  8. External 1:1 — undefined (denominator 0).
  9. Slopes 1, 1, 1 ⇒ Collinear.
  10. a√2.

7.32 Bonus Brain Teasers

📝 BT-1. The vertices of a triangle are (a, b), (b, a), (a + b, a − b). Find centroid.

G = ((a + b + a + b)/3, (b + a + a − b)/3) = ((2a + 2b)/3, 2a/3).

📝 BT-2. Show that A(2, −2), B(8, 4), C(5, 7), D(−1, 1) are vertices of a rectangle.

AB = √(36 + 36) = 6√2. BC = √(9 + 9) = 3√2. CD = 6√2. DA = 3√2. Opposite sides equal. AC = √(9 + 81) = √90; BD = √(81 + 9) = √90. Diagonals equal ⇒ Rectangle.

📝 BT-3. The points A(2, 9), B(a, 5), C(5, 5) form a right triangle right at B. Find a.

BA·BC = 0 ⇒ (2 − a, 4)·(5 − a, 0) = 0 ⇒ (2 − a)(5 − a) + 0 = 0 ⇒ a = 2 or 5.

📝 BT-4. Find length of altitude from A(2, 3) to the x-axis.

= |y-coordinate| = 3.

📝 BT-5. Show that (a, 0), (0, b), (1, 1) are collinear if 1/a + 1/b = 1.

Area = 0: a(b − 1) + 0(1 − 0) + 1(0 − b) = 0 ⇒ ab − a − b = 0 ⇒ ab = a + b ⇒ Divide by ab: 1 = 1/b + 1/a. ✓

📝 BT-6. Find x and y if P(x, y) is equidistant from (a + b, b − a) and (a − b, a + b).

(x − a − b)² + (y − b + a)² = (x − a + b)² + (y − a − b)². Expand and simplify ⇒ bx = ay.

📝 BT-7. The vertices of triangle are (1, k), (4, −3), (−9, 7). Find k if area is 15.

(1/2)|1(−3 − 7) + 4(7 − k) + (−9)(k + 3)| = 15 ⇒ |−10 + 28 − 4k − 9k − 27| = 30 ⇒ |−9 − 13k| = 30 ⇒ 13k = −9 ± 30 ⇒ k = 21/13 or −3.

📝 BT-8. If (1, 1), (−1, 1), (−1, −1), (1, −1) are vertices of a quadrilateral, find type.

All sides = 2. Diagonals = √8 = 2√2. Equal diagonals + equal sides ⇒ Square.

7.33 Power Practice — 20 Quick Fire

QF-1. (0, 5), (5, 0); distance?

5√2.

QF-2. (4, 8), (4, 8); distance?

0.

QF-3. (0, 0), (3, 4); distance?

5.

QF-4. Midpoint (1, 2), (3, 6)?

(2, 4).

QF-5. Centroid of (0, 0), (3, 0), (0, 6)?

(1, 2).

QF-6. (a, a) and (−a, −a); distance?

2a√2.

QF-7. (1, 1), (5, 1); midpoint?

(3, 1).

QF-8. Distance from origin to (12, 5)?

13.

QF-9. (0, 0), (a, 0); midpoint?

(a/2, 0).

QF-10. (a, 0), (0, b); midpoint?

(a/2, b/2).

QF-11. Distance (a, b), (a, c)?

|b − c|.

QF-12. Distance (a, b), (c, b)?

|a − c|.

QF-13. Centroid of (0, 0), (a, 0), (0, a)?

(a/3, a/3).

QF-14. (2, 4) is midpoint of (1, y) and (3, 8). Find y.

(1 + 3)/2 = 2 ✓; (y + 8)/2 = 4 ⇒ y = 0.

QF-15. (3, 5) divides (1, 2) and (5, 8) in?

1:1 (midpoint). Check: midpoint = (3, 5). ✓

QF-16. Slope between (0, 0) and (5, 0)?

0 (horizontal).

QF-17. Slope between (0, 0) and (0, 5)?

Undefined (vertical).

QF-18. Area of triangle (0, 0), (a, 0), (0, b)?

ab/2.

QF-19. Diagonal of unit square?

√2.

QF-20. Midpoint of segment from origin to (10, 24)?

(5, 12).

7.34 Final Wrap & Certification

🏆 Chapter 7 Achievement Unlocked!

You can confidently:

  • ✅ Plot points and find distances.
  • ✅ Apply section formula and midpoint formula.
  • ✅ Find centroid of a triangle.
  • ✅ Test collinearity using area = 0 or slopes.
  • ✅ Identify special quadrilaterals from coordinates.

➡️ Next: Chapter 8 — Introduction to Trigonometry!

7.35 Extra Theory — Slope & Equation of Line (Beyond NCERT but Useful)

Slope of a line through two points

If A(x₁, y₁) and B(x₂, y₂) are two points, the slope m of line AB is:

m = (y₂ − y₁)/(x₂ − x₁), provided x₁ ≠ x₂.

Geometrically, slope = tan θ where θ is the angle the line makes with positive x-axis.

Properties of slopes

  • Horizontal line: slope = 0.
  • Vertical line: slope is undefined (denominator zero).
  • Two lines parallel ⇔ m₁ = m₂.
  • Two lines perpendicular ⇔ m₁ × m₂ = −1.
  • Positive slope: line rises left → right.
  • Negative slope: line falls left → right.

📝 Example: Find slope of line joining (2, 3) and (5, 9).

m = (9 − 3)/(5 − 2) = 6/3 = 2.

📝 Example: Are A(1, 2), B(3, 4), C(5, 6) collinear?

Slope AB = (4 − 2)/(3 − 1) = 1. Slope BC = (6 − 4)/(5 − 3) = 1. Equal slopes + common point B ⇒ Collinear.

7.36 NCERT Exemplar — Selected Solved Problems

📝 EX-1. The points (1, 0), (5, 3), (2, 7), and (−2, 4) are vertices of a quadrilateral. Identify type.

AB = √(16 + 9) = 5. BC = √(9 + 16) = 5. CD = √(16 + 9) = 5. DA = √(9 + 16) = 5. All sides equal. AC = √(1 + 49) = √50. BD = √(49 + 1) = √50. Diagonals equal ⇒ Square.

📝 EX-2. Find the value of x for which the distance between P(2, −3) and Q(x, 5) is 10.

(x − 2)² + 64 = 100 ⇒ (x − 2)² = 36 ⇒ x − 2 = ±6 ⇒ x = 8 or −4.

📝 EX-3. The line segment joining A(−2, 9) and B(6, 3) is a diameter of a circle. Find centre.

Centre = midpoint = ((−2 + 6)/2, (9 + 3)/2) = (2, 6).

📝 EX-4. If A(−2, 2), B(8, −2), C(−4, −3) are vertices of triangle, prove it is right angled.

AB² = 100 + 16 = 116. BC² = 144 + 1 = 145. CA² = 4 + 25 = 29. Check: AB² + CA² = 116 + 29 = 145 = BC². ✓ Right angle at A.

📝 EX-5. Find ratio in which y-axis divides line joining (5, −6) and (−1, −4). Find point too.

On y-axis x = 0. Using section formula: 0 = (k(−1) + 1(5))/(k + 1) ⇒ −k + 5 = 0 ⇒ k = 5. Ratio = 5:1. y-coord = (5(−4) + (−6))/(6) = −26/6 = −13/3. Point: (0, −13/3).

📝 EX-6. Find ratio in which (−3, p) divides line joining (−5, −4) and (−2, 3). Find p.

x: −3 = (k(−2) + (−5))/(k + 1) ⇒ −3k − 3 = −2k − 5 ⇒ k = 2. Ratio = 2:1. p = (2(3) + (−4))/3 = 2/3.

📝 EX-7. Find centroid of triangle whose vertices are (3, −5), (−7, 4), (10, −2).

G = ((3 − 7 + 10)/3, (−5 + 4 − 2)/3) = (6/3, −3/3) = (2, −1).

📝 EX-8. The line segment joining points (3, −4) and (1, 2) is trisected at points P and Q. Find their coordinates.

P divides 1:2 ⇒ P = ((1(1) + 2(3))/3, (1(2) + 2(−4))/3) = (7/3, −6/3) = (7/3, −2).

Q divides 2:1 ⇒ Q = ((2(1) + 1(3))/3, (2(2) + 1(−4))/3) = (5/3, 0).

📝 EX-9. Find area of quadrilateral whose vertices are (−4, −2), (−3, −5), (3, −2), (2, 3).

Split into △ABC and △ACD. Area △ABC = (1/2)|−4(−5 + 2) + (−3)(−2 + 2) + 3(−2 + 5)| = (1/2)|12 + 0 + 9| = 21/2.

Area △ACD = (1/2)|−4(−2 − 3) + 3(3 + 2) + 2(−2 + 2)| = (1/2)|20 + 15 + 0| = 35/2.

Total = 21/2 + 35/2 = 56/2 = 28 sq units.

📝 EX-10. Show that A(3, 1), B(0, −2), C(1, 1) and D(4, 4) are vertices of a parallelogram.

Midpoint of AC = ((3 + 1)/2, (1 + 1)/2) = (2, 1). Midpoint of BD = ((0 + 4)/2, (−2 + 4)/2) = (2, 1). Diagonals bisect each other ⇒ Parallelogram. ✓

7.37 Application Marathon (AM-16 to AM-30)

📝 AM-16. A ladder reaches from base (0, 0) to top (6, 8) of a wall. Length?

= √(36 + 64) = 10 m.

📝 AM-17. Two villages at (0, 0) and (8, 6) km from city. Build a school equidistant from both.

Midpoint = (4, 3) km from city.

📝 AM-18. A treasure is at (5, 12). Distance from origin?

= 13.

📝 AM-19. A drone flies from (1, 2) to (4, 6). Air distance?

= √(9 + 16) = 5.

📝 AM-20. Survey post A(2, 4), B(8, 4), C(5, 10). Find centroid for radio tower location.

G = ((2 + 8 + 5)/3, (4 + 4 + 10)/3) = (5, 6).

📝 AM-21. Two cars at (0, 0) and (60, 80). Meeting point at midpoint?

(30, 40). Distance each travels = 50 units.

📝 AM-22. A garden has corners (0, 0), (10, 0), (10, 6), (0, 6). Area? Perimeter?

Rectangle 10 × 6. Area = 60. Perimeter = 32.

📝 AM-23. A triangular plot has corners (0, 0), (8, 0), (4, 6). Area?

= (1/2) × 8 × 6 = 24 sq units.

📝 AM-24. Park has fountain at (3, 4) and gate at (0, 0). Path length?

= 5 units.

📝 AM-25. Hostel A at (2, 2), Hostel B at (8, 2). Mess equidistant? Where?

Anywhere on perpendicular bisector x = 5. Midpoint = (5, 2).

📝 AM-26. A swimming pool's corners are (0, 0), (25, 0), (25, 10), (0, 10). Diagonal?

= √(625 + 100) = √725 ≈ 26.93 m.

📝 AM-27. A bridge connects (0, 0) to (300, 400). Length in metres?

= √(90000 + 160000) = √250000 = 500 m.

📝 AM-28. Center of mass of three equal weights at (1, 1), (4, 1), (1, 5)?

= centroid = (2, 7/3).

📝 AM-29. Plot of land (0, 0), (4, 0), (4, 3), (0, 3). Cost @ ₹100/sq unit?

Area = 12 sq units. Cost = ₹1200.

📝 AM-30. Robotic arm from (0, 0) needs to reach (7, 24). Reach distance?

= √(49 + 576) = 25 units.

7.38 Higher Order Practice (HOP-1 to HOP-10)

📝 HOP-1. Find a point on y-axis equidistant from (5, −2) and (−3, 2).

Point (0, y). (0 − 5)² + (y + 2)² = (0 + 3)² + (y − 2)² ⇒ 25 + y² + 4y + 4 = 9 + y² − 4y + 4 ⇒ 8y = −16 ⇒ y = −2. Point: (0, −2).

📝 HOP-2. Find a point on x-axis equidistant from (−2, 5) and (2, −3).

Point (x, 0). (x + 2)² + 25 = (x − 2)² + 9 ⇒ 4x + 4 + 25 = −4x + 4 + 9 ⇒ 8x = −16 ⇒ x = −2. Point: (−2, 0).

📝 HOP-3. The line joining (3, 4) and (7, 8) is divided by point P in ratio 1:3. Find P.

P = ((1(7) + 3(3))/4, (1(8) + 3(4))/4) = (16/4, 20/4) = (4, 5).

📝 HOP-4. If origin is the midpoint of segment from (a, b) to (c, d), find relation.

(a + c)/2 = 0 ⇒ a + c = 0. (b + d)/2 = 0 ⇒ b + d = 0. So c = −a, d = −b.

📝 HOP-5. Find k if (k, k − 2), (k + 2, k + 1), (k, k − 1) are vertices of a triangle of area 1.

(1/2)|k(k + 1 − k + 1) + (k + 2)(k − 1 − k + 2) + k(k − 2 − k − 1)| = 1.

= (1/2)|k(2) + (k + 2)(1) + k(−3)| = (1/2)|2k + k + 2 − 3k| = (1/2)|2| = 1. ✓ True for all k.

📝 HOP-6. Find point P on x-axis such that PA = PB where A(3, 2), B(5, −2).

P(x, 0). (x − 3)² + 4 = (x − 5)² + 4 ⇒ −6x + 9 = −10x + 25 ⇒ 4x = 16 ⇒ x = 4. P(4, 0).

📝 HOP-7. The centroid of triangle is (1, 4). Two vertices are (2, 6) and (4, 8). Find third.

(2 + 4 + x)/3 = 1 ⇒ x = −3. (6 + 8 + y)/3 = 4 ⇒ y = −2. Third vertex: (−3, −2).

📝 HOP-8. Find ratio in which x-axis divides segment joining (2, −3) and (5, 6).

On x-axis y = 0. 0 = (k(6) + 1(−3))/(k + 1) ⇒ 6k = 3 ⇒ k = 1/2. Ratio = 1:2.

📝 HOP-9. The points A(1, −2), B(2, 3), C(k, 2), D(−4, −3) form a parallelogram. Find k.

Diagonals bisect: midpoint AC = midpoint BD ⇒ ((1 + k)/2, 0) = ((2 − 4)/2, 0) = (−1, 0) ⇒ 1 + k = −2 ⇒ k = −3.

📝 HOP-10. Find area of triangle whose vertices are (t, t − 2), (t + 3, t), (t + 2, t + 2).

= (1/2)|t(t − t − 2) + (t + 3)(t + 2 − t + 2) + (t + 2)(t − 2 − t)|

= (1/2)|−2t + 4(t + 3) − 2(t + 2)| = (1/2)|−2t + 4t + 12 − 2t − 4| = (1/2)(8) = 4 sq units.

7.39 Mnemonics for Coordinate Geometry

🎯 Easy Memory Tricks

  • "Run before Rise" — x-coordinate (horizontal) comes before y-coordinate (vertical).
  • "DiSTance = √(Sum of sQuares)" — Distance = √[(Δx)² + (Δy)²].
  • "Mid-Mean" — Midpoint is the mean (average) of coordinates.
  • "Cent-Three" — Centroid divides each median in ratio 2:1 from vertex; uses sum/3.
  • "Section: m₁ x₂ + m₂ x₁" — In ratio m₁:m₂, the FIRST ratio multiplies SECOND point's coordinate.
  • "Area-Zero ⇒ Collinear" — If △ area = 0, all points lie on a straight line.

7.40 Common Pitfalls — Avoid These!

⚠️ Top 10 Errors Students Make

  1. Forgetting the square root in distance formula.
  2. Mixing up x and y while plotting.
  3. Wrong sign in section formula (m₁ x₂ + m₂ x₁, NOT m₁ x₁ + m₂ x₂).
  4. Forgetting absolute value in area formula.
  5. Using ratio in wrong order (1:2 vs 2:1).
  6. Treating external division like internal division.
  7. Confusing perpendicular bisector with median.
  8. Wrong quadrant: (−3, 2) is in Q-II, not Q-IV.
  9. Centroid formula uses sum/3, not sum/2.
  10. Forgetting that distance is always positive.

7.41 Final Self-Certification

📜 Certificate of Mastery — Chapter 7

I, __________________________, hereby certify that I have:

  • ☐ Read every section carefully.
  • ☐ Practiced all NCERT exercises.
  • ☐ Solved at least 50 problems from this chapter.
  • ☐ Can explain distance, section, centroid formulas without notes.
  • ☐ Identified all special quadrilaterals from coordinates.
  • ☐ Ready to attempt board exam questions confidently.

Signed: ______________    Date: ____________

🎉 Excellent! You've mastered Coordinate Geometry!

➡️ Get ready for Chapter 8 — Introduction to Trigonometry where you'll learn sin, cos, tan!

7.42 Mega Mixed Practice — Set A (MMA-1 to MMA-15)

MMA-1. Distance between (−5, 7) and (−1, 3)?

= √(16 + 16) = 4√2.

MMA-2. Find x if A(x, 5), B(2, 1) and distance is 5.

(x − 2)² + 16 = 25 ⇒ (x − 2)² = 9 ⇒ x = 5 or −1.

MMA-3. Find centroid of triangle (1, 4), (−1, −1), (3, 3).

= (1, 2).

MMA-4. Are (1, 2), (2, 4), (3, 6) collinear?

Area = (1/2)|1(4 − 6) + 2(6 − 2) + 3(2 − 4)| = (1/2)|−2 + 8 − 6| = 0. Yes, collinear.

MMA-5. Find midpoint of (−3, 8) and (5, −4).

= (1, 2).

MMA-6. Point that divides (−1, 7) and (4, −3) in 2:3 internally?

= ((2(4) + 3(−1))/5, (2(−3) + 3(7))/5) = (5/5, 15/5) = (1, 3).

MMA-7. Area of triangle (5, 2), (4, 7), (7, −4)?

= (1/2)|5(7 + 4) + 4(−4 − 2) + 7(2 − 7)| = (1/2)|55 − 24 − 35| = 2 sq units.

MMA-8. (3, 0), (4, 5), (−1, 4), (−2, −1) — find type of quadrilateral.

All sides = √26. Diagonals: AC = √41, BD = √41. All sides equal + diagonals equal ⇒ Square.

MMA-9. Find slope of line through (3, 5) and (5, 9).

= (9 − 5)/(5 − 3) = 4/2 = 2.

MMA-10. A point divides (5, 4) and (8, 7) in ratio 1:2. Find it.

= ((1(8) + 2(5))/3, (1(7) + 2(4))/3) = (18/3, 15/3) = (6, 5).

MMA-11. Midpoint of vertices A(1, 1) and C(5, 5) — diagonal of square ABCD. Find centre.

= (3, 3) — centre of square.

MMA-12. Find values of y so that distance between (2, −3) and (10, y) is 10.

64 + (y + 3)² = 100 ⇒ (y + 3)² = 36 ⇒ y = 3 or −9.

MMA-13. Find ratio in which y-axis divides line from (3, −4) to (−7, 5).

x = 0 ⇒ 0 = (k(−7) + 3)/(k + 1) ⇒ 7k = 3 ⇒ k = 3/7. Ratio = 3:7.

MMA-14. Find p if points (2, 1), (p, −1), (−1, 3) are collinear.

Area = 0 ⇒ 2(−1 − 3) + p(3 − 1) + (−1)(1 + 1) = 0 ⇒ −8 + 2p − 2 = 0 ⇒ p = 5.

MMA-15. Distance of (3, 4) from origin?

= 5.

7.43 One-Liner Revision Sheet

⭐ Memorize These for Board Exam

  • Distance: d = √[(x₂ − x₁)² + (y₂ − y₁)²]
  • Midpoint: M = ((x₁ + x₂)/2, (y₁ + y₂)/2)
  • Section (internal m:n): P = ((mx₂ + nx₁)/(m+n), (my₂ + ny₁)/(m+n))
  • Section (external m:n): P = ((mx₂ − nx₁)/(m−n), (my₂ − ny₁)/(m−n))
  • Centroid: G = ((x₁ + x₂ + x₃)/3, (y₁ + y₂ + y₃)/3)
  • Area: △ = (1/2)|x₁(y₂ − y₃) + x₂(y₃ − y₁) + x₃(y₁ − y₂)|
  • Collinear: Area = 0
  • Slope: m = (y₂ − y₁)/(x₂ − x₁)

🏁 Chapter 7 Coordinate Geometry — Complete!

You have built a strong foundation in coordinate geometry. Practice daily, and you will excel!

7.44 Mega Mixed Practice — Set B (MMB-1 to MMB-20)

MMB-1. Find distance between (0, 0) and (7, 24).

= √(49 + 576) = 25.

MMB-2. (a, 0) and (0, a) — midpoint?

(a/2, a/2).

MMB-3. Triangle vertices (0, 0), (4, 0), (0, 3). Area?

= 6 sq units.

MMB-4. Find slope of line ⊥ to line with slope 2/3.

= −3/2 (product = −1).

MMB-5. Distance from (3, 4) to (6, 8)?

= √(9 + 16) = 5.

MMB-6. Centroid of (0, 0), (6, 0), (0, 9)?

= (2, 3).

MMB-7. Point dividing (1, 2) and (7, 8) in 1:1?

= (4, 5) — midpoint.

MMB-8. Are (0, 0), (1, 1), (2, 2) collinear?

Yes — all on line y = x.

MMB-9. Distance from (−3, −4) to origin?

= 5.

MMB-10. Midpoint of (a, b) and (c, d)?

((a + c)/2, (b + d)/2).

MMB-11. Find x if (x, 0) divides line from (2, 4) to (6, −4) externally in 1:1.

External 1:1 is undefined (m − n = 0). Question invalid — point at infinity.

MMB-12. Show (2, 3), (4, 5), (6, 7), (8, 9) are collinear.

Slopes between consecutive points all = 1. Hence collinear.

MMB-13. Find y if midpoint of (3, 4) and (5, y) is (4, 6).

(4 + y)/2 = 6 ⇒ y = 8.

MMB-14. Diagonal of rectangle with vertices (0, 0), (6, 0), (6, 8), (0, 8)?

= √(36 + 64) = 10.

MMB-15. Centroid (4, 5). Two vertices (3, 7), (5, 5). Find third.

(3 + 5 + x)/3 = 4 ⇒ x = 4. (7 + 5 + y)/3 = 5 ⇒ y = 3. (4, 3).

MMB-16. If P(2, 3) divides AB in ratio 2:3 internally where A(1, 1), find B.

2 = (2·Bₓ + 3·1)/5 ⇒ 2Bₓ = 7 ⇒ Bₓ = 7/2. 3 = (2·Bᵧ + 3)/5 ⇒ Bᵧ = 6. B = (7/2, 6).

MMB-17. Distance between (cos θ, sin θ) and (sin θ, −cos θ)?

= √[(cos θ − sin θ)² + (sin θ + cos θ)²] = √[2(sin²θ + cos²θ)] = √2.

MMB-18. The point (a, b) lies in Q-III. Sign of a and b?

Both negative.

MMB-19. Area of triangle with vertices (a, b + c), (b, c + a), (c, a + b)?

= (1/2)|a(c + a − a − b) + b(a + b − b − c) + c(b + c − c − a)| = (1/2)|a(c − b) + b(a − c) + c(b − a)| = 0. Collinear!

MMB-20. Find p if (p, p), (2, 5), (4, 7) are collinear.

Slope of (2, 5) and (4, 7) = 1. So slope from (p, p) to (2, 5) = (5 − p)/(2 − p) = 1 ⇒ 5 − p = 2 − p ⇒ 5 = 2 (contradiction). No such p UNLESS we use other pair: slope (p, p)→(4, 7) = (7 − p)/(4 − p) = 1 ⇒ p = 3.

✨ End of Chapter 7

Practice these problems daily for two weeks before your exam. You'll be unstoppable!

7.45 Last-Minute Revision Card

🎯 Day Before Exam — 10 Things to Remember

  1. Plot carefully — x first, y second.
  2. Distance formula always has square root.
  3. Section formula internal: (mx₂ + nx₁)/(m+n).
  4. Midpoint is special section ratio 1:1.
  5. Centroid uses ÷3, not ÷2.
  6. Area formula always has 1/2 and absolute value.
  7. Collinear: area = 0 OR slopes equal.
  8. Square: all sides equal + diagonals equal.
  9. Rhombus: all sides equal but diagonals not equal.
  10. Rectangle: opposite sides equal + diagonals equal.

💪 You've completed Chapter 7. Move forward with confidence!

7.46 Bridge to Chapter 8

🔗 Connecting Coordinate Geometry to Trigonometry

In Chapter 8, you will study Trigonometry — the study of right-angled triangles. Coordinate Geometry helps here too! If a point P(x, y) is on the unit circle (radius 1), then:

  • x = cos θ
  • y = sin θ
  • tan θ = y/x

So distance from origin to (cos θ, sin θ) = √(cos²θ + sin²θ) = √1 = 1. ✓ This is the basic identity!

📚 What's coming in Chapter 8

  • Definitions of sin, cos, tan, cosec, sec, cot.
  • Trigonometric ratios of 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°.
  • Complementary angles: sin(90° − θ) = cos θ.
  • Identities: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1.

🚀 Ready? Let's go to Trigonometry!

✅ Chapter 7 Coordinate Geometry — Officially Complete!

Total formulas mastered: 8 | Total problems solved: 100+

"Tri-go-no-metry" = three-angle-measure! Master sin, cos, tan and unlock right-triangle magic.

  • 8.7 Trig Ratios for 0° and 90°

  • 8.10 Complementary Angles
  • 8.11 Trigonometric Identities
  • 8.12 Proving Identities
  • 8.13 Worked Examples (Set 2)

  • 8.15 NCERT Ex 8.2 — Solved

  • 8.19 Assertion–Reason
  • 8.20 HOTS Problems

  • 8.22 PYQ Questions

  • 8.24 Glossary + Mind Map + Recap

8.1 What is Trigonometry?

Origin of the Word

The word "Trigonometry" comes from three Greek words:

  • Tri = Three
  • Gonia = Angle
  • Metron = Measure

So Trigonometry means "measurement of three angles" — the science of triangles, especially right triangles!

Why study Trigonometry?

Trigonometry helps us:

  • 🏔️ Find heights of mountains and tall buildings WITHOUT climbing them.
  • 🚀 Calculate distances of stars and planets.
  • 🌊 Predict tides and waves.
  • 📡 Design satellite orbits and GPS systems.
  • 🏗️ Build bridges, ramps, and roofs.
  • 🎵 Even understand sound waves and music!

💡 Fun Fact: The famous mathematician Aryabhata (476 AD) was the first to use sine (called "jya") in his book Aryabhatiya!

8.2 The Right Triangle Revisited

Parts of a Right Triangle

A right triangle has one angle = 90°. With respect to an acute angle θ (theta), we name the three sides:

  • Hypotenuse (H) — Always the longest side, OPPOSITE to the right angle.
  • Opposite (P) — The side OPPOSITE to angle θ. Also called Perpendicular.
  • Adjacent (B) — The side NEXT to angle θ (other than hypotenuse). Also called Base.

Right triangle with angle θ at bottom-left, right angle at bottom-right.

⚠️ IMPORTANT: "Opposite" and "Adjacent" CHANGE depending on which acute angle you choose! The hypotenuse stays the same.

8.3 The Six Trigonometric Ratios

The Three Basic Ratios

sin θ = Opposite / Hypotenuse = P / H

cos θ = Adjacent / Hypotenuse = B / H

tan θ = Opposite / Adjacent = P / B

Memorize as SOH-CAH-TOA:

  • Sin = Opp / Hyp ⇒ SOH
  • Cos = Adj / Hyp ⇒ CAH
  • Tan = Opp / Adj ⇒ TOA

The Three Reciprocal Ratios

cosec θ = 1 / sin θ = H / P

sec θ = 1 / cos θ = H / B

cot θ = 1 / tan θ = B / P

Note: cosec is sometimes written as csc.

🧠 Memory Trick: The reciprocals follow a "third letter" rule:

  • Sin ↔ Cosec (third letters s and c)
  • Cos ↔ Sec
  • Tan ↔ Cot

8.4 Relationships Between Ratios

⭐ Key Relations

  • tan θ = sin θ / cos θ
  • cot θ = cos θ / sin θ
  • sin θ × cosec θ = 1
  • cos θ × sec θ = 1
  • tan θ × cot θ = 1

📝 Example: In △ABC right-angled at B, AB = 24 cm, BC = 7 cm. Find sin A, cos A, tan A.

AC (hypotenuse) = √(24² + 7²) = √(576 + 49) = √625 = 25.

For angle A: Opposite = BC = 7, Adjacent = AB = 24, Hypotenuse = AC = 25.

  • sin A = 7/25
  • cos A = 24/25
  • tan A = 7/24

8.5 Trig Ratios of 30°, 45°, 60° (Standard Angles)

Why these angles?

30°, 45°, 60° appear so often in geometry that we memorize their exact values. They come from two special triangles:

  • 45°–45°–90° triangle (isosceles right triangle).
  • 30°–60°–90° triangle (half of equilateral triangle).

Deriving 45° values

Take a square with side 1. Diagonal = √2. Cut diagonally — we get a 45°–45°–90° triangle with legs = 1, hypotenuse = √2.

sin 45° = 1/√2, cos 45° = 1/√2, tan 45° = 1.

Deriving 30° and 60° values

Take an equilateral triangle of side 2. Drop perpendicular from one vertex — we get a 30°–60°–90° triangle with sides 1, √3, 2.

sin 30° = 1/2, cos 30° = √3/2, tan 30° = 1/√3.

sin 60° = √3/2, cos 60° = 1/2, tan 60° = √3.

8.6 Trig Ratios of 0° and 90°

  • sin 0° = 0, cos 0° = 1, tan 0° = 0
  • sin 90° = 1, cos 90° = 0, tan 90° = undefined (∞)
