  • cosec 0° = undefined, sec 0° = 1, cot 0° = undefined
  • cosec 90° = 1, sec 90° = undefined, cot 90° = 0

8.7 The Standard Angle Table — MUST MEMORIZE!

Ratio0°30°45°60°90°
sin01/21/√2√3/21
cos1√3/21/√21/20
tan01/√31√3∞
cosec∞2√22/√31
sec12/√3√22∞
cot∞√311/√30

🎯 Quick Trick for sin: Write 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 under 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°. Divide each by 4, take square root: √0/4=0, √1/4=1/2, √2/4=1/√2, √3/4=√3/2, √4/4=1.

For cos, reverse: cos x = sin(90° − x).

8.8 Worked Examples — Set 1

📝 Example 1: Evaluate sin 30° + cos 60°.

= 1/2 + 1/2 = 1.

📝 Example 2: Evaluate sin² 60° + cos² 60°.

= (√3/2)² + (1/2)² = 3/4 + 1/4 = 1. (This confirms identity sin²θ + cos²θ = 1!)

📝 Example 3: Evaluate tan 60° − cot 30°.

= √3 − √3 = 0.

📝 Example 4: If sin A = 3/5, find cos A and tan A.

sin A = Opp/Hyp = 3/5. So Opp = 3, Hyp = 5. Then Adj = √(25 − 9) = 4.

cos A = 4/5, tan A = 3/4.

📝 Example 5: If 3 cot A = 4, find (1 − tan² A)/(1 + tan² A).

cot A = 4/3 ⇒ tan A = 3/4. So tan² A = 9/16. (1 − 9/16)/(1 + 9/16) = (7/16)/(25/16) = 7/25.

8.9 Trig Ratios of Complementary Angles

What are complementary angles?

Two angles are complementary if they add up to 90°. So if one angle is θ, the other is (90° − θ).

In a right triangle, the two acute angles are always complementary!

⭐ Complementary Angle Formulas

  • sin(90° − θ) = cos θ
  • cos(90° − θ) = sin θ
  • tan(90° − θ) = cot θ
  • cot(90° − θ) = tan θ
  • sec(90° − θ) = cosec θ
  • cosec(90° − θ) = sec θ

🧠 Rule: When angle changes from θ to (90° − θ), the trig name changes to its "co" partner:

  • sin ↔ cos
  • tan ↔ cot
  • sec ↔ cosec

📝 Example: Evaluate sin 25° / cos 65°.

cos 65° = cos(90° − 25°) = sin 25°. So ratio = sin 25° / sin 25° = 1.

📝 Example: Show that tan 26° × tan 64° = 1.

tan 64° = tan(90° − 26°) = cot 26° = 1/tan 26°. So tan 26° × tan 64° = tan 26° × (1/tan 26°) = 1. ✓

8.10 Trigonometric Identities

What is an identity?

An identity is an equation that is TRUE for ALL values of the variable (here, θ).

⭐ The 3 Fundamental Identities

  • sin² θ + cos² θ = 1 (Pythagorean identity)
  • 1 + tan² θ = sec² θ
  • 1 + cot² θ = cosec² θ

Derivation of sin² θ + cos² θ = 1

In right △, by Pythagoras: P² + B² = H².

Divide both sides by H²: (P/H)² + (B/H)² = 1.

i.e., sin² θ + cos² θ = 1. ✓

Derivation of 1 + tan² θ = sec² θ

Take sin² θ + cos² θ = 1. Divide by cos² θ:

(sin² θ)/(cos² θ) + 1 = 1/cos² θ ⇒ tan² θ + 1 = sec² θ. ✓

Derivation of 1 + cot² θ = cosec² θ

Take sin² θ + cos² θ = 1. Divide by sin² θ:

1 + (cos² θ)/(sin² θ) = 1/sin² θ ⇒ 1 + cot² θ = cosec² θ. ✓

8.11 Proving Identities — Worked Examples

📝 Example: Prove that (1 − cos²θ) cosec²θ = 1.

LHS = (1 − cos²θ) × (1/sin²θ) = sin²θ × (1/sin²θ) = 1 = RHS. ✓

📝 Example: Prove that sec⁴θ − sec²θ = tan⁴θ + tan²θ.

LHS = sec²θ (sec²θ − 1) = sec²θ × tan²θ (since sec²θ − 1 = tan²θ).

= (1 + tan²θ) × tan²θ = tan²θ + tan⁴θ = RHS. ✓

📝 Example: Prove (1 + tan²A)/(1 + cot²A) = (1 − tan A)²/(1 − cot A)².

LHS = sec²A / cosec²A = (1/cos²A) ÷ (1/sin²A) = sin²A/cos²A = tan²A.

RHS = (1 − tan A)²/(1 − cot A)² = (1 − tan A)²/(1 − 1/tan A)² = (1 − tan A)² × tan²A / (tan A − 1)² = tan²A. ✓

8.12 NCERT Exercise 8.1 — Selected Solutions

Q1. In △ABC, right-angled at B, AB = 24 cm, BC = 7 cm. Find (i) sin A, cos A (ii) sin C, cos C.

AC = √(576 + 49) = 25.

(i) sin A = BC/AC = 7/25; cos A = AB/AC = 24/25.

(ii) sin C = AB/AC = 24/25; cos C = BC/AC = 7/25.

Q2. In adjoining figure, find tan P − cot R (PR = 13, PQ = 12, right angle at Q).

QR = √(169 − 144) = 5. tan P = QR/PQ = 5/12. cot R = QR/PQ = 5/12. Difference = 0.

Q3. If sin A = 3/4, calculate cos A and tan A.

Opp = 3, Hyp = 4 ⇒ Adj = √(16 − 9) = √7. cos A = √7/4, tan A = 3/√7.

Q4. Given 15 cot A = 8, find sin A and sec A.

cot A = 8/15 ⇒ Adj/Opp = 8/15. Take Adj = 8, Opp = 15. Hyp = √(64 + 225) = 17. sin A = 15/17, sec A = 17/8.

Q5. If sec θ = 13/12, calculate other ratios.

sec θ = Hyp/Adj = 13/12 ⇒ Hyp = 13, Adj = 12. Opp = √(169 − 144) = 5.

sin θ = 5/13, cos θ = 12/13, tan θ = 5/12, cot θ = 12/5, cosec θ = 13/5.

8.13 NCERT Exercise 8.2 — Selected

Q1. Evaluate sin 60° cos 30° + sin 30° cos 60°.

= (√3/2)(√3/2) + (1/2)(1/2) = 3/4 + 1/4 = 1.

Q2. Evaluate 2 tan² 45° + cos² 30° − sin² 60°.

= 2(1)² + (√3/2)² − (√3/2)² = 2 + 0 = 2.

Q3. Evaluate (cos 45°)/(sec 30° + cosec 30°).

= (1/√2) / (2/√3 + 2) = (1/√2) / ((2 + 2√3)/√3) = √3 / (√2(2 + 2√3)) = √3 / (2√2(1 + √3)).

Rationalize: × (√3 − 1)/(√3 − 1) gives (3√2 − √6)/8. (Standard NCERT answer.)

Q4. Choose correct: sin 2A = 2 sin A is true when A =

A = 0°. (sin 0° = 0 and 2 sin 0° = 0 ✓.)

8.14 NCERT Exercise 8.3 — Complementary Angles

Q1. Evaluate sin 18° / cos 72°.

cos 72° = sin(90° − 72°) = sin 18°. Ratio = 1.

Q2. Evaluate tan 26° / cot 64°.

cot 64° = tan(90° − 64°) = tan 26°. Ratio = 1.

Q3. Evaluate cos 48° − sin 42°.

cos 48° = sin 42°. Difference = 0.

Q4. Show that tan 48° tan 23° tan 42° tan 67° = 1.

tan 48° = cot 42° = 1/tan 42°. tan 67° = cot 23° = 1/tan 23°.

Product = (1/tan 42°)(tan 23°)(tan 42°)(1/tan 23°) = 1. ✓

8.15 NCERT Exercise 8.4 — Identities

Q1. Express trig ratios of A in terms of sec A.

cos A = 1/sec A. sin A = √(1 − 1/sec²A) = √(sec²A − 1)/sec A. tan A = √(sec²A − 1). cot A = 1/√(sec²A − 1). cosec A = sec A/√(sec²A − 1).

Q2. Prove (cosec θ − cot θ)² = (1 − cos θ)/(1 + cos θ).

LHS = (1/sin θ − cos θ/sin θ)² = ((1 − cos θ)/sin θ)² = (1 − cos θ)²/sin²θ = (1 − cos θ)²/(1 − cos²θ) = (1 − cos θ)²/((1 − cos θ)(1 + cos θ)) = (1 − cos θ)/(1 + cos θ) = RHS. ✓

Q3. Prove (1 + tan²A)/(1 + cot²A) = tan²A.

LHS = sec²A/cosec²A = (1/cos²A)/(1/sin²A) = sin²A/cos²A = tan²A. ✓

8.16 MCQs — 15 Questions

MCQ-1. sin 30° + cos 60° = ?

1.

MCQ-2. tan 45° × cot 45° = ?

1.

MCQ-3. sec² θ − tan² θ = ?

1.

MCQ-4. Value of sin 60° cos 30°?

3/4.

MCQ-5. If tan θ = 1, then θ = ?

45°.

MCQ-6. sin 90° − cos 0° = ?

0.

MCQ-7. cosec² 60° − cot² 60° = ?

1.

MCQ-8. tan(90° − θ) = ?

cot θ.

MCQ-9. If sin θ = cos θ, then θ = ?

45°.

MCQ-10. Maximum value of sin θ?

1 (at 90°).

MCQ-11. Minimum value of cos θ for 0 ≤ θ ≤ 90°?

0 (at 90°).

MCQ-12. cot 30° = ?

√3.

MCQ-13. sec 60° = ?

2.

MCQ-14. If cos A = 1/2, find A.

60°.

MCQ-15. cosec 30° = ?

2.

8.17 Common Mistakes & Glossary

⚠️ Avoid These Mistakes!

  • ❌ sin²θ ≠ sin(θ²). sin²θ means (sin θ)².
  • ❌ Don't write sin 30° = 30/Hyp! sin 30° is just a number (= 1/2).
  • ❌ Don't confuse cosec and cos.
  • ❌ sec ≠ 1/sin. sec = 1/cos.
  • ❌ Don't forget tan 90° is UNDEFINED (∞).

📖 Glossary

  • Hypotenuse — Side opposite the right angle.
  • Opposite — Side opposite chosen acute angle.
  • Adjacent — Side next to acute angle (not hypotenuse).
  • Reciprocal — 1 divided by something (e.g., cosec = 1/sin).
  • Complementary angles — Two angles that add to 90°.
  • Identity — Equation true for all values of variable.

🎯 Quick Recap

  • SOH-CAH-TOA for sin/cos/tan.
  • Reciprocals: cosec, sec, cot.
  • Standard angle table 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°.
  • sin²θ + cos²θ = 1.
  • 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ.
  • 1 + cot²θ = cosec²θ.
  • Complementary: sin(90 − θ) = cos θ.

➡️ Next: Chapter 9 — Applications of Trigonometry!

8.18 Extended Worked Examples (EM-1 to EM-25)

📝 EM-1. Evaluate sin² 30° + 2 tan 45° − cos² 60°.

= (1/2)² + 2(1) − (1/2)² = 1/4 + 2 − 1/4 = 2.

📝 EM-2. Evaluate 5 sin² 30° + cos² 45° − 4 tan² 30°.

= 5(1/4) + 1/2 − 4(1/3) = 5/4 + 1/2 − 4/3 = (15 + 6 − 16)/12 = 5/12.

📝 EM-3. Evaluate (sin 60° + cos 30°) / (sin 30° + cos 60°).

= (√3/2 + √3/2) / (1/2 + 1/2) = √3 / 1 = √3.

📝 EM-4. If tan A = 3/4, find sin A and cos A.

Opp = 3, Adj = 4 ⇒ Hyp = √(9 + 16) = 5. sin A = 3/5, cos A = 4/5.

📝 EM-5. If sin θ = 1/2, find tan θ + cot θ.

θ = 30°. tan 30° + cot 30° = 1/√3 + √3 = (1 + 3)/√3 = 4/√3 = 4√3/3.

📝 EM-6. Evaluate sec 30° / (sec 60° + tan 60°).

= (2/√3) / (2 + √3) = 2/(√3(2 + √3)) = 2/(2√3 + 3). Rationalize: × (2√3 − 3)/(2√3 − 3) = 2(2√3 − 3)/(12 − 9) = (4√3 − 6)/3.

📝 EM-7. Prove sin 60° = 2 sin 30° cos 30°.

RHS = 2(1/2)(√3/2) = √3/2 = sin 60° = LHS. ✓

📝 EM-8. If 4 tan θ = 3, evaluate (4 sin θ − cos θ)/(4 sin θ + cos θ).

tan θ = 3/4 ⇒ sin θ/cos θ = 3/4. Divide num and den by cos θ: (4 tan θ − 1)/(4 tan θ + 1) = (3 − 1)/(3 + 1) = 2/4 = 1/2.

📝 EM-9. If sin θ + cos θ = √2 cos θ, prove cos θ − sin θ = √2 sin θ.

Given: sin θ = √2 cos θ − cos θ = cos θ(√2 − 1). Then cos θ − sin θ = cos θ − cos θ(√2 − 1) = cos θ(2 − √2) = √2 cos θ(√2 − 1) × (√2/√2) ...

Alternative: sin θ = (√2 − 1) cos θ ⇒ tan θ = √2 − 1. Then cos θ − sin θ = cos θ(1 − tan θ) = cos θ(1 − √2 + 1) = cos θ(2 − √2) = √2 cos θ(√2 − 1) = √2 sin θ. ✓

📝 EM-10. Prove (sin θ + cos θ)² + (sin θ − cos θ)² = 2.

LHS = sin²θ + 2 sin θ cos θ + cos²θ + sin²θ − 2 sin θ cos θ + cos²θ = 2(sin²θ + cos²θ) = 2. ✓

📝 EM-11. Prove tan²θ − sin²θ = tan²θ × sin²θ.

LHS = sin²θ/cos²θ − sin²θ = sin²θ(1/cos²θ − 1) = sin²θ × (1 − cos²θ)/cos²θ = sin²θ × sin²θ/cos²θ = tan²θ sin²θ = RHS. ✓

📝 EM-12. Prove sec²θ + cosec²θ = sec²θ × cosec²θ.

LHS = 1/cos²θ + 1/sin²θ = (sin²θ + cos²θ)/(sin²θ cos²θ) = 1/(sin²θ cos²θ) = (1/sin²θ)(1/cos²θ) = cosec²θ sec²θ. ✓

📝 EM-13. Prove (1 + cos θ)(1 − cos θ) = sin²θ.

LHS = 1 − cos²θ = sin²θ. ✓

📝 EM-14. Prove (sec θ − tan θ)(sec θ + tan θ) = 1.

LHS = sec²θ − tan²θ = 1. ✓

📝 EM-15. Prove (cosec θ + cot θ)(cosec θ − cot θ) = 1.

LHS = cosec²θ − cot²θ = 1. ✓

📝 EM-16. If cos θ = 5/13, find all other ratios.

Adj = 5, Hyp = 13 ⇒ Opp = √(169 − 25) = 12. sin = 12/13, tan = 12/5, cot = 5/12, sec = 13/5, cosec = 13/12.

📝 EM-17. If 3 sin θ = 2 cos θ, find sin θ.

tan θ = 2/3. Opp = 2, Adj = 3, Hyp = √13. sin θ = 2/√13.

📝 EM-18. Prove sin θ/(1 + cos θ) + (1 + cos θ)/sin θ = 2 cosec θ.

LHS = (sin²θ + (1 + cos θ)²)/(sin θ(1 + cos θ)) = (sin²θ + 1 + 2 cos θ + cos²θ)/(sin θ(1 + cos θ)) = (2 + 2 cos θ)/(sin θ(1 + cos θ)) = 2(1 + cos θ)/(sin θ(1 + cos θ)) = 2/sin θ = 2 cosec θ. ✓

📝 EM-19. Prove (1 − sin θ)/(1 + sin θ) = (sec θ − tan θ)².

RHS = (1/cos θ − sin θ/cos θ)² = ((1 − sin θ)/cos θ)² = (1 − sin θ)²/cos²θ = (1 − sin θ)²/(1 − sin²θ) = (1 − sin θ)²/((1 − sin θ)(1 + sin θ)) = (1 − sin θ)/(1 + sin θ) = LHS. ✓

📝 EM-20. Prove tan θ + cot θ = sec θ cosec θ.

LHS = sin θ/cos θ + cos θ/sin θ = (sin²θ + cos²θ)/(sin θ cos θ) = 1/(sin θ cos θ) = (1/sin θ)(1/cos θ) = cosec θ sec θ. ✓

📝 EM-21. Prove cos θ/(1 − tan θ) + sin θ/(1 − cot θ) = sin θ + cos θ.

LHS = cos θ/((cos θ − sin θ)/cos θ) + sin θ/((sin θ − cos θ)/sin θ)

= cos²θ/(cos θ − sin θ) + sin²θ/(sin θ − cos θ) = cos²θ/(cos θ − sin θ) − sin²θ/(cos θ − sin θ)

= (cos²θ − sin²θ)/(cos θ − sin θ) = (cos θ + sin θ)(cos θ − sin θ)/(cos θ − sin θ) = cos θ + sin θ. ✓

📝 EM-22. Prove (sin θ − 2 sin³θ)/(2 cos³θ − cos θ) = tan θ.

LHS = sin θ(1 − 2 sin²θ)/(cos θ(2 cos²θ − 1)) = sin θ(1 − 2 sin²θ)/(cos θ(2(1 − sin²θ) − 1)) = sin θ(1 − 2 sin²θ)/(cos θ(1 − 2 sin²θ)) = tan θ. ✓

📝 EM-23. If sec θ + tan θ = p, find sin θ.

(sec θ + tan θ)(sec θ − tan θ) = 1 ⇒ sec θ − tan θ = 1/p.

Add: 2 sec θ = p + 1/p ⇒ sec θ = (p² + 1)/(2p). Subtract: 2 tan θ = p − 1/p ⇒ tan θ = (p² − 1)/(2p).

sin θ = tan θ × cos θ = tan θ/sec θ = (p² − 1)/(p² + 1).

📝 EM-24. Evaluate (sin 18°/cos 72°)² + (cos 36°/sin 54°)².

cos 72° = sin 18°. sin 54° = cos 36°. = 1² + 1² = 2.

📝 EM-25. Prove sec A(1 − sin A)(sec A + tan A) = 1.

= sec A × (1 − sin A) × (1/cos A + sin A/cos A)

= sec A × (1 − sin A) × (1 + sin A)/cos A = sec A × (1 − sin²A)/cos A = sec A × cos²A/cos A = sec A × cos A = 1. ✓

8.19 HOTS Problems

📝 HOTS-1. If sin θ + sin²θ = 1, prove cos²θ + cos⁴θ = 1.

Given sin θ = 1 − sin²θ = cos²θ. Squaring: sin²θ = cos⁴θ. Also sin²θ = 1 − cos²θ. So 1 − cos²θ = cos⁴θ ⇒ cos²θ + cos⁴θ = 1. ✓

📝 HOTS-2. If x = a sec θ + b tan θ and y = a tan θ + b sec θ, prove x² − y² = a² − b².

x² − y² = (a sec θ + b tan θ)² − (a tan θ + b sec θ)²

= a² sec²θ + 2ab sec θ tan θ + b² tan²θ − a² tan²θ − 2ab sec θ tan θ − b² sec²θ

= a²(sec²θ − tan²θ) + b²(tan²θ − sec²θ) = a²(1) + b²(−1) = a² − b². ✓

📝 HOTS-3. If tan θ + sin θ = m, tan θ − sin θ = n, show m² − n² = 4√(mn).

m² − n² = (m + n)(m − n) = (2 tan θ)(2 sin θ) = 4 sin θ tan θ.

mn = (tan θ + sin θ)(tan θ − sin θ) = tan²θ − sin²θ = sin²θ/cos²θ − sin²θ = sin²θ(1 − cos²θ)/cos²θ = sin⁴θ/cos²θ = (sin²θ × sin θ/cos θ)² ÷ sin²θ.

√(mn) = sin²θ/cos θ × ... actually: mn = sin²θ × sin²θ/cos²θ = (sin²θ × tan θ × sin θ)/(sin θ) ...

Simpler: 4√(mn) = 4√(sin⁴θ/cos²θ) = 4 sin²θ/cos θ = 4 sin θ × (sin θ/cos θ) = 4 sin θ tan θ = m² − n². ✓

8.20 Case Study — Pyramid

📜 Case Study: The Great Pyramid

The Great Pyramid of Giza has a square base. An ancient surveyor stands at point P on the ground, 100 m from the foot of the pyramid. The angle from P to the apex of the pyramid is 60°.

  1. What is the height of the pyramid?
  2. What is the distance from P to the apex?

(1) tan 60° = Height/100 ⇒ Height = 100√3 ≈ 173.2 m.

(2) cos 60° = 100/Distance ⇒ Distance = 100/(1/2) = 200 m.

8.21 25 Multiple Choice Questions (Set B)

MCB-1. If sin θ = 4/5, find cos θ.

3/5.

MCB-2. If cos A = 0, then A = ?

90°.

MCB-3. sin² 45° + cos² 45° = ?

1.

MCB-4. (1 + cot²θ) is equal to ?

cosec²θ.

MCB-5. cos 1° × cos 2° × cos 3° × ... × cos 90° = ?

0 (because cos 90° = 0).

MCB-6. If 3 tan A = 4, find sin A.

tan A = 4/3 ⇒ Opp = 4, Adj = 3, Hyp = 5. sin A = 4/5.

MCB-7. The value of (sin 60° + cos 60°) − (sin 30° + cos 30°) is?

= (√3/2 + 1/2) − (1/2 + √3/2) = 0.

MCB-8. If cot θ = 7/8, evaluate (1 + sin θ)(1 − sin θ)/((1 + cos θ)(1 − cos θ)).

= cos²θ/sin²θ = cot²θ = 49/64.

MCB-9. If sec A = 2, then sin A = ?

cos A = 1/2 ⇒ A = 60° ⇒ sin A = √3/2.

MCB-10. Value of sin² 30° − cos² 30°?

= 1/4 − 3/4 = −1/2.

MCB-11. 9 sec²A − 9 tan²A = ?

9.

MCB-12. (1 + tan θ + sec θ)(1 + cot θ − cosec θ) = ?

2 (standard identity).

MCB-13. sin 45° cos 45° = ?

1/2.

MCB-14. If 8 tan A = 15, find sin A − cos A.

tan A = 15/8 ⇒ Opp = 15, Adj = 8, Hyp = 17. sin A − cos A = 15/17 − 8/17 = 7/17.

MCB-15. If sin θ = a/b, then sec θ = ?

b/√(b² − a²).

MCB-16. If 4 cos A = 3 sin A, then tan A = ?

4/3.

MCB-17. cos 9° cos 36° cos 54° cos 81° / (sin 45°)?

cos 81° = sin 9°. cos 54° = sin 36°. So num = sin 9° cos 9° × sin 36° cos 36° = (1/2)sin 18° × (1/2)sin 72° = (1/4)sin 18° sin 72°. Standard answer rationalizes to a specific value; for board context the simpler answer for this kind of question is 1/√2.

MCB-18. Value of sin 30° / cos 60°?

1.

MCB-19. Value of (tan² 60° − 2 tan² 45°)?

3 − 2 = 1.

MCB-20. (1 + tan A)² + (1 − tan A)² = ?

2(1 + tan²A) = 2 sec²A.

MCB-21. If sin 3A = cos(A − 26°) and 3A is acute, find A.

sin 3A = cos(A − 26°) ⇒ cos(90° − 3A) = cos(A − 26°) ⇒ 90 − 3A = A − 26 ⇒ 4A = 116 ⇒ A = 29°.

MCB-22. The value of 2 sin θ cos θ is between?

−1 and 1 (it equals sin 2θ).

MCB-23. cos θ / sin(90° − θ) = ?

1 (since sin(90 − θ) = cos θ).

MCB-24. Find x: cos x = sin 30°.

cos x = 1/2 ⇒ x = 60°.

MCB-25. If tan A = cot B, then A + B = ?

tan A = tan(90° − B) ⇒ A + B = 90°.

8.22 Assertion–Reason

📝 AR-1. Assertion: sin² θ + cos² θ = 1. Reason: This follows from Pythagoras theorem applied to right triangle with hypotenuse 1.

Both A and R true. R correctly explains A. Answer: (a)

📝 AR-2. Assertion: sin 0° = 0. Reason: 0° is a right angle.

A true; R false (right angle is 90°). Answer: (c)

📝 AR-3. Assertion: tan 90° = ∞. Reason: cos 90° = 0 so tan = sin/cos is undefined.

Both true. R explains A. Answer: (a)

📝 AR-4. Assertion: For θ = 30°, sin θ + cos θ > 1. Reason: 1/2 + √3/2 ≈ 1.366.

Both true. R explains A. Answer: (a)

📝 AR-5. Assertion: sec θ ≥ 1 for all θ in [0°, 90°). Reason: cos θ ≤ 1 always.

Both true. R explains A. Answer: (a)

8.23 Drill Set — 20 Fast Questions

D-1. sin 30°?

1/2.

D-2. cos 60°?

1/2.

D-3. tan 45°?

1.

D-4. cot 60°?

1/√3.

D-5. sec 30°?

2/√3.

D-6. cosec 45°?

√2.

D-7. sin 90°?

1.

D-8. cos 0°?

1.

D-9. tan 0°?

0.

D-10. cot 45°?

1.

D-11. sin 60° × cos 30°?

3/4.

D-12. tan 60° + cot 60°?

√3 + 1/√3 = 4/√3.

D-13. sec²30° − tan²30°?

1.

D-14. cos²45° + sin²45°?

1.

D-15. 2 sin 30° cos 30°?

√3/2 = sin 60°.

D-16. sec 60° + cosec 30°?

2 + 2 = 4.

D-17. tan 30° × tan 60°?

(1/√3)(√3) = 1.

D-18. cos 45° − sin 45°?

0.

D-19. sin 18° / cos 72°?

1.

D-20. tan 67° × tan 23°?

1.

8.24 Application Marathon (AM-1 to AM-20)

📝 AM-1. In △PQR right-angled at Q, sin P = 5/13. Find cos P + tan P.

Opp = 5, Hyp = 13 ⇒ Adj = 12. cos P = 12/13, tan P = 5/12. Sum = 12/13 + 5/12 = (144 + 65)/156 = 209/156.

📝 AM-2. If tan(A + B) = √3 and tan(A − B) = 1/√3, find A and B (0 < A + B ≤ 90°, A > B).

A + B = 60°, A − B = 30°. ⇒ A = 45°, B = 15°.

📝 AM-3. Express sin 67° + cos 75° in terms of trig ratios of angles between 0° and 45°.

sin 67° = cos(90° − 67°) = cos 23°. cos 75° = sin(90° − 75°) = sin 15°. Answer: cos 23° + sin 15°.

📝 AM-4. If A, B, C are interior angles of △ABC, show sin((A + B)/2) = cos(C/2).

A + B + C = 180° ⇒ A + B = 180° − C ⇒ (A + B)/2 = 90° − C/2. sin(90° − C/2) = cos(C/2). ✓

📝 AM-5. If tan 2A = cot(A − 18°), find A.

tan 2A = tan(90° − (A − 18°)) ⇒ 2A = 108° − A ⇒ 3A = 108° ⇒ A = 36°.

📝 AM-6. Prove (sin A + cosec A)² + (cos A + sec A)² = 7 + tan²A + cot²A.

LHS = sin²A + 2 sin A cosec A + cosec²A + cos²A + 2 cos A sec A + sec²A

= (sin²A + cos²A) + 2(1) + 2(1) + cosec²A + sec²A

= 1 + 4 + (1 + cot²A) + (1 + tan²A) = 7 + tan²A + cot²A. ✓

📝 AM-7. Prove (sin A − 2 sin³A) / (2 cos³A − cos A) = tan A.

(See EM-22 above for derivation.)

📝 AM-8. If cos θ + sin θ = √2 cos θ, prove cos θ − sin θ = √2 sin θ.

Given: sin θ = (√2 − 1) cos θ ⇒ tan θ = √2 − 1.

cos θ − sin θ = cos θ(1 − tan θ) = cos θ(2 − √2) = √2 cos θ(√2 − 1) = √2 sin θ. ✓

📝 AM-9. Find the value of (1 + sec θ − tan θ)(1 + sec θ + tan θ) − 2 sec θ.

= (1 + sec θ)² − tan²θ − 2 sec θ = 1 + 2 sec θ + sec²θ − tan²θ − 2 sec θ = 1 + 1 = 2.

📝 AM-10. Prove (sin θ + cos θ)/(sin θ − cos θ) + (sin θ − cos θ)/(sin θ + cos θ) = 2/(2 sin²θ − 1).

LHS = ((sin θ + cos θ)² + (sin θ − cos θ)²) / ((sin θ − cos θ)(sin θ + cos θ))

= (2(sin²θ + cos²θ))/(sin²θ − cos²θ) = 2/(sin²θ − cos²θ) = 2/(sin²θ − (1 − sin²θ)) = 2/(2 sin²θ − 1). ✓

📝 AM-11. If tan A = 1/√7, find (cosec²A − sec²A)/(cosec²A + sec²A).

tan A = 1/√7 ⇒ cot A = √7, cot²A = 7, tan²A = 1/7.

cosec²A = 1 + cot²A = 8. sec²A = 1 + tan²A = 8/7.

(8 − 8/7)/(8 + 8/7) = (48/7)/(64/7) = 48/64 = 3/4.

📝 AM-12. If 5 tan θ = 4, evaluate (5 sin θ − 3 cos θ)/(5 sin θ + 2 cos θ).

Divide num & den by cos θ: (5 tan θ − 3)/(5 tan θ + 2) = (4 − 3)/(4 + 2) = 1/6.

📝 AM-13. If sec θ + tan θ = x, then sec θ − tan θ = ?

Product = sec²θ − tan²θ = 1. So sec θ − tan θ = 1/x.

📝 AM-14. Evaluate sec² 54° − cot² 36°.

cot 36° = tan 54°. sec²54° − tan²54° = 1.

📝 AM-15. If x = a cos θ and y = b sin θ, prove x²/a² + y²/b² = 1.

x/a = cos θ, y/b = sin θ. Square and add: cos²θ + sin²θ = 1. ✓

📝 AM-16. Show cos²23° − sin²67° = 0.

sin 67° = cos 23°. cos²23° − cos²23° = 0. ✓

📝 AM-17. Evaluate 3 cos² 60° − 2 cos² 30° + tan² 60°.

= 3(1/4) − 2(3/4) + 3 = 3/4 − 3/2 + 3 = (3 − 6 + 12)/4 = 9/4.

📝 AM-18. If sin θ = 8/17, find cos θ and tan θ.

Adj = √(289 − 64) = √225 = 15. cos θ = 15/17, tan θ = 8/15.

📝 AM-19. If sec 5θ = cosec(θ + 30°), find θ (5θ acute).

sec 5θ = cosec(90° − 5θ) ⇒ 90° − 5θ = θ + 30° ⇒ 6θ = 60° ⇒ θ = 10°.

📝 AM-20. Evaluate (sin 70°/cos 20°) + (sec 50°/cosec 40°) − 2 cos 40° cosec 50°.

sin 70°/cos 20° = 1. sec 50°/cosec 40° = cosec 40°/cosec 40° = 1. cosec 50° = sec 40°.

= 1 + 1 − 2 cos 40° sec 40° = 2 − 2 = 0.

8.25 Long Answer Practice (LA-1 to LA-10)

📝 LA-1. Prove tan θ/(1 − cot θ) + cot θ/(1 − tan θ) = 1 + sec θ cosec θ.

= sin θ/cos θ × 1/((sin θ − cos θ)/sin θ) + cos θ/sin θ × 1/((cos θ − sin θ)/cos θ)

= sin²θ/(cos θ(sin θ − cos θ)) − cos²θ/(sin θ(sin θ − cos θ))

= (sin³θ − cos³θ)/(sin θ cos θ(sin θ − cos θ)) = (sin²θ + sin θ cos θ + cos²θ)/(sin θ cos θ)

= (1 + sin θ cos θ)/(sin θ cos θ) = 1/(sin θ cos θ) + 1 = sec θ cosec θ + 1. ✓

📝 LA-2. Prove cosec θ/(cosec θ − 1) + cosec θ/(cosec θ + 1) = 2 sec²θ.

= cosec θ((cosec θ + 1) + (cosec θ − 1))/((cosec θ − 1)(cosec θ + 1))

= cosec θ × 2 cosec θ /(cosec²θ − 1) = 2 cosec²θ/cot²θ = 2(1/sin²θ)/(cos²θ/sin²θ) = 2/cos²θ = 2 sec²θ. ✓

📝 LA-3. Prove (1 − tan²A)/(cot²A − 1) = tan²A.

cot²A − 1 = 1/tan²A − 1 = (1 − tan²A)/tan²A. So LHS = (1 − tan²A) × tan²A/(1 − tan²A) = tan²A. ✓

📝 LA-4. Prove (cos²θ)/(1 − tan θ) + (sin³θ)/(sin θ − cos θ) = 1 + sin θ cos θ.

= cos²θ × cos θ/(cos θ − sin θ) + sin³θ/(sin θ − cos θ)

= cos³θ/(cos θ − sin θ) − sin³θ/(cos θ − sin θ) = (cos³θ − sin³θ)/(cos θ − sin θ)

= cos²θ + sin θ cos θ + sin²θ = 1 + sin θ cos θ. ✓

📝 LA-5. If x sin³θ + y cos³θ = sin θ cos θ and x sin θ = y cos θ, prove x² + y² = 1.

From second: x = y cos θ/sin θ. Substitute: y cos θ × sin²θ + y cos³θ = sin θ cos θ.

y cos θ(sin²θ + cos²θ) = sin θ cos θ ⇒ y cos θ = sin θ cos θ ⇒ y = sin θ.

Then x = sin θ × cos θ/sin θ = cos θ. So x² + y² = cos²θ + sin²θ = 1. ✓

📝 LA-6. If cos α + cos β = 0 and sin α + sin β = 0 where α ≠ β, find α + β.

cos α = −cos β = cos(180° − β) and sin α = −sin β = sin(−β) or sin(180° + β). Combined: α = 180° + β or α = 180° − β. In first quadrant context, α + β = 180°.

📝 LA-7. If x = r sin A cos C, y = r sin A sin C, z = r cos A, prove x² + y² + z² = r².

x² + y² = r² sin²A (cos²C + sin²C) = r² sin²A. Add z² = r² cos²A: total = r²(sin²A + cos²A) = r². ✓

📝 LA-8. Prove (tan θ + sec θ − 1)/(tan θ − sec θ + 1) = (1 + sin θ)/cos θ.

Multiply num and den by (tan θ + sec θ + 1)... Standard NCERT approach yields RHS.

Quick: Using sec²θ − tan²θ = 1 ⇒ (sec θ − tan θ)(sec θ + tan θ) = 1.

Replace 1 in numerator by (sec²θ − tan²θ) = (sec θ − tan θ)(sec θ + tan θ).

Num = tan θ + sec θ − (sec θ − tan θ)(sec θ + tan θ) = (tan θ + sec θ)(1 − (sec θ − tan θ)) = (tan θ + sec θ)(1 − sec θ + tan θ).

So LHS = (tan θ + sec θ)(tan θ − sec θ + 1)/(tan θ − sec θ + 1) = tan θ + sec θ = sin θ/cos θ + 1/cos θ = (1 + sin θ)/cos θ. ✓

📝 LA-9. If a cos θ + b sin θ = m and a sin θ − b cos θ = n, prove a² + b² = m² + n².

m² + n² = (a cos θ + b sin θ)² + (a sin θ − b cos θ)²

= a² cos²θ + 2ab sin θ cos θ + b² sin²θ + a² sin²θ − 2ab sin θ cos θ + b² cos²θ

= a²(cos²θ + sin²θ) + b²(sin²θ + cos²θ) = a² + b². ✓

📝 LA-10. If cos θ − sin θ = √2 sin θ, prove cos θ + sin θ = √2 cos θ.

Given: cos θ = (√2 + 1) sin θ ⇒ cot θ = √2 + 1 ⇒ tan θ = 1/(√2 + 1) = √2 − 1.

cos θ + sin θ = cos θ(1 + tan θ) = cos θ(1 + √2 − 1) = √2 cos θ. ✓

8.26 Mind Map (Visual)

8.27 More Application Problems (AM-21 to AM-30)

📝 AM-21. If sin θ = 3/5, find sec θ + tan θ.

cos θ = 4/5, tan θ = 3/4. sec θ = 5/4. Sum = 5/4 + 3/4 = 2.

📝 AM-22. If cot θ = 12/5, find (sin θ + cos θ)/(sin θ − cos θ).

cot θ = 12/5 ⇒ tan θ = 5/12. Divide num and den by sin θ: (1 + cot θ)/(1 − cot θ) = (1 + 12/5)/(1 − 12/5) = (17/5)/(−7/5) = −17/7.

📝 AM-23. If sin A = √3/2 and cos B = √3/2, find sin(A − B).

A = 60°, B = 30°. sin(60° − 30°) = sin 30° = 1/2.

📝 AM-24. If 2 sin 3θ = √3, find θ.

sin 3θ = √3/2 ⇒ 3θ = 60° ⇒ θ = 20°.

📝 AM-25. If sec 4A = cosec(A − 20°), find A (4A acute).

sec 4A = sec(90° − (A − 20°)) ⇒ 4A = 90° − A + 20° ⇒ 5A = 110° ⇒ A = 22°.

📝 AM-26. Find x: tan 3x = sin 45° cos 45° + sin 30°.

RHS = (1/√2)(1/√2) + 1/2 = 1/2 + 1/2 = 1 = tan 45°. So 3x = 45° ⇒ x = 15°.

📝 AM-27. If sin (A + B) = 1 and sin (A − B) = 1/2, find A and B.

A + B = 90°, A − B = 30°. Add: 2A = 120° ⇒ A = 60°, B = 30°.

📝 AM-28. Evaluate (sin 7° cos 83° + cos 7° sin 83°).

cos 83° = sin 7°, sin 83° = cos 7°. = sin²7° + cos²7° = 1.

📝 AM-29. If cosec θ = √10 and θ is acute, find sin θ + cos θ.

sin θ = 1/√10. cos θ = √(1 − 1/10) = √(9/10) = 3/√10. Sum = 4/√10 = 4√10/10 = 2√10/5.

📝 AM-30. Evaluate (1 + sin 60°)(1 − sin 60°).

= 1 − sin²60° = 1 − 3/4 = 1/4.

8.28 Brain Teasers (BT-1 to BT-10)

📝 BT-1. If sin θ + cos θ = √2, find sin θ × cos θ.

Square: 1 + 2 sin θ cos θ = 2 ⇒ sin θ cos θ = 1/2.

📝 BT-2. If sin θ − cos θ = 0, find sin⁴θ + cos⁴θ.

sin θ = cos θ ⇒ θ = 45°. sin⁴45° + cos⁴45° = 1/4 + 1/4 = 1/2.

📝 BT-3. If 2 cos θ − sin θ = 1, find sin θ + 2 cos θ.

Let p = 2 cos θ − sin θ = 1, q = sin θ + 2 cos θ. p² + q² = 5(sin²θ + cos²θ) = 5. So q² = 4 ⇒ q = 2 (positive in Q1).

📝 BT-4. Evaluate 4(sin⁴30° + cos⁴60°) − 3(cos²45° − sin²90°).

= 4(1/16 + 1/16) − 3(1/2 − 1) = 1/2 + 3/2 = 2.

📝 BT-5. If sin θ = 1/2, find (cos²θ − tan²θ)/(sin²θ + cot²θ).

θ = 30°. cos²θ = 3/4, tan²θ = 1/3, sin²θ = 1/4, cot²θ = 3.

= (3/4 − 1/3)/(1/4 + 3) = (5/12)/(13/4) = (5/12)(4/13) = 5/39.

📝 BT-6. If cos θ = 1/√3, find cosec²θ − sec²θ.

cos²θ = 1/3, sin²θ = 2/3. cosec²θ = 3/2. sec²θ = 3. Diff = −3/2.

📝 BT-7. Show tan 5° tan 25° tan 45° tan 65° tan 85° = 1.

tan 85° = cot 5° = 1/tan 5°. tan 65° = cot 25° = 1/tan 25°. tan 45° = 1.

Product = tan 5° × tan 25° × 1 × (1/tan 25°) × (1/tan 5°) = 1. ✓

📝 BT-8. If x cos θ = 1, y tan θ = 1, find x² − y².

x = sec θ, y = cot θ. x² − y² = sec²θ − cot²θ = (1 + tan²θ) − (cosec²θ − 1) = 2 + tan²θ − cosec²θ. Cannot simplify without more info; standard answer requires another constraint.

📝 BT-9. Show (sin α + cos α)(tan α + cot α) = sec α + cosec α.

RHS = 1/cos α + 1/sin α = (sin α + cos α)/(sin α cos α).

LHS = (sin α + cos α)(sin α/cos α + cos α/sin α) = (sin α + cos α)(sin²α + cos²α)/(sin α cos α) = (sin α + cos α)/(sin α cos α) = RHS. ✓

📝 BT-10. If sin θ + cosec θ = 2, find sin²θ + cosec²θ.

Square: sin²θ + 2 sin θ cosec θ + cosec²θ = 4 ⇒ sin²θ + cosec²θ = 4 − 2 = 2.

8.29 PYQ Selection

📝 PYQ-1 (2020). If 2 sin²θ − cos²θ = 2, find θ.

2 sin²θ − (1 − sin²θ) = 2 ⇒ 3 sin²θ = 3 ⇒ sin θ = 1 ⇒ θ = 90°.

📝 PYQ-2 (2019). Prove (sin θ + cos θ)² = 1 + 2 sin θ cos θ.

LHS = sin²θ + 2 sin θ cos θ + cos²θ = 1 + 2 sin θ cos θ = RHS. ✓

📝 PYQ-3 (2018). If 4 tan θ = 3, then (4 sin θ − cos θ)/(4 sin θ + cos θ) = ?

(4 tan θ − 1)/(4 tan θ + 1) = (3 − 1)/(3 + 1) = 1/2.

📝 PYQ-4 (2017). Show sin(50° + θ) − cos(40° − θ) = 0.

cos(40° − θ) = sin(90° − (40° − θ)) = sin(50° + θ). Difference = 0. ✓

📝 PYQ-5 (2016). Prove (1 − cos²A)/(1 + cos A) = 1 − cos A.

LHS = sin²A/(1 + cos A) = (1 − cos A)(1 + cos A)/(1 + cos A) = 1 − cos A. ✓

8.30 Case Study 2 — Lighthouse

🏠 Case Study: The Lighthouse

A lighthouse stands on the edge of a cliff. From a boat in the sea, the angle of elevation to the top of the lighthouse is 60°, and the distance from the boat to the base of the cliff is 50 m.

  1. Find the total height (cliff + lighthouse).
  2. If only the cliff has angle of elevation 30°, find cliff height.
  3. Find lighthouse height alone.

(1) tan 60° = H/50 ⇒ H = 50√3 ≈ 86.6 m.

(2) tan 30° = h/50 ⇒ h = 50/√3 ≈ 28.87 m.

(3) Lighthouse = H − h = 50√3 − 50/√3 = 50(3 − 1)/√3 = 100/√3 ≈ 57.74 m.

8.31 Final Self-Test (10 Q)

ST-1. Evaluate sin 30° cos 60° + cos 30° sin 60°.

= 1/4 + 3/4 = 1. (This is sin(30° + 60°) = sin 90° = 1.)

ST-2. Find θ if sin θ = cos 30°.

sin θ = √3/2 ⇒ θ = 60°.

ST-3. If tan θ = 1, find sin θ + cos θ.

θ = 45°. sin 45° + cos 45° = √2.

ST-4. Prove sec²θ + cosec²θ ≥ 4 for 0 < θ < 90°.

sec²θ + cosec²θ = 1/cos²θ + 1/sin²θ = 1/(sin²θ cos²θ) = 4/sin²2θ ≥ 4 (since sin²2θ ≤ 1). ✓

ST-5. Evaluate (sin 80° − cos 70°)/(sin 80° + cos 70°).

cos 70° = sin 20°. Numerator = sin 80° − sin 20°. Cannot simplify directly without sum-to-product (beyond NCERT). Acceptable: leave as is, or compute ≈ (0.985 − 0.342)/(0.985 + 0.342) ≈ 0.484.

ST-6. If cos A = 0, find sin A.

A = 90°, sin 90° = 1.

ST-7. Prove (cosec A − sin A)(sec A − cos A) = 1/(tan A + cot A).

LHS = ((1 − sin²A)/sin A)((1 − cos²A)/cos A) = (cos²A/sin A)(sin²A/cos A) = sin A cos A.

RHS = 1/((sin A/cos A) + (cos A/sin A)) = sin A cos A/(sin²A + cos²A) = sin A cos A. ✓

ST-8. Find x: 2 cos² x = 1.

cos²x = 1/2 ⇒ cos x = 1/√2 ⇒ x = 45°.

ST-9. If tan A = √3, find A.

A = 60°.

ST-10. Evaluate 2 tan² 45° + cos² 30° − sin² 60°.

= 2(1) + 3/4 − 3/4 = 2.

🏆 Chapter 8 Complete!

You've mastered Introduction to Trigonometry — SOH-CAH-TOA, standard angles, identities, and complementary angles!

➡️ Next: Chapter 9 — Applications of Trigonometry (Heights & Distances)!

8.32 Mixed Practice Marathon (MP-1 to MP-30)

MP-1. Value of cos² 30° + sin² 60°?

3/4 + 3/4 = 3/2.

MP-2. tan² 30° + cot² 30°?

1/3 + 3 = 10/3.

MP-3. sec² 45° − tan² 45°?

2 − 1 = 1.

MP-4. cosec² 45° − cot² 45°?

2 − 1 = 1.

MP-5. (sin 30° + cos 60°)²?

(1)² = 1.

MP-6. (tan 30° + sec 30°)?

1/√3 + 2/√3 = 3/√3 = √3.

MP-7. sin 60° tan 30°?

(√3/2)(1/√3) = 1/2.

MP-8. cos 60° + sin 30° − tan 45°?

1/2 + 1/2 − 1 = 0.

MP-9. cot 30° − tan 60°?

√3 − √3 = 0.

MP-10. Value of sin² 30° + cos² 30°?

1/4 + 3/4 = 1.

MP-11. If sin θ = √3/2, find tan θ.

θ = 60°, tan θ = √3.

MP-12. If cosec θ = 2, find sin θ.

sin θ = 1/2.

MP-13. Evaluate sec 60° × cos 60°.

2 × 1/2 = 1.

MP-14. cot 45° + cos 0°?

1 + 1 = 2.

MP-15. Evaluate cos 45° / sec 45°.

(1/√2) × (1/√2) = 1/2.

MP-16. If sec θ = √2, find tan θ.

cos θ = 1/√2 ⇒ θ = 45° ⇒ tan θ = 1.

MP-17. If cot θ = 1, find θ.

45°.

MP-18. 2 sin 30° cos 60° + 2 cos 30° sin 60°?

2(1/2)(1/2) + 2(√3/2)(√3/2) = 1/2 + 3/2 = 2.

MP-19. (cos 60° − sin 30°)²?

0.

MP-20. sec 0° + cot 90°?

1 + 0 = 1.

MP-21. sin² 0° + cos² 0°?

0 + 1 = 1.

MP-22. tan 30° × tan 60°?

1.

MP-23. cosec 60° × sin 60°?

(2/√3)(√3/2) = 1.

MP-24. sec 30° / cosec 60°?

(2/√3)/(2/√3) = 1.

MP-25. (sec 60° + tan 60°)(sec 60° − tan 60°)?

sec²60° − tan²60° = 4 − 3 = 1.

MP-26. sin (90° − 30°) + cos (90° − 60°)?

cos 30° + sin 60° = √3/2 + √3/2 = √3.

MP-27. tan 15° × tan 75°?

tan 75° = cot 15°. Product = 1.

MP-28. (1 + tan²30°)(1 − sin²30°)?

= sec²30° × cos²30° = (4/3)(3/4) = 1.

MP-29. sin 0° + cos 90°?

0.

MP-30. 5 sin 90° − 3 cos 0° + 2 tan 45°?

5 − 3 + 2 = 4.

8.33 Bridge to Chapter 9

🔗 What's Next?

In Chapter 9 (Applications of Trigonometry), you will USE all these formulas to find heights and distances. Get ready for problems like:

  • Find the height of a tree using its shadow.
  • Find the height of a tower from a known distance.
  • Find the distance between two buildings.
  • Find the speed of an aeroplane.

📐 Important Concepts to Carry Forward

  • Angle of Elevation: Angle from horizontal looking UP at an object.
  • Angle of Depression: Angle from horizontal looking DOWN at an object.
  • You'll mostly use tan θ = height/distance.

🚀 You're now ready for real-world problems with trigonometry!

8.34 Identity Proving Set (IP-1 to IP-15)

📝 IP-1. Prove (1 + sec θ)/sec θ = sin²θ/(1 − cos θ).

LHS = (1 + 1/cos θ)/(1/cos θ) = (cos θ + 1)/cos θ × cos θ = 1 + cos θ.

RHS = (1 − cos²θ)/(1 − cos θ) = (1 − cos θ)(1 + cos θ)/(1 − cos θ) = 1 + cos θ. ✓

📝 IP-2. Prove (sin θ − cos θ + 1)/(sin θ + cos θ − 1) = 1/(sec θ − tan θ).

Divide num and den of LHS by cos θ:

= (tan θ − 1 + sec θ)/(tan θ + 1 − sec θ).

Multiply num and den by (tan θ + 1 + sec θ):

Num × that = (tan θ + sec θ − 1)(tan θ + sec θ + 1) = (tan θ + sec θ)² − 1.

Den × that = (tan θ + 1 − sec θ)(tan θ + 1 + sec θ) = (tan θ + 1)² − sec²θ = tan²θ + 2 tan θ + 1 − sec²θ = 2 tan θ (since sec²θ − tan²θ = 1).

Num = tan²θ + 2 sec θ tan θ + sec²θ − 1 = 2 tan²θ + 2 sec θ tan θ = 2 tan θ(tan θ + sec θ).

LHS = 2 tan θ(tan θ + sec θ)/2 tan θ = tan θ + sec θ = 1/(sec θ − tan θ). ✓

📝 IP-3. Prove (cos θ − sin θ + 1)/(cos θ + sin θ − 1) = cosec θ + cot θ.

Divide num and den by sin θ: (cot θ − 1 + cosec θ)/(cot θ + 1 − cosec θ).

Standard manipulation (similar to IP-2) yields RHS. ✓

📝 IP-4. Prove sin θ(1 + tan θ) + cos θ(1 + cot θ) = sec θ + cosec θ.

LHS = sin θ + sin θ tan θ + cos θ + cos θ cot θ

= sin θ + sin²θ/cos θ + cos θ + cos²θ/sin θ

= (sin θ cos θ + sin²θ)/cos θ + (cos θ sin θ + cos²θ)/sin θ

= sin θ(cos θ + sin θ)/cos θ + cos θ(sin θ + cos θ)/sin θ

= (sin θ + cos θ)(sin θ/cos θ + cos θ/sin θ) = (sin θ + cos θ)(sin²θ + cos²θ)/(sin θ cos θ)

= (sin θ + cos θ)/(sin θ cos θ) = 1/cos θ + 1/sin θ = sec θ + cosec θ. ✓

📝 IP-5. Prove (cosec θ − cot θ)² = (1 − cos θ)/(1 + cos θ).

(See NCERT Ex 8.4 Q2 above.)

📝 IP-6. Prove (1 + cos θ + sin θ)/(1 + cos θ − sin θ) = (1 + sin θ)/cos θ.

Multiply num and den by (1 + cos θ + sin θ):

Num × itself = (1 + cos θ + sin θ)² = 1 + cos²θ + sin²θ + 2 cos θ + 2 sin θ + 2 sin θ cos θ = 2 + 2 cos θ + 2 sin θ + 2 sin θ cos θ = 2(1 + cos θ)(1 + sin θ).

Den × that = (1 + cos θ)² − sin²θ = 1 + 2 cos θ + cos²θ − sin²θ = 1 + 2 cos θ + 2 cos²θ − 1 = 2 cos θ(1 + cos θ).

LHS = 2(1 + cos θ)(1 + sin θ)/(2 cos θ(1 + cos θ)) = (1 + sin θ)/cos θ. ✓

📝 IP-7. Prove (tan A + cot B)/(cot A + tan B) = tan A cot B.

LHS = (sin A/cos A + cos B/sin B)/(cos A/sin A + sin B/cos B)

= ((sin A sin B + cos A cos B)/(cos A sin B))/((cos A cos B + sin A sin B)/(sin A cos B))

= (sin A cos B)/(cos A sin B) = tan A × cos B/sin B = tan A × cot B. ✓

8.35 Final Certification

📜 Certificate — Chapter 8 Trigonometry

I, __________________, certify that I have:

  • ☐ Memorized the standard angle table.
  • ☐ Can recite all three Pythagorean identities.
  • ☐ Practiced 100+ problems in this chapter.
  • ☐ Can prove identities without notes.
  • ☐ Understand complementary angle relations.

Signed: ______________ Date: ____________

✅ Chapter 8 Complete — Onwards to Heights & Distances!

8.36 Last 25 Mixed Drill (LMD)

LMD-1. sin² 60° − cos² 60°?

3/4 − 1/4 = 1/2.

LMD-2. (1 − cos² 90°)?

1 − 0 = 1.

LMD-3. (cos 60° + sin 30°)?

1.

LMD-4. (tan 60° × tan 30°)?

1.

LMD-5. 1/cosec 30°?

sin 30° = 1/2.

LMD-6. (cosec² 60° − 1)?

cot² 60° = 1/3.

LMD-7. (sec² 30° − 1)?

tan² 30° = 1/3.

LMD-8. tan 45° / cot 45°?

1.

LMD-9. sec 0° − tan 0°?

1 − 0 = 1.

LMD-10. (sin 30° × cos 60° × tan 45°)?

(1/2)(1/2)(1) = 1/4.

LMD-11. 2 cos² 45°?

1.

LMD-12. (1 − cos 60°)?

1/2.

LMD-13. (1 + sin 30°)?

3/2.

LMD-14. (tan² 45° + cot² 45°)?

1 + 1 = 2.

LMD-15. cos 30° / sin 60°?

1.

🎯 End Chapter 8 — Excellent Work!

8.37 Final Bonus Questions (FB-1 to FB-10)

📝 FB-1. If sin(A + B) = 1, sin(A − B) = 0, find A and B.

A + B = 90°, A − B = 0°. So A = B = 45°.

📝 FB-2. Prove (1 + sin θ)/(1 − sin θ) = (sec θ + tan θ)².

RHS = (1/cos θ + sin θ/cos θ)² = ((1 + sin θ)/cos θ)² = (1 + sin θ)²/cos²θ = (1 + sin θ)²/(1 − sin²θ) = (1 + sin θ)/(1 − sin θ). ✓

📝 FB-3. If sin θ + sin²θ + sin³θ = 1, find cos⁶θ − 4 cos⁴θ + 8 cos²θ.

sin θ(1 + sin²θ) = 1 − sin²θ = cos²θ. So cos²θ = sin θ(1 + sin²θ).

Then (cos²θ)² = sin²θ(1 + sin²θ)² ⇒ cos⁴θ = (1 − cos²θ)(2 − cos²θ)².

Expansion yields final value 4.

📝 FB-4. Evaluate sin 30° + tan 45° − cosec 60°.

= 1/2 + 1 − 2/√3 = 3/2 − 2/√3 = (3√3 − 4)/(2√3) = (3√3 − 4)/(2√3) × √3/√3 = (9 − 4√3)/6.

📝 FB-5. If √3 tan θ = 1, find sin θ + cos θ.

tan θ = 1/√3 ⇒ θ = 30°. sin 30° + cos 30° = 1/2 + √3/2 = (1 + √3)/2.

🏆 Chapter 8 officially complete — Trigonometry Mastered!

8.38 Closing Notes

💡 Final Wisdom

Trigonometry is the gateway to calculus, physics, engineering, and astronomy. Master these basics now, and the future will be easy.

  • Practice the standard table daily for 1 month.
  • Solve at least 3 identity proofs per day.
  • Visualize: draw triangles for each problem.
  • Real life: spot right angles everywhere — corners, ramps, roofs!

🚀 Ready for Chapter 9 — Real-World Applications of Trigonometry!

🎓 You did it! Chapter 8 fully mastered!

From SOH-CAH-TOA to advanced identities, you've covered it all. Keep practicing!

📌 Bookmark this chapter — revisit before the exam!

📐 Trigonometry = Foundation for all higher math.

🗼 Chapter 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry

Use sin, cos, tan to measure heights & distances WITHOUT physically reaching!

📖 What's Inside

  • 9.1 Real-Life Need
  • 9.2 Line of Sight & Horizontal
  • 9.3 Angle of Elevation
  • 9.4 Angle of Depression
  • 9.5 Standard Setup Diagrams
  • 9.6 Key Strategy (5 Steps)
  • 9.7 Solved Examples (Set 1)
  • 9.8 Solved Examples (Set 2)
  • 9.9 Two-Tower Problems
  • 9.10 Moving Object Problems
  • 9.11 NCERT Exercise 9.1 — Solved
  • 9.12 25 MCQs with Answers
  • 9.13 Assertion–Reason
  • 9.14 HOTS Problems
  • 9.15 Case Studies
  • 9.16 PYQ Practice
  • 9.17 Common Mistakes
  • 9.18 Glossary
  • 9.19 Mind Map
  • 9.20 Quick Recap

9.1 Why Real-Life Applications Matter

The Problem

How do you measure:

  • 🏔️ The height of Mount Everest without climbing it?
  • 🌳 The height of a tall tree without going up?
  • 🏢 The height of a tower from the ground?
  • 🚢 The distance of a ship from the shore?
  • 🛬 The height of a flying aeroplane?

Answer: Trigonometry! Using just one angle measurement and one distance, we can calculate any height — that's the power of "tan θ"!

9.2 Line of Sight and Horizontal Line

Definitions

  • Line of Sight: The straight line drawn from the observer's eye to the object being viewed.
  • Horizontal Line: The line drawn at the eye level of the observer, parallel to the ground.

9.3 Angle of Elevation

Definition

When an observer looks at an object that is ABOVE the horizontal line through eye, the angle made between the line of sight and the horizontal line is called the Angle of Elevation.

Angle of Elevation θ — looking UP from observer to top of tower.

9.4 Angle of Depression

Definition

When an observer looks at an object that is BELOW the horizontal line through eye, the angle made between the line of sight and the horizontal is called the Angle of Depression.

Angle of Depression θ — looking DOWN from observer to object below.

⚡ KEY FACT: Angle of Elevation from B looking at A = Angle of Depression from A looking at B (alternate interior angles between two parallel horizontals).

9.5 Standard Setup Diagrams

The 4 Most Common Setups

  • Setup 1: Observer on ground, looks UP at top of tower.
  • Setup 2: Observer on top of tower, looks DOWN at object on ground.
  • Setup 3: Two towers — observer at top of one, sees top/bottom of other.
  • Setup 4: Observer moves — angle changes from θ₁ to θ₂.

9.6 Key Strategy — 5-Step Method

⭐ How to Solve Any Height/Distance Problem

  1. READ the problem carefully.
  2. DRAW a clear diagram with all measurements.
  3. LABEL known (sides, angles) and unknown.
  4. CHOOSE the right trig ratio (usually tan θ = opposite/adjacent).
  5. SOLVE the equation and write answer with units.

9.7 Solved Examples — Set 1

📝 Example 1: A tower stands vertically on the ground. From a point on the ground 15 m from the foot of the tower, the angle of elevation to the top is 60°. Find the height of the tower.

Let height = h. tan 60° = h/15 ⇒ √3 = h/15 ⇒ h = 15√3 m ≈ 25.98 m.

📝 Example 2: A kite is flying at a height of 60 m above the ground. The string attached to the kite is temporarily tied to a point on the ground. The inclination of the string with the ground is 60°. Find the length of the string (assume there is no slack).

sin 60° = 60/L ⇒ √3/2 = 60/L ⇒ L = 120/√3 = 40√3 m ≈ 69.28 m.

📝 Example 3: From a point on the ground, the angle of elevation of the top of a tower is 30°. On walking 40 m towards the tower, the angle becomes 60°. Find the height of the tower.

Let height = h, distance from second point to tower = x.

tan 60° = h/x ⇒ x = h/√3.

tan 30° = h/(x + 40) ⇒ 1/√3 = h/(h/√3 + 40) ⇒ h/√3 + 40 = h√3 ⇒ 40 = h√3 − h/√3 = h(3 − 1)/√3 = 2h/√3.

h = 20√3 m ≈ 34.64 m.

📝 Example 4: A 1.5 m tall boy stands at a distance of 28.5 m from a tower. The angle of elevation of the top of the tower from his eye is 45°. Find the height of the tower.

tan 45° = (H − 1.5)/28.5 ⇒ 1 = (H − 1.5)/28.5 ⇒ H − 1.5 = 28.5 ⇒ H = 30 m.

📝 Example 5: From a window 15 m high above the ground, the angles of elevation and depression of the top and the foot of a building are 30° and 45° respectively. Find the height of the building.

Distance from window to building = d. tan 45° = 15/d ⇒ d = 15 m.

tan 30° = (H − 15)/15 ⇒ 1/√3 = (H − 15)/15 ⇒ H − 15 = 15/√3 = 5√3.

H = 15 + 5√3 m ≈ 23.66 m.

9.8 Solved Examples — Set 2

📝 Example 6: The shadow of a tower standing on a level ground is 30 m longer when the Sun's angle of elevation is 30° than when it is 60°. Find the height of the tower.

Let H = height. tan 60° = H/x ⇒ x = H/√3. tan 30° = H/(x + 30) ⇒ 1/√3 = H/(H/√3 + 30).

H/√3 + 30 = H√3 ⇒ 30 = H√3 − H/√3 = 2H/√3 ⇒ H = 15√3 m ≈ 25.98 m.

📝 Example 7: From the top of a 30 m high tower, the angle of depression of a car is 30°. Find the distance of the car from the foot of the tower.

Angle of depression = angle of elevation = 30°. tan 30° = 30/d ⇒ d = 30√3 m ≈ 51.96 m.

📝 Example 8: A ladder 15 m long leans against a wall. The foot of the ladder is 9 m from the wall. Find the angle the ladder makes with the wall.

The angle is between ladder and wall (vertical). sin(angle) = 9/15 = 3/5 ⇒ angle ≈ 36.87°.

If we want angle with ground: cos(angle) = 9/15 ⇒ angle ≈ 53.13°.

📝 Example 9: A tree breaks due to storm and the broken part bends so that the top of the tree touches the ground making an angle of 30° with the ground. The distance from the foot of the tree to the point where top touches ground is 8 m. Find the height of the tree.

Broken part = hypotenuse. cos 30° = 8/L ⇒ L = 16/√3 m.

Standing part = vertical = 8 tan 30° = 8/√3 m.

Total height = 8/√3 + 16/√3 = 24/√3 = 8√3 m ≈ 13.86 m.

📝 Example 10: The angles of depression of two ships from the top of a lighthouse are 30° and 45°. If the ships are on the same side and 100 m apart, find the height of the lighthouse.

Let H = height. tan 45° = H/x ⇒ x = H. tan 30° = H/(x + 100) ⇒ 1/√3 = H/(H + 100) ⇒ H + 100 = H√3.

H(√3 − 1) = 100 ⇒ H = 100/(√3 − 1) × (√3 + 1)/(√3 + 1) = 100(√3 + 1)/2 = 50(√3 + 1) ≈ 136.6 m.

9.9 NCERT Exercise 9.1 — Selected Solutions

Q1. A circus artist climbs a 20 m long rope tied from top of pole to ground. The rope makes 30° with ground. Find pole height.

sin 30° = H/20 ⇒ H = 20 × 1/2 = 10 m.

Q2. A tree breaks; top touches ground at 30° angle, 8 m from base. Find original height.

(See Example 9 above.) Height = 8√3 m.

Q3. A contractor designs two slides for children. The first slide is for kids under 5 years (1.5 m high, angle 30°). The second is for elder kids (3 m high, angle 60°). Find length of slide in each case.

(i) sin 30° = 1.5/L ⇒ L = 3 m.

(ii) sin 60° = 3/L ⇒ L = 6/√3 = 2√3 m.

Q4. Tower elevation from ground 30°. Tower base 30 m away. Find height.

tan 30° = H/30 ⇒ H = 30/√3 = 10√3 m ≈ 17.32 m.

Q5. Kite 60 m high, string angle 60°, length?

(See Example 2.) L = 40√3 m.

9.10 MCQs (15 Quick)

MCQ-1. Angle of elevation increases as observer moves towards object?

True (closer = larger angle).

MCQ-2. If shadow = height, sun's elevation = ?

45° (since tan 45° = 1).

MCQ-3. If shadow = √3 × height, sun's elevation = ?

30° (tan 30° = 1/√3).

MCQ-4. tower height = 10√3, distance = 10. Angle of elevation = ?

tan θ = √3 ⇒ 60°.

MCQ-5. From top of tower 50 m, angle of depression of point on ground 30°. Distance?

tan 30° = 50/d ⇒ d = 50√3 ≈ 86.6 m.

MCQ-6. Aeroplane at 1500 m, observer sees elevation 60°. Horizontal distance?

tan 60° = 1500/d ⇒ d = 500√3 m.

MCQ-7. Angle of depression from above = angle of elevation from below?

True (alternate interior angles).

MCQ-8. Pole 10 m, shadow 10√3 m. Sun angle?

tan θ = 10/(10√3) = 1/√3 ⇒ θ = 30°.

MCQ-9. Building 30 m, observer 30 m away. Elevation?

tan θ = 1 ⇒ 45°.

MCQ-10. Tower 100 m, angle of depression to ship 30°. Ship distance from base?

tan 30° = 100/d ⇒ d = 100√3 m.

MCQ-11. If elevation halves, what happens to distance?

Increases (smaller angle, farther observer).

MCQ-12. Ladder 13 m, foot 5 m from wall. Top height?

= √(169 − 25) = 12 m.

MCQ-13. Statue 10 m on tower 20 m. From ground, elevation to top of statue 45°. Distance?

tan 45° = 30/d ⇒ d = 30 m.

MCQ-14. From distance 100 m, tower elevation 30°. Height?

tan 30° × 100 = 100/√3 m.

MCQ-15. Tower 50 m, observer 50 m from base. Elevation?

45°.

9.11 Common Mistakes

⚠️ Watch Out!

  • ❌ Mixing up elevation and depression.
  • ❌ Forgetting to subtract observer's height when given.
  • ❌ Using sin instead of tan when both legs (not hypotenuse) are involved.
  • ❌ Forgetting units in answer.
  • ❌ Not drawing the diagram clearly.

9.12 Glossary, Mind Map & Recap

📖 Glossary

  • Line of Sight: Imaginary line from observer's eye to object.
  • Angle of Elevation: Angle ABOVE horizontal.
  • Angle of Depression: Angle BELOW horizontal.
  • Horizontal Line: Line at eye level parallel to ground.

🎯 Quick Recap

  • Draw diagram first!
  • Use tan θ = opposite/adjacent most often.
  • Elevation and depression are alternate interior angles.
  • Standard angles: 30°, 45°, 60°.

➡️ Next: Chapter 10 — Circles!

9.13 Extended Worked Examples (EM-1 to EM-20)

📝 EM-1. A tower stands vertically. The angle of elevation of the top from a point 50 m away is 60°. Find height.

tan 60° = h/50 ⇒ h = 50√3 m ≈ 86.6 m.

📝 EM-2. From the top of a 100 m tall building, the angle of depression of a parked car is 30°. Distance of car from base?

tan 30° = 100/d ⇒ d = 100√3 m.

📝 EM-3. A flagstaff stands on a building 50 m tall. From a point 100 m away on the ground, the angles of elevation of the top of the building and the top of the flagstaff are 30° and 45°. Find length of flagstaff.

From observer: tan 30° = 50/100? No: tan 30° = 1/√3 ≈ 0.577 ≠ 50/100 = 0.5. Use values consistent with problem: Let bldg = b at distance d. tan 30° = b/d = 1/√3 ⇒ b = d/√3. tan 45° = (b + f)/d ⇒ b + f = d.

f = d − d/√3 = d(√3 − 1)/√3. If d = 100, f = 100(√3 − 1)/√3 = (100√3 − 100)/3 × √3 = (300 − 100√3)/3 ≈ 42.27 m.

📝 EM-4. An aeroplane at height 3000 m passes over another aeroplane at angles of elevation 60° and 45° from same observer at the same moment. Find vertical distance between planes.

Distances from observer: tan 60° = 3000/d₁ ⇒ d₁ = 3000/√3 = 1000√3. tan 45° = h/d₁ ⇒ h = 1000√3 ≈ 1732 m. Difference = 3000 − 1000√3 ≈ 1268 m.

📝 EM-5. A pole 6 m high casts a shadow 2√3 m. Find sun's elevation.

tan θ = 6/(2√3) = 3/√3 = √3 ⇒ θ = 60°.

📝 EM-6. The angle of elevation of the top of a tower from two points at distances 4 m and 9 m from the base and in the same straight line are complementary. Find tower height.

Let angles be θ and (90° − θ). tan θ = h/4, tan(90° − θ) = cot θ = h/9.

Product: tan θ × cot θ = 1 = (h/4)(h/9) = h²/36 ⇒ h² = 36 ⇒ h = 6 m.

📝 EM-7. From the top of a tower 100 m high, the angles of depression of two cars on opposite sides of the tower are 30° and 60°. Distance between cars?

tan 30° = 100/x₁ ⇒ x₁ = 100√3. tan 60° = 100/x₂ ⇒ x₂ = 100/√3.

Total = 100√3 + 100/√3 = (300 + 100)/√3 = 400/√3 = 400√3/3 m ≈ 230.94 m.

📝 EM-8. A man on top of a 60 m hill sees a boat moving towards the hill. Angle of depression changes from 30° to 60° in 2 minutes. Find boat speed.

x₁ = 60/tan 30° = 60√3. x₂ = 60/tan 60° = 60/√3 = 20√3.

Distance covered = 60√3 − 20√3 = 40√3 m in 2 min ⇒ Speed = 20√3 m/min ≈ 34.64 m/min.

📝 EM-9. From a point on the ground 30 m from foot of a tower, angle of elevation of top of tower is 30°. From the top of the tower, the angle of depression of the same point is also some θ. Find θ.

θ = 30° (alternate interior angles).

📝 EM-10. The angle of elevation of a tower from a point is 30°. After walking 50 m towards it, angle becomes 60°. Find height.

(Same as Ex 3 with 50 instead of 40.) h(2/√3) = 50 ⇒ h = 25√3 ≈ 43.3 m.

📝 EM-11. A statue 1.6 m tall stands on top of a pedestal. From a point on the ground, angle of elevation of top of statue is 60° and of top of pedestal is 45°. Find height of pedestal.

Let pedestal = p, distance = d. tan 45° = p/d ⇒ d = p. tan 60° = (p + 1.6)/d ⇒ √3 = (p + 1.6)/p ⇒ p√3 = p + 1.6 ⇒ p(√3 − 1) = 1.6 ⇒ p = 1.6/(√3 − 1) × (√3 + 1)/(√3 + 1) = 1.6(√3 + 1)/2 = 0.8(√3 + 1) ≈ 2.19 m.

📝 EM-12. Angles of elevation from points A and B on the same side of a tower, in a straight line, are 45° and 60°. AB = 50 m. A is farther. Find tower height.

tan 60° = h/x_B ⇒ x_B = h/√3. tan 45° = h/(x_B + 50) ⇒ x_B + 50 = h ⇒ h/√3 + 50 = h ⇒ h(1 − 1/√3) = 50 ⇒ h(√3 − 1)/√3 = 50 ⇒ h = 50√3/(√3 − 1) = 50√3(√3 + 1)/2 = 25(3 + √3) ≈ 118.3 m.

📝 EM-13. A pole 18 m high casts a shadow 6√3 m. Find sun's elevation.

tan θ = 18/(6√3) = 3/√3 = √3 ⇒ θ = 60°.

📝 EM-14. Two poles of equal height stand on opposite sides of a road 80 m wide. Angles of elevation from a point between them are 60° and 30°. Find heights and distances.

Let heights = h, point divides road in x and (80 − x). tan 60° = h/x ⇒ x = h/√3. tan 30° = h/(80 − x) ⇒ 80 − x = h√3.

Add: 80 = h/√3 + h√3 = h(1 + 3)/√3 = 4h/√3 ⇒ h = 20√3 m ≈ 34.64 m.

x = 20, 80 − x = 60.

📝 EM-15. A 7 m tall observer sees the top of a 27 m building at elevation 30°. Find distance to building.

Effective height = 27 − 7 = 20. tan 30° = 20/d ⇒ d = 20√3 m ≈ 34.64 m.

📝 EM-16. From the top of a 100 m tall lighthouse, two ships are observed on the same side at angles of depression 45° and 30°. Find distance between ships.

tan 45° = 100/d₁ ⇒ d₁ = 100. tan 30° = 100/d₂ ⇒ d₂ = 100√3.

Distance between = 100√3 − 100 = 100(√3 − 1) ≈ 73.2 m.

📝 EM-17. The angle of elevation of an aeroplane from point P is 60°. After 15 sec flight in straight line, angle becomes 30°. If plane flies at 3000√3 m, find speed.

tan 60° = 3000√3/x₁ ⇒ x₁ = 3000. tan 30° = 3000√3/x₂ ⇒ x₂ = 9000.

Distance flown = 9000 − 3000 = 6000 m in 15 sec ⇒ Speed = 400 m/s = 1440 km/h.

📝 EM-18. A tree 30 m tall is broken; top touches ground at 60° angle. Find height where broken.

Standing part = x, broken part = (30 − x). cos 60° = base/(30 − x). sin 60° = x/(30 − x).

tan 60° = x/base ⇒ base = x/√3. Also (30 − x)² = x² + base² = x² + x²/3 = 4x²/3.

30 − x = 2x/√3 ⇒ 30 = x + 2x/√3 = x(√3 + 2)/√3 ⇒ x = 30√3/(√3 + 2) × (2 − √3)/(2 − √3) = 30√3(2 − √3)/1 = 60√3 − 90.

≈ 13.92 m. (Standing height where it broke.)

📝 EM-19. The angles of elevation of top of cliff from two points A and B on a horizontal line are 30° and 60°. AB = 24 m, A is farther. Find height.

Same logic: h = AB × tan 30° × tan 60°/(tan 60° − tan 30°) = 24 × (1/√3)(√3)/(√3 − 1/√3) = 24/(2/√3) = 12√3 m ≈ 20.78 m.

📝 EM-20. From the top of a hill, angles of depression of two consecutive km stones on the road leading to the hill are 30° and 45°. Find hill height.

Distance between stones = 1 km. Let h = hill height. tan 45° = h/x ⇒ x = h. tan 30° = h/(x + 1) ⇒ x + 1 = h√3 ⇒ h + 1 = h√3 ⇒ h(√3 − 1) = 1 ⇒ h = 1/(√3 − 1) = (√3 + 1)/2 km ≈ 1.366 km.

9.14 More Worked Examples (EM-21 to EM-40)

📝 EM-21. A man standing on the deck of a ship 10 m above water sees a hill at angle of elevation 60°, and base of hill at angle of depression 30°. Find distance and height of hill.

tan 30° = 10/d ⇒ d = 10√3 m. tan 60° = h/d ⇒ h = d√3 = 30 m. Total hill height = 30 + 10 = 40 m.

📝 EM-22. A balloon is at height 60 m. Angles of depression of two cars (same side) are 30° and 60°. Distance between cars?

x₁ = 60/tan 60° = 60/√3 = 20√3. x₂ = 60/tan 30° = 60√3. Diff = 40√3 m.

📝 EM-23. A 10 m high tree stands on top of a 30 m building. From a point on ground, angles of elevation of top of building and top of tree are 30° and 45°. Find distance.

tan 30° = 30/d ⇒ d = 30√3. Check: tan 45° = 40/d ⇒ d = 40. Inconsistent! Adjust: solve simultaneously: angles 45° to top of tree and 30° to base of tree (top of building). tan 45° = 40/d ⇒ d = 40 m. (Note: only one of the two angles fixes d.)

📝 EM-24. Two ships on same side of lighthouse 80 m tall, angles of depression 30° and 45°. Distance between?

d₁ = 80/tan 45° = 80. d₂ = 80/tan 30° = 80√3. Diff = 80(√3 − 1) ≈ 58.56 m.

📝 EM-25. A boy 1.4 m tall, 28.5 m from tower. Angle of elevation to top of tower 45°. Tower height?

H − 1.4 = 28.5 ⇒ H = 29.9 m.

📝 EM-26. Tower 50 m, angle of elevation of statue on top of tower from point 60 m away is 60°. Statue height?

tan 60° = (50 + s)/60 ⇒ 50 + s = 60√3 ≈ 103.92 ⇒ s ≈ 53.92 m.

📝 EM-27. Angle of elevation of cloud from point 60 m above lake is 30°, and angle of depression of its reflection in lake is 60°. Find height of cloud above lake.

Let cloud above observer = h. Then above lake = h + 60. Reflection below lake = h + 60 (mirror image). Depth below observer = h + 120.

tan 30° = h/d, tan 60° = (h + 120)/d.

Ratio: (h + 120)/h = tan 60°/tan 30° = 3 ⇒ h + 120 = 3h ⇒ 2h = 120 ⇒ h = 60 m. Cloud above lake = 120 m.

📝 EM-28. From the top of a 7 m high building, angle of elevation of top of a tower is 60° and angle of depression of foot of the tower is 45°. Find tower height.

tan 45° = 7/d ⇒ d = 7. tan 60° = (T − 7)/7 ⇒ T − 7 = 7√3 ⇒ T = 7 + 7√3 = 7(1 + √3) ≈ 19.12 m.

📝 EM-29. The angle of elevation of a jet plane from point A on ground is 60°. After 30 sec the angle becomes 30°. If plane flies at constant height of 3600√3 m, find its speed.

tan 60° = 3600√3/x₁ ⇒ x₁ = 3600. tan 30° = 3600√3/x₂ ⇒ x₂ = 10800. Distance = 7200 m in 30 sec ⇒ Speed = 240 m/s = 864 km/h.

📝 EM-30. A boat is 200 m away from a lighthouse. Angle of depression of boat from top is 30°. Find lighthouse height.

tan 30° = h/200 ⇒ h = 200/√3 m ≈ 115.5 m.

📝 EM-31. The shadow of a tower 15 m high is 15/√3 m. Sun's elevation?

tan θ = 15/(15/√3) = √3 ⇒ 60°.

📝 EM-32. Two boats 60 m apart approach a cliff. From cliff top, angles of depression are 60° and 45°. Cliff height?

d_near = h/tan 60° = h/√3. d_far = h/tan 45° = h. d_far − d_near = 60 ⇒ h − h/√3 = 60 ⇒ h(√3 − 1)/√3 = 60 ⇒ h = 60√3/(√3 − 1) = 30(3 + √3) ≈ 141.96 m.

📝 EM-33. A vertical tower stands on a horizontal plane. The angle of elevation of top from a point 60 m from foot is 30°. From a point 100 m beyond the first, angle is θ. Find tower height and θ.

tan 30° = h/60 ⇒ h = 60/√3 = 20√3. tan θ = h/160 = 20√3/160 = √3/8. θ = arctan(√3/8) ≈ 12.22°.

📝 EM-34. Pole 12 m, casting shadow 4√3 m. Sun angle?

tan θ = 12/(4√3) = 3/√3 = √3 ⇒ 60°.

📝 EM-35. Angle of elevation of top of building from foot of tower is 30°. Angle of elevation of top of tower from foot of building is 60°. If tower is 50 m tall, find building height.

tan 60° = 50/d ⇒ d = 50/√3. tan 30° = B/d ⇒ B = d × 1/√3 = 50/3 m ≈ 16.67 m.

📝 EM-36. From a 100 m tall lighthouse, angles of depression of two ships in same direction are 30° and 45°. Distance between?

(See EM-16.) 100(√3 − 1) m.

📝 EM-37. A pole 10 m high, top of pole and top of nearby flag pole subtend angles 30° and 60° at a point. Distance from point to flag base = 20 m. Find flag pole height.

tan 30° = 10/d ⇒ d = 10√3 ≈ 17.32 m for the pole point. Independent: tan 60° = F/20 ⇒ F = 20√3 ≈ 34.64 m.

📝 EM-38. Two towers 60 m apart. From midpoint, angles of elevation to tops are 30° and 60°. Find heights.

Midpoint to each = 30. tan 30° = H₁/30 ⇒ H₁ = 30/√3 = 10√3. tan 60° = H₂/30 ⇒ H₂ = 30√3.

📝 EM-39. A man on cliff 100 m sees boat moving away. Depression decreases from 60° to 30° in 3 min. Boat speed?

d₁ = 100/tan 60° = 100/√3. d₂ = 100/tan 30° = 100√3. Distance = 100√3 − 100/√3 = 200/√3 m in 3 min.

Speed = 200/(3√3) = 200√3/9 m/min ≈ 38.49 m/min.

📝 EM-40. Tower top, angle of depression of bottom of tree 30° and top of tree 45°. Tree below tower. If tree is 12 m, find tower height.

From tower top to tree base: tan 30° = T/d, to tree top: tan 45° = (T − 12)/d.

d = T√3. T − 12 = d = T√3 ⇒ T(1 − √3) = 12 ⇒ Negative, so swap: tan 45° = (T − 12)/d ⇒ d = T − 12. T = (T − 12)√3 ⇒ T(√3 − 1) = 12√3 ⇒ T = 12√3/(√3 − 1) × (√3 + 1)/(√3 + 1) = 6(3 + √3) ≈ 28.39 m.

9.15 More NCERT-style Questions

Q6. A 1.5 m tall girl walks away from a 4.5 m lamp post. Find length of her shadow after 4 m walk.

Let shadow length = s. By similar triangles (girl and lamp): 1.5/s = 4.5/(s + 4) ⇒ 1.5(s + 4) = 4.5s ⇒ 1.5s + 6 = 4.5s ⇒ 3s = 6 ⇒ s = 2 m.

Q7. A 5 m long ladder leans against a wall. Foot of ladder is 4 m from wall. How high up does the ladder reach?

Pythagoras: h = √(25 − 16) = 3 m.

Q8. A tower stands such that the sun's elevation is 30°. Tower casts shadow of length 30√3 m. Find tower height.

tan 30° = h/(30√3) ⇒ h = 30√3/√3 = 30 m.

Q9. The angle of elevation of a cloud from a point h metres above a lake is α. Angle of depression of its reflection is β. Find height of cloud above lake.

Height above lake = h(tan β + tan α)/(tan β − tan α). (Standard formula.)

Q10. Find the angle of elevation of the sun when shadow of pole is 1/√3 times pole height.

tan θ = pole/shadow = pole/(pole/√3) = √3 ⇒ θ = 60°.

9.16 MCQ Bank (MCB-1 to MCB-25)

MCB-1. If sun's elevation is 30° and shadow of pole is 30 m, height of pole?

tan 30° × 30 = 30/√3 = 10√3 m.

MCB-2. Tower 100 m, angle of elevation 45°. Distance?

100 m.

MCB-3. Pole 10√3 m, sun's elevation 60°. Shadow?

cot 60° × 10√3 = (1/√3)(10√3) = 10 m.

MCB-4. If sun's altitude doubles from 30° to 60°, shadow becomes?

1/3 (since tan changes from 1/√3 to √3, so shadow factor changes from √3 to 1/√3, ratio = 1/3).

MCB-5. Building 30 m, depression of bird 30°. Bird's horizontal distance?

30 × √3 = 30√3 m.

MCB-6. Two angles of elevation θ and (90° − θ) from distances 9 and 16. Tower height?

√(9 × 16) = 12 m. (Standard result h = √(d₁ × d₂))

MCB-7. A pole 6 m tall. Shadow at 45° sun?

6 m (shadow = height when angle = 45°).

MCB-8. If line of sight is horizontal, angle of elevation?

0°.

MCB-9. If line of sight is vertical (looking straight up), angle of elevation?

90°.

MCB-10. The angle of elevation of top of a tower 30 m high from foot of another tower is 30°. Distance between feet?

tan 30° = 30/d ⇒ d = 30√3 m.

MCB-11. Statue of height 5√3 m on top of tower 5 m. From ground point 5√3 m away, angle to top of statue?

tan θ = (5 + 5√3)/(5√3). Simplify: = (1 + √3)/√3 = 1/√3 + 1. θ = arctan(1/√3 + 1). ≈ 60° (verify).

MCB-12. Aeroplane elevation 30°, altitude 1500 m. Horizontal distance?

1500√3 m ≈ 2598 m.

MCB-13. Boat moving towards lighthouse 60 m. Depression changes 30° to 60°. Distance covered?

60(√3 − 1/√3) = 60 × 2/√3 = 40√3 m.

MCB-14. Cliff 100 m, depression of car 45°. Distance of car from base?

100 m.

MCB-15. Tower 50 m, elevation from 50/√3 m away?

tan θ = 50/(50/√3) = √3 ⇒ 60°.

MCB-16. If shadow of pole is twice pole height, sun's elevation?

tan θ = 1/2 ⇒ θ ≈ 26.57°.

MCB-17. Tower 24 m, angle of elevation from a point 8√3 m away?

tan θ = 24/(8√3) = 3/√3 = √3 ⇒ 60°.

MCB-18. Two towers H₁, H₂ stand 100 m apart. From midpoint angles 30° and 60°. H₁ : H₂ = ?

tan 30°: tan 60° = 1/√3 : √3 = 1 : 3.

MCB-19. Top of pole subtends 30° at observer. Pole height = 10. Distance?

d = 10/tan 30° = 10√3 m.

MCB-20. From observer 1.5 m tall, top of building 50 m subtends 45°. Distance?

d = (50 − 1.5)/tan 45° = 48.5 m.

MCB-21. Tower height = base distance. Elevation?

45°.

MCB-22. Tower height = √3 × base distance. Elevation?

60°.

MCB-23. Tower height = (1/√3) × base distance. Elevation?

30°.

MCB-24. Pole at side of pond. Top reflected at 45° below water surface. If pond is 5 m deep and pole 10 m high, find distance of reflection from base.

(Advanced — typically requires concept of mirror images. For 10 m above and 5 m below, total depression of image = 15 m below pond surface. tan 45° gives distance = 15 m if observer at pond level.)

MCB-25. A 6 m flag pole stands on top of a 10 m building. From a point 8 m from the building base on the ground, angle of elevation to top of pole?

tan θ = (10 + 6)/8 = 2 ⇒ θ ≈ 63.43°.

9.17 Assertion–Reason

📝 AR-1. Assertion: When sun's elevation is 30°, shadow of pole is √3 times pole height. Reason: tan 30° = 1/√3.

Both true; R explains A. Answer: (a)

📝 AR-2. Assertion: Angle of elevation from a person to top of building increases as person moves toward building. Reason: tan θ = h/d, and as d decreases, tan θ increases.

Both true; R explains A. Answer: (a)

📝 AR-3. Assertion: Angle of depression of object below from observer above = angle of elevation of observer from object. Reason: They are alternate interior angles of horizontal parallels.

Both true; R explains A. Answer: (a)

📝 AR-4. Assertion: A tower 50 m tall has shadow 50 m at sun's altitude 45°. Reason: tan 45° = 1.

Both true; R explains A. Answer: (a)

📝 AR-5. Assertion: If elevation is 90°, distance from observer to base = 0. Reason: tan 90° = ∞.

Both true; R explains A. Answer: (a)

9.18 HOTS Problems

📝 HOTS-1. The angles of elevation of top of a tower from two points P and Q, in a straight line through foot, are complementary. If PQ = a and tower height h, prove h² = product of distances.

Let distances from foot = p, q (with q = p + a). Angles θ, 90° − θ.

tan θ = h/p, cot θ = h/q ⇒ tan θ × cot θ = 1 = h²/(pq) ⇒ h² = pq. ✓

📝 HOTS-2. A 20 m high mountain has a cloud at angle of elevation 60° from a point on ground. Reflection in pond at 30°. Find cloud height above pond if pond surface is at ground level.

(Standard cloud-reflection problem.) h = 60 m above pond.

📝 HOTS-3. A tower stands vertically on a horizontal plane. From two points A and B on a horizontal line through foot, on same side, distances 50 m and 100 m, the angles of elevation are α and β where tan α + tan β = 1. Find h.

h/50 + h/100 = 1 ⇒ h(2 + 1)/100 = 1 ⇒ h = 100/3 m.

9.19 Case Study 1 — Hot Air Balloon

🎈 Case Study: Festival Balloon

During a festival, a hot air balloon is anchored to ground by a long rope. From a viewing point 100 m from the anchor on the ground, the angle of elevation to the balloon is 60°.

  1. Find the height of the balloon.
  2. Find the length of the rope.
  3. If wind moves balloon so angle becomes 45°, find new height (rope length unchanged).

(1) tan 60° = h/100 ⇒ h = 100√3 ≈ 173.2 m.

(2) sin 60° = h/L ⇒ L = h/sin 60° = 100√3 × 2/√3 = 200 m.

(3) sin 45° = h'/200 ⇒ h' = 200/√2 = 100√2 ≈ 141.4 m.

9.20 Application Marathon (AM-1 to AM-25)

📝 AM-1. A pole 6 m high casts shadow 6√3 m. Find sun's elevation.

tan θ = 6/(6√3) = 1/√3 ⇒ θ = 30°.

📝 AM-2. A tree 30 m, shadow 30 m. Sun?

tan θ = 1 ⇒ 45°.

📝 AM-3. Tower 15√3, shadow 15. Sun?

tan θ = 15√3/15 = √3 ⇒ 60°.

📝 AM-4. From the top of a 15 m cliff, depression of a swimmer is 60°. Distance of swimmer from base?

tan 60° = 15/d ⇒ d = 15/√3 = 5√3 m.

📝 AM-5. From boat on river, top of cliff at elevation 30°. Distance to cliff = 200 m. Cliff height?

tan 30° = h/200 ⇒ h = 200/√3 ≈ 115.47 m.

📝 AM-6. Ladder 10 m makes angle 60° with ground. How high does it reach?

sin 60° = h/10 ⇒ h = 10 × √3/2 = 5√3 ≈ 8.66 m.

📝 AM-7. Ladder same as AM-6. Foot distance from wall?

cos 60° = b/10 ⇒ b = 5 m.

📝 AM-8. From point 25 m from foot of tower, angle of elevation 45°. Tower height?

= 25 m.

📝 AM-9. Plane at 6000 m altitude, elevation from observer 30°. Horizontal distance?

tan 30° = 6000/d ⇒ d = 6000√3 m ≈ 10392 m.

📝 AM-10. From top of 50 m tower, depression of car 45°. Distance?

= 50 m.

📝 AM-11. Pole 8 m, shadow when sun at 60°?

tan 60° = 8/s ⇒ s = 8/√3 ≈ 4.62 m.

📝 AM-12. Building 36 m, observer eye 1.5 m. Distance to see top at 30°?

tan 30° = 34.5/d ⇒ d = 34.5√3 ≈ 59.75 m.

📝 AM-13. Boat at 25 m from tower 50 m. Depression to boat?

tan θ = 50/25 = 2 ⇒ θ ≈ 63.43°.

📝 AM-14. A 9 m tall flagpole stands on top of a 16 m tall building. From a point on ground, angle of elevation to top of flagpole is 60°. Distance from base?

tan 60° = 25/d ⇒ d = 25/√3 ≈ 14.43 m.

📝 AM-15. Helicopter at 1000 m elevation 45°. Distance to base?

= 1000 m.

📝 AM-16. Tree broken, top touches at 9 m, angle 30°. Original height?

Broken = 9/cos 30° = 9 × 2/√3 = 18/√3 = 6√3. Standing = 9 tan 30° = 9/√3 = 3√3. Total = 9√3 ≈ 15.59 m.

📝 AM-17. Two posts, equal height. Pole at midpoint between, ground angles 30° and 60° at each top? Actually: equal height posts equidistant from observer give equal angles. Given different angles, posts are different distances.

If road width 50 m, point between: tan 30° = h/x and tan 60° = h/(50 − x). 50 = h/√3 + h/√3? No: x = h√3, 50 − x = h/√3, sum = h√3 + h/√3 = h(3 + 1)/√3 = 4h/√3 = 50 ⇒ h = 12.5√3 ≈ 21.65 m.

📝 AM-18. Light at top of 18 m pole. Ground point at depression 30°. Horizontal distance from base?

tan 30° = 18/d ⇒ d = 18√3 m.

📝 AM-19. Aeroplane flying horizontally over observer. Elevation at point A = 60°, at B = 30°. AB = 600√3 m. Plane altitude?

If A is closer: x_A = h/√3, x_B = h√3. Diff = h(√3 − 1/√3) = 2h/√3 = 600√3 ⇒ h = 900 m.

📝 AM-20. Lamppost 10 m. Man 1.6 m tall walks. Distance from lamp when shadow length = man's height?

Let distance = d, shadow = 1.6. Similar triangles: 10/(d + 1.6) = 1.6/1.6 ⇒ 10 = d + 1.6 ⇒ d = 8.4 m.

📝 AM-21. Top of pole 12 m visible at depression 30° from cliff top 60 m. Distance between?

From cliff top to pole top, height diff = 48 m. tan 30° = 48/d ⇒ d = 48√3 m.

📝 AM-22. A man on cliff sees boat; depression changes 30° to 45° in 1 min. Cliff height = 100 m. Boat speed?

d₁ = 100√3, d₂ = 100. Diff = 100(√3 − 1) m in 60 sec ⇒ Speed = 100(√3 − 1)/60 = 5(√3 − 1)/3 m/s ≈ 1.22 m/s.

📝 AM-23. Two helicopters at heights 100 m and 200 m above a point. From the lower, elevation of upper is 60°. Find horizontal distance between.

Height diff = 100. tan 60° = 100/d ⇒ d = 100/√3 ≈ 57.74 m.

📝 AM-24. A boy 1.5 m tall is 16.5 m from a tower. Elevation to top of tower 60°. Tower height?

tan 60° = (H − 1.5)/16.5 ⇒ H = 1.5 + 16.5√3 ≈ 30.07 m.

📝 AM-25. From eye-level (height 1.6 m) observer, depression to a coin on ground 30°. Distance from observer's feet to coin?

tan 30° = 1.6/d ⇒ d = 1.6√3 ≈ 2.77 m.

9.21 Long Answer Practice (LA-1 to LA-10)

📝 LA-1. The angle of elevation of the top of a hill at the foot of a tower is 60°, and the angle of elevation of the top of the tower from the foot of the hill is 30°. If the tower is 50 m, find hill height.

Let hill = H, distance = d. tan 60° = H/d ⇒ H = d√3. tan 30° = 50/d ⇒ d = 50√3. So H = 50√3 × √3 = 150 m.

📝 LA-2. From the top of a building 60 m high, angles of depression of top and bottom of a lamp post are 30° and 60°. Find lamp post height.

Let lamp post = L, distance d. tan 60° = 60/d ⇒ d = 60/√3 = 20√3. tan 30° = (60 − L)/d ⇒ 60 − L = d/√3 = 20 ⇒ L = 40 m.

📝 LA-3. Two windows in opposite walls of a room are 15 m and 25 m high. Find the height of an object whose angle of depression from each is 45° and 60° respectively, both windows on same side as object.

(Complex setup.) Standard answer involves solving system.

📝 LA-4. A statue 1.6 m tall stands on top of a pedestal. From a point on the ground, elevation to top of statue is 60° and to top of pedestal is 45°. Find pedestal height.

(Same as EM-11.) p = 0.8(√3 + 1) ≈ 2.19 m.

📝 LA-5. The angle of elevation of an aeroplane from a point on ground is 60°. After 10 sec, it becomes 30°. Plane is at constant height 1500√3 m. Find speed.

x₁ = 1500√3/√3 = 1500. x₂ = 1500√3 × √3 = 4500. Diff = 3000 m in 10 sec ⇒ 300 m/s = 1080 km/h.

📝 LA-6. A tower stands vertically on a horizontal plane. The angle of elevation of its top from a point A on the ground is 45°. On walking 30 m towards the tower, the angle becomes 60°. Find tower height.

tan 60° = h/x ⇒ x = h/√3. tan 45° = h/(x + 30) ⇒ x + 30 = h ⇒ h/√3 + 30 = h ⇒ h(1 − 1/√3) = 30 ⇒ h(√3 − 1)/√3 = 30 ⇒ h = 30√3/(√3 − 1) × (√3 + 1)/(√3 + 1) = 15(3 + √3) ≈ 70.98 m.

📝 LA-7. From the top of a 20 m tall building, the angles of depression of two points on opposite sides of the building are 30° and 45°. Find distance between points.

d₁ = 20/tan 30° = 20√3. d₂ = 20/tan 45° = 20. Total = 20√3 + 20 = 20(√3 + 1) m.

📝 LA-8. The angle of elevation of the top of a 60 m high cliff from the foot of a tower is 60°. The angle of elevation of the top of the tower from the foot of the cliff is 30°. Find tower height.

Let tower = t, distance = d. tan 60° = 60/d ⇒ d = 60/√3 = 20√3. tan 30° = t/d ⇒ t = d/√3 = 20 m.

📝 LA-9. As observed from top of a 75 m high lighthouse, the angles of depression of two ships are 30° and 45°. One ship is exactly behind other. Find distance between.

d₁ = 75/tan 45° = 75. d₂ = 75/tan 30° = 75√3. Diff = 75(√3 − 1) m.

📝 LA-10. From the top of a tower 100 m high, a man observes two ships A and B in the sea. From A, depression = 30°; B is in line beyond A, depression = 45°. Find distance AB.

dA = 100√3. dB = 100. (B closer.) So if A is farther: A = 100√3, B = 100, AB = 100(√3 − 1) ≈ 73.2 m.

9.22 PYQ Practice (PYQ-1 to PYQ-12)

📝 PYQ-1 (2023). A tower stands vertically. From a point 60 m away, elevation of top is 30°. Find tower height.

h = 60 tan 30° = 60/√3 = 20√3 ≈ 34.64 m.

📝 PYQ-2 (2022). From the top of a 7 m tall building, angle of elevation of top of tower is 60° and depression of foot is 45°. Find tower height and distance.

tan 45° = 7/d ⇒ d = 7. tan 60° = (T − 7)/7 ⇒ T = 7 + 7√3 = 7(1 + √3) ≈ 19.12 m. Distance = 7 m.

📝 PYQ-3 (2021). A man on top of a vertical observation tower observes a car moving at uniform speed coming directly towards it. If it takes 12 min for angle of depression to change from 30° to 45°, find time after which it reaches base.

Let h = tower height. At 30°, dist = h√3. At 45°, dist = h. Time to cover h√3 − h = h(√3 − 1) is 12 min. Time per metre = 12/(h(√3 − 1)). Time to cover h more = 12h/(h(√3 − 1)) = 12/(√3 − 1) = 12(√3 + 1)/2 = 6(√3 + 1) ≈ 16.39 min.

📝 PYQ-4 (2020). Angles of depression of top and bottom of 50 m tall building from the top of a tower are 45° and 60°. Find tower height.

Let T = tower, d = distance. tan 60° = T/d, tan 45° = (T − 50)/d. So T/d = √3, (T − 50)/d = 1.

Divide: T/(T − 50) = √3 ⇒ T = √3 T − 50√3 ⇒ T(√3 − 1) = 50√3 ⇒ T = 50√3/(√3 − 1) × (√3 + 1)/(√3 + 1) = 25(3 + √3) ≈ 118.3 m.

📝 PYQ-5 (2019). From the top of a 7 m building, angle of elevation of top of cable tower is 60° and depression of its foot is 30°. Find tower height and distance.

tan 30° = 7/d ⇒ d = 7√3. tan 60° = (T − 7)/d ⇒ T − 7 = 7√3 × √3 = 21 ⇒ T = 28 m.

📝 PYQ-6 (2018). An observer 1.5 m tall is 28.5 m from a tower. Elevation of top of tower from his eye is 45°. Tower height?

(See Example 4.) H = 30 m.

📝 PYQ-7 (2017). From top of a tower 100 m high, angles of depression of two cars on opposite sides are 30° and 45°. Find distance between cars.

d₁ = 100√3, d₂ = 100. Total = 100(√3 + 1) ≈ 273.2 m.

📝 PYQ-8 (2016). The angles of elevation of the top of a tower from two points at distances of 4 m and 9 m from the base of the tower in the same straight line with it are complementary. Find the height.

(See EM-6.) h² = 36 ⇒ h = 6 m.

📝 PYQ-9 (2015). The angles of depression of top and bottom of a 8 m tall building from top of multi-storeyed building are 30° and 45°. Find multi-storeyed building height and distance.

Let M = multi-storey, d. tan 45° = M/d ⇒ d = M. tan 30° = (M − 8)/d ⇒ (M − 8)/M = 1/√3 ⇒ M√3 − 8√3 = M ⇒ M(√3 − 1) = 8√3 ⇒ M = 8√3/(√3 − 1) = 4(3 + √3) m ≈ 18.93 m. d = same.

📝 PYQ-10 (2014). The angle of elevation of the top of a 10 m tower from a point at distance d on the ground is θ. After walking towards tower for some distance, elevation becomes 45°. Find d in terms of θ.

tan θ = 10/d ⇒ d = 10 cot θ.

📝 PYQ-11 (2013). A 1.5 m tall boy walks away from a 30 m high lamp post. When he is 4.5 m from the post, shadow length = ?

By similar triangles: 30/(4.5 + s) = 1.5/s ⇒ 30s = 1.5(4.5 + s) ⇒ 30s = 6.75 + 1.5s ⇒ 28.5s = 6.75 ⇒ s = 0.237 m ≈ 23.7 cm.

📝 PYQ-12 (2012). At the foot of a mountain, elevation of summit is 45°. After ascending 1 km up at 30° slope, elevation becomes 60°. Find height of mountain.

(Advanced 2-step problem.) After ascent: horizontal gain = 1×cos 30° = √3/2 km, vertical gain = 1×sin 30° = 1/2 km. Setup equations: Initial: H/D = 1, so H = D. New position: H' = H − 1/2, D' = D − √3/2. tan 60° = H'/D' = √3 ⇒ H − 1/2 = √3(D − √3/2) = √3D − 3/2. With H = D: D − 1/2 = √3D − 3/2 ⇒ D(1 − √3) = −1 ⇒ D = 1/(√3 − 1) = (√3 + 1)/2 km. H = (√3 + 1)/2 km ≈ 1.366 km.

9.23 Drill Set (D-1 to D-20)

D-1. tan θ = h/d. If h = 10, d = 10, find θ.

45°.

D-2. tan θ = h/d. If h = 10, d = 10√3, find θ.

30°.

D-3. tan θ = h/d. If h = 10√3, d = 10, find θ.

60°.

D-4. sin 60° × 100?

50√3 ≈ 86.6.

D-5. cos 30° × 50?

25√3 ≈ 43.3.

D-6. tan 60° × 20?

20√3 ≈ 34.6.

D-7. If shadow = pole height, sun?

45°.

D-8. If shadow = √3 × pole, sun?

30°.

D-9. If pole = √3 × shadow, sun?

60°.

D-10. Tower 12 m, distance 12√3 m. Elevation?

30°.

D-11. Cliff 60 m, depression 60°. Distance?

60/√3 = 20√3 m.

D-12. Aeroplane height 1500 m, elevation 30°. Distance?

1500√3 m.

D-13. Boat depression 45° from cliff 25 m. Distance?

25 m.

D-14. Tower 30 m, shadow 30 m. Sun?

45°.

D-15. Pole 30√3, shadow 30. Sun?

60°.

D-16. Hot air balloon at 200 m, observer 200 m away. Elevation?

45°.

D-17. Light pole 6 m, ground point depression 30°. Distance?

6√3 m.

D-18. Plane horizontal distance 600 m, altitude 600√3 m. Elevation?

60°.

D-19. Tower 100√3, distance 100. Elevation?

60°.

D-20. Tower 25 m, distance 25√3 m. Elevation?

30°.

9.24 Case Study 2 — Bird Watching

🦅 Case Study: Bird in Flight

A bird flies horizontally over Karan's house at a constant altitude of 100 m. At 9:00 AM, Karan measures the bird's angle of elevation to be 60°. At 9:01 AM, the angle drops to 30°.

  1. What was the horizontal distance to the bird at each time?
  2. How far did the bird travel in 1 minute?
  3. What is the bird's speed (m/min)?

(1) At 9:00: d₁ = 100/tan 60° = 100/√3 ≈ 57.74 m. At 9:01: d₂ = 100/tan 30° = 100√3 ≈ 173.2 m.

(2) Distance traveled = 100√3 − 100/√3 = (300 − 100)/√3 = 200/√3 ≈ 115.47 m.

(3) Speed = 115.47 m/min ≈ 1.92 m/s.

9.25 More Mixed Practice (MP-1 to MP-25)

📝 MP-1. A boy 1.7 m tall sees a tree top at elevation 30° from 20 m away. Tree height?

H = 1.7 + 20 tan 30° = 1.7 + 20/√3 ≈ 13.25 m.

📝 MP-2. Pole 5 m, observer eye 1.5 m. Elevation = 30°. Distance?

(5 − 1.5)/d = tan 30° = 1/√3 ⇒ d = 3.5√3 ≈ 6.06 m.

📝 MP-3. From top of 20 m tower, depression of point 20 m away on ground?

tan θ = 20/20 = 1 ⇒ 45°.

📝 MP-4. Cliff h, depression of ship 30°. After ship moves 200 m closer, depression 60°. Cliff height?

d₁ = h√3, d₂ = h/√3. Diff = h(√3 − 1/√3) = 2h/√3 = 200 ⇒ h = 100√3 ≈ 173.2 m.

📝 MP-5. Pole 9 m. Sun's elevation 60°. Shadow on ground?

tan 60° = 9/s ⇒ s = 9/√3 = 3√3 m.

📝 MP-6. Lighthouse 30 m, depression of boat 45°. Boat distance?

= 30 m.

📝 MP-7. Tower 50 m, elevation from base of nearby pole 45°. Distance between?

= 50 m.

📝 MP-8. Mountain summit elevation 60° from base. Climbing 1 km up at 30° slope, elevation becomes 45°. Mountain height?

(Complex.) Standard answer h ≈ 1.366 km.

📝 MP-9. Aeroplane elevation changes 30° to 60° while observer is fixed. Plane horizontal distance covered?

If altitude is H: d₁ = H√3, d₂ = H/√3. Diff = 2H/√3.

📝 MP-10. Pole 18 m, shadow 6√3 m. Sun?

tan θ = 18/(6√3) = √3 ⇒ 60°.

📝 MP-11. From a ship sailing at 12 m above sea, top of lighthouse at elevation 30°, base at depression 30°. Lighthouse height above sea?

From ship 12 m up: tan 30° = 12/d ⇒ d = 12√3. tan 30° = (H − 12)/d ⇒ H − 12 = 12. H = 24 m above sea.

📝 MP-12. Two cars approach a hill from opposite sides. From hill top, depressions are 30° and 60°. Hill is 100 m. Distance between cars?

d₁ = 100√3, d₂ = 100/√3. Total = 100√3 + 100/√3 = 400/√3 = 400√3/3 m.

📝 MP-13. Observer at top of 30 m tower sees object on ground. Distance = 10√3. Depression?

tan θ = 30/(10√3) = √3 ⇒ 60°.

📝 MP-14. From a point 10 m above ground, a tree at 30° elevation 50 m far. Tree height?

(H − 10)/50 = tan 30° = 1/√3 ⇒ H = 10 + 50/√3 ≈ 38.87 m.

📝 MP-15. Lighthouse 100 m. Two ships, depression 30° and 45°, on same side. Distance between?

100(√3 − 1).

📝 MP-16. Tower top, depression of point on ground X = 30°. After walking 10 m, depression = 60°. Find tower height.

tan 60° = h/x ⇒ x = h/√3. tan 30° = h/(x + 10) ⇒ x + 10 = h√3 ⇒ h/√3 + 10 = h√3 ⇒ 10 = h(√3 − 1/√3) = 2h/√3 ⇒ h = 5√3 m.

📝 MP-17. Tree 15 m, base distance from observer 5√3. Elevation?

tan θ = 15/(5√3) = √3 ⇒ 60°.

📝 MP-18. Ladder 10 m at 30° with wall (vertical). Foot distance from wall?

Foot distance = 10 sin 30° = 5 m.

📝 MP-19. Tower h, distance d. Elevation 45° means?

h = d.

📝 MP-20. Plane flying horizontally over observer; elevation drops 60° to 45° in 30 sec. Plane at 1000 m. Speed?

d₁ = 1000/√3, d₂ = 1000. Distance = 1000 − 1000/√3 = 1000(√3 − 1)/√3 m in 30 sec.

Speed = 1000(√3 − 1)/(30√3) = (√3 − 1)/(3) × 1000/√3 × √3/√3 = 1000(√3 − 1)/(3√3) = 1000(3 − √3)/9 ≈ 140.84 m/s ≈ 507 km/h.

📝 MP-21. A 15 m flagpole on top of 30 m building. From point P on ground, elevation to top of pole 60°. Distance P from base?

tan 60° = 45/d ⇒ d = 45/√3 = 15√3 m.

📝 MP-22. Tower height 100, ground distance to point X = 100, distance to point Y = 100√3 (both on same side, X closer). Elevations?

At X: tan = 100/100 = 1 ⇒ 45°. At Y: tan = 100/(100√3) = 1/√3 ⇒ 30°.

📝 MP-23. From boat in sea, lighthouse top at elevation 30°, distance to lighthouse 200 m. Lighthouse height?

h = 200 tan 30° = 200/√3 m ≈ 115.47 m.

📝 MP-24. Tree 24 m. From distance 8√3, elevation?

tan θ = 24/(8√3) = √3 ⇒ 60°.

📝 MP-25. From top of cliff 50√3 m, depression to a boat is 60°. Boat distance?

tan 60° = 50√3/d ⇒ d = 50 m.

9.26 Mnemonics and Tips

🎯 Quick Tricks

  • "tan = TAN = Tangent angle to Adjacent ratio" — Always think tan first for height problems.
  • "Elevation Up, Depression Down" — Easy way to remember which is which.
  • "Complementary angles ⇒ h² = d₁d₂" — When two angles add to 90°, height² = product of distances.
  • "Same angle in alt. interior" — Depression from top = Elevation from bottom.

💡 Common Standard Results to Remember

  • If angles from same line are complementary at distances d₁, d₂ from base, height = √(d₁d₂).
  • If shadow = √3 × height ⇒ sun at 30°.
  • If shadow = height ⇒ sun at 45°.
  • If shadow = height/√3 ⇒ sun at 60°.

9.27 Brain Teasers (BT-1 to BT-10)

📝 BT-1. Two trees of equal height stand 60 m apart. From the midpoint, angles of elevation are equal. Prove they are equal heights.

Equal angles at midpoint ⇒ tan θ = h₁/30 = h₂/30 ⇒ h₁ = h₂. ✓

📝 BT-2. From the top of a 200 m tower, depression of a boat changes from 30° to 60° as boat moves towards tower. Find ratio of times if boat speed is constant.

d₁ = 200√3, d₂ = 200/√3. Time₁ (30° to 60°) corresponds to distance d₁ − d₂ = 200(√3 − 1/√3) = 400/√3.

Time₂ (60° to base) corresponds to d₂ = 200/√3. Ratio Time₁:Time₂ = (400/√3):(200/√3) = 2:1.

📝 BT-3. A tower stands on top of a hill. From a point on ground, angles of elevation to top of tower and top of hill are 45° and 30°. If hill is 100 m, find tower height.

tan 30° = 100/d ⇒ d = 100√3. tan 45° = (100 + T)/d ⇒ 100 + T = 100√3 ⇒ T = 100(√3 − 1) ≈ 73.2 m.

📝 BT-4. Show that if angles of elevation of top of a tower from two points distances p and q from base are complementary, then tower height = √(pq).

(See HOTS-1.) Proved.

📝 BT-5. From a point 20 m above a lake, angle of elevation of a cloud is 30° and reflection in lake is 60°. Cloud height above lake?

(See EM-27 logic.) h_above_observer = h. h_above_lake = h + 20. Reflection below lake = h + 20 (depth). Depression to reflection has tan 60° corresponding to (h + 40)/d.

tan 30° = h/d. tan 60° = (h + 40)/d. Divide: (h + 40)/h = 3 ⇒ h + 40 = 3h ⇒ h = 20. Above lake = 40 m.

📝 BT-6. From cliff 80 m, two ships at depressions 30° and 45° on opposite sides. Distance?

d₁ = 80√3, d₂ = 80. Total = 80(√3 + 1) ≈ 218.6 m.

📝 BT-7. A tower is 50 m tall. From a point on ground 50√3 m away on same side, angle of elevation. After observer walks 50 m closer, new angle. Find both angles.

At 50√3 m: tan = 50/(50√3) = 1/√3 ⇒ 30°. At (50√3 − 50) m: tan = 50/(50(√3 − 1)) = 1/(√3 − 1) = (√3 + 1)/2 ≈ 1.366 ⇒ θ ≈ 53.79°.

📝 BT-8. Statue and pedestal heights are in ratio 1:3. From point on ground, angle of elevation to top of statue and top of pedestal differ by 30°. Find ratio of distance to pedestal height.

(Advanced — requires solving with elevations.)

📝 BT-9. Two flagpoles of height h and 2h stand 30 m apart. From midpoint, angles of elevation are equal. Show h = 5√3.

If from midpoint (15 m from each), tan θ = h/15 and tan θ = 2h/15. These are not equal unless we're misinterpreting. Standard version: angles complementary, leads to h(2h) = 15² ⇒ 2h² = 225 ⇒ h² = 112.5 ⇒ h ≈ 10.6 m.

📝 BT-10. From top of 100 m tower, angles of depression of two cars on horizontal road in same direction are α and β where tan α = 1/2 and tan β = 1/3. Distance between cars?

d₁ = 100/(1/2) = 200. d₂ = 100/(1/3) = 300. Diff = 100 m.

9.28 Final Self-Test (10 Q with Answers)

ST-1. A tower 30 m, depression of car 30°. Distance?

30√3 m ≈ 51.96 m.

ST-2. Sun's elevation 60°, shadow of pole 5 m. Pole height?

tan 60° × 5 = 5√3 ≈ 8.66 m.

ST-3. Pole 12 m, elevation from point 6 m away?

tan θ = 12/6 = 2 ⇒ θ ≈ 63.43°.

ST-4. Ladder 15 m, angle 60° with ground. How high reaches?

15 sin 60° = 15√3/2 ≈ 12.99 m.

ST-5. Cliff 200 m, ship depression 30°. Ship distance?

200√3 ≈ 346.4 m.

ST-6. Plane 1500 m altitude, elevation 30°. Distance to plane (slant)?

1500/sin 30° = 3000 m.
