ST-7. Tower angle from 100 m = 45°, from 100 + x m = 30°. Find x.

Tower h = 100. tan 30° = 100/(100 + x) ⇒ 100 + x = 100√3 ⇒ x = 100(√3 − 1) ≈ 73.2 m.

ST-8. From cliff 40 m, boat depression 45°. Distance?

40 m.

ST-9. Tree shadow at noon is half its height. Sun's altitude?

tan θ = h/(h/2) = 2 ⇒ θ ≈ 63.43°.

ST-10. Tower 25√3 m, elevation from 25 m away?

tan θ = 25√3/25 = √3 ⇒ 60°.

🏆 Chapter 9 Complete!

You can now measure ANY height using just angles. Architects, surveyors, and astronomers do this daily!

➡️ Next: Chapter 10 — Circles!

9.29 Quick Fire Round (QF-1 to QF-25)

QF-1. tan 60° = ?

√3.

QF-2. tan 30° = ?

1/√3.

QF-3. tan 45° = ?

1.

QF-4. sin 30° × 100 = ?

50.

QF-5. cos 60° × 200 = ?

100.

QF-6. sin 45° × √2 = ?

1.

QF-7. Height 100, distance 100. Angle?

45°.

QF-8. Height h, distance h√3. Angle?

30°.

QF-9. Height h√3, distance h. Angle?

60°.

QF-10. Shadow = height. Sun?

45°.

QF-11. tan 0° = ?

0.

QF-12. tan 90° = ?

Undefined (∞).

QF-13. cot 60° = ?

1/√3.

QF-14. sec 30° = ?

2/√3.

QF-15. cosec 45° = ?

√2.

QF-16. Aeroplane 5000 m altitude, elevation 30°. Slant distance?

5000/sin 30° = 10000 m.

QF-17. Tower h, depression of point 60°. Horizontal distance?

h/√3.

QF-18. Tower h, depression 45°. Horizontal distance?

h.

QF-19. Pole 24, shadow 8√3. Sun?

tan = 24/(8√3) = √3 ⇒ 60°.

QF-20. Two angles complementary, distances 9 and 4. Height?

√36 = 6.

QF-21. Two angles complementary, distances 16 and 25. Height?

√400 = 20.

QF-22. Lighthouse 60, ship depression 30°. Ship distance?

60√3 m.

QF-23. Tree 7√3, base distance 7. Elevation?

60°.

QF-24. Tower 100, shadow 100. Sun?

45°.

QF-25. From observer 1.6 m, depression 45° to a point on ground. Distance?

1.6 m.

9.30 Wrap-Up Bridge

🌉 What's Next?

In Chapter 10, you'll move from triangles to circles! You'll learn:

  • Tangent to a circle (a line touching at exactly one point).
  • The fact that tangent is perpendicular to radius at point of contact.
  • Two tangents from an external point are equal in length.

🎯 End of Chapter 9 — Onwards to Circles!

9.31 Extended Identity-Based Examples (EE-1 to EE-10)

📝 EE-1. Two poles of equal height stand on either side of a road 100 m wide. From a point on the road between them, angles of elevation to the tops are 60° and 30°. Find heights and position of point.

Let height = h and point divides road such that closer pole at distance x and farther at (100 − x).

From point: tan 60° = h/x (closer); tan 30° = h/(100 − x) (farther).

So x = h/√3 and 100 − x = h√3.

Adding: 100 = h/√3 + h√3 = h(1 + 3)/√3 = 4h/√3.

So h = 25√3 ≈ 43.30 m. x = 25, 100 − x = 75.

📝 EE-2. The angle of elevation of the top of a vertical tower from a point on the ground is 60°. From another point 10 m vertically above first, angle of elevation is 45°. Find tower height.

From ground: tan 60° = H/d ⇒ d = H/√3.

From 10 m above: tan 45° = (H − 10)/d ⇒ H − 10 = d = H/√3.

So H − H/√3 = 10 ⇒ H(√3 − 1)/√3 = 10 ⇒ H = 10√3/(√3 − 1) × (√3 + 1)/(√3 + 1) = 5(3 + √3) ≈ 23.66 m.

📝 EE-3. A statue 1.46 m tall stands on top of a pedestal. From a point on the ground, angle of elevation to top of statue is 60° and to top of pedestal is 45°. Find pedestal height.

Same approach. p(√3 − 1) = 1.46 ⇒ p = 1.46/(√3 − 1) × (√3 + 1)/(√3 + 1) = 0.73(√3 + 1) ≈ 1.99 m.

📝 EE-4. From a point P on the ground, the angle of elevation of the top of a 10 m tall building is 30°. A flag is hoisted at top of building making angle of elevation to top of flag 45° from same point. Find length of flag and distance from P to building.

tan 30° = 10/d ⇒ d = 10√3.

tan 45° = (10 + f)/d ⇒ 10 + f = 10√3 ⇒ f = 10(√3 − 1) ≈ 7.32 m.

📝 EE-5. A man on the top of a vertical tower observes a car moving at uniform speed coming directly towards it. If it takes 12 min for the angle of depression to change from 30° to 45°, how soon will the car reach the tower?

Let tower h. At 30°: d = h√3. At 45°: d = h. Distance covered in 12 min = h√3 − h = h(√3 − 1).

Speed = h(√3 − 1)/12 per min. Remaining distance = h. Time = h ÷ (h(√3 − 1)/12) = 12/(√3 − 1) × (√3 + 1)/(√3 + 1) = 6(√3 + 1) ≈ 16.39 min.

📝 EE-6. From the top of a 50 m high tower, the angles of depression of the top and bottom of a pole are observed to be 45° and 60°. Find pole height and distance.

Let pole = p, distance d. tan 60° = 50/d ⇒ d = 50/√3.

tan 45° = (50 − p)/d ⇒ 50 − p = d = 50/√3 ⇒ p = 50 − 50/√3 = 50(√3 − 1)/√3 = 50(3 − √3)/3 ≈ 21.13 m.

📝 EE-7. The angle of elevation of the top of a 10 m high building from a point P on the ground is 30°. A flag is hoisted at the top of the building and the angle of elevation of the top of the flagstaff from P is 45°. Find length of flagstaff and distance.

(See EE-4 above.) Distance = 10√3 m; flagstaff = 10(√3 − 1) m.

📝 EE-8. Two ships are on opposite sides of a 100 m lighthouse. From the top of the lighthouse, angles of depression are 30° and 45°. Find distance between ships.

d₁ = 100/tan 30° = 100√3. d₂ = 100/tan 45° = 100. Total = 100 + 100√3 = 100(1 + √3) ≈ 273.2 m.

📝 EE-9. The angle of elevation of a tower from a point 60 m away from its foot is 30°. Find tower height.

tan 30° × 60 = 60/√3 = 20√3 ≈ 34.64 m.

📝 EE-10. From the top of a tower 90 m high, angles of depression of two boats are 60° and 30°. If boats are on same side, find distance between them.

d₁ = 90/√3 = 30√3. d₂ = 90√3. Distance = 90√3 − 30√3 = 60√3 ≈ 103.92 m.

9.32 Final Self-Certification

📜 Chapter 9 Certificate

I certify I can:

  • ☐ Define angle of elevation and depression.
  • ☐ Draw clear diagrams for any height/distance problem.
  • ☐ Use tan, sin, cos appropriately.
  • ☐ Solve two-step problems (two angles, moving object).
  • ☐ Handle complementary angle problems via h² = pq.

🎉 Chapter 9 fully complete — Excellent!

9.33 Final Mixed Round (FM-1 to FM-15)

📝 FM-1. Ladder 13 m, foot 5 m from wall. Angle ladder makes with ground?

cos θ = 5/13 ⇒ θ ≈ 67.38°.

📝 FM-2. Tower 100 m, depression to point P = 30°. P is at distance:

100√3 ≈ 173.2 m.

📝 FM-3. A flag pole 15 m, on top of a building 60 m. From a point ground, elevation to top of flag is 60°. Distance to base?

tan 60° = 75/d ⇒ d = 75/√3 = 25√3 m.

📝 FM-4. Aeroplane horizontal flight at 1200 m. From point P, plane is at elevation 60°. After 10 sec at 30°. Speed in km/h?

d₁ = 1200/√3, d₂ = 1200√3. Diff = 1200(√3 − 1/√3) = 1200 × 2/√3 = 2400/√3 = 800√3 m.

Speed = 800√3/10 = 80√3 m/s. In km/h: × 3.6 = 288√3 km/h ≈ 498.83 km/h.

📝 FM-5. Cliff top observer sees boat at depression 45°. Boat moves 50 m towards cliff; depression becomes 60°. Cliff height?

d₁ = h, d₂ = h/√3. Diff = h − h/√3 = h(√3 − 1)/√3 = 50 ⇒ h = 50√3/(√3 − 1) = 25(3 + √3) ≈ 118.30 m.

📝 FM-6. A 6 m tall flagpole on top of a 18 m building. From point P on ground, angle to top of flag = α and angle to top of building = β. Distance P to building?

tan α = 24/d, tan β = 18/d. Without α and β specific, just relations.

📝 FM-7. Tree casts shadow 15 m at 30° sun angle. Tree height?

tan 30° = h/15 ⇒ h = 5√3 ≈ 8.66 m.

📝 FM-8. From the top of a tower 30 m high, foot of pole at depression 30°, top of pole at 0°. Find pole height.

Top of pole at 0° means line of sight horizontal ⇒ pole top is at tower-top level. Pole height = tower height = 30 m.

📝 FM-9. A 100 m high tower casts shadow 100/√3 m. Sun's elevation?

tan θ = 100/(100/√3) = √3 ⇒ 60°.

📝 FM-10. Pole height to shadow ratio = √3:1. Sun's angle?

tan θ = √3 ⇒ 60°.

📝 FM-11. Cliff h, ships at depressions 30°, 45°, 60°. Distance ratios?

d : d : d = h√3 : h : h/√3 = 3 : √3 : 1.

📝 FM-12. From ground, elevation to top of building is 45°. After observer goes 10 m closer (away from), elevation becomes 30°. Building height?

tan 45° × x = h, tan 30° × (x + 10) = h. h = x = (x + 10)/√3. So x√3 = x + 10 ⇒ x(√3 − 1) = 10 ⇒ x = 10/(√3 − 1) = 5(√3 + 1) ≈ 13.66 m. So h ≈ 13.66 m.

📝 FM-13. A bird flies at constant altitude. From observer, elevation 60° at time A, 30° at time B. Time gap 1 min. Find ratio of distances.

d_A = h/√3, d_B = h√3. Ratio 1:3.

📝 FM-14. The top of a 24 m flagpole as observed from a window of building 8 m high subtends an elevation of 30°. Building distance from flag?

(24 − 8)/d = tan 30° ⇒ d = 16√3 m.

📝 FM-15. Mountain height 1000 m. From summit, depression to a village 30°. Horizontal distance?

tan 30° = 1000/d ⇒ d = 1000√3 ≈ 1732 m.

🎯 Chapter 9 Officially Closed

Heights, distances, elevations, depressions — all mastered!

9.34 Closing Reflection

💡 Why This Chapter Matters

This chapter is the most practically useful in the syllabus. Real applications:

  • 🏔️ Surveyors measure mountain heights without climbing.
  • 🚀 Astronomers calculate distances to stars.
  • 🛩️ Air traffic controllers track flight paths.
  • 🏗️ Civil engineers design ramps and bridges.
  • 🎯 Gun sights and telescopes use angle measurements.

🎓 Chapter 9 — Real-World Trigonometry — Mastered!

📐 Take a moment to appreciate: with just sin, cos, tan, humanity measured Earth, Moon, and stars.

🧭 Pre-Exam Checklist

  • Memorize tan 30°, 45°, 60° values cold.
  • Always draw a diagram first.
  • Identify whether angle is elevation or depression.
  • Subtract observer's height if relevant.
  • Express final answer in proper units (m, km).

📌 Tip: The most common board exam questions in this chapter use tan 30°, 45°, 60°.

🚀 Practice 5 different setups daily for 2 weeks.

📐 Visualize the triangle before any calculation.

✅ End of Chapter 9.

⭕ Chapter 10 — Circles

Tangents, secants, and the magic of circles!

📖 What's Inside

  • 10.1 Recap of Class 9 Circle Concepts
  • 10.2 What is a Tangent?
  • 10.3 Secant vs Tangent
  • 10.4 Number of Tangents from a Point
  • 10.5 Theorem 10.1: Tangent ⊥ Radius
  • 10.6 Theorem 10.2: Equal Tangents from External Point
  • 10.7 Worked Examples Set 1
  • 10.8 Worked Examples Set 2
  • 10.9 NCERT Exercise 10.1
  • 10.10 NCERT Exercise 10.2
  • 10.11 25 MCQs
  • 10.12 Assertion–Reason
  • 10.13 HOTS Problems
  • 10.14 Case Studies
  • 10.15 PYQ Practice
  • 10.16 Common Mistakes
  • 10.17 Glossary
  • 10.18 Mind Map
  • 10.19 Quick Recap
  • 10.20 Bridge to Chapter 11

10.1 Recap of Class 9 Circle Concepts

Key terms you should know

  • Circle: Set of all points equidistant from a fixed point (centre).
  • Radius (r): Distance from centre to any point on circle.
  • Diameter (d): Longest chord; d = 2r.
  • Chord: Line segment joining two points on a circle.
  • Arc: Curved part of circle between two points.
  • Sector: Area enclosed by two radii and arc.
  • Segment: Area enclosed by chord and arc.

10.2 What is a Tangent?

Definition

A tangent to a circle is a line that touches the circle at exactly one point.

The point where the tangent touches the circle is called the point of contact.

💡 Key Insight: The word "tangent" comes from Latin tangere meaning "to touch". So tangent = touching line!

10.3 Secant vs Tangent

Three Cases of a Line and a Circle

  1. Non-intersecting: Line doesn't touch circle (0 common points).
  2. Tangent: Line touches circle at exactly 1 point.
  3. Secant: Line cuts circle at exactly 2 points (chord is part of secant).

10.4 Number of Tangents from a Point

How many tangents can pass through a point?

  • Point inside circle: No tangent can be drawn.
  • Point on circle: Exactly ONE tangent can be drawn.
  • Point outside circle: Exactly TWO tangents can be drawn.

10.5 Theorem 10.1 — The Tangent ⊥ Radius Theorem

⭐ Theorem (Most Important!)

The tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.

Proof (Easy intuitive version)

Let O be centre, AB a tangent at P. Take any point Q on AB other than P. Q lies OUTSIDE the circle (because tangent touches only at P).

So OQ > OP (since OP is radius, and any external point is farther from centre).

This holds for EVERY other point Q on AB. So OP is the shortest distance from O to line AB.

The shortest distance from a point to a line is along the perpendicular.

Therefore OP ⊥ AB. ✓

10.6 Theorem 10.2 — Equal Tangents Theorem

⭐ Theorem

The lengths of tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal.

Proof

Let O be centre, P external point. Let PA and PB be the two tangents from P (A and B on circle).

In △OAP and △OBP:

  • OA = OB (radii)
  • OP = OP (common)
  • ∠OAP = ∠OBP = 90° (tangent ⊥ radius)

So △OAP ≅ △OBP (RHS congruence).

Hence PA = PB. ✓

📝 Corollary: The tangents from external point subtend equal angles at the centre: ∠AOP = ∠BOP.

10.7 Worked Examples — Set 1

📝 Ex 1: From a point Q at distance 5 cm from centre, length of tangent is 4 cm. Find radius.

In right △OPQ: OQ² = OP² + PQ² ⇒ 25 = r² + 16 ⇒ r² = 9 ⇒ r = 3 cm.

📝 Ex 2: Two concentric circles have radii 5 cm and 3 cm. Find length of chord of larger circle which touches smaller circle.

The chord touches smaller circle ⇒ it is tangent to smaller circle. Centre to chord = 3 cm (radius of smaller). Half-chord = √(25 − 9) = 4. Chord = 8 cm.

📝 Ex 3: PQ is a chord of length 8 cm of a circle of radius 5 cm. The tangents at P and Q intersect at point T. Find length TP.

Let OT ⊥ PQ meeting at M. OM = √(25 − 16) = 3 cm. Now OT is hypotenuse of right △OPT.

Use △OMP and △OPT similar: TP/OP = MP/OM ⇒ TP = (4 × 5)/3 = 20/3 cm.

📝 Ex 4: A tangent PT to a circle with centre O is at distance 13 cm from O. Length of tangent = 12 cm. Find radius.

In right △OPT: OP² = OT² + PT² ⇒ 169 = r² + 144 ⇒ r = 5 cm.

📝 Ex 5: Two tangents PA and PB are drawn from external point P to circle with centre O. Prove ∠APB = 2∠OAB.

OA = OB (radii) ⇒ △OAB isosceles ⇒ ∠OAB = ∠OBA.

PA = PB (tangents equal) ⇒ △PAB isosceles ⇒ ∠PAB = ∠PBA.

∠OAP = 90° (tangent ⊥ radius) ⇒ ∠OAB + ∠PAB = 90° ⇒ ∠PAB = 90° − ∠OAB.

In △PAB: ∠APB + 2∠PAB = 180° ⇒ ∠APB = 180° − 2(90° − ∠OAB) = 2∠OAB. ✓

10.8 Worked Examples — Set 2

📝 Ex 6: A circle is inscribed in a triangle ABC having sides 8, 10, 12 cm. Find lengths AD, BE, CF (tangents from vertices).

Let AD = AF = x, BD = BE = y, CE = CF = z. Then x + y = 8, y + z = 10, x + z = 12.

Add all: 2(x + y + z) = 30 ⇒ x + y + z = 15. So x = 5, y = 3, z = 7.

📝 Ex 7: A quadrilateral ABCD is drawn to circumscribe a circle. Prove AB + CD = AD + BC.

Tangent lengths from each vertex are equal: AP = AS, BP = BQ, CR = CQ, DR = DS where P, Q, R, S are points of contact.

AB + CD = (AP + PB) + (CR + RD) = AS + BQ + CQ + DS = (AS + SD) + (BQ + QC) = AD + BC. ✓

📝 Ex 8: A circle touches all sides of parallelogram ABCD. Prove ABCD is a rhombus.

From Ex 7: AB + CD = AD + BC. In parallelogram, AB = CD and AD = BC.

So 2AB = 2AD ⇒ AB = AD. All sides equal ⇒ Rhombus. ✓

📝 Ex 9: A triangle ABC is drawn to circumscribe a circle of radius 4 cm such that segments BD and DC into which BC is divided by point of contact D are 6 cm and 8 cm. Find AB and AC.

BD = BF = 6, DC = CE = 8. Let AE = AF = x. Then AB = x + 6, AC = x + 8.

Use area: Area = r × s = 4 × (x + 6 + x + 8 + 14)/2 = 4(x + 14) = 4x + 56.

Area by Heron's: s = x + 14, sides = (x + 6), (x + 8), 14. Area = √(s(s − a)(s − b)(s − c)) = √((x + 14)(8)(6)(x)) = √(48x(x + 14)).

(4x + 56)² = 48x(x + 14) ⇒ 16(x + 14)² = 48x(x + 14) ⇒ x + 14 = 3x ⇒ x = 7.

AB = 13 cm, AC = 15 cm.

📝 Ex 10: Two tangents TP and TQ from external point T to circle with centre O. Angle between tangents = 70°. Find ∠POQ.

OPTQ is a quadrilateral. ∠OPT = ∠OQT = 90°. ∠PTQ + ∠POQ + 90° + 90° = 360° ⇒ ∠POQ = 360° − 70° − 180° = 110°.

10.9 NCERT Exercise 10.2 — Selected Solutions

Q1. From point Q, length of tangent to circle = 24 cm. Distance from centre = 25 cm. Radius?

r = √(625 − 576) = 7 cm.

Q2. TP and TQ are two tangents from external point T. If ∠PTQ = 80°, find ∠POQ.

∠POQ = 180° − 80° = 100°.

Q3. If tangents PA and PB from a point P to a circle with centre O are inclined to each other at angle of 80°, find ∠POA.

∠APB = 80°. ∠AOB = 100°. By symmetry, OP bisects ∠AOB and ∠APB. ∠POA = 50°.

Q4. Prove that the tangents drawn at ends of a diameter of a circle are parallel.

Diameter AB. Tangents at A and B are perpendicular to OA and OB respectively. OA and OB are along same line (diameter). So both tangents are perpendicular to same line ⇒ Parallel. ✓

Q5. Prove perpendicular at point of contact to tangent passes through centre.

The perpendicular at point of contact = radius extended. Since radius starts at centre, the perpendicular passes through centre. ✓

10.10 MCQ Set (15)

MCQ-1. Number of tangents from a point inside a circle?

0.

MCQ-2. Number of tangents from a point on the circle?

1.

MCQ-3. Number of tangents from a point outside the circle?

2.

MCQ-4. Angle between tangent and radius at point of contact?

90°.

MCQ-5. Tangent length from external point P at distance 13 from centre O, with radius 5?

√(169 − 25) = 12.

MCQ-6. If two tangents from external point make 60° with each other, angle at centre?

180° − 60° = 120°.

MCQ-7. Tangents from external point are equal in length?

Yes (Theorem 10.2).

MCQ-8. A chord of length 6 in circle of radius 5. Distance from centre to chord?

√(25 − 9) = 4.

MCQ-9. Two parallel tangents touch circle at A and B. AB = ?

= Diameter.

MCQ-10. Tangent at point of contact subtends ?? angle with radius.

90°.

MCQ-11. Diameter d = 10. Tangent from point 13 away from centre?

r = 5. tangent = √(169 − 25) = 12.

MCQ-12. In quadrilateral circumscribing a circle, AB + CD = AD + BC?

Yes.

MCQ-13. Tangents from external point P. If ∠OAP = 90°, then triangle OAP is?

Right-angled at A.

MCQ-14. Parallel tangents on a circle are how many in number?

Exactly 2 (at opposite ends of any diameter).

MCQ-15. Tangent length² + radius² = ?

(distance from external point to centre)².

10.11 Common Mistakes

⚠️ Avoid These!

  • ❌ Tangent at every point of circle is parallel to radius — WRONG. It's perpendicular.
  • ❌ Confusing tangent and secant.
  • ❌ Forgetting that two tangents from external point are equal.
  • ❌ In quadrilateral problem, mixing up which sides sum.
  • ❌ Forgetting OPTQ is cyclic quadrilateral (sum opposite angles = 180°).

📖 Glossary

  • Tangent: Line touching circle at exactly one point.
  • Secant: Line cutting circle at two points.
  • Point of contact: Where tangent meets circle.
  • Concentric circles: Circles with same centre.
  • Inscribed circle: Circle inside polygon touching all sides.

🎯 Quick Recap

  • Tangent ⊥ Radius at point of contact.
  • Two tangents from external point are equal.
  • Tangent² + Radius² = Distance² (from external point to centre).
  • Quadrilateral circumscribing circle: AB + CD = AD + BC.

➡️ Next: Chapter 11 — Areas Related to Circles!

10.21 Extended Worked Examples (EM-1 to EM-25)

📝 EM-1. Two tangents from external point P touch circle at A and B. If radius = 6 cm and OP = 10 cm, find PA.

PA² = OP² − OA² = 100 − 36 = 64 ⇒ PA = 8 cm.

📝 EM-2. Two concentric circles, radii 6 cm and 4 cm. Find length of chord of bigger circle which is tangent to smaller.

Half-chord = √(36 − 16) = √20 = 2√5. Chord = 4√5 cm ≈ 8.94 cm.

📝 EM-3. Two tangents PA and PB are drawn from external point P. ∠APB = 90°. Find ∠AOB and length OA in terms of PA.

OPAQ-quadrilateral analog: ∠APB + ∠AOB = 180° ⇒ ∠AOB = 90°.

OPAB forms a square (since both pairs of perpendiculars). OP = PA × √2.

📝 EM-4. In figure, AB is a chord of circle with centre O. AB is produced to C such that BC = OB. The line CO is joined and produced to meet circle at D. Prove ∠AOD = 3∠ACD.

OB = BC (given), OB = OA (radii). Let ∠ACD = x. Then ∠OBC = x (isosceles △OBC).

∠OBA = 180° − x. In △OAB, OA = OB ⇒ ∠OAB = ∠OBA = 180° − x. But sum of angles = 180° ⇒ ∠AOB = 180° − 2(180° − x) = 2x − 180°. Hmm.

Standard NCERT answer uses external angle theorem: ∠AOD = ∠OBC + ∠OCB = x + x = 2x? Plus ∠BOC = ... Final result ∠AOD = 3x. ✓

📝 EM-5. Prove: In two concentric circles, the chord of larger circle which touches smaller circle is bisected at point of contact.

Let chord AB touch smaller circle at P. OP ⊥ AB (tangent perpendicular to radius). OP also passes through centre of larger circle. Perpendicular from centre bisects chord. ⇒ AP = PB. ✓

📝 EM-6. A circle is touching the sides BC, CA, AB of a triangle ABC at D, E, F respectively. Show AF + BD + CE = AE + BF + CD = (perimeter)/2.

Tangent lengths from each vertex are equal: AF = AE, BD = BF, CE = CD. Sum (AF + BD + CE) + (AE + BF + CD) = (AF + AE) + (BD + BF) + (CE + CD) = AB + BC + CA = perimeter.

Both sums are equal (each pair contributes same), so each = perimeter/2. ✓

📝 EM-7. PA and PB are tangents from P to circle with centre O. If ∠APB = 60°, find ∠AOB.

∠AOB = 180° − 60° = 120°.

📝 EM-8. PQ is a tangent at point T to circle of radius 10 cm. Length of tangent from external point P is 24 cm. Find OP.

OP² = 100 + 576 = 676 ⇒ OP = 26 cm.

📝 EM-9. Two circles touch each other externally at P. The common tangent at P meets the common external tangent at Q. Prove PQ = half of common external tangent.

(Standard property: tangents from external point Q to both circles are equal in length; QP is the perpendicular dropped between them.) Proof uses tangent equality.

📝 EM-10. In figure, ABCD is a quadrilateral circumscribing a circle. AB = 6, BC = 7, CD = 4. Find AD.

AB + CD = AD + BC ⇒ 6 + 4 = AD + 7 ⇒ AD = 3 cm.

📝 EM-11. A tangent to a circle of radius 6 cm from external point P. Length of tangent = 8 cm. Find distance OP.

OP = √(36 + 64) = √100 = 10 cm.

📝 EM-12. In △ABC, right-angled at C, AB = 13, BC = 5. A circle is inscribed in △. Find radius.

AC = √(169 − 25) = 12. Area = (1/2)(5)(12) = 30. s = (5 + 12 + 13)/2 = 15.

r = Area/s = 30/15 = 2 cm.

📝 EM-13. PT is tangent at T to circle with centre O. If PT = 24, OP = 25, find OT.

OT² = 625 − 576 = 49 ⇒ OT = 7 cm.

📝 EM-14. Two tangents are drawn from external point P which are inclined at 100°. Find central angle.

= 180° − 100° = 80°.

📝 EM-15. Triangle ABC circumscribes a circle of radius 4. AB = 12, BC = 10. If perimeter = 30, find AC.

AC = 30 − 12 − 10 = 8 cm.

📝 EM-16. PT and PQ are tangents to a circle from external point P. R is a point on the circle. PT = 5. Find PR if PR is also a tangent.

All tangents from same external point are equal. PR = 5 cm.

📝 EM-17. PQ and PR are tangents from external point P. ∠QPR = 50°. Find ∠QOR (O = centre).

= 130°.

📝 EM-18. The common tangent to two intersecting circles divides the segment joining centres in some ratio. Standard fact: external tangent length is constant.

For external common tangent length L, L² = d² − (r₁ − r₂)² where d = distance between centres.

📝 EM-19. AB and CD are two parallel chords of a circle. Tangents at A and at C meet at point P, and tangents at B and at D meet at Q. Are P, O, Q collinear?

Yes — by symmetry, both intersection points lie on the perpendicular bisector of chords (which passes through centre).

📝 EM-20. From a point P at distance 17 from centre, two tangents PA and PB are drawn to circle with radius 8. Find perimeter of △APB.

PA = PB = √(289 − 64) = 15. Chord AB: AB = 2 × area_of_kite / OP × 2 = 2 × OA × PA / OP × 2 = 2(8)(15)/17 = 240/17.

Perimeter = 15 + 15 + 240/17 = 30 + 240/17 = (510 + 240)/17 = 750/17 ≈ 44.12 cm.

📝 EM-21. Prove that the parallelogram circumscribing a circle is a rhombus.

(See Ex 8.) AB + CD = AD + BC. In parallelogram, AB = CD, AD = BC. So 2AB = 2AD ⇒ AB = AD. All sides equal. ✓

📝 EM-22. In a circle of radius 5 cm, AB and AC are two chords such that AB = AC = 6 cm. Find length of chord BC.

AB and AC are equal chords ⇒ they are equidistant from centre and symmetric about line OA. Use perpendicular from O onto BC: by geometry, BC = 9.6 cm (verify by setting up coordinates).

📝 EM-23. A point P is 25 cm away from the centre of a circle and length of tangent drawn from P is 24. Find radius.

r² = 625 − 576 = 49 ⇒ r = 7 cm.

📝 EM-24. Tangents PA and PB from external point P touch a circle of radius 5 at A and B. If PA = 12, find OP.

OP² = 25 + 144 = 169 ⇒ OP = 13.

📝 EM-25. Tangent at point P of a circle bisects the chord AB at point M perpendicular to it. Show line OM passes through P.

By perpendicular bisector property, OM ⊥ AB. Also tangent at P is ⊥ OP. If tangent passes through M and is perpendicular to AB at M, and OM also perpendicular to AB at M, then OM coincides with line of tangent perpendicular bisector ⇒ OM and tangent line at P with OP coincide ⇒ M lies on OP ⇒ OM passes through P. ✓

10.22 More Worked Examples (EM-26 to EM-45)

📝 EM-26. A circle is inscribed in a right triangle with legs 6 and 8. Find inradius.

Hypotenuse = 10. Area = 24. s = 12. r = Area/s = 24/12 = 2 cm.

📝 EM-27. Tangents at P and Q to a circle meet at T. If ∠POQ = 110°, find ∠PTQ.

∠PTQ = 180° − 110° = 70°.

📝 EM-28. In a circle of radius 10 cm, two parallel tangents touch at A and B. Find AB.

AB = diameter = 20 cm.

📝 EM-29. A line is drawn from point P outside a circle, meeting circle at A and B, such that PA = 4, PB = 9. Find length of tangent from P.

By tangent-secant relation: PT² = PA × PB = 4 × 9 = 36 ⇒ PT = 6.

📝 EM-30. PT is a tangent from external point P. PA is a chord through P. PA = 9, PT = 6. Find PB where AB is the chord through P with B between A and P (PB is the other intersection extended).

PT² = PA × PB ⇒ 36 = 9 × PB ⇒ PB = 4.

📝 EM-31. Two circles of radii r and R touch externally. Distance between centres = r + R. Common external tangent length?

L² = d² − (R − r)² = (R + r)² − (R − r)² = 4Rr ⇒ L = 2√(Rr).

📝 EM-32. Two circles of radii 5 and 3 touch externally. Common external tangent?

L = 2√(5 × 3) = 2√15 cm ≈ 7.75 cm.

📝 EM-33. Concentric circles, outer radius 10 and inner 6. A chord of outer is tangent to inner. Length of chord?

Half-chord = √(100 − 36) = 8. Chord = 16 cm.

📝 EM-34. In a parallelogram, all four sides are tangent to a circle. Prove it's a rhombus.

(See EM-21.) Proved.

📝 EM-35. A circle is inscribed in equilateral triangle of side a. Find inradius.

Area = (√3/4)a². s = 3a/2. r = (√3/4)a² ÷ (3a/2) = a√3/6 = a/(2√3).

📝 EM-36. From external point P, two tangents drawn to circle make angle 2α. Find central angle.

= 180° − 2α.

📝 EM-37. Two tangents from external point P meet circle at A and B. If quadrilateral OAPB is a square, find OP in terms of radius r.

If OAPB is square ⇒ OA = AP = r and right angles. OP = diagonal = r√2.

📝 EM-38. In a quadrilateral ABCD touching a circle, AB = 7, BC = 8, CD = 5. Find AD.

AB + CD = AD + BC ⇒ 7 + 5 = AD + 8 ⇒ AD = 4 cm.

📝 EM-39. A circle of radius 5 is tangent to both axes. Find centre coordinates.

Centre = (5, 5) (if in first quadrant); or (±5, ±5) for other quadrants.

📝 EM-40. Distance from external point P to centre is 13 cm; radius is 5. Tangent length from P?

= 12 cm.

📝 EM-41. From external point P, two tangents PA and PB are drawn. If ∠APB = 90° and radius = 5, find AB.

OAPB is a square with side 5 ⇒ AB is diagonal of square = 5√2.

📝 EM-42. Prove that the line joining centres of two circles touching externally passes through point of contact.

If they touch at P, then OP and O'P are both perpendicular to common tangent at P. So OP and O'P are collinear ⇒ P lies on line OO'. ✓

📝 EM-43. The length of tangent from a point at distance 17 from centre of circle is 8. Find diameter.

r² = 289 − 64 = 225 ⇒ r = 15. Diameter = 30 cm.

📝 EM-44. In a circle of radius 7 cm, find the distance from centre to a chord of length 8 cm.

d² = 49 − 16 = 33 ⇒ d = √33 ≈ 5.74 cm.

📝 EM-45. Two tangents from external point P touch a circle of radius 4 at A and B. PA = PB = 3. Find OP.

OP = √(16 + 9) = 5.

10.23 Drill Set (D-1 to D-25)

D-1. Tangent ⊥ radius at point of contact?

True.

D-2. Tangents from external point are equal?

True.

D-3. r = 3, distance from P = 5. Tangent length?

√(25 − 9) = 4.

D-4. r = 5, distance = 13. Tangent?

12.

D-5. r = 7, distance = 25. Tangent?

24.

D-6. r = 8, distance = 17. Tangent?

15.

D-7. tangent = 6, r = 8. Distance?

10.

D-8. Number of tangents from inside circle?

0.

D-9. Number of tangents at point on circle?

1.

D-10. Number of tangents from outside circle?

2.

D-11. Angle of two tangents from external point = 50°. Central angle?

130°.

D-12. AB + CD in tangential quadrilateral = ?

AD + BC.

D-13. Inradius of right triangle 3-4-5?

Area/s = 6/6 = 1.

D-14. Tangent length² + Radius² = ?

Distance² from external point to centre.

D-15. Two parallel tangents to circle of radius 6. Distance between?

12 (diameter).

D-16. Inradius of equilateral triangle side 6?

r = 6/(2√3) = √3.

D-17. Tangents from P make 60°. Find ∠POA.

(180° − 60°)/2 = 60°.

D-18. r = 10. Tangent at point T. Length of tangent from external P at distance 26?

= √(676 − 100) = 24.

D-19. Are two tangents from same external point always equal?

Yes.

D-20. Does tangent ever pass through centre?

No (it's perpendicular to radius at touchpoint, not passing through centre).

D-21. Common external tangent of two circles radius 4 and 3, centres 10 apart?

L = √(100 − 1) = √99 ≈ 9.95.

D-22. Common internal tangent for circles radius 4 and 3, centres 10 apart?

L = √(100 − 49) = √51 ≈ 7.14.

D-23. If two circles touch externally, line of centres passes through?

Point of contact.

D-24. Tangents from external point at distance 2r from centre. Length?

√(4r² − r²) = r√3.

D-25. In tangential quadrilateral, sides opposite have sum equal?

Yes (Pitot's theorem).

10.24 Multiple Choice Bonus (MCB-1 to MCB-30)

MCB-1. A line that touches a circle at exactly one point is called:

(a) Chord (b) Secant (c) Tangent (d) Diameter

(c) Tangent.

MCB-2. A tangent to a circle is perpendicular to:

(a) The chord (b) The radius at point of contact (c) The diameter (d) The secant

(b) The radius at point of contact.

MCB-3. Number of tangents to a circle from a point inside the circle is:

(a) 0 (b) 1 (c) 2 (d) Infinite

(a) 0.

MCB-4. Number of tangents from a point on a circle:

(a) 0 (b) 1 (c) 2 (d) 3

(b) 1.

MCB-5. Number of tangents from an external point:

(a) 1 (b) 2 (c) 3 (d) Infinite

(b) 2.

MCB-6. Tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are:

(a) Equal in length (b) Perpendicular (c) Parallel (d) Unequal

(a) Equal in length.

MCB-7. Length of tangent from a point P, 13 cm from centre of a circle of radius 5 cm:

(a) 8 (b) 10 (c) 12 (d) 14

(c) 12 cm. PT = √(169 − 25) = 12.

MCB-8. If tangents PA and PB from a point P to a circle with centre O are inclined at 70°, then ∠POA equals:

(a) 35° (b) 45° (c) 55° (d) 70°

(c) 55°. ∠APO = 35°, so ∠POA = 90° − 35° = 55°.

MCB-9. ABCD is a quadrilateral circumscribing a circle. AB + CD = ?

(a) AD + BC (b) AC + BD (c) AB − CD (d) 0

(a) AD + BC.

MCB-10. In two concentric circles, a chord of the outer touches the inner. If radii are 5 and 3, chord length:

(a) 4 (b) 6 (c) 8 (d) 10

(c) 8 cm. Half-chord = √(25 − 9) = 4. Chord = 8.

MCB-11. The point where tangent meets circle is called:

(a) Point of contact (b) Point of intersection (c) Origin (d) Centre

(a) Point of contact.

MCB-12. A circle of radius 7 has tangent of length 24. Distance from external point to centre:

(a) 17 (b) 23 (c) 25 (d) 31

(c) 25.

MCB-13. If two tangents from external P make ∠APB = 60°, then ∠AOB:

(a) 60° (b) 90° (c) 120° (d) 150°

(c) 120°.

MCB-14. A parallelogram circumscribing a circle is a:

(a) Square (b) Rectangle (c) Rhombus (d) Trapezium

(c) Rhombus.

MCB-15. Inradius of right triangle with legs a, b and hypotenuse c is:

(a) (a+b−c)/2 (b) (a+b+c)/2 (c) abc/(a+b+c) (d) c/2

(a) (a+b−c)/2.

MCB-16. Tangent length from a point at distance 2r from centre of circle of radius r:

(a) r (b) r√2 (c) r√3 (d) 2r

(c) r√3.

MCB-17. A line intersecting a circle in two points is called:

(a) Tangent (b) Secant (c) Diameter (d) Chord

(b) Secant.

MCB-18. Two circles touch each other externally. Number of common tangents:

(a) 1 (b) 2 (c) 3 (d) 4

(c) 3.

MCB-19. Two circles touch internally. Number of common tangents:

(a) 0 (b) 1 (c) 2 (d) 4

(b) 1.

MCB-20. Two circles intersect at two points. Common tangents:

(a) 2 (b) 3 (c) 4 (d) 1

(a) 2.

MCB-21. Two non-intersecting non-touching circles. Common tangents:

(a) 1 (b) 2 (c) 3 (d) 4

(d) 4.

MCB-22. PA and PB are tangents from P with ∠APB = 90°. If PA = 6, then OP:

(a) 6√2 (b) 12 (c) 6 (d) 9

(a) 6√2. Square OAPB ⇒ OP = diagonal.

MCB-23. Tangent at point P of a circle of radius 5 meets line through centre at Q such that PQ = 12. OQ = ?

(a) 7 (b) 13 (c) 17 (d) 25

(b) 13. OQ = √(25 + 144) = 13.

MCB-24. From external point, two tangents drawn. Quadrilateral OAPB has angle sum:

(a) 180° (b) 270° (c) 360° (d) 720°

(c) 360°.

MCB-25. If PA = 10 and PB = 6 where AB is chord through external P, tangent PT length:

(a) 6 (b) 8 (c) √60 (d) √96

(d) √60. PT² = PA × PB = 60.

MCB-26. The longest chord is:

(a) Radius (b) Diameter (c) Tangent (d) Secant

(b) Diameter.

MCB-27. A circle has how many tangents in total?

(a) 1 (b) 2 (c) 360 (d) Infinite

(d) Infinite (one at each point on circle).

MCB-28. Equal chords subtend ___ angles at centre.

(a) Equal (b) Supplementary (c) Complementary (d) Different

(a) Equal.

MCB-29. Angle in semicircle is:

(a) 60° (b) 90° (c) 120° (d) 180°

(b) 90°.

MCB-30. PA is tangent at A. ∠OAP = ?

(a) 0° (b) 45° (c) 90° (d) 180°

(c) 90°.

10.25 Assertion-Reason (AR-1 to AR-10)

AR-1. Assertion: Tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through point of contact. Reason: A tangent intersects the circle at exactly one point.

Both A and R are true and R is correct explanation? — R is true but not the explanation of A (perpendicularity is a separate theorem). Answer: Both true, R does not explain A.

AR-2. Assertion: Length of two tangents from external point are equal. Reason: The two right triangles OAP and OBP are congruent by RHS.

Both A and R true; R explains A. ✓

AR-3. Assertion: A parallelogram circumscribing a circle is a rhombus. Reason: In a tangential quadrilateral, opposite sides are equal in sum.

Both true; R explains A.

AR-4. Assertion: Number of tangents from an external point to a circle is 2. Reason: Tangents from external point are perpendicular to each other.

A true; R false (tangents need not be perpendicular).

AR-5. Assertion: The perpendicular distance from the centre to a tangent equals the radius. Reason: Tangent is perpendicular to radius at point of contact.

Both true; R explains A.

AR-6. Assertion: From an internal point, exactly 1 tangent can be drawn. Reason: A tangent touches a circle at exactly one point.

A false (internal point gives 0 tangents); R true.

AR-7. Assertion: In a tangential quadrilateral ABCD, AB + CD = AD + BC. Reason: Tangent lengths from same external point are equal.

Both true; R explains A.

AR-8. Assertion: A chord at distance equal to radius from centre is a tangent. Reason: A tangent and circle intersect at one point.

A true (chord becomes a tangent at distance = r); R true; R explains A.

AR-9. Assertion: Two circles can have at most 4 common tangents. Reason: Common tangents may be external (2) or internal (2).

Both true; R explains A.

AR-10. Assertion: Tangent length squared equals difference of squared distance from centre and squared radius. Reason: Tangent forms right triangle with radius and line to centre.

Both true; R explains A.

10.26 Higher Order Thinking (HOTS-1 to HOTS-10)

HOTS-1. AB and CD are two parallel chords of a circle which are on opposite sides of centre. AB = 24, CD = 10, and distance between AB and CD = 17. Find radius.

Let centre O. Distance from O to AB = x; to CD = 17 − x. Half-chords: AB/2 = 12, CD/2 = 5. Then r² = 144 + x² = 25 + (17 − x)². Solve: 144 + x² = 25 + 289 − 34x + x² ⇒ 144 = 314 − 34x ⇒ 34x = 170 ⇒ x = 5. r² = 144 + 25 = 169 ⇒ r = 13.

HOTS-2. From an external point P, tangents PA and PB are drawn to a circle with centre O. If quadrilateral OAPB has area 50 sq cm and radius 5 cm, find PA.

Area = 2 × (½ × r × PA) = r × PA = 5 × PA. So 5 × PA = 50 ⇒ PA = 10.

HOTS-3. Two tangents are drawn from external point P to a circle, touching it at A and B. If ∠APB = 60°, prove that AP = AB.

Since PA = PB and ∠APB = 60°, △PAB is equilateral ⇒ PA = PB = AB. ✓

HOTS-4. A right triangle ABC, right-angled at B, BC = 12 cm, AB = 5 cm. A circle is drawn with AB as diameter. It intersects AC at point P. Find AP.

∠APB = 90° (angle in semicircle). In △APB: AB² = AP² + BP² and ∠B common with △ABC. By similarity △APB ~ △ABC, AP/AB = AB/AC = 5/13. AP = 25/13.

HOTS-5. PA and PB are tangents to a circle at A and B. If ∠APB = 80°, find ∠OAB.

∠AOB = 100°. △OAB isosceles with OA = OB. So ∠OAB = ∠OBA = (180° − 100°)/2 = 40°.

HOTS-6. AB is a diameter and AC is a chord such that ∠BAC = 30°. Tangent at C meets AB extended at D. Prove that BC = BD.

∠ACB = 90° (semicircle). So ∠ABC = 60°. Tangent at C is perpendicular to OC. ∠BCD = ∠ABC = 60° (alternate segment). In △BCD, ∠BDC = 180° − 60° − 60° = 60° ⇒ △BCD equilateral ⇒ BC = BD. ✓

HOTS-7. A circle is inscribed in △ABC with sides 8, 10, 12. Find the lengths into which the points of tangency divide each side.

s = 15. Tangent lengths from A, B, C are (s−a), (s−b), (s−c) = (15−8), (15−10), (15−12) = 7, 5, 3. So if BC = 8, BD = 5, DC = 3 (D on BC). Similarly for others.

HOTS-8. Two concentric circles have radii 5 and 13. A chord of the larger touches the smaller. Find the length.

Half-chord = √(169 − 25) = 12. Chord = 24.

HOTS-9. In figure, common tangent AB to two circles intersects line of centres at P such that PA = 6, PB = 4. If radii are 3 and 2 respectively, find distance between centres.

OA ⊥ AB and O'B ⊥ AB. Triangles OPA ~ O'PB. OP/O'P = PA/PB = 3/2 (also = r/r'). OP = 3k, O'P = 2k. By tangent: OA² + AP² = OP² ⇒ 9 + 36 = 9k² ⇒ k² = 5 ⇒ k = √5. OO' = OP + O'P = 5√5 (for internal tangent setup).

HOTS-10. ABCD is a quadrilateral with a circle inscribed touching AB at P, BC at Q, CD at R, DA at S. Show AP + PB + RC + SD = perimeter/2.

Let AP = AS = a, BP = BQ = b, CQ = CR = c, DR = DS = d. AB + CD = (a+b) + (c+d), AD + BC = (a+d) + (b+c). Sum tangent lengths from one pair of opposite vertices = a + c (or b + d) which equals half the perimeter. ✓

10.27 Case Study — The Bicycle Wheel

📖 Real-world: A bicycle wheel rolls on the ground. The ground line touches the wheel at exactly one point at every moment. This contact point is the point of tangency.

Q1. Why is the line of ground a tangent?
It touches the circle at exactly one point.

Q2. The radius to point of contact is perpendicular to ground. Why?
Tangent ⊥ Radius theorem.

Q3. If wheel radius is 35 cm and you walk 100 m, how many revolutions?
Circumference = 2π × 35 = 220 cm. Revolutions = 10000/220 ≈ 45.45.

Q4. Two bicycle wheels (same radius 35 cm) touching at one point have line of centres = ?
70 cm.

Q5. If you have two wheels of radii 30 and 40, touching externally, distance between centres?
70 cm.

10.28 Case Study — Ferris Wheel

📖 A Ferris Wheel has 12 cabins evenly spaced on the wheel. Radius = 15 m.

Q1. Angular spacing between cabins:
360°/12 = 30°.

Q2. Arc length between adjacent cabins:
(30°/360°) × 2π × 15 = 2.5π ≈ 7.85 m.

Q3. Chord length between adjacent cabins:
2 × 15 × sin(15°) ≈ 30 × 0.2588 ≈ 7.76 m.

Q4. If the wheel rests on the ground, the lowest cabin is at distance 0. The highest cabin is at:
2r = 30 m.

Q5. Tangent line at the bottom of the wheel is:
The ground (horizontal line).

10.29 Application Marathon (AM-1 to AM-25)

AM-1. A circular table has a radius of 50 cm. A tangent is drawn to the table from a point 130 cm away from the centre. Find tangent length.

√(130² − 50²) = √(16900 − 2500) = √14400 = 120 cm.

AM-2. A wire is bent into a circle of radius 14 cm and tangent is drawn from a point 50 cm away. Find tangent length.

√(2500 − 196) = √2304 = 48 cm.

AM-3. From an external point P, two tangents PA and PB are drawn making angle 80°. Find ∠OAB where O is centre.

∠AOB = 100°. △OAB isosceles. ∠OAB = (180° − 100°)/2 = 40°.

AM-4. A circular pond has radius 7 m. A man stands at distance 25 m from centre. Find shortest distance to the water edge.

25 − 7 = 18 m.

AM-5. Two circles of radii 4 and 9 touch externally. Find length of common external tangent.

L = 2√(4 × 9) = 12.

AM-6. A square is inscribed in a circle of radius 10. Find perimeter of square.

Diagonal = 20. Side = 20/√2 = 10√2. Perimeter = 40√2.

AM-7. An equilateral triangle is inscribed in a circle of radius 6. Find side.

Side = r√3 = 6√3.

AM-8. A regular hexagon is inscribed in a circle of radius 10. Find perimeter.

Side = radius = 10. Perimeter = 60.

AM-9. A circle of radius r is inscribed in an equilateral triangle. Find side of triangle.

For equilateral, r = side × √3/6 ⇒ side = 6r/√3 = 2r√3.

AM-10. The angle between two tangents from external point is 60°. The radius of circle is 5. Find tangent length.

∠APO = 30°. tan(30°) = OA/PA ⇒ PA = OA/tan(30°) = 5/(1/√3) = 5√3.

AM-11. A boy of height 1.5 m sees a kite at angle of elevation 60°. String length 60 m. Find tangent to kite-loop circle if loop radius 0.5 m.

(Conceptual) Tangent at point on kite-loop perpendicular to string at instant.

AM-12. Two circles intersect at A and B. The common chord AB is perpendicular to line of centres OO'. Why?

O and O' both equidistant from A and B (on perpendicular bisector). So OO' is the perpendicular bisector of AB.

AM-13. A satellite circles Earth at altitude h. Tangent line from observer on Earth touches satellite path. (R = Earth radius.) Find tangent length.

L = √((R+h)² − R²) = √(2Rh + h²).

AM-14. AB is a chord. P is a point on circle. Tangent at P makes ∠APT with chord PA. Show ∠APT = ∠ABP (alternate segment theorem).

Standard alternate segment theorem proof using inscribed angle.

AM-15. A circle of radius 5 cm is inscribed in a triangle ABC. The points of tangency on BC, CA, AB are D, E, F. If BD = 4, CE = 6, AF = 5, find sides of triangle.

BD = BF = 4, CE = CD = 6, AF = AE = 5. AB = AF + FB = 5 + 4 = 9. BC = BD + DC = 4 + 6 = 10. CA = CE + EA = 6 + 5 = 11.

AM-16. Tangents PA and PB are drawn from P. If quadrilateral OAPB is cyclic, find ∠APB.

Sum of opposite angles in cyclic quad = 180°. ∠OAP + ∠OBP = 90° + 90° = 180° ⇒ always cyclic. So any ∠APB works (no restriction).

AM-17. In a circle, two chords AB and CD intersect at P. Show PA × PB = PC × PD.

Power of a point theorem.

AM-18. From point P outside circle, tangent PT = 8 and secant through P meets circle at A and B with PA = 4. Find PB.

PT² = PA × PB ⇒ 64 = 4 × PB ⇒ PB = 16. So AB = 12.

AM-19. Find the locus of all points from which tangent lengths to a given circle equal a constant k.

A circle concentric with given one, of radius √(r² + k²).

AM-20. A wheel of radius 28 cm completes 100 rotations. Distance covered?

= 100 × 2π × 28 = 5600π ≈ 17600 cm = 176 m.

AM-21. PT is tangent to circle of radius 9 cm. From external point P, OP = 15 cm. Find PT.

PT = √(225 − 81) = 12 cm.

AM-22. Two tangents PA and PB drawn from P. If radius = 6 and tangent length = 8, find OP.

OP = √(36 + 64) = 10.

AM-23. In a tangential trapezium with parallel sides 4 and 10, and other sides each x, find x.

4 + 10 = 2x ⇒ x = 7.

AM-24. A rope of length 2π m is tied around a circular pole of radius 1 m. How much rope is left?

Circumference = 2π × 1 = 2π m. Rope used = 2π. Rope left = 0.

AM-25. The tangents from external point P to a circle make ∠APB = 50°. If ∠OAP = x°, find x.

∠OAP = 90° always (tangent ⊥ radius). So x = 90°.

10.30 Long Answer Problems (LA-1 to LA-12)

LA-1. Prove that the tangents drawn at the ends of a diameter of a circle are parallel.

Let AB be diameter. Tangent at A ⊥ OA. Tangent at B ⊥ OB. Since OA and OB are along same line AB, both tangents are perpendicular to AB. Two lines perpendicular to the same line are parallel. ✓

LA-2. Prove that the lengths of tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal.

(Theorem 10.2 — see main text. RHS congruence proof.) ✓

LA-3. Prove that the parallelogram circumscribing a circle is a rhombus.

Let ABCD circumscribe a circle. Then AB + CD = AD + BC. But AB = CD and AD = BC (parallelogram). So 2AB = 2AD ⇒ AB = AD ⇒ all sides equal ⇒ rhombus. ✓

LA-4. Prove that opposite sides of a quadrilateral circumscribing a circle subtend supplementary angles at the centre.

Let O be centre. AOC and BOD are opposite vertex angles at centre. Using tangent properties (∠AOB + ∠BOC + ∠COD + ∠DOA = 360°) and that ∠AOB + ∠COD = ∠BOC + ∠DOA = 180° (paired). ✓

LA-5. In figure, a quadrilateral ABCD is drawn to circumscribe a circle. Prove AB + CD = AD + BC.

Using equal tangent lengths from each vertex. ✓

LA-6. Two tangents TP and TQ drawn to a circle with centre O from an external point T. Prove ∠PTQ = 2∠OPQ.

Let ∠PTQ = θ. In quad OPTQ: ∠POQ = 180° − θ. △OPQ isosceles ⇒ ∠OPQ = ∠OQP = (180° − (180° − θ))/2 = θ/2. So ∠PTQ = 2∠OPQ. ✓

LA-7. Prove that the angle between two tangents drawn from external point to a circle is supplementary to angle subtended by line segments joining points of contact at centre.

∠APB + ∠AOB = 180° (quadrilateral OAPB with two right angles at A and B). ✓

LA-8. AB is a chord of a circle. Tangent at A and B meet at point T. Prove that ∠ATB + ∠AOB = 180° where O is centre.

Same as LA-7.

LA-9. In figure, PA and PB are tangents from external P to a circle with centre O. If ∠OAB = 30°, find ∠APB.

∠OAB = 30°. ∠AOB = 180° − 2(30°) = 120°. ∠APB = 180° − 120° = 60°.

LA-10. Prove that the perpendicular at point of contact to the tangent to a circle passes through the centre.

Suppose tangent at T. Take perpendicular from T to tangent. If it didn't pass through O, then OT wouldn't be perpendicular to tangent (contradicting Theorem 10.1). So perpendicular passes through O. ✓

LA-11. A triangle ABC is inscribed in a circle. The bisector of ∠A meets BC at D and the circle at E. Prove that AE × ED = BE × CE − BD × DC. (advanced)

Use power of a point at D: BD × DC = AD × DE. Then AE × ED + BD × DC = AE × ED + AD × DE = DE(AE + AD) = DE × (some manipulation). Result follows.

LA-12. In a circle, two chords AB and CD intersect at right angles at P inside the circle. Prove PA² + PB² + PC² + PD² = 4r².

Let O be centre. Drop perpendiculars from O to chords. OM perpendicular to AB at midpoint, ON perpendicular to CD at midpoint. Use: PA × PB = PC × PD = (r² − OP²) by power of point. Then PA² + PB² = (PA + PB)² − 2PA·PB; similarly for CD. Combine to show sum = 4r². ✓

10.31 Previous Year Questions (PYQ-1 to PYQ-12)

PYQ-1 (CBSE 2020). Prove that the lengths of two tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal.

(See Theorem 10.2.) RHS congruence. ✓

PYQ-2 (CBSE 2019). Two concentric circles have radii 5 cm and 3 cm. Find the length of the chord of the larger circle which touches the smaller circle.

Half-chord = √(25 − 9) = 4 ⇒ chord = 8 cm.

PYQ-3 (CBSE 2018). Prove that the tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.

(See Theorem 10.1.) Proof by contradiction. ✓

PYQ-4 (CBSE 2017). PA and PB are tangents to a circle with centre O from external point P. If ∠APB = 60°, find ∠OAB.

∠AOB = 120°; ∠OAB = ∠OBA = 30°.

PYQ-5 (CBSE 2016). In figure, PA and PB are tangents from P to circle with centre O. Show OP is perpendicular bisector of AB.

OA = OB = r, PA = PB ⇒ OAPB has equal pairs ⇒ OP is the axis of symmetry ⇒ OP is perpendicular bisector of AB. ✓

PYQ-6 (CBSE 2015). Two tangents from external point are inclined at 60°. Length of each tangent is 4√3. Find radius.

tan(30°) = r/4√3 ⇒ r = 4√3 × 1/√3 = 4. Radius = 4 cm.

PYQ-7 (CBSE 2014). In figure, common tangent AB of two circles intersects line of centres at point P. Prove triangles OAP and O'BP are similar.

∠A = ∠B = 90° (tangent ⊥ radius). ∠APO = ∠BPO' (common). So triangles similar by AA. ✓

PYQ-8 (CBSE 2013). Prove that a parallelogram circumscribing a circle is a rhombus.

(See LA-3.)

PYQ-9 (CBSE 2012). The length of tangent from a point A at distance 5 cm from centre O of a circle is 4 cm. Find radius.

r² = 25 − 16 = 9 ⇒ r = 3 cm.

PYQ-10 (CBSE 2011). Two tangents from external point P to a circle make angle 60°. Show that they form an equilateral triangle with chord of contact.

PA = PB (equal tangents) and ∠APB = 60° ⇒ △PAB equilateral. ✓

PYQ-11 (CBSE Sample 2023). In figure, AB is a chord of a circle of radius 10 cm. The tangent at A and the chord BA make angle 30°. Find AB.

Alternate segment: ∠ABA' = 30° where A' is opposite end of diameter. Use chord-angle relations. AB = 2r sin(60°) = 10√3 cm.

PYQ-12 (CBSE Sample 2022). The angle between two tangents from an external point P to a circle of radius a is 60°. Find OP.

∠APO = 30°. sin(30°) = a/OP ⇒ OP = a/sin(30°) = 2a.

10.32 Brain Teasers (BT-1 to BT-10)

BT-1. A circular dartboard has 20 sectors. Each sector is bounded by two radii. How many distinct radii?

20 radii (each separates two adjacent sectors).

BT-2. Can a single tangent line touch two different circles? At how many points?

Yes — common tangent. One point per circle.

BT-3. If three circles are mutually externally tangent, how many tangents are common to all three?

0 (no line can be tangent to all three at once in general position).

BT-4. A wheel of radius 1 m rolls along a straight road for distance 10 m. How many full rotations?

10/(2π) ≈ 1.59 rotations.

BT-5. A coin of radius r is placed at the centre of a square of side 2r. Does any side touch the coin?

Yes — all four sides are tangent.

BT-6. Two circles touch internally. Smaller radius 3, distance between centres 4. Larger radius?

7 (since larger − smaller = distance).

BT-7. From an external point you draw two tangents. Their lengths are 5 and 5. What guarantees they're equal?

Theorem 10.2 — they're always equal regardless.

BT-8. A circle of radius 5 has inscribed square. Diagonal of square = ?

= Diameter = 10.

BT-9. A circle is inscribed in regular hexagon of side 6. Radius of circle?

= Apothem = side × √3/2 = 6 × √3/2 = 3√3.

BT-10. A line passes through centre of a circle. Is it a tangent?

No — it's a diameter (intersects circle at 2 points).

10.33 Self Test (ST-1 to ST-15)

ST-1. A tangent is perpendicular to ___ at point of contact.

Radius.

ST-2. Tangents from external point are ___.

Equal in length.

ST-3. From an internal point, ___ tangents can be drawn.

Zero.

ST-4. A parallelogram circumscribing a circle is a ___.

Rhombus.

ST-5. Tangent length from P at distance 10 from centre of circle radius 6?

8.

ST-6. ABCD circumscribes circle, AB = 6, BC = 7, CD = 4. AD?

AB + CD = AD + BC ⇒ 10 = AD + 7 ⇒ AD = 3.

ST-7. Two tangents at 60°. ∠AOB?

120°.

ST-8. Diameter is a special kind of ___.

Chord (the longest).

ST-9. Number of common tangents to two circles that touch externally?

3.

ST-10. Number of common tangents to two circles that intersect at two points?

2.

ST-11. Inradius of triangle with sides 3, 4, 5?

1.

ST-12. ∠APB + ∠AOB = ?

180°.

ST-13. Tangent at end of diameter has what relation with diameter?

Perpendicular.

ST-14. Distance of tangent from centre = ?

Radius.

ST-15. Can a tangent intersect circle at 2 points?

No. Then it would be a secant.

10.34 Mixed Practice (MP-1 to MP-20)

MP-1. A tangent to circle of radius 7 has length 24 from point P. OP = ?

25.

MP-2. OP = 17, r = 8. PT = ?

15.

MP-3. Two parallel tangents to circle r = 4. Distance?

8.

MP-4. PA = PB = 6, ∠APB = 60°. AB = ?

6 (equilateral).

MP-5. If two circles touch externally, r=3, R=5. OO' = ?

8.

MP-6. If two circles touch internally, r=3, R=8. OO' = ?

5.

MP-7. Common ext tangent of circles r=2, R=8, d=10?

L = √(100 − 36) = 8.

MP-8. Triangle ABC inscribed in circle radius 5. ∠A = 30°. BC = ?

BC = 2R sin A = 10 × 1/2 = 5.

MP-9. Circle inscribed in square of side 8. Area of circle?

r = 4 ⇒ Area = 16π.

MP-10. Equilateral triangle inscribed in circle of radius 4. Side?

4√3.

MP-11. Chord of length 16 in circle of radius 10. Perp distance from centre?

√(100 − 64) = 6.

MP-12. Angle subtended at centre by chord = ?

Twice the angle at any point on major arc.

MP-13. PT² = PA × PB (secant-tangent). True/False?

True.

MP-14. From external P, ∠APB = 90°, PA = r. r? (in terms of PA)

r = PA.

MP-15. Quadrilateral with all sides tangent and equal diagonals is a ___.

Square.

MP-16. Sum of opposite angles of cyclic quad?

180°.

MP-17. Inradius of equilateral triangle side 12?

r = 12/(2√3) = 2√3.

MP-18. Circumradius of equilateral triangle side 12?

R = 12/√3 = 4√3.

MP-19. R = 2r in equilateral. True/False?

True.

MP-20. A regular polygon with n sides has incircle. All sides are ___ to incircle.

Tangent.

10.35 Final Drill (FD-1 to FD-10)

FD-1. PA = 5, PB = 5, ∠APB = 90°. AB?

5√2.

FD-2. Tangents at ends of diameter are ___.

Parallel.

FD-3. OP = 13, OT = 5 (T point of contact). PT?

12.

FD-4. r = 6, OP = 10. PT?

8.

FD-5. PT = 12, PO = 20. r?

16.

FD-6. ABCD tangential, sides 5, 6, 7, 8 (in order). Are these compatible?

AB + CD = 5+7 = 12 = AD + BC = 8+6 = 14? No, 12 ≠ 14 ⇒ NOT tangential.

FD-7. Three circles in a row, each tangent to next. Radii 2, 3, 4. Distance between centres of 1st and 3rd?

(2+3) + (3+4) = 5 + 7 = 12.

FD-8. Tangent length from external P at distance 3r from centre?

√(9r² − r²) = 2r√2.

FD-9. Two tangents from same external point are always ___.

Equal.

FD-10. The line joining centre and external point bisects the angle between the two tangents. True/False?

True.

🏆 Chapter 10 Mastery Certificate

You have learned: tangent definition, Theorems 10.1 & 10.2, equal tangent lengths, tangential quadrilaterals, parallelogram→rhombus, inradius formulas, common tangents between circles, alternate segment theorem, and power of a point. Excellent work — you are now ready for Chapter 11: Areas Related to Circles! 🎉

10.36 Last-Minute Revision (LMR-1 to LMR-15)

LMR-1. Quick formula: Tangent length

PT² = OP² − r².

LMR-2. Quick formula: Angle between two tangents and central angle.

∠APB + ∠AOB = 180°.

LMR-3. Tangents from external point — equal.

PA = PB always.

LMR-4. Tangent and radius at point of contact — perpendicular.

OT ⊥ tangent at T.

LMR-5. Tangential quadrilateral property.

AB + CD = AD + BC (Pitot).

LMR-6. Parallelogram circumscribing circle → Rhombus.

Combine AB = CD with AB + CD = AD + BC.

LMR-7. Number of tangents at different positions.

Inside: 0. On: 1. Outside: 2.

LMR-8. Inradius of right triangle.

r = (a + b − c)/2 where c is hypotenuse.

LMR-9. Common ext tangent length (touching externally).

L = 2√(Rr).

LMR-10. Concentric circles, chord of outer tangent to inner.

Half-chord = √(R² − r²); chord = 2√(R² − r²).

LMR-11. Secant-tangent (power of point).

PT² = PA × PB.

LMR-12. Alternate segment theorem.

Angle between tangent and chord = inscribed angle in alternate segment.

LMR-13. Tangents at ends of diameter — parallel.

Two lines perp. to same line are parallel.

LMR-14. OP bisects ∠APB.

Line joining external point and centre bisects angle between tangents.

LMR-15. OP is perpendicular bisector of chord of contact AB.

OAPB symmetric across OP.

💡 Exam Tip

Always identify: (1) Where is the external point? (2) Mark the radius perpendicular to tangent. (3) Use Pythagoras in right triangle OTP. (4) Apply equal tangents property when two tangents meet at a point. These four steps solve 90% of Ch10 problems!

10.37 Extra Examples for Mastery (EX-1 to EX-12)

EX-1. From an external point at distance 17 cm, the tangent length is 15 cm. Find radius.

r = √(17² − 15²) = √(289 − 225) = √64 = 8 cm.

EX-2. Two circles of radii 8 and 6 cm have centres 10 cm apart. Are they intersecting?

Sum of radii = 14, difference = 2. Distance 10 lies between 2 and 14 ⇒ they intersect at two points.

EX-3. PA and PB are tangents to circle of radius 6 from external point P, OP = 10. Find PA and ∠OPA.

PA = √(100 − 36) = 8. sin(∠OPA) = 6/10 ⇒ ∠OPA = sin⁻¹(0.6) ≈ 36.87°.

EX-4. A circle inscribed in triangle of sides 13, 14, 15. Find inradius.

s = 21. Area by Heron's = √(21 × 8 × 7 × 6) = √7056 = 84. r = 84/21 = 4.

EX-5. Find tangent length from origin to circle x² + y² − 6x − 8y + 9 = 0.

L² = power of point = 0 + 0 + 9 = 9 ⇒ L = 3.

EX-6. Two tangents from external P make 40°. Find ∠OAB.

∠APB = 40°, ∠AOB = 140°, ∠OAB = (180° − 140°)/2 = 20°.

EX-7. Circle of radius 10 is inscribed in triangle of perimeter 60. Area?

Area = r × s = 10 × 30 = 300 sq units.

EX-8. Hexagon inscribed in circle of radius 6. Perimeter?

Side = 6, perimeter = 36.

EX-9. Chord of length 10 in circle of radius 13. Distance from centre?

√(169 − 25) = 12.

EX-10. Tangent at end A of chord AB makes angle 50° with AB. ∠ACB (in alternate segment)?

= 50° (alternate segment theorem).

EX-11. Circle radius 5 with two parallel chords on same side of centre, lengths 8 and 6. Distance between chords?

d₁ = √(25 − 16) = 3, d₂ = √(25 − 9) = 4. Distance = 4 − 3 = 1.

EX-12. From external P, ∠APB = 2θ, PA = ℓ, radius r. Show ℓ = r cot θ.

∠OPA = θ, tan θ = r/ℓ ⇒ ℓ = r/tan θ = r cot θ. ✓

🌟 You Did It! Bridge to Chapter 11

You've mastered all the theorems and tangent properties for circles! Up next: Chapter 11 — Areas Related to Circles, where we'll calculate areas of sectors, segments, and intricate shapes built from circles. The skills you developed here will help you visualize those regions clearly.

10.38 Closing Summary

📚 Chapter 10 Quick Recap

  • Tangent: Line touching circle at exactly one point.
  • Theorem 10.1: Tangent ⊥ Radius at point of contact.
  • Theorem 10.2: Tangents from external point are equal.
  • Tangent length: PT = √(OP² − r²).
  • Tangential quadrilateral: AB + CD = AD + BC.
  • Parallelogram circumscribing circle: Rhombus.
  • Angles: ∠APB + ∠AOB = 180°.

Well done! On to Chapter 11. 🚀

🏁 Final Thought

Circles surround us — from wheels to clocks to coins. Understanding tangents and how lines interact with circles is a powerful skill in geometry, physics, and engineering. Keep practicing and you'll always be one step ahead!

— End of Chapter 10 —

✅ Practice Daily

Review the theorems weekly.

★ Chapter 10 Complete ★

Chapter 11 — Areas Related to Circles

🎯 Welcome!

Hi! Now that you understand circles and tangents, let's measure the regions they create. Imagine a pizza — the whole circle is the pizza, a slice is a sector, the crust at the edge of a slice is the arc, and the curved-edge piece of one slice is a segment. By the end of this chapter, you'll calculate any of these areas with confidence!

11.1 Table of Contents

  • 11.2 Recap: Circumference & Area
  • 11.3 Value of π
  • 11.4 Sector of a Circle
  • 11.5 Length of Arc
  • 11.6 Area of Sector
  • 11.7 Segment of a Circle
  • 11.8 Area of Segment
  • 11.9 Minor vs Major Sectors
  • 11.10 Examples 1–10
  • 11.11 Combination of Figures
  • 11.12 NCERT Ex 11.1
  • 11.13 NCERT Ex 11.2
  • 11.14 MCQs (15)
  • 11.15 Extended Examples
  • 11.16 HOTS
  • 11.17 PYQs
  • 11.18 Application Marathon
  • 11.19 Case Studies
  • 11.20 Glossary & Recap
  • 11.21 Bridge to Ch12

11.2 Recap — Circumference & Area

From earlier classes:

  • Circumference (perimeter of a circle) = 2πr = πd, where d = 2r is the diameter.
  • Area of a circle = πr².

Think of circumference as the length of a string that wraps once around the circle, and area as the amount of paint needed to fill it!

📐 Key Formulas

Circumference = 2πr

Area = πr²

Diameter = 2r

11.3 Value of π (Pi)

π (pi) is an irrational number — its decimal goes on forever without repeating:

π ≈ 3.14159265…

For board exam problems, we commonly use:

  • π = 22/7 (when the question mentions radius like 7, 14, 21, 28, 35…)
  • π = 3.14 (when decimal answers are expected)

👉 Always check what value of π the question asks for!

11.4 Sector of a Circle

A sector is the region enclosed by two radii of a circle and the arc between them. Think of a slice of pizza!

The angle θ at the centre between the two radii is the sector angle.

11.5 Length of an Arc

If the angle at the centre is θ° (degrees) and radius is r:

The full circle (θ = 360°) has circumference 2πr. So a fraction θ/360° of the circle gives:

📐 Length of Arc

Arc length = (θ/360°) × 2πr

📝 Quick Example: Arc length

Find arc length of sector with θ = 60° and r = 21 cm. (Use π = 22/7)

Arc = (60/360) × 2 × (22/7) × 21 = (1/6) × 132 = 22 cm.

11.6 Area of a Sector

Similarly, area of sector is the fraction θ/360° of the full circle area πr²:

📐 Area of Sector

Area = (θ/360°) × πr²

Equivalently: Area = (1/2) × r × ℓ, where ℓ is the arc length.

📝 Quick Example: Sector area

θ = 60°, r = 21 cm. (π = 22/7)

Area = (60/360) × (22/7) × 21² = (1/6) × (22/7) × 441 = (1/6) × 1386 = 231 cm².

11.7 Segment of a Circle

A segment is the region between a chord and the arc cut off by the chord.

  • Minor segment — smaller part (smaller arc + chord).
  • Major segment — larger part (larger arc + chord).

11.8 Area of Segment

The area of a segment = Area of sector − Area of triangle formed by chord and two radii.

📐 Area of Minor Segment

Area of minor segment = Area of sector OAB − Area of △OAB

= (θ/360°) × πr² − (1/2) × r² × sin θ

For major segment: Area of major segment = πr² − Area of minor segment.

📝 Example: Area of segment

In a circle of radius 10 cm, find the area of minor segment cut off by chord that subtends 90° at the centre. (Take π = 3.14)

Area of sector = (90/360) × 3.14 × 100 = 78.5 cm².

Area of △OAB = (1/2) × 10 × 10 × sin 90° = 50 cm².

Area of segment = 78.5 − 50 = 28.5 cm².

11.9 Minor vs Major Sectors

When chord divides the circle into two sectors with angles θ and 360° − θ:

  • Minor sector: Angle < 180°, smaller area.
  • Major sector: Angle > 180°, larger area.

Area of minor sector + Area of major sector = πr² (total circle area).

11.10 Worked Examples (1 to 10)

📝 Example 1. Find area of sector of radius 7 cm with angle 60°. (π = 22/7)

Area = (60/360) × (22/7) × 49 = (1/6) × 154 = 25.67 cm² (approximately 25⅔ cm²).

📝 Example 2. Arc length of sector r = 14, θ = 90°. (π = 22/7)

Arc = (90/360) × 2 × (22/7) × 14 = (1/4) × 88 = 22 cm.

📝 Example 3. Area of sector r = 14, θ = 90°. (π = 22/7)

Area = (90/360) × (22/7) × 196 = (1/4) × 616 = 154 cm².

📝 Example 4. A chord of 60° in circle radius 7 — find minor segment area. (π = 22/7)

Sector area = (60/360) × (22/7) × 49 = 25.67 cm².

Triangle area = (1/2) × 49 × sin 60° = 24.5 × (√3/2) ≈ 21.22 cm².

Segment ≈ 4.45 cm².

📝 Example 5. Find perimeter of sector with r = 10.5 cm and θ = 60°. (π = 22/7)

Arc = (60/360) × 2 × (22/7) × 10.5 = (1/6) × 66 = 11.

Perimeter = 2r + arc = 21 + 11 = 32 cm.

📝 Example 6. A pendulum swings through an angle of 30° and describes an arc of length 11 cm. Find pendulum length. (π = 22/7)

11 = (30/360) × 2 × (22/7) × r ⇒ 11 = (1/12) × (44r/7) ⇒ r = 21 cm.

📝 Example 7. Area of major segment when minor segment area is 28.5 cm² and circle area is 314 cm².

Major segment area = 314 − 28.5 = 285.5 cm².

📝 Example 8. A square of side 14 cm has a circle inscribed in it. Area between square and circle?

Area of square = 196. Area of circle = π × 49 = (22/7) × 49 = 154. Difference = 42 cm².

📝 Example 9. A circle is inscribed in a square. Square diagonal is 28 cm. Find area of circle.

Side of square = 28/√2 = 14√2. Diameter of inscribed circle = side = 14√2 ⇒ r = 7√2. Area = π × 98 = (22/7) × 98 = 308 cm².

📝 Example 10. Two concentric circles have radii 7 and 14. Area between them?

Area = π(14² − 7²) = (22/7) × 147 = 462 cm².

11.11 Combinations of Figures

Many problems combine circles with squares, triangles, or other shapes. Strategy:

  1. Identify each individual shape.
  2. Calculate area of each.
  3. Add or subtract as the figure demands.

11.12 NCERT Exercise 11.1 (Selected)

Q1. Find area of sector r = 6, θ = 60°. (π = 22/7)

(60/360) × (22/7) × 36 = (1/6) × (792/7) = 132/7 ≈ 18.86 cm².

Q2. Arc length r = 21, θ = 60°.

(60/360) × 2 × (22/7) × 21 = 22 cm.

Q3. Sector area when circumference is 22 cm and angle 90°.

2πr = 22 ⇒ r = 7/2. Area = (90/360) × π × (7/2)² = π × 49/16 ≈ 9.625 cm².

11.13 NCERT Exercise 11.2 (Selected)

Q1. Find area of segment chord 90°, r = 14.

Sector = (90/360) × (22/7) × 196 = 154. Triangle = 98. Segment = 56 cm².

Q2. Find area of segment chord 120°, r = 12. (π = 3.14)

Sector = (120/360) × 3.14 × 144 = 150.72. Triangle = (1/2) × 144 × sin 120° = 72 × (√3/2) ≈ 62.35. Segment ≈ 88.37 cm².

11.14 Multiple Choice (MCQ-1 to MCQ-15)

MCQ-1. Area of circle radius 7?

(a) 49 (b) 154 (c) 22 (d) 14

(b) 154 cm².

MCQ-2. Circumference radius 14?

(a) 44 (b) 22 (c) 88 (d) 56

(c) 88 cm.

MCQ-3. Area of sector with θ = 90° and r = 14?

(a) 154 (b) 308 (c) 77 (d) 22

(a) 154 cm².

MCQ-4. Arc length for θ = 60°, r = 21?

(a) 11 (b) 22 (c) 33 (d) 44

(b) 22 cm.

MCQ-5. Perimeter of sector r = 7, arc = 22.

(a) 28 (b) 36 (c) 14 (d) 22

(b) 36 cm. (2r + arc = 14 + 22)

MCQ-6. Area of segment = Area of ___ − Area of triangle.

(a) Square (b) Sector (c) Triangle (d) Circle

(b) Sector.

MCQ-7. Major segment area when minor is 25 and circle area 100?

(a) 25 (b) 50 (c) 75 (d) 100

(c) 75.

MCQ-8. Area between square side 14 and inscribed circle?

(a) 28 (b) 42 (c) 56 (d) 70

(b) 42.

MCQ-9. Ratio of areas of two circles whose radii are 1:2?

(a) 1:2 (b) 1:4 (c) 2:1 (d) 4:1

(b) 1:4.

MCQ-10. Diameter of circle whose area is 154?

(a) 7 (b) 14 (c) 21 (d) 28

(b) 14.

MCQ-11. Area of quadrant of circle radius 14?

(a) 77 (b) 154 (c) 308 (d) 22

(b) 154.

MCQ-12. Area of semicircle radius 7?

(a) 11 (b) 77 (c) 154 (d) 22

(b) 77.

MCQ-13. Area of sector r = 6, θ = 30°? (π = 22/7)

(a) 18 (b) 66/7 (c) 132/7 (d) 22

(b) 66/7 ≈ 9.43.

MCQ-14. Minor sector + Major sector = ?

(a) πr (b) πr² (c) 2πr (d) 2πr²

(b) πr².

MCQ-15. Angle of 1 minute on clock represents what sector angle?

(a) 1° (b) 6° (c) 30° (d) 60°

(b) 6° (since 360°/60 = 6°).

11.15 Common Mistakes

  • ❌ Confusing arc length formula with area formula. Arc uses 2πr; area uses πr².
  • ❌ Forgetting to convert degrees in sin θ (for segment area).
  • ❌ Forgetting to multiply by (θ/360°).
  • ❌ Using r when diameter is given.
  • ❌ Forgetting 2r in perimeter of sector.

11.16 Glossary

  • Arc: Portion of circumference.
  • Sector: Region bounded by two radii and arc.
  • Segment: Region bounded by chord and arc.
  • π: Ratio of circumference to diameter; π ≈ 22/7 or 3.14.

11.17 Extended Examples (EM-1 to EM-25)

📝 EM-1. The minute hand of a clock is 10 cm long. Find area swept in 5 minutes. (π = 3.14)

In 60 min, angle = 360°. In 5 min, angle = 30°. Area = (30/360) × 3.14 × 100 = 26.17 cm².

📝 EM-2. Minute hand 14 cm, find area swept in 10 min. (π = 22/7)

Angle in 10 min = 60°. Area = (60/360) × (22/7) × 196 = (1/6) × 616 = 102.67 cm².

📝 EM-3. Hour hand 7 cm. Area swept in 1 hour.

In 12 h, hour hand traces 360°. In 1 h, angle = 30°. Area = (30/360) × (22/7) × 49 = (1/12) × 154 = 12.83 cm².

📝 EM-4. A horse is tethered with rope 14 m in a corner of a rectangular field. Area horse can graze?

Corner gives quarter circle. Area = (1/4) × π × 14² = (1/4) × (22/7) × 196 = 154 m².

📝 EM-5. Horse tethered with 7 m rope in middle of side of square field of side 20 m. Grazing area?

Half circle of radius 7: Area = (1/2) × (22/7) × 49 = 77 m².

📝 EM-6. A car wheel has radius 28 cm. How many revolutions per minute if car moves 66 km/h?

Speed = 66 km/h = 66000 m/h = 1100 m/min. Circumference = 2 × (22/7) × 0.28 = 1.76 m. RPM = 1100/1.76 = 625.

📝 EM-7. Find area swept by a 14 cm minute hand in 35 min.

Angle = (35/60) × 360° = 210°. Area = (210/360) × (22/7) × 196 = (7/12) × 616 = 359.33 cm².

📝 EM-8. The perimeter of a sector is 25 cm and radius is 5. Find arc length and area.

Arc = 25 − 10 = 15. Area = (1/2) × r × arc = (1/2) × 5 × 15 = 37.5 cm².

📝 EM-9. A pendulum of length 14 cm swings through 60°. Find arc length.

Arc = (60/360) × 2 × (22/7) × 14 = (1/6) × 88 = 14.67 cm.

📝 EM-10. A circular flower bed has diameter 14 m. Find perimeter.

Circumference = π × 14 = (22/7) × 14 = 44 m.

📝 EM-11. A wire is bent into a circle of radius 14. If straightened, length?

= 2π × 14 = 88 cm.

📝 EM-12. A square of side 7 has a quadrant of circle of radius 7 drawn from one corner. Area of shaded (square minus quadrant)?

Square area = 49. Quadrant = (1/4) × (22/7) × 49 = 38.5. Shaded = 10.5 cm².

📝 EM-13. Four quadrants of radius 7 are drawn inside a square of side 14, at each corner. Area of remaining (uncovered) region?

Four quadrants = full circle of radius 7 = (22/7) × 49 = 154. Square = 196. Remaining = 42 cm².

📝 EM-14. Area between two concentric circles of radii 21 and 14?

= π(441 − 196) = (22/7) × 245 = 770 cm².

📝 EM-15. Diameter of a circle is to be increased to triple its current value. By what factor does area increase?

Area scales as r². So area becomes 9 times.

📝 EM-16. The wheel of a car makes 1000 revolutions to cover 1.76 km. Find radius.

Circumference = 1760/1000 = 1.76 m. 2πr = 1.76 ⇒ r = 1.76 × 7/44 = 0.28 m = 28 cm.

📝 EM-17. A circle of radius 21 is divided into 6 equal sectors. Area of each sector?

θ = 60°. Area = (60/360) × (22/7) × 441 = 231 cm².

📝 EM-18. Length of arc of sector with angle 120° and r = 6?

= (120/360) × 2 × (22/7) × 6 = (1/3) × (264/7) = 88/7 ≈ 12.57 cm.

📝 EM-19. Half a circle of radius 7. Perimeter (including diameter)?

Semicircle perimeter = π r + 2r = (22/7) × 7 + 14 = 22 + 14 = 36 cm.

📝 EM-20. A pulley has radius 10 cm. Belt wraps half its circumference. Length of belt in contact?

= π × 10 = 10π ≈ 31.4 cm.

📝 EM-21. A circular park has diameter 56 m. Find circumference.

= π × 56 = (22/7) × 56 = 176 m.

📝 EM-22. Two circles have areas in ratio 9:16. Ratio of radii?

= 3:4.

📝 EM-23. Two circles have circumferences in ratio 2:3. Ratio of areas?

= 4:9.

📝 EM-24. A rope encloses a square of side 20. If rebent as circle, area?

Perimeter = 80. Circumference = 80 ⇒ r = 40/π. Area = π × (40/π)² = 1600/π ≈ 509.3 sq units.

📝 EM-25. A wheel rotates 11 times to cover 220 m. Find radius. (π = 22/7)

Circumference = 20 m. 2πr = 20 ⇒ r = 20 × 7/44 = 70/22 = 35/11 ≈ 3.18 m.

11.18 HOTS (Higher Order Thinking)

🧠 HOTS-1. A square is inscribed in a circle of radius r. Find ratio of area of circle to area of square.

Side of square = r√2 (since diagonal = 2r). Square area = 2r². Circle = πr². Ratio = π:2.

🧠 HOTS-2. From each corner of a square of side 14, a quadrant of radius 7 is removed. Find remaining area.

Quadrants total = (4 × ¼) × π × 49 = 154. Square = 196. Remaining = 42 cm².

🧠 HOTS-3. A circular grass plot is surrounded by a path of width 2 m all around. If the radius of plot is 7, find area of path.

Outer r = 9, inner r = 7. Path = π(81 − 49) = 32π = (22/7) × 32 = 100.57 m².

🧠 HOTS-4. An equilateral triangle of side 14 has a circle inscribed. Find shaded region (triangle minus circle).

Triangle area = (√3/4) × 196 = 49√3 ≈ 84.87. Inradius = a/(2√3) = 14/(2√3) = 7/√3. Circle area = π × 49/3 = 154/3 ≈ 51.33. Shaded ≈ 33.54 cm².

🧠 HOTS-5. Three circles of radius 7 touch each other externally. Find area enclosed between them.

Centres form equilateral triangle of side 14. Triangle area = (√3/4)(14)² = 49√3 ≈ 84.87. Three sectors each (60°/360°) × π × 49 = (1/6) × 154 = 25.67. Total sectors = 77. Enclosed = 84.87 − 77 ≈ 7.87 cm².

11.19 Previous Year Questions (PYQ-1 to PYQ-10)

PYQ-1 (CBSE 2020). Find area of sector r = 6, θ = 60°. (π = 22/7)

(60/360) × (22/7) × 36 = 132/7 ≈ 18.86 cm².

PYQ-2 (CBSE 2019). Find perimeter of sector r = 7, θ = 90°. (π = 22/7)

Arc = (90/360) × 2 × (22/7) × 7 = 11. Perimeter = 14 + 11 = 25 cm.

PYQ-3 (CBSE 2018). Area of minor segment cut off by chord of length 10 cm in circle r = 10. (π = 3.14)

Chord = r√2 ⇒ θ = 90°. Sector = (1/4) × 3.14 × 100 = 78.5. Triangle = 50. Segment = 28.5 cm².

PYQ-4 (CBSE 2017). The minute hand of a clock is 14 cm long. Area swept in 5 min?

5 min → 30°. Area = (30/360) × (22/7) × 196 = 51.33 cm².

PYQ-5 (CBSE 2016). Find length of arc of sector r = 21, θ = 120°. (π = 22/7)

Arc = (120/360) × 2 × (22/7) × 21 = (1/3) × 132 = 44 cm.

PYQ-6 (CBSE 2015). Find area of sector with arc length 22 cm and radius 21 cm.

Area = (1/2) × r × ℓ = (1/2) × 21 × 22 = 231 cm².

PYQ-7 (CBSE 2014). Find area of segment, r = 21 cm, chord subtends 60° at centre. (π = 22/7)

Sector = (60/360) × (22/7) × 441 = 231. Triangle (equilateral!) = (√3/4) × 441 = 110.25√3 ≈ 190.95. Segment ≈ 40.05 cm².

PYQ-8 (CBSE 2013). A square has each side 4 cm. With each vertex as centre, a circle is drawn with radius 2 cm. Area of shaded region (square minus four quadrants)?

Square = 16. Four quadrants = full circle of r = 2 = π × 4 = 22 × 4/7 = 88/7 ≈ 12.57. Shaded ≈ 3.43 cm².

PYQ-9 (CBSE 2012). A wheel makes 4500 revolutions per minute and the diameter is 70 cm. Find speed in km/h.

Circumference = π × 70 = 220 cm. Distance per min = 4500 × 220 = 990000 cm = 9.9 km. Per hour = 594 km/h.

PYQ-10 (CBSE 2011). A pendulum 21 cm long swings through 30°. Find arc.

= (30/360) × 2 × (22/7) × 21 = (1/12) × 132 = 11 cm.

11.20 Application Marathon (AM-1 to AM-20)

AM-1. A car wheel has diameter 70 cm. How many revolutions to cover 11 km?

Circumference = 220 cm = 2.2 m. Revolutions = 11000/2.2 = 5000.

AM-2. A circular table has diameter 1.4 m. Cost to polish at ₹15/m²?

Area = (22/7) × 0.49 = 1.54 m². Cost = ₹23.10.

AM-3. Tin can of radius 10.5, height 14. Find lateral and base area.

Base = π × (10.5)² = (22/7) × 110.25 = 346.5. Lateral = 2πrh = 2 × (22/7) × 10.5 × 14 = 924.

AM-4. Race track is two semicircles connected by parallel lines. Inner radius 35, outer 42. Length of straight = 90. Find area of track.

Straight part (2 strips) = 2 × 90 × 7 = 1260. Semicircles annulus = π(42² − 35²) = π × 539 = 1694. Total ≈ 2954 m².

AM-5. A bicycle wheel rolls 100 m in 100 revolutions. Find diameter.

Circumference = 1 m ⇒ diameter = 1/π ≈ 0.318 m.

AM-6. Pizza of diameter 14. Find area.

= (22/7) × 49 = 154 cm². (radius = 7)

AM-7. Fan blade swept area: blade length 60 cm, angle 60°.

= (60/360) × (22/7) × 3600 = 1885.71 cm² ≈ 0.189 m².

AM-8. Circular running track outer r = 30, inner r = 25. Width and area?

Width = 5. Area = π(900 − 625) = 275π ≈ 863.94 m².

AM-9. Tractor wheel radius 0.7 m. How many revolutions to travel 22 km?

Circumference = 2 × (22/7) × 0.7 = 4.4 m. Revolutions = 22000/4.4 = 5000.

AM-10. Goat tied at corner of triangular field side 7, with rope 7. Area grazed?

Equilateral triangle vertex angle = 60°. Sector area = (60/360) × (22/7) × 49 = 25.67 m².

AM-11. Goat tied at vertex of square field, rope 5. Area?

= (90/360) × π × 25 = (1/4) × (550/7) = 19.64 m².

AM-12. A flowerbed is in shape of quadrant of radius 14. Find area and perimeter.

Area = (1/4) × (22/7) × 196 = 154 m². Perimeter = 2r + arc = 28 + 22 = 50 m.

AM-13. CD has radius 5. Area?

= (22/7) × 25 = 78.57 cm².

AM-14. Coin of diameter 2.8 cm. Circumference?

= (22/7) × 2.8 = 8.8 cm.

AM-15. Athletics track (oval) two semicircles end + rectangle. Inner straight = 84, inner curve radius = 28. Inner perimeter?

= 2 × 84 + 2π × 28 = 168 + 176 = 344 m.

AM-16. Diameter to triple → Circumference becomes how many times?

3 times (linear scale).

AM-17. Area between square and inscribed circle (side 14)?

= 196 − 154 = 42 cm².

AM-18. Floor of room is 12 × 16 with circular hole of radius 7. Area to be carpeted?

= 192 − 154 = 38 m².

AM-19. Hexagonal park with circle inscribed of radius 14. Side of hexagon (apothem = 14)?

Apothem = side × √3/2 ⇒ side = 28/√3 = 28√3/3 ≈ 16.17 m.

AM-20. A flower bed shaped as circular segment, chord 14, radius 14. Find area.

Chord = r ⇒ equilateral triangle ⇒ θ = 60°. Sector = 102.67. Triangle = (√3/4)(14²) = 49√3 ≈ 84.87. Segment = 17.80 cm².

11.21 Case Study — Designer Wall Clock

📖 A wall clock has minute hand 14 cm and hour hand 7 cm.

Q1. Area swept by minute hand in 1 hour? = full circle = (22/7) × 196 = 616 cm².

Q2. Area swept by hour hand in 1 hour? = (30/360) × (22/7) × 49 = 12.83 cm².

Q3. Distance covered by tip of minute hand in 1 hour? = 2π × 14 = 88 cm.

Q4. Area swept by minute hand in 20 min? = (120/360) × (22/7) × 196 = 205.33 cm².

Q5. If clock face has radius 15 cm, area of face = ? = (22/7) × 225 ≈ 707 cm².

11.22 Case Study — Designer Tile

📖 A tile is square 14 × 14 with 4 quadrants of radius 7 at corners and a circle at centre of radius 3.5.

Q1. Quadrants total area: = π × 49 = 154 cm².

Q2. Centre circle area: = (22/7) × 12.25 = 38.5 cm².

Q3. Square area: = 196 cm².

Q4. Area of design (white region only)? = 196 − 154 − 38.5 = 3.5 cm².

Q5. If tile costs ₹50, what is cost per cm² of design? = 50/3.5 ≈ ₹14.29.

11.23 More Examples (EM-26 to EM-50)

EM-26. Circumference of circle is 44 cm. Find area.

2πr = 44 ⇒ r = 7. Area = (22/7) × 49 = 154 cm².

EM-27. Area of circle is 616 cm². Find circumference.

πr² = 616 ⇒ r² = 196 ⇒ r = 14. Circumference = 2π × 14 = 88 cm.

EM-28. The wheel of a car has diameter 84 cm. How many revolutions per hour for speed 66 km/h?

Circumference = π × 84 = 264 cm = 2.64 m. Speed = 66 km/h = 66000 m/h. Revolutions = 66000/2.64 = 25000 per hour.

EM-29. A circular park has radius 21 m. A path of width 7 m runs along boundary inside. Area of path?

Outer radius = 21, inner = 14. Path = π(441 − 196) = π × 245 = 770 m².

EM-30. Sectors of two circles of radii 7 and 21 each have angle 60°. Difference in areas?

(60/360) × π × (441 − 49) = (1/6) × (22/7) × 392 = 205.33 cm².

EM-31. Find area of sector subtending 200° at centre of circle radius 9. (π = 22/7)

= (200/360) × (22/7) × 81 = (5/9) × (1782/7) = 990/7 ≈ 141.43 cm².

EM-32. Arc subtends 90°, has length 11. Radius?

11 = (1/4) × 2π × r ⇒ r = 22/π = 22 × 7/22 = 7 cm.

EM-33. Length of arc of semi-circle radius 10?

= π × 10 = 31.4 cm.

EM-34. A bicycle wheel rolls and covers 220 m. Each rotation = 88 cm. Number of rotations?

= 22000/88 = 250.

EM-35. A circular protractor has diameter 14. Area?

= (22/7) × 49 = 154 cm².

EM-36. A circle is inscribed in equilateral triangle of side 12. Find area of circle.

r = 12/(2√3) = 2√3. Area = π × 12 = (22/7) × 12 = 264/7 ≈ 37.71 cm².

EM-37. Two circles of radii 7 and 14 touch externally. Area enclosed in the figure of two circles together?

π(49) + π(196) = 49π + 196π = 245π = (22/7) × 245 = 770 cm². (They share no overlap if externally tangent.)

EM-38. A square of side 28 has a circle inscribed. Find shaded (square − circle).

Square = 784. Circle = π × 196 = 616. Shaded = 168 cm².

EM-39. A square of side 14 has a circle circumscribed. Area of circle?

Diagonal = 14√2 ⇒ r = 7√2. Area = π × 98 = (22/7) × 98 = 308 cm².

EM-40. Area between square (side 14) circumscribed about circle (r = 7) and another square (diagonal 14)?

Outer square = 196. Smaller square area = (14²)/2 = 98. Difference = 98 cm².

EM-41. A pendulum makes 12 swings, each swing 30°. Length 50 cm. Total distance traveled by tip.

Each arc = (30/360) × 2π × 50 = (1/12) × 100π = (100π)/12 = 25π/3. 12 arcs = 100π = 314.16 cm.

EM-42. A circular table top has area 1386 cm². Find diameter.

πr² = 1386 ⇒ r² = 441 ⇒ r = 21. Diameter = 42 cm.

EM-43. Sector area = 154, radius = 7. Find angle.

(θ/360) × 154 = 154 ⇒ θ = 360°. (full circle).

EM-44. Sector area = 77, radius = 14. Find angle.

(θ/360) × (22/7) × 196 = 77 ⇒ (θ/360) × 616 = 77 ⇒ θ = 45°.

EM-45. Inscribed regular hexagon in circle radius 6. Hexagon area?

Side = 6. Area = (3√3/2) × 36 = 54√3 ≈ 93.5 cm².

EM-46. A bicycle wheel of radius 35 cm covers 220 m in how many revolutions?

Circumference = 2 × (22/7) × 35 = 220 cm = 2.2 m. Revolutions = 220/2.2 = 100.

EM-47. Roundabout of radius 20 m has flowerbed in centre as smaller circle radius 5. Grass area between (annulus)?

π(400 − 25) = 375π = (22/7) × 375 = 1178.57 m².

EM-48. A circle of radius 7 has 12 equal sectors. Area of each = ?

= (1/12) × (22/7) × 49 = 154/12 ≈ 12.83 cm².

EM-49. Length of belt around two pulleys (both radius 7, centres 21 apart) — for straight portions only.

2 × 21 = 42 cm (two straight portions).

EM-50. A semicircle has perimeter 36 cm. Find radius.

πr + 2r = 36 ⇒ r(π + 2) = 36 ⇒ r = 36/(22/7 + 2) = 36/(36/7) = 7 cm.

11.24 Drill Set (D-1 to D-25)

D-1. Area of circle r = 21?

(22/7) × 441 = 1386 cm².

D-2. Area of circle r = 35?

(22/7) × 1225 = 3850 cm².

D-3. Circumference of circle r = 35?

2 × (22/7) × 35 = 220 cm.
