Closing thought: Probability extends far beyond classrooms — it powers AI, finance, science, and everyday decisions. Master it well!

Final Examples (FE-1 to FE-15)

FE-1. A box has 30 chits numbered 1-30. P(odd number divisible by 3)?
Soln: Odd multiples of 3: 3, 9, 15, 21, 27 = 5. P = 5/30 = 1/6.
FE-2. A bag has 14 R, 8 W, 6 B. P(red or blue)?
Soln: 20/28 = 5/7.
FE-3. Two dice. P(both even or both odd)?
Soln: Both even: 9. Both odd: 9. Total = 18. P = 18/36 = 1/2.
FE-4. 3 coins. P(more heads than tails)?
Soln: 2 or 3 heads: 3 + 1 = 4. P = 4/8 = 1/2.
FE-5. A bag of 40 marbles — 10 red, 12 blue, 8 green, 10 yellow. P(red or yellow)?
Soln: 20/40 = 1/2.
FE-6. Two dice. P(at least one ace)? (ace = 1)
Soln: 1 − (5/6)² = 11/36.
FE-7. Bag: 6 white, 4 black. P(white)?
Soln: 3/5.
FE-8. A card. P(black OR face)?
Soln: Black 26 + Face 12 − Black Face 6 = 32. P = 32/52 = 8/13.
FE-9. 2 dice. P(differ by 1)?
Soln: Pairs (1,2),(2,1),(2,3),(3,2),...,(5,6),(6,5) → 10. P = 10/36 = 5/18.
FE-10. A bag has 15 balls, all coloured. P(red) = 0 means?
Soln: No red balls in the bag.
FE-11. A coin tossed twice. P(getting 1 head OR 1 tail at least)?
Soln: Always at least one of either, except HH and TT. Outcomes {HT, TH} satisfy both. Actually "at least 1 head AND at least 1 tail" → 2/4 = 1/2. (Question interpretation.)
FE-12. 50 tickets 1-50. P(neither prime nor composite)?
Soln: Such number is 1. P = 1/50.
FE-13. A card. P(numbered card)? (i.e., 2 through 10)
Soln: 9 ranks × 4 suits = 36. P = 36/52 = 9/13.
FE-14. 2 dice. P(both dice show prime)?
Soln: 3 primes × 3 = 9. P = 9/36 = 1/4.
FE-15. Two coins and a die. P(both heads AND die shows 6)?
Soln: 1/4 × 1/6 = 1/24.
🎓 Congratulations on completing all 14 chapters!

Last Sprint (LS-1 to LS-10)

LS-1. A coin tossed. P(getting H or T)?
Ans: 1 (certain).
LS-2. P(neither H nor T)?
Ans: 0 (impossible).
LS-3. Bag: 5R 6G 9B. P(blue)?
Ans: 9/20.
LS-4. A die. P(odd OR multiple of 3)?
Ans: Odd: 1,3,5. Multiples of 3: 3,6. Union {1,3,5,6} = 4. P = 2/3.
LS-5. A card. P(spade OR red)?
Ans: Spade 13 + Red 26 = 39 (no overlap, spades are black). P = 39/52 = 3/4.
LS-6. 2 coins. P(1 H 1 T)?
Ans: 2/4 = 1/2.
LS-7. 2 dice. P(sum = 3)?
Ans: (1,2),(2,1) = 2. P = 1/18.
LS-8. 2 dice. P(both = even)?
Ans: 9/36 = 1/4.
LS-9. Tickets 1-100. P(div by 11)?
Ans: 9 multiples (11..99). P = 9/100.
LS-10. A bag has 24 balls. P(red) = 1/4. Number of red balls?
Ans: 6.
End of Chapter 14 — Probability! 🎉 You've completed the entire NCERT Class 10 Maths syllabus. Best of luck for your boards!
Probability — Mastered!
14 Chapters Completed Successfully!
Best of luck for your CBSE Class 10 Mathematics board exam!
Course complete!
🎓 All the best!
🧠 Master Mind MapEverything in Chapter 14 — at a glance

🗺️ Chapter 14 — Probability · Mind Map

📌 The 5 Most-Asked Probability Setups (memorise these!)

  1. 1 die: S = {1,2,3,4,5,6}.   P(prime) = 3/6, P(even) = 3/6, P(>4) = 2/6.
  2. 2 dice: 36 outcomes. P(sum = 7) = 6/36 = 1/6, P(doublet) = 6/36 = 1/6.
  3. 52 cards: P(king) = 4/52, P(red) = 26/52, P(face card) = 12/52, P(black queen) = 2/52.
  4. 3 coins: 8 outcomes. P(exactly 2 H) = 3/8, P(at least 1 T) = 7/8.
  5. Bag with x red & y blue: P(red) = x / (x + y).

💡 When you can't list outcomes easily, use P(E) = 1 − P(not E).

⭐ All Important Formulas — Class 10 Maths

Complete chapter-wise formula sheet — bookmark this page and revise before every test!

📌 How to use this page: Each chapter lists the most-tested formulas with a one-line meaning. Click any chapter heading in the sidebar to read the full theory + worked examples.

🔢 Chapter 1 — Real Numbers

  • Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic: Every composite number = unique product of primes.
  • HCF × LCM = a × b   (true for TWO numbers only)
  • HCF = product of smallest powers of common primes
  • LCM = product of greatest powers of all primes that occur
  • Decimal expansion of p/q (in lowest form) terminates ⇔ q = 2ᵐ × 5ⁿ
  • √p is irrational for every prime p (proof by contradiction)
  • Rational ± Irrational = Irrational;   (non-zero rational) × Irrational = Irrational

📐 Chapter 2 — Polynomials

  • Degree: highest power of x in the polynomial
  • Linear: ax + b (degree 1)  ·  Quadratic: ax² + bx + c (degree 2)  ·  Cubic: ax³ + bx² + cx + d (degree 3)
  • Zero of ax + b = −b/a
  • Quadratic ax² + bx + c with zeroes α, β:
    • Sum of zeroes: α + β = −b/a
    • Product of zeroes: αβ = c/a
  • Cubic ax³ + bx² + cx + d with zeroes α, β, γ:
    • α + β + γ = −b/a
    • αβ + βγ + γα = c/a
    • αβγ = −d/a
  • Form a quadratic from zeroes:   x² − (sum)x + (product) = 0

📊 Chapter 3 — Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables

For two equations   a₁x + b₁y + c₁ = 0   and   a₂x + b₂y + c₂ = 0:

  • Unique solution (lines intersect)   ⇔   a₁/a₂ ≠ b₁/b₂
  • No solution (parallel lines)   ⇔   a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ ≠ c₁/c₂
  • Infinite solutions (coincident lines)   ⇔   a₁/a₂ = b₁/b₂ = c₁/c₂
  • Methods: Graphical, Substitution, Elimination, Cross-multiplication
  • Cross-multiplication:   x/(b₁c₂ − b₂c₁) = y/(c₁a₂ − c₂a₁) = 1/(a₁b₂ − a₂b₁)

📈 Chapter 4 — Quadratic Equations

  • Standard form: ax² + bx + c = 0,   a ≠ 0
  • Quadratic formula:   x = [−b ± √(b² − 4ac)] / (2a)
  • Discriminant:   D = b² − 4ac
    • D > 0 → two distinct real roots
    • D = 0 → two equal real roots (= −b/2a)
    • D < 0 → no real roots
  • Sum of roots: α + β = −b/a  ·  Product: αβ = c/a
  • Methods: Factorisation, Completing the square, Quadratic formula

🔢 Chapter 5 — Arithmetic Progressions (AP)

  • General AP:   a,   a + d,   a + 2d,   a + 3d,   …
  • nth term:   aₙ = a + (n − 1)d
  • Sum of first n terms:   Sₙ = (n/2) · [2a + (n − 1)d]   =   (n/2) · (a + ℓ)   where ℓ = last term
  • Sum of first n natural numbers:   1 + 2 + … + n = n(n + 1)/2
  • nth term from end:   ℓ − (n − 1)d
  • If three nos. in AP: take them as   (a − d), a, (a + d)

📏 Chapter 6 — Triangles (Similarity)

  • Similarity criteria: AA (or AAA), SSS, SAS
  • Basic Proportionality Theorem (BPT / Thales): If a line is drawn parallel to one side of a triangle, it divides the other two sides in the same ratio.
  • Converse of BPT: If a line divides any two sides of a triangle in the same ratio, then the line is parallel to the third side.
  • If △ABC ~ △DEF:   AB/DE = BC/EF = CA/FD
  • Areas of similar triangles:   ar(△ABC) / ar(△DEF) = (AB/DE)² = (BC/EF)² = (CA/FD)²
  • Pythagoras Theorem: In a right triangle, (hypotenuse)² = (base)² + (perpendicular)²
  • Converse of Pythagoras: If c² = a² + b², then the triangle is right-angled (right angle opposite to side c).

📍 Chapter 7 — Coordinate Geometry

  • Distance formula:   d = √[(x₂ − x₁)² + (y₂ − y₁)²]
  • Distance from origin:   √(x² + y²)
  • Section formula (internal division, ratio m : n):
    P = ( (m·x₂ + n·x₁)/(m + n),   (m·y₂ + n·y₁)/(m + n) )
  • Midpoint:   M = ( (x₁ + x₂)/2,   (y₁ + y₂)/2 )
  • Centroid of triangle with vertices (x₁,y₁), (x₂,y₂), (x₃,y₃):
    G = ( (x₁ + x₂ + x₃)/3,   (y₁ + y₂ + y₃)/3 )
  • Area of triangle = ½ · | x₁(y₂ − y₃) + x₂(y₃ − y₁) + x₃(y₁ − y₂) |
  • Collinearity: three points are collinear ⇔ area of triangle they form = 0

📐 Chapter 8 — Introduction to Trigonometry

For a right triangle with angle θ (other than 90°):

  • sin θ = Opposite / Hypotenuse  ·  cos θ = Adjacent / Hypotenuse  ·  tan θ = Opposite / Adjacent
  • cosec θ = 1/sin θ  ·  sec θ = 1/cos θ  ·  cot θ = 1/tan θ
  • tan θ = sin θ / cos θ  ·  cot θ = cos θ / sin θ

Specific angle values:

θ0°30°45°60°90°
sin θ01/21/√2√3/21
cos θ1√3/21/√21/20
tan θ01/√31√3∞

Pythagorean (fundamental) identities:

  • sin²θ + cos²θ = 1
  • 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ
  • 1 + cot²θ = cosec²θ

Complementary angle identities:

  • sin(90° − θ) = cos θ  ·  cos(90° − θ) = sin θ
  • tan(90° − θ) = cot θ  ·  cot(90° − θ) = tan θ
  • sec(90° − θ) = cosec θ  ·  cosec(90° − θ) = sec θ

🗼 Chapter 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry

  • Angle of elevation: angle above the horizontal (looking up).
  • Angle of depression: angle below the horizontal (looking down).
  • Both are equal when measured between the same two points (alternate angles).
  • Height & Distance toolkit:   tan θ = (height) / (horizontal distance)   — choose the trig ratio that contains the known and unknown sides.

⭕ Chapter 10 — Circles

  • Tangent to a circle is perpendicular to the radius at the point of contact.
  • Length of two tangents from an external point are equal.
  • If PA & PB are tangents from external point P to a circle with centre O: PA = PB and OP bisects ∠APB.
  • Number of tangents from a point: outside = 2, on circle = 1, inside = 0.
  • Length of tangent from external point:   PT = √(OP² − r²)   where r = radius.

🍕 Chapter 11 — Areas Related to Circles

  • Circumference of circle: C = 2πr = πd
  • Area of circle: A = πr²
  • Length of arc subtending angle θ° at centre:   ℓ = (θ/360°) × 2πr
  • Area of sector with angle θ°:   Asec = (θ/360°) × πr²   =   ½ × ℓ × r
  • Area of segment (minor) = Area of sector − Area of triangle   =   (θ/360°)πr² − ½ r² sin θ
  • Perimeter of sector = 2r + arc length
  • Use π = 22/7 unless the question states otherwise.

🧊 Chapter 12 — Surface Areas & Volumes

For 3-D solids — units must be consistent (cm × cm² ≠ cm³):

SolidCurved / Lateral Surface AreaTotal Surface AreaVolume
Cuboid (l, b, h)LSA = 2h(l + b)TSA = 2(lb + bh + hl)V = l · b · h
Cube (side a)LSA = 4a²TSA = 6a²V = a³
Cylinder (r, h)CSA = 2πrhTSA = 2πr(r + h)V = πr²h
Cone (r, h, ℓ)CSA = πrℓTSA = πr(ℓ + r)V = ⅓ πr²h
Sphere (r)—TSA = 4πr²V = (4/3) πr³
Hemisphere (r)CSA = 2πr²TSA = 3πr²V = (2/3) πr³
  • Slant height of cone:   ℓ = √(r² + h²)
  • Combined solids: Add CSAs (not TSAs!) and add volumes.
  • Volume of frustum of a cone (radii R, r and height h):   V = (1/3) πh (R² + r² + Rr)
  • CSA of frustum:   π(R + r)ℓ   where ℓ = √[h² + (R − r)²]
  • TSA of frustum:   π(R + r)ℓ + πR² + πr²
  • Melted/recast problems: Volume is conserved → V₁ = V₂.

📊 Chapter 13 — Statistics

Mean of grouped data — three methods:

  • Direct Method:   x̄ = Σ(fᵢxᵢ) / Σfᵢ   (xᵢ = class mark = average of class limits)
  • Assumed-Mean Method:   x̄ = a + Σ(fᵢdᵢ) / Σfᵢ   where dᵢ = xᵢ − a
  • Step-Deviation Method:   x̄ = a + h × [Σ(fᵢuᵢ) / Σfᵢ]   where uᵢ = (xᵢ − a)/h,   h = class width

Mode of grouped data:

  • Modal class = class with highest frequency.
  • Mode = l + [(f₁ − f₀) / (2f₁ − f₀ − f₂)] × h
    where l = lower limit of modal class, f₁ = frequency of modal class, f₀ = freq. of class before, f₂ = freq. of class after, h = class width.

Median of grouped data:

  • Median class = class where cumulative frequency first reaches or exceeds n/2.
  • Median = l + [(n/2 − CF) / f] × h
    where l = lower limit of median class, n = Σfᵢ, CF = cumulative freq. just before median class, f = freq. of median class, h = class width.

Empirical relation:   Mode = 3·Median − 2·Mean   (approximation for moderately skewed data)

Ogive: cumulative-frequency curve (less-than or more-than type). Median = x-coordinate where ogive crosses y = n/2.

🎲 Chapter 14 — Probability

  • Probability of event E:
    P(E) = (Number of favourable outcomes) / (Total number of equally likely outcomes)
  • Range:   0 ≤ P(E) ≤ 1
  • Sure event: P = 1  ·  Impossible event: P = 0
  • Complementary event:   P(E) + P(not E) = 1   ⇒   P(not E) = 1 − P(E)
  • Sum of all probabilities:   Σ P(every outcome) = 1

Standard sample spaces (memorise these!):

  • 1 coin → {H, T} (2 outcomes)  ·  2 coins → 4  ·  3 coins → 8
  • 1 die → 6 outcomes  ·  2 dice → 36 outcomes
  • 52 playing cards = 4 suits × 13 cards = 26 red + 26 black = 12 face cards + 40 number cards

🎯 30-Second Final Cheat Sheet (the most-asked formulas)

  1. Quadratic roots: x = [−b ± √(b² − 4ac)] / 2a
  2. AP nth term: aₙ = a + (n − 1)d
  3. AP sum: Sₙ = (n/2)[2a + (n − 1)d]
  4. Distance: √[(x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)²]
  5. Section formula: P = ((mx₂+nx₁)/(m+n), (my₂+ny₁)/(m+n))
  6. sin²θ + cos²θ = 1,   1+tan²θ = sec²θ,   1+cot²θ = cosec²θ
  7. Area of sector: (θ/360°)·πr²   Arc: (θ/360°)·2πr
  8. Cone volume: ⅓ πr²h   Sphere volume: (4/3)πr³
  9. Mean (step-dev): x̄ = a + h·(Σfu/Σf)
  10. Median: l + ((n/2 − CF)/f) · h
  11. Probability: P(E) = favourable / total

🌟 Print this page and stick it on your study wall — review it every day before the exam! 🌟
